symptoms: need to choose a Kubernetes distribution for on-premise deployment
evaluating kubeadm vs RKE2 vs Talos Linux
unsure which control-plane approach fits the stack
root_cause: On-premise Kubernetes offers three mainstream options: vanilla kubeadm (maximum control, manual upgrades), RKE2 (enterprise hardening, CIS benchmarks, SELinux support), and Talos Linux (immutable, API-driven OS with minimal attack surface). The right choice depends on operational maturity and compliance requirements.
fix: Use kubeadm for full control and when the team has strong Kubernetes ops experience
Use RKE2 for enterprise environments requiring CIS benchmarks, SELinux, or Rancher integration
Use Talos Linux when you want an immutable, minimal OS managed entirely through the Kubernetes API — no SSH, no shell
All three support this stack (Node.js, PostgreSQL, BullMQ, KEDA) without modification

symptoms: LoadBalancer service stuck in Pending on bare-metal cluster
no external IP assigned to LoadBalancer service
cloud load balancer not available on-premise
root_cause: Cloud-managed Kubernetes auto-provisions load balancers via a cloud controller. On bare-metal, no such controller exists, so LoadBalancer services remain in Pending indefinitely without MetalLB or Kube-VIP.
fix: Install MetalLB and configure an IP address pool from your LAN range in a IPAddressPool resource
Alternatively use Kube-VIP in BGP or ARP mode for a lighter-weight option
For BGP mode, configure MetalLB's BGPPeer to point at your router — gives true ECMP load balancing
For Layer 2 (ARP) mode, MetalLB elects a leader node to respond to ARP; simpler but single-node bottleneck
Verify kube-proxy mode is ipvs or iptables — MetalLB requires kube-proxy not to be disabled

symptoms: Ingress or Gateway not routing traffic correctly
need modern traffic management beyond basic Ingress
want HTTP header manipulation, traffic splitting, or mTLS at the gateway
root_cause: The classic Ingress API is limited and varies by controller. The Kubernetes Gateway API is the successor — it separates infrastructure (GatewayClass, Gateway) from routing (HTTPRoute, TCPRoute) and supports advanced traffic management. Cilium implements the Gateway API natively alongside its eBPF-based networking.
fix: Install Cilium as the CNI with Gateway API support: helm install cilium cilium/cilium --set gatewayAPI.enabled=true
Create a GatewayClass referencing cilium, then a Gateway resource for each public entry point
Define HTTPRoute resources to route by hostname, path, header, or weight — attach them to the Gateway
Cilium Gateway API replaces both Ingress and the need for a separate ingress controller
Verify Cilium version ≥ 1.13 for stable Gateway API support

symptoms: V8 heap exceeds container memory limit
container restarts due to out-of-memory errors
root_cause: By default Node.js (V8) sets its heap limit based on the machine's total RAM, not the container's memory limit. In Kubernetes, the container sees the node's full RAM via /proc/meminfo, so V8 may allocate far more heap than the cgroup limit allows, triggering an OOMKill.
fix: Set --max-old-space-size to ~75% of the container's memory limit, e.g. for a 2Gi limit: NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=1536'
Always specify both requests and limits in the Pod spec to ensure the scheduler places pods correctly
Example: requests.memory=512Mi limits.memory=2Gi → set --max-old-space-size=1536
Use Kubernetes Downward API to inject the memory limit into the environment variable dynamically
Monitor heap usage with clinic.js or --expose-gc + custom metrics before tuning

symptoms: PostgreSQL pod data lost after node restart
need high-availability storage for stateful workloads
root_cause: Kubernetes does not provide storage — it requires a CSI driver that provisions PersistentVolumes. On bare-metal, Rook-Ceph aggregates local disks into replicated block/file/object storage, while Longhorn offers a simpler alternative for smaller clusters.
fix: For production: deploy Rook-Ceph — it creates a Ceph cluster from available raw disks and exposes block (RBD), filesystem (CephFS), and object (RGW) storage classes
For simpler setups (≤10 nodes): use Longhorn — it's easier to install and operate with a built-in UI
Map CloudNativePG storage class to a high-performance NVMe-backed Ceph pool for PostgreSQL
Set replication factor ≥ 3 for data durability; tune PG count per pool based on OSD count
Use local PVs (no replication) only for ephemeral workloads like Redis replicas with AOF persistence

symptoms: PostgreSQL failover is manual and slow
database pods not recovering automatically after node failure
root_cause: Running a plain PostgreSQL StatefulSet requires manual management of replication, failover, and backups. CloudNativePG is a Kubernetes operator that manages the full PostgreSQL lifecycle natively — primary election, streaming replication, automated failover, and scheduled backups.
fix: Install CloudNativePG operator: kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cloudnative-pg/cloudnative-pg/release-1.23/releases/cnpg-1.23.0.yaml
Define a Cluster resource with numberOfInstances: 3 for high availability
Set storage.storageClass to a fast (NVMe-backed) Ceph RBD class for the WAL and data volumes
Configure backup.barmanObjectStore pointing at an S3-compatible endpoint (MinIO on-cluster or external)
Enable PgBouncer sidecar via the .spec.managed.roles and pooler resource to manage connection pooling at the operator level

symptoms: BullMQ workers not scaling to match queue depth
worker pods stay at minimum replicas even when queue is backed up
need queue-depth-based autoscaling for background jobs
root_cause: The standard Kubernetes HPA scales on CPU/memory, which doesn't correlate with job queue depth. KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) adds external scalers that can read Redis list length, BullMQ queue depth, or NATS lag to drive HPA-compatible scaling — including scale-to-zero.
fix: Install KEDA: helm install keda kedacore/keda --namespace keda --create-namespace
Create a ScaledObject targeting your worker Deployment with a redis scaler pointing at the BullMQ list key (bull:<queue-name>:wait)
Set minReplicaCount: 0 to scale to zero during idle periods and maxReplicaCount based on max concurrency
Use threshold to define the queue depth per replica (e.g., 10 jobs per worker pod)
For NATS JetStream, use the nats scaler with lagThreshold targeting the consumer's pending message count

symptoms: Redis not highly available — single point of failure
BullMQ jobs lost when Redis pod restarts
need Redis Cluster or Sentinel for production
root_cause: A single Redis pod provides no redundancy. For BullMQ in production, Redis must be deployed as a Cluster (sharded) or with Sentinel (HA with failover). A Redis Operator automates this on Kubernetes.
fix: Use the Redis Operator (OpsTree or Spotahome) to deploy a Redis Cluster or Sentinel setup
For BullMQ, prefer Redis Sentinel mode — BullMQ's ioredis client supports Sentinel natively
Set appendonly yes and appendfsync everysec in Redis config for durability (AOF persistence)
Back AOF files with a PVC on fast local storage; replicas provide HA, not backup
Configure KEDA's redis scaler with the Sentinel endpoint so autoscaling survives Redis failover

symptoms: secrets committed to Git in plain text
need to rotate API keys without redeploying pods
Kubernetes Secrets are base64 only — not encrypted at rest
root_cause: Kubernetes Secrets are only base64-encoded, not encrypted by default. Committing them to Git or relying on etcd without encryption at rest is a security risk. HashiCorp Vault with External Secrets Operator (ESO) provides dynamic secret injection and centralized rotation.
fix: Deploy HashiCorp Vault (via the Vault Helm chart) in HA mode backed by Raft or Consul
Install External Secrets Operator and create a SecretStore pointing at Vault
Define ExternalSecret resources that map Vault paths to Kubernetes Secrets — ESO syncs them on a schedule
Use Vault dynamic secrets for PostgreSQL credentials — short-lived, auto-rotated DB users
Enable Kubernetes Auth Method in Vault so pods authenticate via their ServiceAccount JWT without a static token

symptoms: AI provider traffic bypasses firewall egress rules
cannot audit which pods are calling OpenAI/Anthropic
need IP safelisting for external AI API calls
root_cause: By default all pods share the node's egress IP, making it impossible to safelists a stable IP range at the AI provider's firewall. Cilium Egress Gateway assigns a stable egress IP (from a dedicated node or NAT pool) to specific pod selectors, making all AI traffic exit through a predictable IP.
fix: Label AI worker nodes with egress-gateway=true and configure a CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy selecting AI worker pods
The policy routes matching traffic through the dedicated egress node's IP — register this IP with OpenAI/Anthropic IP allowlists
Combine with a CiliumNetworkPolicy to restrict AI pods to HTTPS (port 443) to specific FQDNs only
Use Cilium's FQDN-based egress filtering (toFQDNs) to block unexpected outbound domains
Monitor egress flows with Hubble CLI: hubble observe --to-identity world

symptoms: no visibility into pod CPU/memory trends
need Prometheus metrics for Node.js and PostgreSQL
Grafana dashboards not showing application metrics
root_cause: Kubernetes does not ship with a metrics stack. The LGTM stack (Loki, Grafana, Tempo, Mimir/Prometheus) is the standard open-source observability suite for Kubernetes. kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart bootstraps Prometheus, Grafana, and alerting rules in one install.
fix: Install kube-prometheus-stack: helm install monitoring prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack --namespace monitoring --create-namespace
Add prom-client to Node.js apps and expose a /metrics endpoint; configure a ServiceMonitor to scrape it
CloudNativePG exposes a /metrics endpoint automatically — create a PodMonitor targeting cnpg pods
Install Grafana Loki with Promtail (or Vector/Fluent Bit) for log aggregation; add Loki as a Grafana datasource
Install Grafana Tempo for distributed tracing and instrument Node.js with @opentelemetry/sdk-node exporting to Tempo's OTLP endpoint

symptoms: need distributed tracing across Node.js API, BullMQ workers, and external AI calls
cannot correlate a slow request through the queue to the AI provider
latency bottlenecks are invisible without trace context propagation
root_cause: Without distributed tracing, spans from the HTTP API, BullMQ job processing, and outbound AI calls are siloed. OpenTelemetry (OTel) provides a vendor-neutral instrumentation library; Grafana Tempo stores and queries traces.
fix: Add @opentelemetry/sdk-node, @opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node to Node.js apps
Configure the OTLP exporter to send traces to Tempo's OTLP HTTP endpoint (http://tempo:4318/v1/traces)
Propagate trace context through BullMQ jobs by serializing the OTel trace context into job data and restoring it in the worker
Use the @opentelemetry/instrumentation-http and @opentelemetry/instrumentation-express auto-instrumentations for zero-code API tracing
In Grafana, correlate logs (Loki) and traces (Tempo) using the TraceID extracted from structured log fields

symptoms: need GitOps-based deployment for Kubernetes resources
want consistent environments across dev, staging, production
Helm chart upgrades are error-prone when done manually
root_cause: Helm packages Kubernetes resources into versioned, templated charts. Combined with a GitOps tool (Argo CD or Flux), Helm releases are reconciled automatically from Git, providing audit trails, rollbacks, and environment parity.
fix: Package each service (Node.js API, workers) as a Helm chart with configurable values.yaml
Use a separate values file per environment (values-staging.yaml, values-prod.yaml) — pass with helm install -f
For GitOps, install Argo CD and define an Application resource pointing at your chart repo and target cluster
Pin chart versions and image tags in the values file — avoid :latest tags in production
Use helm diff plugin before upgrades to preview changes: helm diff upgrade <release> <chart>

symptoms: NATS JetStream messages lost when NATS pod restarts
NATS not retaining messages for replay
need persistent event streaming between microservices
root_cause: NATS core (pub/sub) has no persistence. JetStream must be explicitly enabled and configured with a FileStore backend so messages survive pod restarts. Without persistence configuration, JetStream defaults to MemoryStore which is lost on restart.
fix: Deploy NATS with JetStream enabled: set config.jetstream.enabled=true and config.jetstream.fileStore.dir=/data in the NATS Helm chart
Mount a PersistentVolumeClaim at the fileStore dir so stream data survives pod restarts
Set retention policy (limits, interest, or workqueue) and maxAge per stream based on replay requirements
Configure replicas≥3 for clustered NATS to avoid single-point-of-failure in the stream
Use durable consumers with AckExplicit to guarantee at-least-once delivery to worker services

symptoms: scheduled jobs running at wrong times after daylight saving time change
CronJob and BullMQ repeatable jobs have different scheduling granularity
unsure when to use CronJob vs BullMQ repeatable jobs
root_cause: Kubernetes CronJobs use standard cron syntax, run at the cluster level, and are best for infrastructure tasks (backups, cleanup, reports) that don't need application context. BullMQ repeatable jobs run within the application process, support sub-minute intervals, and share application state — better for application-level scheduling (digest emails, retry sweeps).
fix: Use Kubernetes CronJob for: database backups, log rotation, external health checks, certificate renewal triggers
Use BullMQ repeatable jobs for: application-level scheduled tasks, tasks needing DB access, sub-minute intervals
Set spec.timeZone on CronJob (Kubernetes ≥ 1.27) to avoid DST-related drift
For BullMQ repeatable jobs, store the cron expression in config (not hardcoded) so it can be updated without redeployment
Ensure idempotency in both cases — CronJob pods may overlap if the previous run is still running when spec.concurrencyPolicy is Allow

symptoms: pod cannot connect to PostgreSQL after CloudNativePG failover
connection string still points to old primary after failover
application hardcodes PostgreSQL pod IP instead of service
root_cause: CloudNativePG creates three Services: -rw (primary only), -ro (replicas only), -r (any instance). If an application hardcodes the pod IP or uses the wrong service, it loses connectivity after a failover because the primary IP changes.
fix: Always connect to the <cluster-name>-rw Service for read-write connections — CloudNativePG updates this service's endpoint automatically on failover
Use <cluster-name>-ro for read-only replicas to distribute query load
Set the connection string in an ExternalSecret or ConfigMap referencing the service DNS name, not a pod IP
Enable PgBouncer via CloudNativePG's Pooler resource — it handles connection pooling and survives failover transparently
Test failover by deleting the primary pod and verifying the app reconnects within the TCP keepalive timeout

symptoms: how to bootstrap a kubeadm cluster
kubeadm init first control plane node
root_cause: A new cluster requires initializing the first control-plane node with kubeadm init, which sets up the API server, controller-manager, scheduler, and etcd, then generates a bootstrap token and join command for worker nodes.
fix: kubeadm init --control-plane-endpoint '<LB_IP_OR_DNS>:6443' --pod-network-cidr '10.244.0.0/16' --upload-certs
mkdir -p $HOME/.kube && cp -i /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf $HOME/.kube/config
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cilium/cilium/v1.15.0/install/kubernetes/quick-install.yaml (or deploy Cilium via Helm)
kubeadm token create --print-join-command  # generate fresh join command if token expired
Save the 'kubeadm join' command and '--certificate-key' output for control-plane and worker joins

symptoms: how to join a worker node to kubeadm cluster
add new node to existing kubernetes cluster
root_cause: Worker nodes join by presenting a bootstrap token and CA hash to the API server. The join command is produced at init time or regenerated via kubeadm token create and must be used before the token TTL (default 24 h) expires.
fix: kubeadm token create --print-join-command  # run on control-plane to get fresh join command
On the worker node: kubeadm join <LB_IP>:6443 --token <TOKEN> --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<HASH>
Verify: kubectl get nodes  # new node appears in NotReady then Ready within ~60 s once CNI is running
kubectl describe node <NEW_NODE>  # check conditions if node stays NotReady
Ensure container runtime (containerd) is running and correct cgroup driver matches kubelet config

symptoms: how to add a second control plane node kubeadm
root_cause: High-availability control planes require joining additional control-plane nodes with --control-plane and a certificate key that was uploaded during kubeadm init --upload-certs. The certificate key expires after 2 hours.
fix: On existing control-plane: kubeadm init phase upload-certs --upload-certs  # re-upload certs if key expired; note the new certificate-key
kubeadm token create --print-join-command  # get fresh token
On new control-plane node: kubeadm join <LB_IP>:6443 --token <TOKEN> --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<HASH> --control-plane --certificate-key <CERT_KEY>
Verify: kubectl get nodes  # new control-plane node appears

Check etcd membership: etcdctl --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key member list

symptoms: how to upgrade kubernetes cluster with kubeadm
root_cause: Cluster upgrades with kubeadm follow a strict sequence: upgrade kubeadm on the first control-plane, run kubeadm upgrade apply, then upgrade kubelet/kubectl on each node in turn. Skipping minor versions is not supported.
fix: apt-mark unhold kubeadm && apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.30.* && apt-mark hold kubeadm  # on control-plane
kubeadm upgrade plan  # shows available versions and pre-flight checks
kubeadm upgrade apply v1.30.0  # upgrades control-plane components

kubectl drain <CONTROL_PLANE_NODE> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data && apt-get install -y kubelet=1.30.* kubectl=1.30.* && systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl restart kubelet && kubectl uncordon <CONTROL_PLANE_NODE>

For each worker: kubectl drain <NODE> --ignore-daemonsets && ssh <NODE> 'apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.30.* kubelet=1.30.* kubectl=1.30.*; kubeadm upgrade node; systemctl restart kubelet' && kubectl uncordon <NODE>
Verify: kubectl get nodes  # all nodes should show new version

root_cause: Kubeadm-generated certificates have a 1-year TTL by default. Before expiry, all control-plane component certificates must be renewed; failure causes API server and component authentication to break cluster-wide.
fix: kubeadm certs check-expiration  # list all cert expiry dates
kubeadm certs renew all  # renew all control-plane certificates (run on each control-plane node)
After renewal, restart static pods: crictl ps | grep -E 'kube-apiserver|kube-controller|kube-scheduler|etcd' | awk '{print $1}' | xargs crictl stop
kubeadm certs renew admin.conf && cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config  # also renew kubeconfig
Verify: kubeadm certs check-expiration  # all certs should show ~365 days remaining
Consider setting --auto-renew-certificates=true on kubelet for automatic annual renewal

symptoms: kubeadm error required cgroups subsystem not mounted
root_cause: kubeadm init runs pre-flight checks before bootstrapping; common failures include missing cgroup subsystems (memory/swap), container runtime not running or using wrong socket, disabled firewall ports, or incompatible OS settings.
fix: kubeadm init --ignore-preflight-errors=<SPECIFIC_ERROR>  # only for non-critical errors
Ensure containerd is running: systemctl status containerd && systemctl start containerd
Disable swap permanently: swapoff -a && sed -i '/swap/d' /etc/fstab
Enable required kernel modules: modprobe overlay && modprobe br_netfilter
Set sysctl: echo 'net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-iptables=1\nnet.ipv4.ip_forward=1' > /etc/sysctl.d/k8s.conf && sysctl --system
Check /etc/containerd/config.toml: SystemdCgroup = true must match kubelet --cgroup-driver=systemd

symptoms: kubeadm join control-plane fails certificate key invalid
root_cause: The certificate key generated by kubeadm init --upload-certs expires after 2 hours. Attempting to join an additional control-plane node after this window fails with a decryption error because the encrypted certs are no longer in the kube-system secret.
fix: On an existing control-plane: kubeadm init phase upload-certs --upload-certs  # generates a new certificate-key valid for 2 hours
kubeadm token create --print-join-command  # also regenerate token if expired
Re-run join with new certificate-key: kubeadm join <LB>:6443 --token <NEW_TOKEN> --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<HASH> --control-plane --certificate-key <NEW_CERT_KEY>
Verify: kubectl get secrets -n kube-system | grep kubeadm-certs  # secret should exist with recent timestamp

symptoms: kubeadm upgrade node stuck waiting for control plane
root_cause: During kubeadm upgrade, the upgrader contacts the API server to fetch the target config; if the API server is temporarily unavailable (e.g., during restart), the upgrade command can hang or time out waiting for cluster connectivity.
fix: Check API server static pod status: crictl ps | grep kube-apiserver
Check API server logs: crictl logs <APISERVER_CONTAINER_ID> --tail=50
If API server not starting, check /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml for syntax errors introduced by upgrade
Restore previous manifest from backup if corrupted: cp /etc/kubernetes/tmp/kubeadm-backup-manifests-*/kube-apiserver.yaml /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
Retry upgrade: kubeadm upgrade apply v<TARGET_VERSION> --force  # --force skips version check if partial upgrade
Monitor: watch -n2 'kubectl get nodes && kubectl get pods -n kube-system'

symptoms: how to reset a node with kubeadm
remove node from kubernetes cluster kubeadm
root_cause: Before reprovisioning or decommissioning a node, it must be drained and then reset to clean up kubelet state, CNI configuration, iptables rules, and kubeadm configuration files, otherwise re-joining will fail with stale state.
fix: On control-plane: kubectl drain <NODE> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
On control-plane: kubectl delete node <NODE>
On the target node: kubeadm reset -f
Clean up CNI config: rm -rf /etc/cni/net.d/* /opt/cni/bin/*
Clean up iptables/ipvs: iptables -F && iptables -t nat -F && iptables -t mangle -F && iptables -X; ipvsadm --clear (if using ipvs)
Optionally purge kubelet data: rm -rf /var/lib/kubelet /var/lib/etcd /etc/kubernetes

symptoms: how to check kubernetes certificate expiration
root_cause: Certificate expiration is a silent failure mode in kubeadm clusters. Certificates expire 1 year after issuance unless auto-renewed, and expired certificates cause immediate authentication failures for all control-plane components and kubectl.
fix: kubeadm certs check-expiration  # shows all cert expiry dates and whether they need renewal
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -noout -enddate  # check individual cert
kubectl get csr  # check if any Certificate Signing Requests are pending
Set up monitoring alert: check cert expiry via Prometheus node-exporter x509 cert exporter or kubeadm certs check-expiration in a CronJob
Automate: add kubeadm certs renew all to a systemd timer or CronJob that runs 90 days before expiry

symptoms: etcd not healthy after kubeadm upgrade
root_cause: During a kubeadm upgrade, the etcd static pod manifest is updated, causing a container restart. If the etcd data directory has permission issues, the volume mount path changed, or the peer TLS certs mismatch, etcd fails to start after upgrade.
fix: Check etcd logs: crictl logs $(crictl ps -q --name etcd) --tail=100
Verify etcd data dir: ls -la /var/lib/etcd  # must be owned by root, not drifted permissions

Check etcd member health: etcdctl --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/peer.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/peer.key endpoint health
If peer cert mismatch: kubeadm certs renew etcd-peer && crictl stop <ETCD_CONTAINER>  # force restart
Restore from backup if corrupted: stop etcd, restore snapshot with 'etcdctl snapshot restore', restart etcd static pod
Verify: etcdctl endpoint status --cluster -w table  # all members should show isLeader true or false with valid revision

symptoms: best practice for kubeadm cluster backup
root_cause: Cluster recovery from control-plane failure requires an etcd snapshot and the PKI directory. Without a backup strategy, a failed control-plane means complete cluster data loss.
fix: Schedule etcd snapshots: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /backup/etcd-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).db --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/healthcheck-client.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/healthcheck-client.key
Backup PKI: tar czf /backup/k8s-pki-$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz /etc/kubernetes/pki/
Verify snapshot: etcdctl snapshot status /backup/etcd-<DATE>.db -w table
Store backups off-node (S3-compatible, NFS, or separate host)
Test restore procedure in staging before relying on it in production
Use a Kubernetes CronJob with hostPath or an init container to automate this on each control-plane node

symptoms: skipping minor versions in kubernetes upgrade
root_cause: Kubernetes only supports upgrading one minor version at a time (e.g., 1.28 -> 1.29 -> 1.30). Skipping minor versions violates the version skew policy and can cause API removals, etcd schema mismatches, and kubeadm validation errors.
fix: Always upgrade sequentially: 1.28 -> 1.29 -> 1.30, never skip a minor version
Check current version: kubectl version --short
Check available upgrade path: kubeadm upgrade plan  # shows only the next valid minor version
On apt-based systems: apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.29.* then kubeadm upgrade apply v1.29.x, then repeat for 1.30
Review API deprecations between each version: https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/using-api/deprecation-guide/
Test upgrades in a staging cluster before applying to production

symptoms: kubeadm init fails with port already in use
root_cause: kubeadm pre-flight checks verify that required ports (6443, 2379-2380, 10250-10252, 10257, 10259) are not already bound. A previous failed kubeadm init, a running kubelet, or another process occupying these ports causes initialization to fail.
fix: Run kubeadm reset -f first if a previous partial init occurred
Kill orphaned processes: systemctl stop kubelet && crictl ps | awk 'NR>1 {print $1}' | xargs crictl stop
Remove static pod manifests from previous init: rm -f /etc/kubernetes/manifests/*.yaml
If another service legitimately owns 6443, change kubeadm --apiserver-bind-port <OTHER_PORT>
Re-run kubeadm init after clearing ports

symptoms: what to do before upgrading kubernetes
root_cause: Kubernetes control-plane upgrades are high-risk operations. Skipping pre-upgrade validation steps (backup, drain, drain verification, addon compatibility) is a primary cause of upgrade-related outages.
fix: 1. Take etcd snapshot: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /backup/pre-upgrade-etcd.db ...
2. Back up PKI: tar czf /backup/pre-upgrade-pki.tar.gz /etc/kubernetes/pki/
3. Check addon compatibility (CNI, CoreDNS, metrics-server) with target k8s version
4. Review API deprecations: kubectl api-resources --verbs=list; cross-check with https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/using-api/deprecation-guide/
5. Drain worker nodes one at a time: kubectl drain <NODE> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
6. Run: kubeadm upgrade plan  # confirm version and pre-flight status
7. After upgrade, run: kubectl get nodes && kubectl get pods -A | grep -v Running | grep -v Completed  # verify all healthy

symptoms: node not joining cluster token expired
kubeadm join token no longer valid
root_cause: Bootstrap tokens generated by kubeadm init have a default TTL of 24 hours. After expiry, the token is deleted from the cluster and the join command fails with 'token does not exist or not valid'.
fix: On control-plane: kubeadm token list  # shows existing tokens and expiry
Create a new token: kubeadm token create --print-join-command  # prints ready-to-use join command
Or create a non-expiring token (not recommended for production): kubeadm token create --ttl 0
Get the CA cert hash if needed separately: openssl x509 -pubkey -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt | openssl rsa -pubin -outform der 2>/dev/null | openssl dgst -sha256 -hex | sed 's/^.* //'
Construct join command manually: kubeadm join <LB>:6443 --token <NEW_TOKEN> --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<HASH>

symptoms: kubeadm control plane component not healthy
root_cause: Control-plane components run as static pods managed by kubelet. If the manifest in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ has an error, or the cert/kubeconfig it references is missing or malformed, the component fails to start and kubelet shows it as a failed static pod.
fix: Check static pod status: crictl ps -a | grep -E 'kube-controller|kube-scheduler|kube-apiserver|etcd'
View component logs: crictl logs <CONTAINER_ID> --tail=100
Validate manifests: cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml  # check for YAML syntax errors
Verify referenced kubeconfig exists: ls -la /etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf
Re-generate manifests if corrupted: kubeadm init phase control-plane controller-manager (or scheduler, apiserver)
Check kubelet for static pod errors: journalctl -u kubelet --since '5 minutes ago' | grep -i 'static\|mirror\|manifest'

root_cause: Passing all options on the kubeadm init command line is error-prone and hard to version-control. Using a kubeadm ClusterConfiguration YAML allows reproducible cluster initialization, audit history in Git, and easier upgrades via kubeadm upgrade --config.
fix: Generate default config: kubeadm config print init-defaults > kubeadm-config.yaml
Edit key fields: controlPlaneEndpoint, podSubnet (networking.podSubnet), serviceSubnet, kubernetesVersion, etcd stacked/external

Example snippet: apiVersion: kubeadm.k8s.io/v1beta3
kind: ClusterConfiguration
kubernetesVersion: v1.30.0
controlPlaneEndpoint: 'lb.example.com:6443'
networking:
  podSubnet: '10.244.0.0/16'
Bootstrap with config: kubeadm init --config kubeadm-config.yaml --upload-certs
Store config in Git for GitOps; use kubeadm upgrade --config kubeadm-config.yaml at upgrade time
Validate config before apply: kubeadm config validate --config kubeadm-config.yaml

symptoms: kubelet not starting after kubeadm join
kubelet service failed after node join
node stays NotReady after kubeadm join
root_cause: After kubeadm join completes the bootstrap, the kubelet must successfully communicate with the API server and have a working CNI plugin. Common failure causes are cgroup driver mismatch between kubelet and containerd, missing CNI binaries, or a firewall blocking kubelet port 10250.
fix: Check kubelet status: systemctl status kubelet && journalctl -u kubelet -n 100
Common cgroup driver mismatch fix: ensure /etc/containerd/config.toml has 'SystemdCgroup = true' and kubelet --cgroup-driver=systemd

Fix: containerd config default > /etc/containerd/config.toml; then set SystemdCgroup = true in [plugins."io.containerd.grpc.v1.cri".containerd.runtimes.runc.options]; systemctl restart containerd kubelet
Check CNI: ls /opt/cni/bin/  # should have loopback, bridge, etc. after CNI plugin DaemonSet runs
Check firewall: ufw allow 10250/tcp && ufw allow 179/tcp  # BGP for Cilium
Verify node: kubectl describe node <NODE>  # look for KubeletNotReady reason

root_cause: kubeadm init and join are composed of discrete phases that can be run individually. Understanding and using phases allows operators to customize the bootstrap process, inject custom steps, re-run only failed phases, and avoid full re-initialization.
fix: List all phases: kubeadm init --dry-run  # or kubeadm init phase --help
Key phases for init: preflight, certs, kubeconfig, kubelet-start, control-plane, etcd, upload-config, upload-certs, mark-control-plane, bootstrap-token, kubelet-finalize, addon
Re-run only certs phase: kubeadm init phase certs all --config kubeadm-config.yaml
Re-run only addon phase: kubeadm init phase addon all  # re-deploys CoreDNS and kube-proxy
For kubeadm join phases: kubeadm join phase --help  # shows preflight, control-plane-prepare, kubelet-start, control-plane-join
Useful for debugging: run phases one at a time with --dry-run first: kubeadm init phase certs all --dry-run

symptoms: how to install RKE2 server on first node
root_cause: RKE2 server initialization requires specific steps to bootstrap the first control plane node including downloading the install script, configuring /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml before starting the service, and waiting for the embedded etcd to initialize.
fix: curl -sfL https://get.rke2.io | sh -
mkdir -p /etc/rancher/rke2/

cat > /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml <<EOF
tls-san:
  - 192.168.1.10
  - rke2.example.com
token: mysharedtoken
EOF
systemctl enable rke2-server.service && systemctl start rke2-server.service
export KUBECONFIG=/etc/rancher/rke2/rke2.yaml
kubectl get nodes

root_cause: RKE2 agents fail to register when the token in the agent's config.yaml does not match the server token, or when the server URL is unreachable. The token must be identical on all nodes and the server URL must resolve to a reachable control plane endpoint.
fix: On server: cat /var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/node-token  # retrieve cluster token
On agent node: mkdir -p /etc/rancher/rke2/

cat > /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml <<EOF
server: https://192.168.1.10:9345
token: <token-from-server>
EOF
systemctl enable rke2-agent.service && systemctl start rke2-agent.service
journalctl -u rke2-agent -f  # watch for registration errors
On server: kubectl get nodes --watch  # confirm agent appears

root_cause: The RKE2 API server TLS certificate is only generated for SANs listed in tls-san at install time. If the config.yaml tls-san list is missing the VIP, load balancer DNS name, or additional IPs used to reach the API server, TLS verification fails for clients connecting via those addresses.
fix:
Add all required IPs/hostnames to /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml before first start:
tls-san:
  - 192.168.1.10
  - rke2-vip.example.com
  - 127.0.0.1
If already running, update tls-san in config.yaml then rotate certs: rke2 certificate rotate
Restart RKE2 server: systemctl restart rke2-server.service
Verify new SANs: openssl s_client -connect 192.168.1.10:6443 </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -text | grep -A2 'Subject Alternative Name'
Update KUBECONFIG server URL to match a listed SAN

symptoms: how does rke2 embedded etcd work
root_cause: RKE2 embeds etcd as the default datastore for HA clusters. When three or more server nodes join the cluster with matching tokens, RKE2 automatically forms an etcd cluster. The etcd data is stored at /var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/db/etcd and managed entirely by RKE2 without separate etcd installation.
fix: Bootstrap first server: systemctl start rke2-server.service
Join second server: add server URL and token to /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml, then systemctl start rke2-server.service
Join third server the same way to achieve quorum

Verify etcd health: /var/lib/rancher/rke2/bin/etcdctl --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/tls/etcd/server-ca.crt --cert=/var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/tls/etcd/server-client.crt --key=/var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/tls/etcd/server-client.key endpoint health
Check etcd member list: /var/lib/rancher/rke2/bin/etcdctl member list

root_cause: In air-gapped environments without internet access, RKE2 must be installed using pre-downloaded artifacts including the install script, binary tarball, and container images tarball. The images tarball must be placed in the correct directory before starting RKE2 so it can import them locally.
fix: On internet-connected machine: curl -OLf https://github.com/rancher/rke2/releases/download/v1.28.8+rke2r1/rke2.linux-amd64.tar.gz
Also download: curl -OLf https://github.com/rancher/rke2/releases/download/v1.28.8+rke2r1/rke2-images.linux-amd64.tar.zst
Transfer artifacts to airgap node
On airgap node: mkdir -p /var/lib/rancher/rke2/agent/images/ && cp rke2-images.linux-amd64.tar.zst /var/lib/rancher/rke2/agent/images/
INSTALL_RKE2_ARTIFACT_PATH=/path/to/artifacts sh install.sh
Create /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml then: systemctl enable --now rke2-server.service

symptoms: how to manage rke2 tokens securely
root_cause: RKE2 uses a shared token for node registration. Using weak or default tokens, storing tokens in plain text, or never rotating them increases the attack surface. Tokens should be randomly generated, stored securely, and rotated periodically using RKE2's built-in rotation capability.
fix: Generate a strong token: openssl rand -hex 32
Set token in config.yaml before cluster init: token: <generated-value>
Store the token in a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault) rather than plain files
To rotate the token: rke2 token rotate --new-token <new-value> (RKE2 v1.27+)
After rotation, update /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml on all nodes and restart agents: systemctl restart rke2-agent.service
Verify all nodes rejoin: kubectl get nodes

root_cause: RKE2 upgrades should be performed in a controlled rolling fashion: control plane nodes first, then worker agents. The system-upgrade-controller can automate this with a Plan CRD, or manual upgrade can be done node by node to maintain availability.
fix: Automated method: install system-upgrade-controller: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/rancher/system-upgrade-controller/releases/latest/download/system-upgrade-controller.yaml

Create upgrade Plan for servers:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: upgrade.cattle.io/v1
kind: Plan
metadata:
  name: rke2-server
  namespace: system-upgrade
spec:
  concurrency: 1
  cordon: true
  nodeSelector:
    matchLabels:
      node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane: 'true'
  serviceAccountName: system-upgrade
  upgrade:
    image: rancher/rke2-upgrade
  version: v1.28.8+rke2r1
EOF
Create similar Plan for agents with node-role.kubernetes.io/worker label
Manual method: on each node: curl -sfL https://get.rke2.io | INSTALL_RKE2_VERSION=v1.28.8+rke2r1 sh -
Then: systemctl restart rke2-server.service  # or rke2-agent.service
Verify: kubectl get nodes -o wide

symptoms: rke2 etcd failed to find local peer
rke2 server failed to start etcd
root_cause: When an RKE2 server node is removed from the cluster and then re-added, or when a snapshot is restored incorrectly, the local etcd member may have a stale cluster ID that conflicts with the existing etcd cluster, causing it to refuse to start.
fix: Check etcd member list from a healthy node: /var/lib/rancher/rke2/bin/etcdctl member list
Remove the stale member: /var/lib/rancher/rke2/bin/etcdctl member remove <member-id>
On the problematic node, stop RKE2: systemctl stop rke2-server.service
Remove stale etcd data: rm -rf /var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/db/etcd
Rejoin the node as a fresh server: ensure correct config.yaml with server URL and token
Start RKE2: systemctl start rke2-server.service
Monitor with: journalctl -u rke2-server -f

symptoms: what to check before installing rke2
root_cause: RKE2 has specific OS-level, network, and firewall requirements that must be satisfied before installation. Missing prerequisites such as required ports, kernel modules, or cgroup v2 configuration cause silent failures or hard-to-diagnose errors during installation.
fix: Check OS compatibility: RKE2 supports RHEL/CentOS 7/8/9, Ubuntu 18.04/20.04/22.04, SLES 15
Open required ports: 9345 (server registration), 6443 (API), 2379-2380 (etcd), 10250 (kubelet), 8472/UDP (Flannel VXLAN), 51820/UDP (WireGuard if used)
Disable NetworkManager management of canal/flannel interfaces: add /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/rke2-canal.conf with [keyfile] unmanaged-devices=interface-name:cali*;flannel*
Ensure cgroup v2 if on RHEL9: grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args='systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=1'
Disable firewalld or create permissive zone: systemctl disable --now firewalld
Set hostname: hostnamectl set-hostname rke2-server-01
Verify time sync: timedatectl status

symptoms: rke2 worker node stuck not ready
root_cause: After RKE2 agent starts, the node may stay in NotReady state if the CNI plugin (Canal by default) has not finished deploying its DaemonSet pods, or if there is a pod CIDR conflict. The CNI must be fully running before the node transitions to Ready.
fix: Check node status: kubectl get nodes -o wide
Check CNI DaemonSet pods: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=canal
Describe stuck node: kubectl describe node <node-name>
Check agent logs: journalctl -u rke2-agent --since '10 minutes ago'
Verify CNI config written: ls /etc/cni/net.d/
If pod CIDR conflict: set cluster-cidr in config.yaml to a non-overlapping range, then reinstall
Force CNI pod restart: kubectl rollout restart daemonset/canal -n kube-system

symptoms: rke2 server configuration not taking effect
root_cause: RKE2 reads /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml only at startup. If the file is created or modified after the service has already started, the changes have no effect until the service is restarted. Also, config.yaml must use valid YAML syntax with correct option names as per the RKE2 reference.
fix: Always create /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml BEFORE starting rke2-server or rke2-agent services
Validate YAML syntax: python3 -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('/etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml'))"
Check valid server options: rke2 server --help
After changing config: systemctl restart rke2-server.service
Verify config is loaded: journalctl -u rke2-server | grep -i 'config'
Key options example: disable: [rke2-ingress-nginx], cni: cilium, node-label: ['env=prod']

root_cause: RKE2 supports automated etcd snapshots stored locally or in S3. Regular snapshots are essential for disaster recovery. Restoring from a snapshot requires stopping all server nodes, running the restore command on one node, then restarting all servers.
fix:
Enable scheduled snapshots in config.yaml:
etcd-snapshot-schedule-cron: '0 */6 * * *'
etcd-snapshot-retention: 5
etcd-snapshot-dir: /opt/rke2/snapshots
Trigger manual snapshot: rke2 etcd-snapshot save --name manual-$(date +%Y%m%d)
List snapshots: rke2 etcd-snapshot list
To restore: stop all server nodes: systemctl stop rke2-server.service
On first server: rke2 server --cluster-reset --cluster-reset-restore-path=/var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/db/snapshots/manual-20240101
Remove the reset flag file and restart: rm /var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/db/reset-flag && systemctl start rke2-server.service
Restart remaining servers after first is healthy

symptoms: rke2 all server nodes down cluster unrecoverable
rke2 single control plane in production
root_cause: Running RKE2 with a single server node or an even number of server nodes creates etcd quorum vulnerabilities. A single node cluster loses all control plane functionality on node failure. Even numbers (2, 4) offer no quorum advantage over odd numbers minus one (1, 3) yet risk split-brain scenarios.
fix: Always deploy odd numbers of server nodes: 3 or 5 for production
For 3-node HA: tolerate loss of 1 server; for 5-node: tolerate loss of 2
Do not use 2 or 4 server nodes in production
Spread server nodes across failure domains (racks/AZs)
Set node anti-affinity for critical workloads to avoid co-scheduling on control plane
Monitor etcd quorum: /var/lib/rancher/rke2/bin/etcdctl endpoint status --cluster

symptoms: rke2 failed to connect to proxy
root_cause: RKE2 agents connect to the server node via a WebSocket tunnel on port 9345. If a proxy or firewall terminates WebSocket connections or does not support HTTP CONNECT tunneling, the agent cannot register and shows 'failed to connect to proxy' or tunnel-related errors in the agent log.
fix: Verify port 9345 is open bidirectionally between agent and server: nc -zv <server-ip> 9345
Check no HTTP proxy is intercepting: unset HTTPS_PROXY HTTP_PROXY in the environment, or add server IP to NO_PROXY
In /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml on agent, set: server: https://<server-ip>:9345
If using a load balancer, ensure TCP passthrough on port 9345 (not HTTP mode)
Verify with: journalctl -u rke2-agent | grep -i 'tunnel\|proxy\|websocket'
Check firewalld/iptables rules: iptables -L -n | grep 9345

root_cause: RKE2's embedded etcd is extremely sensitive to disk I/O latency. etcd uses fsync for every write, and on slow spinning disks or network-attached storage, fsync latency above 10ms causes etcd heartbeat timeouts, leader elections, and eventually API server instability.
fix: Measure disk fsync speed: fio --rw=write --ioengine=sync --fdatasync=1 --directory=/var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/db/etcd --size=22m --bs=2300 --name=etcd-benchmark
etcd target: 99th percentile fdatasync < 10ms; SSD recommended
Move etcd data to SSD: stop rke2, rsync data to SSD mount, symlink or update etcd-data-dir in config
Avoid placing etcd on NFS, Ceph RBD, or other network storage
Monitor etcd metrics: etcd_disk_wal_fsync_duration_seconds via Prometheus
Set ionice for etcd: ionice -c2 -n0 -p $(pgrep etcd)

root_cause: RKE2 does not use kube-proxy by default when Canal or Cilium CNI is selected with eBPF/kube-proxy replacement mode. Users migrating from kubeadm expect kube-proxy to exist and may misdiagnose missing iptables rules as a bug when they are actually managed by the CNI directly.
fix: Verify CNI mode: kubectl get cm rke2-config -n kube-system -o yaml | grep cni
With Canal (default): kube-proxy IS present, check: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
With Cilium in kube-proxy replacement mode: kube-proxy is intentionally absent; Cilium handles service routing
To re-enable kube-proxy with Cilium: set --set kubeProxyReplacement=false in Cilium Helm values
Check iptables rules via Cilium: kubectl exec -n kube-system ds/cilium -- cilium service list
If truly missing: disable: [] in config.yaml ensures no components are disabled

symptoms: rke2 server vs agent config differences
root_cause: RKE2 server and agent nodes use different config.yaml keys. Mixing server-only keys (like tls-san, cluster-cidr) in agent config.yaml has no effect but can cause confusion. Maintaining separate, well-structured config files per role avoids drift and misconfiguration.
fix: Server config (/etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml) keys: token, tls-san, cluster-cidr, service-cidr, cni, disable, etcd-snapshot-schedule-cron, node-label
Agent config (/etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml) keys: server, token, node-label, node-taint
Use node-label for role identification: node-label: ['role=worker', 'env=prod']
Use node-taint on dedicated nodes: node-taint: ['CriticalAddonsOnly=true:NoExecute']
Store configs in version control and deploy via configuration management (Ansible/Salt)
Do not set server-only options on agent nodes; they will be silently ignored

symptoms: rke2 failed to get node IP
rke2 wrong IP advertised for node
root_cause: On hosts with multiple network interfaces, RKE2 may select the wrong IP for the node. This causes pods scheduled on that node to be unreachable via the node's advertised IP, and can break kube-proxy rules or CNI routing. The node-ip config option must be set explicitly.
fix: Identify the correct interface IP: ip addr show

Set node-ip in /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml:
node-ip: 192.168.1.20

On server nodes, also set advertise-address to the correct IP:
advertise-address: 192.168.1.10
Restart the service: systemctl restart rke2-server.service  # or rke2-agent.service
Verify: kubectl get node <node-name> -o jsonpath='{.status.addresses}'
For agent nodes, also ensure the server URL resolves to the correct server IP

root_cause: RKE2 logs via systemd journal for both server and agent services. Understanding common log patterns helps quickly diagnose installation failures, registration issues, and runtime errors without guessing.
fix: View server logs: journalctl -u rke2-server -f --since '30 minutes ago'
View agent logs: journalctl -u rke2-agent -f --since '30 minutes ago'

Key error patterns:
  'failed to connect to proxy' → port 9345 blocked or wrong server URL
  'Unable to authenticate' → token mismatch
  'x509: certificate signed by unknown authority' → tls-san missing or cert rotation needed
  'etcd cluster is unavailable' → etcd quorum lost, check etcd member health
  'failed to get node IP' → set node-ip in config.yaml
Enable debug logging: add 'debug: true' to config.yaml and restart
Check static pod manifests: ls /var/lib/rancher/rke2/agent/pod-manifests/
Tail containerd logs: journalctl -u rke2 -f | grep containerd

symptoms: rke2 pull images from internal registry
root_cause: In air-gapped or enterprise environments, RKE2 must be configured to pull images from a private registry instead of public registries. RKE2 supports a registries.yaml file that defines mirrors and authentication for private registries, enabling full operation without internet access.
fix:
Create /etc/rancher/rke2/registries.yaml on every node:
mirrors:
  docker.io:
    endpoint:
      - 'https://registry.internal.example.com'
  registry.k8s.io:
    endpoint:
      - 'https://registry.internal.example.com'
configs:
  'registry.internal.example.com':
    auth:
      username: rke2user
      password: secretpassword
    tls:
      ca_file: /etc/ssl/certs/internal-ca.crt
Restart RKE2 after creating registries.yaml
Verify mirror in use: crictl pull nginx:latest  # should pull from internal registry
Pre-load airgap images: cp rke2-images.linux-amd64.tar.zst /var/lib/rancher/rke2/agent/images/
List loaded images: crictl images | grep internal

symptoms: how to generate talos machine config
root_cause: Talos Linux requires machine configuration to be generated with talosctl before nodes can be bootstrapped. The generated configs (controlplane.yaml, worker.yaml, talosconfig) are signed and must be applied to each node before the cluster can be initialized.
fix: talosctl gen config my-cluster https://<VIP>:6443 --output-dir ./talos-config
talosctl apply-config --insecure --nodes <node-ip> --file ./talos-config/controlplane.yaml
talosctl bootstrap --nodes <first-controlplane-ip> --talosconfig ./talos-config/talosconfig
talosctl kubeconfig --nodes <first-controlplane-ip> --talosconfig ./talos-config/talosconfig -f
Use --config-patch flag to merge additional patches during gen: talosctl gen config --config-patch @patch.yaml

symptoms: how to apply talos machine config
root_cause: Talos is an immutable OS where configuration changes are applied via talosctl apply-config. Changes to machineconfig are staged and applied on next reboot or immediately depending on the mode flag used.
fix: talosctl apply-config --nodes <node-ip> --file controlplane.yaml --talosconfig talosconfig
Use --mode=reboot to apply and reboot immediately: talosctl apply-config --nodes <ip> --file cp.yaml --mode=reboot
Use --mode=staged to queue apply until next reboot: talosctl apply-config --nodes <ip> --file cp.yaml --mode=staged
Use --mode=no-reboot for changes that don't require restart: talosctl apply-config --nodes <ip> --file cp.yaml --mode=no-reboot
Dry-run first: talosctl apply-config --nodes <ip> --file cp.yaml --dry-run
Patch inline: talosctl patch mc --nodes <ip> --patch '[{"op":"add","path":"/machine/network/hostname","value":"talos-cp1"}]'

root_cause: Talos Linux is a purpose-built, immutable OS for Kubernetes. It has no shell, no SSH, no package manager, and no unnecessary services. The entire OS is managed as a single signed image via machineconfig API, reducing attack surface and configuration drift.
fix: All node interaction is done via talosctl over mutual TLS (port 50000)
Read-only root filesystem: /system and /lib are immutable; only /var and /run are writable
Control plane components run as static pods managed by Talos, not systemd units requiring manual intervention
Use 'talosctl dmesg --nodes <ip>' instead of SSH for kernel logs
Use 'talosctl logs --nodes <ip> kubelet' for service logs
Use 'talosctl console --nodes <ip>' for emergency console access

root_cause: When decommissioning or reprovisioning a Talos node, the disk must be wiped to remove all cluster state, PKI, and etcd data. talosctl reset performs a secure wipe and optionally reboots into maintenance mode.
fix: talosctl reset --nodes <ip> --talosconfig talosconfig --graceful=false --reboot
To wipe only system disk: talosctl reset --nodes <ip> --system-labels-to-wipe STATE --system-labels-to-wipe EPHEMERAL
To wipe all user disks: talosctl reset --nodes <ip> --user-disks-to-wipe /dev/sdb
After reset, node reboots into agent mode waiting for new apply-config
For stuck etcd member removal before reset: talosctl etcd remove-member --nodes <cp-ip> <member-id>

root_cause: Talos Image Factory at factory.talos.dev allows generating custom Talos images with specific system extensions, kernel args, and target platforms baked in. This is the recommended way to produce installation media for bare-metal or VM deployments.
fix: Visit https://factory.talos.dev and select Talos version, extensions, and target (ISO/PXE/disk image)
Or use the API: curl -X POST https://factory.talos.dev/schematics -d '{"customization":{"systemExtensions":{"officialExtensions":["siderolabs/iscsi-tools","siderolabs/util-linux-tools"]}}}'
Retrieve the schematic ID from the response and download: curl -L https://factory.talos.dev/image/<schematic-id>/v1.7.0/metal-amd64.iso -o talos.iso
Reference the schematic ID in machineconfig for upgrades: machine.install.image: factory.talos.dev/installer/<schematic-id>:v1.7.0
Schematic IDs are deterministic — same extensions always yield same ID

root_cause: Talos system extensions extend the base Talos image with additional packages, kernel modules, or services (e.g., iscsi-tools for Longhorn, Nvidia GPU drivers, WireGuard). Extensions are applied at image build time or via upgrade using an image with extensions baked in.
fix: List official extensions: https://github.com/siderolabs/extensions
Common on-prem extensions: siderolabs/iscsi-tools (for Longhorn), siderolabs/util-linux-tools, siderolabs/drbd (for DRBD-based storage)
Build image with extensions via Image Factory schematic (see image-factory recipe)
Upgrade existing node to use extension image: talosctl upgrade --nodes <ip> --image factory.talos.dev/installer/<schematic-id>:v1.7.0
Verify extensions loaded: talosctl get extensions --nodes <ip>
Extensions are read-only overlays; they cannot be installed/removed at runtime without an upgrade

root_cause: Talos nodes are upgraded by pointing them to a new installer image. talosctl upgrade handles the process: it writes the new image to the install partition, then reboots. Upgrades are rolling and can be done node by node.
fix: Upgrade single node: talosctl upgrade --nodes <ip> --image ghcr.io/siderolabs/installer:v1.7.0 --talosconfig talosconfig
If using Image Factory with extensions: talosctl upgrade --nodes <ip> --image factory.talos.dev/installer/<schematic-id>:v1.7.0
Upgrade workers first, then control plane nodes one at a time
Check upgrade status: talosctl get machinestages --nodes <ip>
After Talos upgrade, upgrade Kubernetes: talosctl upgrade-k8s --nodes <cp-ip> --to 1.30.0
Verify node healthy before proceeding: kubectl get node <name>

root_cause: When a Talos node is unhealthy, the investigation path differs from traditional Linux since there is no SSH. Debugging is done via talosctl using dmesg, logs, health, and service sub-commands over the mTLS API.
fix: Check node health: talosctl health --nodes <ip> --talosconfig talosconfig
View kernel messages: talosctl dmesg --nodes <ip> --talosconfig talosconfig
View kubelet logs: talosctl logs --nodes <ip> kubelet --talosconfig talosconfig
View etcd logs: talosctl logs --nodes <ip> etcd --talosconfig talosconfig
List all services: talosctl services --nodes <ip> --talosconfig talosconfig
Get full machineconfig applied: talosctl get mc v1alpha1 --nodes <ip> -o yaml
Check disk usage: talosctl df --nodes <ip>

root_cause: The talosctl bootstrap command triggers etcd initialization on the first control plane node. If the node is not yet running in maintenance mode, the network is misconfigured, or the VIP/endpoint is unreachable, bootstrap will hang or fail.
fix: Confirm node is in maintenance mode before bootstrapping: talosctl get machinestages --nodes <ip>
Verify the controlplane endpoint IP resolves correctly and is reachable from the bootstrap client
Check that the machineconfig was successfully applied: talosctl get mc --nodes <ip>
View etcd logs for errors: talosctl logs --nodes <ip> etcd
Bootstrap must only be called ONCE on ONE control plane node — calling it again on an existing cluster corrupts etcd
If bootstrap is stuck, check firewall rules — port 2379/2380 (etcd) and 6443 (API server) must be open between control planes

root_cause: Talos supports a built-in Virtual IP (VIP) for the control plane endpoint without requiring kube-vip or MetalLB. The VIP is managed by Talos itself using ARP and moves to a healthy control plane node. Misconfiguration of the network interface or VIP address causes HA to fail.
fix: Configure VIP in machineconfig under machine.network.interfaces[].vip.ip: '192.168.1.100'
The VIP must be on the same subnet as the interface and not assigned to any node statically
Set the cluster endpoint to the VIP: cluster.controlPlane.endpoint: https://192.168.1.100:6443
Verify VIP assignment: talosctl get addresses --nodes <ip> | grep vip
VIP requires at least one healthy control plane with etcd quorum to respond
If VIP is not moving on node failure, check that gratuitous ARP is allowed on the network switch (no ARP inspection blocking it)

root_cause: Talos machine configurations contain sensitive PKI material (CA certs, etcd certs, join tokens). These must be stored securely and managed as code to support reproducible cluster provisioning.
fix: Store generated secrets bundle separately: talosctl gen secrets -o secrets.yaml, then reference in gen config --with-secrets secrets.yaml
Encrypt secrets.yaml at rest using SOPS + age before committing to git
Commit controlplane.yaml and worker.yaml (without secrets) to git; regenerate full config from secrets bundle in CI
Use talosctl gen config --config-patch-control-plane and --config-patch-worker to apply environment-specific patches from separate files
Never commit talosconfig (admin credential) to git unencrypted — it grants full cluster admin access
Store talosconfig in Vault or as a SOPS-encrypted secret

symptoms: trying to install packages on talos
root_cause: Talos has no package manager, no apt/yum/apk, and the root filesystem is read-only. Attempting to modify the OS at runtime is not possible by design. Any tooling needed must be added via system extensions baked into the image.
fix: Do NOT attempt to mount -o remount,rw / — the rootfs is a squashfs overlay and cannot be made writable
Do NOT use talosctl exec/enter to run arbitrary shell commands — there is no shell
For additional kernel modules, add them as system extensions via Image Factory
For node-level debugging tools (tcpdump, strace), use a privileged debug pod: kubectl debug node/<name> -it --image=nicolaka/netshoot
For persistent configuration, use machineconfig extraFiles or extraKernelArgs sections
For iSCSI/NFS support (Longhorn), add the siderolabs/iscsi-tools extension, not host packages

root_cause: Talos upgrades replace the OS image on the install partition and reboot. Without proper preparation, upgrades can cause downtime, etcd quorum loss, or workload disruption on single-node or misconfigured clusters.
fix: Verify etcd health before any upgrade: talosctl etcd status --nodes <cp-ip>
Check all nodes are Ready: kubectl get nodes
Ensure PodDisruptionBudgets allow for node drain: kubectl get pdb -A
Drain worker nodes before upgrading: kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
Upgrade workers one at a time, wait for Ready before next
Upgrade control plane nodes one at a time, checking etcd quorum after each: talosctl etcd status
After Talos upgrade, run: talosctl upgrade-k8s --nodes <cp-ip> --to <k8s-version>

symptoms: talosconfig cert invalid after a year
root_cause: Talos admin talosconfig certificates expire after 1 year by default. Kubernetes certificates managed by Talos also expire. Expired certificates cause talosctl to fail with x509 errors and can prevent API server access.
fix: Renew talosconfig client cert: talosctl config new --talosconfig talosconfig --crt <node-ip>
For Kubernetes certs managed by Talos, they auto-renew on rotation: talosctl rotate-ca --nodes <cp-ip> --talosconfig talosconfig
Check current cert expiry: talosctl get secrets --nodes <ip> -o yaml | grep -A5 certs
If API server cert expired: talosctl service restart apid --nodes <ip>
Regenerate admin talosconfig from secrets bundle if cert is invalid: talosctl gen config --with-secrets secrets.yaml and extract talosconfig
Set up monitoring: alert when less than 30 days remain on cert expiry using Prometheus kubeadm_cert_expiration_timestamp_seconds

root_cause: In a Talos-managed control plane, etcd is a static pod managed by the OS. If a control plane node fails permanently or its etcd data is corrupted, the member must be manually removed from the cluster before reprovisioning.
fix: Check etcd member list: talosctl etcd members --nodes <healthy-cp-ip>
Check etcd status: talosctl etcd status --nodes <healthy-cp-ip>
Remove failed member: talosctl etcd remove-member --nodes <healthy-cp-ip> <member-id>
Force-remove if needed: talosctl etcd forfeit-leadership --nodes <failed-ip> (if node is still reachable)
After removing, reprovision the node: talosctl reset on the node, then reapply controlplane.yaml
Rejoin: after apply-config on fresh node, etcd will auto-join existing cluster

root_cause: Talos uses a fixed partition layout: STATE (cluster state and machineconfig), EPHEMERAL (containerd, kubelet data), and BOOT/EFI partitions. The install disk must be specified in machineconfig. Using the wrong disk or incorrect layout causes node boot failures or data loss.
fix: Specify install disk in machineconfig: machine.install.disk: /dev/sda
Use disk selectors if disk name varies: machine.install.diskSelector.model: 'Samsung SSD' or size: '>= 100GB'
Verify disk names on the node before applying: talosctl disks --nodes <ip> --insecure (in maintenance mode)
For separate data disk for kubelet: add machine.kubelet.extraMounts with a separate disk mounted at /var/lib/kubelet
For Longhorn on separate disk: partition it manually via machine.disks configuration in machineconfig
Ephemeral partition resizing: machine.ephemeral.overlayMountpoints for adding extra storage

symptoms: talos kubelet slow to start pods
root_cause: On Talos, kubelet and containerd store all container images and runtime data under the EPHEMERAL partition. If EPHEMERAL is on a slow disk (HDD or shared RAID), pod startup latency increases significantly. Separating ephemeral data to a dedicated SSD improves performance.
fix: Check current ephemeral disk: talosctl df --nodes <ip> | grep EPHEMERAL
Add a dedicated fast disk for kubelet: configure machine.disks in machineconfig to create a partition
Mount the partition to /var/lib/kubelet and /var/lib/containerd via machine.kubelet.extraMounts
Monitor containerd image pull latency via container_pull_bytes_total in Prometheus
Enable XFS for ephemeral partition for better container workload performance (Talos uses XFS by default)
For NVMe disks, ensure I/O scheduler is none: check via talosctl read /sys/block/nvme0n1/queue/scheduler

root_cause: Talos machineconfig supports inline patches using RFC 6902 JSON Patch or strategic merge patch format. Structuring patches in separate files per concern (network, kubelet, extra-manifests) enables clean GitOps management and environment-specific overrides.
fix: Use separate patch files per concern: network.yaml, kubelet.yaml, extensions.yaml
Apply multiple patches during gen: talosctl gen config --config-patch @network.yaml --config-patch @kubelet.yaml
Apply patches at runtime: talosctl patch mc --nodes <ip> --patch @kubelet.yaml
JSON Patch example to add kubelet extra arg: [{"op":"add","path":"/machine/kubelet/extraArgs/max-pods","value":"250"}]
Strategic merge patch for adding extra SANs: machine: {certSANs: ["10.0.0.1", "k8s.internal"]}
Validate patch before applying: talosctl apply-config --dry-run --nodes <ip> --file patched.yaml

symptoms: talos node stuck in maintenance mode
root_cause: After initial boot from ISO or PXE, a Talos node enters maintenance mode on port 50000 without TLS auth, waiting for machineconfig. If the node does not receive config within the boot timeout, it may loop or exhibit networking issues that prevent config delivery.
fix: Confirm node is in maintenance mode: talosctl --nodes <ip> --insecure get machinestages
Check that port 50000 is reachable: nc -zv <node-ip> 50000
If port is unreachable, verify the node got a DHCP lease: check DHCP server logs for the node's MAC
If using static IP, ensure network config is passed as kernel args: talos.network.interface.ignore or ip= kernel parameter
Re-apply config: talosctl apply-config --insecure --nodes <ip> --file controlplane.yaml
If node rebooted out of maintenance mode without config (timeout), re-image the node from ISO and retry

root_cause: On Talos, Kubernetes component versions (kube-apiserver, kube-controller-manager, kube-scheduler, kubelet, kube-proxy) are managed separately from the OS version using talosctl upgrade-k8s. This command updates static pod manifests and kubelet configuration across all nodes.
fix: Check current k8s version: kubectl version
Check available upgrade targets: talosctl upgrade-k8s --nodes <cp-ip> --to 1.30.0 --dry-run
Run upgrade: talosctl upgrade-k8s --nodes <cp-ip> --talosconfig talosconfig --to 1.30.0
The command automatically updates all control plane static pods and worker kubelet versions
Monitor progress: talosctl get machinestages --nodes <ip> and kubectl get nodes -w
If upgrade fails midway, check: talosctl logs --nodes <ip> kube-apiserver and kubectl describe node <name>

root_cause: etcd uses the Raft consensus algorithm and requires a majority quorum (n/2 + 1) of members to be healthy to accept writes. With 3 members, 2 must be healthy; with 5 members, 3 must be healthy. Falling below quorum causes the cluster to become read-only.
fix: Always deploy etcd with an odd number of members: 3 or 5 (never 2 or 4)
Monitor quorum health: etcdctl endpoint health --endpoints=$(etcdctl member list -w json | jq -r '.members[].clientURLs[]' | paste -sd,)
Check member count and status: etcdctl member list -w table
Alert on etcd_server_has_quorum metric dropping to 0 via Prometheus rule
With kubeadm, use --control-plane-endpoint with a load balancer IP for multi-master setups
Never remove more than one member at a time to avoid losing quorum

symptoms: etcd leader election taking too long
etcdserver: failed to send out heartbeat on time
root_cause: etcd leader election is triggered when the current leader fails to send heartbeats within the election timeout. Frequent re-elections usually indicate disk latency, CPU contention, or network issues between etcd members causing heartbeat delays.
fix: Check current leader: etcdctl endpoint status --endpoints=<all-endpoints> -w table | grep 'IS LEADER'
Monitor leader changes: etcdctl watch --prefix /registry/leases/ (indirect proxy)
Check etcd metrics: curl -s http://localhost:2381/metrics | grep etcd_server_leader_changes_seen_total
Inspect disk latency: etcdctl check perf --endpoints=<endpoint>
Review etcd logs for 'elected leader' and 'became follower' messages: journalctl -u etcd --since='1 hour ago' | grep -E 'leader|follower'
Tune heartbeat and election timeouts if disk is slow: --heartbeat-interval=250 --election-timeout=1250
Ensure etcd data dir is on dedicated fast NVMe/SSD storage

symptoms: etcdserver: mvcc: database space exceeded after defragmentation
root_cause: etcd's MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) store accumulates historical revisions over time. Even after compaction removes old revisions, the space is not immediately reclaimed in the underlying bbolt database until defragmentation is performed.
fix: Check current DB size per member: etcdctl endpoint status --endpoints=<all-endpoints> -w table

Perform defragmentation on one member at a time (causes brief unavailability): etcdctl defrag --endpoints=https://<member>:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
Always defrag followers before the leader to minimize cluster disruption
Verify size reduction after each member: etcdctl endpoint status --endpoints=https://<member>:2379 -w table
Schedule defragmentation via CronJob or systemd timer when cluster is low-traffic
Alert when etcd_mvcc_db_total_size_in_bytes > 80% of quota (default 2GiB)

root_cause: etcd stores all changes as versioned revisions and never deletes them unless explicitly compacted. Without periodic compaction, etcd's database grows continuously, eventually hitting the quota and causing the cluster to reject all writes.
fix: Enable auto-compaction in etcd config: --auto-compaction-mode=periodic --auto-compaction-retention=8h
Alternatively use revision mode: --auto-compaction-mode=revision --auto-compaction-retention=100000
Manual compaction to current revision: REV=$(etcdctl endpoint status --write-out=json | egrep -o '"revision":[0-9]*' | egrep -o '[0-9].*'); etcdctl compact $REV
Verify compaction: etcdctl get / --prefix --keys-only | wc -l (before vs after)
Check current revision: etcdctl endpoint status --write-out=json | jq '.[].Status.header.revision'
After compaction, run defrag to reclaim space: etcdctl defrag --endpoints=<all-endpoints>

root_cause: etcd is the single source of truth for all Kubernetes cluster state. Regular snapshot backups are essential for disaster recovery. A snapshot captures the full etcd state at a point in time and can be used to restore the cluster.
fix: Take a snapshot: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /backup/etcd-snapshot-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M).db --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/healthcheck-client.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/healthcheck-client.key
Verify snapshot integrity: etcdctl snapshot status /backup/etcd-snapshot.db -w table
Automate with Kubernetes CronJob or cron on control plane nodes running every 6h
Store snapshots off-cluster (S3-compatible, NFS, or remote storage)
Retain at least 5 snapshots and delete older ones automatically
Test restore procedure quarterly in a staging environment

symptoms: how to restore etcd from snapshot
root_cause: When the etcd cluster is unrecoverable (all members lost, data corruption), restoring from a snapshot is the only option to recover the Kubernetes cluster. The restore creates a new etcd data directory from the snapshot file.
fix: Stop kube-apiserver on all control plane nodes: mv /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml /tmp/ (for kubeadm static pods)

Restore snapshot on each control plane node with unique peer URLs: etcdctl snapshot restore /backup/etcd-snapshot.db --name=master-1 --initial-cluster=master-1=https://10.0.0.1:2380,master-2=https://10.0.0.2:2380,master-3=https://10.0.0.3:2380 --initial-cluster-token=etcd-cluster-1 --initial-advertise-peer-urls=https://10.0.0.1:2380 --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored
Update etcd static pod manifest to point to new --data-dir
Restore kube-apiserver manifest: mv /tmp/kube-apiserver.yaml /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
Verify cluster recovery: kubectl get nodes && kubectl get pods -A
Check etcd health after restore: etcdctl endpoint health --endpoints=<all>

symptoms: apiserver unable to write to etcd
root_cause: etcd sets a NOSPACE alarm when the database size exceeds the configured quota (default 2GiB). Once the alarm is active, etcd rejects all write requests, making the Kubernetes API server unable to create or update any resources.
fix: Check active alarms: etcdctl alarm list --endpoints=<endpoints> --cacert=<ca> --cert=<cert> --key=<key>
Immediately compact old revisions: REV=$(etcdctl endpoint status -w json | jq '.[].Status.header.revision' | sort -n | tail -1); etcdctl compact $REV
Defragment all members: etcdctl defrag --endpoints=<all-endpoints>
Disarm the alarm after space is freed: etcdctl alarm disarm --endpoints=<endpoints>
Increase quota if needed (requires restart): add --quota-backend-bytes=8589934592 (8GiB) to etcd flags
Enable auto-compaction to prevent recurrence: --auto-compaction-mode=periodic --auto-compaction-retention=1h

symptoms: etcd slow disk latency affecting cluster
slow Kubernetes API responses due to etcd
root_cause: etcd is extremely sensitive to disk write latency because it must fsync WAL (Write-Ahead Log) entries before acknowledging writes to Raft. High disk latency (>10ms p99 for WAL fsync) causes request timeouts, leader elections, and cascading API server slowness.
fix: Measure WAL fsync latency: curl -s http://localhost:2381/metrics | grep etcd_disk_wal_fsync_duration_seconds
Measure backend commit latency: curl -s http://localhost:2381/metrics | grep etcd_disk_backend_commit_duration_seconds
Use dedicated NVMe SSD for etcd data dir (/var/lib/etcd) — never HDD or network-attached storage
Isolate etcd data dir from other I/O: use a separate disk/partition for etcd
Set ionice for etcd process: ionice -c 1 -n 0 -p $(pgrep etcd)
On Linux, set disk scheduler to none or mq-deadline for NVMe: echo mq-deadline > /sys/block/nvme0n1/queue/scheduler
Consider separate dedicated control-plane nodes for etcd to avoid CPU/IO contention with workloads

root_cause: Adding a new etcd member to an existing cluster requires the new member to be pre-registered before starting etcd, so it can join as a learner or voting member. Incorrect procedure can break quorum or cause split-brain.
fix: Add member on existing cluster: etcdctl member add <new-member-name> --peer-urls=https://<new-node-ip>:2380 --endpoints=<existing-endpoint> --cacert=<ca> --cert=<cert> --key=<key>
Note the ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER and ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER_STATE=existing output from the add command
Start etcd on new node with: --initial-cluster-state=existing --initial-cluster=<full-list-including-new> --initial-advertise-peer-urls=https://<new-node-ip>:2380
Verify new member joined: etcdctl member list -w table
Check cluster health after addition: etcdctl endpoint health --endpoints=<all-including-new>
For kubeadm multi-master: kubeadm join --control-plane handles etcd member add automatically

root_cause: Removing a failed or decommissioned etcd member must be done through etcdctl to update the cluster membership. If a dead member is left in the member list, it counts against quorum and can prevent future quorum from being achieved with remaining healthy members.
fix: List members to get the member ID: etcdctl member list --endpoints=<healthy-endpoint> -w table
Remove the failed member by ID: etcdctl member remove <member-id> --endpoints=<healthy-endpoint> --cacert=<ca> --cert=<cert> --key=<key>
Verify removal: etcdctl member list -w table (member should no longer appear)
Delete etcd data dir on the removed node to prevent stale data: rm -rf /var/lib/etcd/*
Update any etcd ConfigMaps or static pod manifests that reference the old member peer URLs
For kubeadm: also run kubeadm reset on the removed node to clean up control-plane components

symptoms: etcd on shared disk with workloads
etcd data dir on same volume as containers
root_cause: Running etcd data on the same disk as container images, kubelet data, or application workloads causes I/O contention. Because etcd requires consistent low-latency disk writes for WAL fsyncs, any competing disk I/O can cause fsync delays, triggering leader elections and API server instability.
fix: Mount a dedicated disk/partition exclusively for etcd: /var/lib/etcd on its own NVMe SSD
Separate /var/lib/containers (or /var/lib/docker) and /var/lib/kubelet onto different disks from /var/lib/etcd
Verify no other processes writing to etcd disk: iostat -x 1 or iotop -d 1
Use cgroups I/O weight to prioritize etcd: set etcd cgroup blkio.weight to 1000
Monitor I/O wait on control plane nodes: node_cpu_seconds_total{mode='iowait'} in Prometheus
In kubeadm setups, configure --data-dir in etcd static pod manifest to point to dedicated mount

root_cause: etcdctl requires TLS certificates matching those configured on the etcd server. In kubeadm clusters, etcd uses mTLS with certificates in /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/. Missing or incorrect certificate flags cause connection failures.
fix: Use the correct certificate paths for kubeadm clusters: etcdctl --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key endpoint health

Set environment variables to avoid repeating flags: export ETCDCTL_API=3; export ETCDCTL_ENDPOINTS=https://127.0.0.1:2379; export ETCDCTL_CACERT=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt; export ETCDCTL_CERT=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt; export ETCDCTL_KEY=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
Alternatively use peer certs: --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/peer.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/peer.key
Verify cert validity: openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt -noout -dates

Run etcdctl inside the etcd container if on kubeadm: kubectl exec -it -n kube-system etcd-<node> -- etcdctl --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key member list

symptoms: how to tune etcd for production
etcd slow under heavy Kubernetes load
root_cause: Default etcd settings are conservative and may not be optimal for large Kubernetes clusters with many resources, high API server request rates, or slow disks. Tuning compaction, heartbeat intervals, quotas, and snapshot settings improves stability under load.
fix: Increase backend quota to 8GiB for large clusters: --quota-backend-bytes=8589934592
Enable periodic auto-compaction every hour: --auto-compaction-mode=periodic --auto-compaction-retention=1h
Tune heartbeat for slower networks/disks (500ms heartbeat, 2500ms election): --heartbeat-interval=500 --election-timeout=2500
Increase snapshot count to reduce WAL log churn on busy clusters: --snapshot-count=10000
Set GOMAXPROCS to number of available cores for etcd process
Use --max-request-bytes=10485760 (10MiB) if large Helm chart values or CRD schemas cause 'request too large' errors
Monitor etcd_network_peer_round_trip_time_seconds and etcd_disk_wal_fsync_duration_seconds_bucket in Grafana

symptoms: how to check etcd cluster health
root_cause: Regular etcd health verification is essential for Kubernetes cluster stability. etcd issues are often silent until they cause API server failures. A systematic health check routine catches problems early before they impact workloads.
fix: Check endpoint health: etcdctl endpoint health --endpoints=<all-3-or-5> -w table
Check endpoint status (leader, DB size, revision): etcdctl endpoint status --endpoints=<all> -w table
