List all members: etcdctl member list -w table (verify expected count and all are 'started')
Check for active alarms: etcdctl alarm list
Check DB size vs quota: compare etcd_mvcc_db_total_size_in_bytes against --quota-backend-bytes
Check WAL fsync p99: curl -s http://localhost:2381/metrics | grep 'etcd_disk_wal_fsync_duration_seconds{quantile="0.99"}'
Check leader stability: etcd_server_leader_changes_seen_total should be low (< 5 per hour)
Verify backup snapshot freshness: check timestamp of latest snapshot in backup storage

symptoms: etcd learner member stuck not promoted
etcd member never becomes voting member
root_cause: When adding an etcd member, it starts as a learner (non-voting) until it catches up with the cluster's data. If the learner falls too far behind or the promotion is not triggered, it remains a learner indefinitely and does not contribute to quorum.
fix: Check if member is a learner: etcdctl member list -w table (look for 'isLearner: true')
Promote learner to full voting member: etcdctl member promote <member-id> --endpoints=<leader-endpoint>
Verify learner is caught up before promoting: etcdctl endpoint status should show similar revisions
If learner is too far behind, consider re-adding with a fresh data dir snapshot from a healthy member
Check etcd logs on learner for sync progress: journalctl -u etcd | grep -i 'snapshot\|apply\|learner'
Ensure network connectivity between learner and all existing members on port 2380 (peer port)

symptoms: etcd log took too long to execute warning
etcd apply entries took too long
etcd slow apply warning in logs
etcdserver: read-only range request took too long
root_cause: etcd logs warnings when operations take longer than expected thresholds. 'apply entries took too long' indicates high CPU or disk pressure during Raft log application. 'read-only range request took too long' suggests the etcd backend is under heavy read load or the data set is large.
fix: Search etcd logs for slow operation warnings: journalctl -u etcd | grep -E 'took too long|slow' | tail -50
Correlate with disk latency metrics: etcd_disk_backend_commit_duration_seconds histogram
Reduce API server request rate by enabling API server request throttling: --max-requests-inflight=800 --max-mutating-requests-inflight=400
Enable etcd request audit logging to identify frequent expensive callers
Run etcdctl check perf to baseline performance: etcdctl check perf --endpoints=<endpoint>
Consider separating etcd onto dedicated nodes away from kube-apiserver workloads
If using RKE2, check /var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/db/etcd/ data dir disk health

symptoms: should etcd run on separate nodes
root_cause: Kubernetes supports two etcd topologies: stacked (etcd runs on the same nodes as control-plane components) and external (etcd runs on dedicated nodes). Stacked is simpler but couples etcd availability to control-plane node availability. External provides isolation but requires more nodes.
fix: For most on-premise clusters: use stacked etcd on 3 control-plane nodes (cost-effective HA)
For large production clusters (>50 nodes, high API churn): use external etcd on 5 dedicated nodes with NVMe SSDs
Ensure control-plane/etcd nodes have reserved CPU and memory: taint them with node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule
With kubeadm stacked: 3 control-plane nodes = 3 etcd members automatically
With kubeadm external etcd: use --external-etcd-endpoints flag pointing to dedicated etcd cluster
For RKE2: etcd runs embedded by default on server nodes; use 3 or 5 server nodes for HA
For Talos: etcd is managed automatically when bootstrapping; use talosctl etcd members to inspect

root_cause: etcdctl is the primary CLI tool for managing etcd clusters. Most Kubernetes administrators need a reference for the most common operational commands. The v3 API (ETCDCTL_API=3) is required for Kubernetes and supports the gRPC endpoint.
fix: Always set: export ETCDCTL_API=3
List all keys under a prefix (e.g., deployments): etcdctl get /registry/deployments/default --prefix --keys-only
Get a specific resource: etcdctl get /registry/pods/default/mypod -w json | python3 -c 'import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d["kvs"][0]["value"])' | base64 -d
Watch for changes: etcdctl watch /registry/configmaps/default --prefix
Check cluster performance: etcdctl check perf
List endpoints and their status: etcdctl endpoint status -w table
Move leader to another member: etcdctl move-leader <member-id>
Get current revision: etcdctl endpoint status -w json | jq '.[0].Status.header.revision'

symptoms: error: rafthttp: request cluster ID mismatch
root_cause: Each etcd cluster has a unique cluster ID. When an etcd member's stored cluster ID doesn't match the cluster it's trying to join (e.g., after restoring a snapshot or accidentally connecting to wrong peers), it refuses to join to prevent data corruption.
fix: Identify the cluster ID mismatch in logs: journalctl -u etcd | grep 'cluster ID mismatch'
Remove the conflicting member's data dir: rm -rf /var/lib/etcd/member
Re-add the member to the cluster following proper member-add procedure: etcdctl member add <name> --peer-urls=https://<ip>:2380
Start etcd with --initial-cluster-state=existing (not 'new') to join existing cluster
If restoring from snapshot, ensure all members use same --initial-cluster-token to establish cluster identity
Never copy data dirs between etcd members — each member must sync independently

root_cause: etcd exposes Prometheus metrics on port 2381 (metrics endpoint) by default. Monitoring key etcd metrics is critical for early detection of performance degradation, disk issues, leader instability, and storage quota violations before they cause cluster outages.
fix: Verify etcd metrics endpoint: curl -s http://localhost:2381/metrics | head -20

Key metrics to alert on: etcd_server_has_quorum==0 (critical), etcd_server_leader_changes_seen_total>5/hour (high), etcd_disk_wal_fsync_duration_seconds{quantile='0.99'}>0.01 (high), etcd_mvcc_db_total_size_in_bytes>80% of quota (warning)
Configure Prometheus scrape in kube-prometheus-stack values: add etcd job pointing to https://127.0.0.1:2381 with client certs
Import Grafana etcd dashboard: https://grafana.com/grafana/dashboards/3070 (official etcd dashboard ID 3070)
Enable etcd alerts from kube-prometheus-stack: etcd alert rules are included by default when etcd scraping is configured
Check etcd_server_slow_apply_total and etcd_server_slow_read_indexes_total for processing slowness
In RKE2: etcd metrics are accessible at https://127.0.0.1:2381/metrics using RKE2 etcd certs at /var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/tls/etcd/

symptoms: how to create a CloudNativePG Cluster CRD
root_cause: CloudNativePG manages PostgreSQL via a Cluster custom resource. A minimal Cluster spec requires instances count, storage configuration, and optionally a bootstrap section. Getting the schema right avoids reconciliation errors.
fix:
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: pg-primary
  namespace: postgres
spec:
  instances: 3
  storage:
    size: 20Gi
    storageClass: longhorn
  postgresql:
    parameters:
      max_connections: "200"
      shared_buffers: "512MB"
EOF
kubectl get cluster pg-primary -n postgres -o wide
kubectl describe cluster pg-primary -n postgres   # check Status.Phase == 'Cluster in healthy state'
kubectl get pods -n postgres -l cnpg.io/cluster=pg-primary

root_cause: CloudNativePG implements synchronous or asynchronous streaming replication using PostgreSQL's built-in WAL streaming. Each instance beyond the primary becomes a hot-standby replica, automatically configured via pg_basebackup and replication slots managed by the operator.
fix: Set spec.instances: 3 for one primary + two standbys
Configure synchronous replication: spec.postgresql.synchronous: { method: any, number: 1, maxStandbyNamesFromCluster: 2 }
Check replication lag: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- psql -c "SELECT * FROM pg_stat_replication;"
Inspect replication slots: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- psql -c "SELECT slot_name, active, restart_lsn FROM pg_replication_slots;"
Monitor sync status via metric: cnpg_pg_replication_lag (exposed on :9187/metrics)

symptoms: how to configure failover in CloudNativePG
root_cause: CloudNativePG implements automated failover without external tooling. When the primary pod becomes unhealthy, the operator elects the most-advanced replica as new primary, updates the service endpoints, and demotes the old primary if it recovers.
fix: Failover is automatic; ensure spec.failoverDelay is set (default 0s): spec: { failoverDelay: 0 }
Trigger a manual switchover: kubectl cnpg promote pg-primary pg-primary-2 -n postgres
Monitor events: kubectl get events -n postgres --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | grep -i failover
Verify new primary: kubectl get cluster pg-primary -n postgres -o jsonpath='{.status.currentPrimary}'
After failover, old primary rejoins as replica automatically — confirm with: kubectl get pods -n postgres -l cnpg.io/cluster=pg-primary

root_cause: A switchover is a planned, graceful promotion of a replica to primary with zero data loss. It is used for maintenance, node draining, or rolling upgrades. The operator coordinates the handoff using the cnpg kubectl plugin.
fix: Install kubectl plugin: kubectl krew install cnpg
List instances to pick the target replica: kubectl cnpg status pg-primary -n postgres
Initiate switchover: kubectl cnpg promote pg-primary pg-primary-2 -n postgres
Watch status: kubectl get cluster pg-primary -n postgres -w
Confirm: kubectl get cluster pg-primary -n postgres -o jsonpath='{.status.currentPrimary}'

symptoms: how to configure Barman backup CloudNativePG
root_cause: CloudNativePG uses Barman Cloud (barman-cloud-backup) to ship base backups and WAL segments to object storage (S3-compatible, Azure Blob, GCS). The backup destination is configured in the Cluster spec under spec.backup.barmanObjectStore.
fix: Create secret with S3 credentials: kubectl create secret generic s3-creds -n postgres --from-literal=ACCESS_KEY_ID=<key> --from-literal=ACCESS_SECRET_KEY=<secret>

Add backup config to Cluster spec:
spec:
  backup:
    barmanObjectStore:
      destinationPath: s3://pg-backups/pg-primary
      endpointURL: https://minio.storage.svc
      s3Credentials:
        accessKeyId:
          name: s3-creds
          key: ACCESS_KEY_ID
        secretAccessKey:
          name: s3-creds
          key: ACCESS_SECRET_KEY
      wal:
        compression: gzip
    retentionPolicy: 30d
Trigger an immediate backup: kubectl cnpg backup pg-primary -n postgres
List backups: kubectl get backup -n postgres
Inspect backup status: kubectl describe backup <backup-name> -n postgres

symptoms: how to do point-in-time recovery CloudNativePG
root_cause: CloudNativePG supports PITR by bootstrapping a new Cluster from an existing backup and replaying WAL up to a target time. This uses the recoveryTarget.targetTime field and requires continuous WAL archiving to be configured.
fix: Ensure WAL archiving is enabled in the source cluster (spec.backup.barmanObjectStore.wal configured)

Create a new Cluster with bootstrap from recovery:
apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: pg-restored
  namespace: postgres
spec:
  instances: 1
  storage:
    size: 20Gi
    storageClass: longhorn
  bootstrap:
    recovery:
      source: pg-primary
      recoveryTarget:
        targetTime: "2024-06-15 14:30:00"
  externalClusters:
  - name: pg-primary
    barmanObjectStore:
      destinationPath: s3://pg-backups/pg-primary
      endpointURL: https://minio.storage.svc
      s3Credentials:
        accessKeyId:
          name: s3-creds
          key: ACCESS_KEY_ID
        secretAccessKey:
          name: s3-creds
          key: ACCESS_SECRET_KEY
Monitor recovery progress: kubectl logs -n postgres pg-restored-1 -f
Verify data: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-restored-1 -- psql -c 'SELECT now();'

symptoms: WAL not shipped to object storage
root_cause: WAL archiving failures prevent PITR capability. Common causes include incorrect S3 credentials, wrong endpoint URL, missing bucket, network policy blocking egress to object storage, or insufficient permissions on the barman secret.
fix: Check archiving errors in pod logs: kubectl logs -n postgres pg-primary-1 | grep -i 'archive'
Verify barman-cloud-wal-archive can reach S3: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- barman-cloud-wal-archive --test s3://pg-backups/pg-primary <WALFILE>
Confirm secret keys match spec: kubectl get secret s3-creds -n postgres -o jsonpath='{.data}' | base64 -d
Verify Cilium NetworkPolicy allows egress to minio: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- curl -v https://minio.storage.svc/
Inspect cluster status for archive warnings: kubectl get cluster pg-primary -n postgres -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions}'
Check archive_status in PostgreSQL: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- psql -c "SELECT * FROM pg_stat_archiver;"

symptoms: best practice storage class for CloudNativePG
which storage class to use for postgres
root_cause: PostgreSQL is I/O-intensive and requires a storage class that provides consistent low-latency block access. Longhorn with replica count 2-3 provides resilience; local-path provides raw performance but no node portability. The choice impacts failover behavior.
fix: Use Longhorn with dataLocality: best-effort and replicaCount: 2 for balanced performance and redundancy
Set storageClass annotation: longhorn.io/data-locality: best-effort on the Longhorn StorageClass
In Cluster spec: spec.storage.storageClass: longhorn and spec.storage.size: 50Gi (size according to data growth estimates)
For write-heavy workloads consider: longhorn.io/number-of-replicas: '2' to reduce write amplification
Avoid ReadWriteMany (RWX) PVCs for PostgreSQL data — always use ReadWriteOnce (RWO)
Enable Longhorn recurring snapshots as an additional backup layer separate from Barman

symptoms: how to set shared_buffers max_connections CloudNativePG
root_cause: CloudNativePG exposes all PostgreSQL GUC parameters via spec.postgresql.parameters. Insufficient shared_buffers, work_mem, or max_connections causes query slowdowns and OOM kills. Resource requests/limits must be set in the Cluster spec to prevent eviction.
fix:
Set PostgreSQL parameters in Cluster spec:
spec:
  postgresql:
    parameters:
      shared_buffers: "1GB"
      work_mem: "32MB"
      maintenance_work_mem: "256MB"
      effective_cache_size: "3GB"
      max_connections: "200"
      wal_buffers: "64MB"

Set container resources:
spec:
  resources:
    requests:
      memory: "2Gi"
      cpu: "500m"
    limits:
      memory: "4Gi"
      cpu: "2"
Use PgBouncer sidecar for connection pooling: add spec.managed.services.disabledDefaultServices and deploy a Pooler CRD
Monitor pg_stat_statements: kubectl exec pg-primary-1 -n postgres -- psql -c 'SELECT query, calls, mean_exec_time FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC LIMIT 10;'

symptoms: how to set up monitoring CloudNativePG
root_cause: CloudNativePG exposes a Prometheus metrics endpoint on port 9187 of each instance pod. The operator also provides a PodMonitor CRD resource. Grafana dashboards are available from the official CloudNativePG repository.
fix:
Enable monitoring in Cluster spec:
spec:
  monitoring:
    enablePodMonitor: true
Verify PodMonitor was created: kubectl get podmonitor -n postgres
Import Grafana dashboard ID 20417 (CloudNativePG) from grafana.com/grafana/dashboards
Key metrics to alert on: cnpg_pg_replication_lag, cnpg_collector_pg_wal_files_total, cnpg_pg_database_size_bytes, cnpg_backends_total
Check metrics endpoint directly: kubectl port-forward -n postgres pg-primary-1 9187:9187 && curl http://localhost:9187/metrics | grep cnpg_

root_cause: The CloudNativePG operator reconciles Cluster resources continuously. Stuck states usually arise from pending PVC provisioning, image pull errors, incorrect bootstrap config, or resource quota exhaustion. The operator logs and cluster status conditions are the primary diagnostic surface.
fix: Check cluster conditions: kubectl get cluster pg-primary -n postgres -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions}' | jq .
Check operator logs: kubectl logs -n cnpg-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=cloudnative-pg --tail=100
Check PVC status: kubectl get pvc -n postgres -l cnpg.io/cluster=pg-primary
Describe stuck pod: kubectl describe pod pg-primary-1 -n postgres | grep -A20 Events
Check resource quotas: kubectl describe resourcequota -n postgres
Look for image pull issues: kubectl get events -n postgres --field-selector reason=Failed

root_cause: Backup failures via barman-cloud-backup are commonly caused by S3 bucket permissions, expired credentials, insufficient disk space for local WAL staging, or network timeouts to the object store endpoint. The Backup object status.phase will show 'failed' with a message.
fix: Check failed backup reason: kubectl describe backup <name> -n postgres | grep -A10 Status
Inspect pod logs during backup: kubectl logs -n postgres pg-primary-1 | grep barman
Test connectivity manually: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- barman-cloud-check-wal-archive --cloud-provider aws-s3 s3://pg-backups/pg-primary
Verify IAM/RBAC permissions on the bucket (ListBucket, GetObject, PutObject, DeleteObject)
Check if disk pressure is preventing WAL staging: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- df -h /var/lib/postgresql/data

Rotate credentials and update secret: kubectl create secret generic s3-creds -n postgres --from-literal=ACCESS_KEY_ID=<new> --from-literal=ACCESS_SECRET_KEY=<new> --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

root_cause: Using hostPath or emptyDir for PostgreSQL data in CloudNativePG bypasses the operator's PVC lifecycle management, preventing proper pod rescheduling, backup integration, and failover. Data is lost on node failure or pod restart.
fix: Always use a proper StorageClass with RWO PVCs: spec.storage.storageClass: longhorn (or rook-ceph-block)
Never set spec.storage.pvcTemplate with hostPath volumes
Verify PVC binding after cluster creation: kubectl get pvc -n postgres
If accidentally deployed without proper storage, scale down and recreate with correct storage config — data migration may be required from Barman backups
Set PVC reclaim policy to Retain on the StorageClass to prevent accidental data loss: kubectl patch storageclass longhorn -p '{"reclaimPolicy":"Retain"}'

symptoms: how to configure PgBouncer connection pooling CloudNativePG
root_cause: Node.js applications with many pods can exhaust PostgreSQL max_connections. CloudNativePG provides a Pooler CRD that deploys a managed PgBouncer instance in front of the cluster, dramatically reducing connection count at the PostgreSQL level.
fix:
Create a Pooler resource:
apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1
kind: Pooler
metadata:
  name: pg-pooler-rw
  namespace: postgres
spec:
  cluster:
    name: pg-primary
  instances: 2
  type: rw
  pgbouncer:
    poolMode: transaction
    parameters:
      max_client_conn: "400"
      default_pool_size: "20"
kubectl apply -f pooler.yaml
Applications connect to pg-pooler-rw service instead of pg-primary-rw
Monitor PgBouncer stats: kubectl exec -n postgres deploy/pg-pooler-rw -- psql pgbouncer -c 'SHOW POOLS;'
Check Pooler status: kubectl get pooler pg-pooler-rw -n postgres

root_cause: A production CloudNativePG deployment requires attention to backup, monitoring, resource limits, storage durability, network policies, and secret management to ensure data safety and availability.
fix: [ ] spec.instances >= 3 for HA (1 primary + 2 standbys)
[ ] spec.backup.barmanObjectStore configured and tested with at least one successful backup
[ ] spec.backup.retentionPolicy set (e.g., 30d)
[ ] spec.monitoring.enablePodMonitor: true and alerts configured in Prometheus
[ ] spec.resources.requests and limits set for CPU and memory
[ ] StorageClass with RWO, Retain policy, and at least 2 replicas (Longhorn)
[ ] Vault/ESO managing s3-creds and app DB passwords (no hardcoded secrets)
[ ] NetworkPolicy allowing only authorized pods to port 5432
[ ] kubectl cnpg status run and reviewed before going live
[ ] PITR tested end-to-end in staging

root_cause: In rare network partition scenarios, the old primary may continue serving writes while the operator promotes a new primary, risking data divergence. CloudNativePG uses Kubernetes API server as the fencing authority and employs instance poisoning (pg_ctl stop) on the demoted primary to prevent split-brain.
fix: Verify fencing is working: check that old primary pod was terminated or restarted after promotion event
Inspect cluster events for fencing action: kubectl get events -n postgres | grep -i fencing
Ensure RBAC allows the operator to delete pods: kubectl get clusterrolebinding | grep cnpg
If manual intervention needed, stop the old primary: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- pg_ctl stop -D /var/lib/postgresql/data
Review operator logs around the partition time: kubectl logs -n cnpg-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=cloudnative-pg | grep -i fenc
After recovery, verify timeline IDs match: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-2 -- psql -c 'SELECT pg_last_wal_replay_lsn();'

root_cause: The ScheduledBackup CRD uses a cron expression in spec.schedule. If the schedule is misconfigured, the cluster name is wrong, or the operator lacks permissions, backups silently fail. The ScheduledBackup status reveals last backup time.
fix:
Create a ScheduledBackup:
apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1
kind: ScheduledBackup
metadata:
  name: pg-daily-backup
  namespace: postgres
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  backupOwnerReference: self
  cluster:
    name: pg-primary
Check last scheduled run: kubectl get scheduledbackup pg-daily-backup -n postgres -o jsonpath='{.status}'
Verify operator saw the schedule: kubectl logs -n cnpg-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=cloudnative-pg | grep ScheduledBackup
List all backups to confirm they ran: kubectl get backup -n postgres --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
Fix cron syntax if needed — CloudNativePG uses 6-field cron (seconds-inclusive) in some versions; check docs for version

symptoms: CloudNativePG WAL volume growing too large
root_cause: WAL accumulation occurs when archiving is lagging (network issues to object store), replication slots are held by disconnected replicas, or max_wal_size is too large. A full WAL volume causes PostgreSQL to stop accepting writes.
fix: Check WAL archiving lag: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- psql -c "SELECT * FROM pg_stat_archiver;"

Identify inactive replication slots consuming WAL: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- psql -c "SELECT slot_name, active, pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) AS retained FROM pg_replication_slots;"
Drop stale slots if safe: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- psql -c "SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('<slot_name>');"
Tune WAL settings in spec.postgresql.parameters: max_wal_size: '2GB', min_wal_size: '512MB'
Monitor cnpg_collector_pg_wal_files_total metric in Prometheus and alert at threshold
Ensure object storage egress is not throttled by checking Cilium EgressPolicy for postgres namespace

symptoms: how to manage CloudNativePG secrets with Vault
root_cause: CloudNativePG uses Kubernetes secrets for superuser and application user credentials. Integrating with External Secrets Operator (ESO) and HashiCorp Vault ensures credentials are rotated automatically and never stored in Git, following least-privilege principles.
fix: Create a Vault KV secret: vault kv put secret/postgres/pg-primary superuser-password=<pwd> app-password=<pwd>

Create an ExternalSecret to sync to K8s:
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: pg-primary-superuser
  namespace: postgres
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h
  secretStoreRef:
    name: vault-backend
    kind: ClusterSecretStore
  target:
    name: pg-primary-superuser
    creationPolicy: Owner
  data:
  - secretKey: password
    remoteRef:
      key: secret/postgres/pg-primary
      property: superuser-password
Reference the secret in Cluster spec: spec.superuserSecret.name: pg-primary-superuser
Trigger credential rotation: vault kv patch secret/postgres/pg-primary superuser-password=<new-pwd>  (ESO will sync within refreshInterval)
Verify secret updated: kubectl get secret pg-primary-superuser -n postgres -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d

symptoms: standby lagging too far behind primary
root_cause: Replica lag can be caused by high write throughput on the primary, network bandwidth limits between nodes, resource throttling on the replica pod, or a replica falling behind during a temporary network disruption. Extended lag may trigger the operator to rebuild the replica from scratch.
fix: Check replication lag metric: kubectl port-forward -n postgres pg-primary-1 9187:9187 && curl -s localhost:9187/metrics | grep cnpg_pg_replication_lag
Inspect replication from primary: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-1 -- psql -c "SELECT application_name, write_lag, flush_lag, replay_lag FROM pg_stat_replication;"
Check replica pod resource pressure: kubectl top pod -n postgres
Inspect streaming replication on the replica: kubectl exec -n postgres pg-primary-2 -- psql -c "SELECT status, sender_host, latency FROM pg_stat_wal_receiver;"
If lag is permanent, let the operator rebuild: kubectl delete pod pg-primary-2 -n postgres  (operator will re-create and resync from primary)
Set alert in Prometheus: cnpg_pg_replication_lag > 300 (seconds) fires a warning

symptoms: how to deploy PgBouncer with CloudNativePG Pooler CRD
how to configure connection pooling cloudnativepg
root_cause: CloudNativePG provides a first-class Pooler CRD that deploys PgBouncer as a sidecar-free proxy managed by the CNPG operator, removing the need to manage PgBouncer deployments manually.
fix: Create a Pooler resource: kubectl apply -f pooler.yaml referencing your Cluster name

Pooler YAML: apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1, kind: Pooler, metadata.name: app-pooler, spec.cluster.name: <ClusterName>, spec.instances: 2, spec.type: rw, spec.pgbouncer.poolMode: transaction, spec.pgbouncer.parameters.max_client_conn: '500'
Verify pooler pods: kubectl get pods -l cnpg.io/poolerName=app-pooler -n <namespace>
Check pooler service: kubectl get svc app-pooler -n <namespace> — it exposes port 5432 to applications
View PgBouncer config: kubectl exec -it <pooler-pod> -- cat /etc/pgbouncer/pgbouncer.ini
Check operator logs: kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=cloudnative-pg -n cnpg-system --tail=50

symptoms: pgbouncer transaction mode prepared statement error
ERROR prepared statement does not exist pgbouncer
node pg prepared statements failing with pgbouncer
root_cause: PgBouncer in transaction pooling mode does not persist server connections across transactions, so named prepared statements created in one transaction are unavailable in the next because a different backend connection may be assigned.
fix: Set pgbouncer.poolMode to 'session' if prepared statements are required, or disable them in the client
For Node.js pg driver: pass { statement_timeout: 30000 } and avoid pg.Pool query caching; use parameterized queries without named prepared statements
For Prisma: set datasource db { url = env('DATABASE_URL') } and append ?pgbouncer=true&connection_limit=1 to DATABASE_URL to disable Prisma's prepared statement cache
In Pooler CRD: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.ignore_startup_parameters: 'extra_float_digits,options' to suppress harmless parameter errors
Alternatively pin pgbouncer.poolMode: session in spec.pgbouncer and set pool_size conservatively to avoid exhausting max Postgres connections
Validate mode in pgbouncer console: kubectl exec -it <pooler-pod> -- psql -p 6432 pgbouncer -c 'SHOW POOLS;'

symptoms: connection refused too many clients pgbouncer
sorry too many clients already pgbouncer
root_cause: PgBouncer rejects new client connections when the total number of active client connections across all pools exceeds max_client_conn, which defaults to 100. Under high concurrency or aggressive pod autoscaling this limit is reached quickly.
fix: Increase max_client_conn in Pooler CRD: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.max_client_conn: '1000'
Reload without restart: kubectl exec -it <pooler-pod> -- psql -p 6432 pgbouncer -c 'RELOAD;'
Monitor current client count: kubectl exec -it <pooler-pod> -- psql -p 6432 pgbouncer -c 'SHOW CLIENTS;'
Tune default_pool_size per database user: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.default_pool_size: '25'
Ensure Node.js application pools are bounded: pass { max: 5 } to new Pool() in pg to prevent each pod from opening unbounded connections
Set reserve_pool_size for burst tolerance: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.reserve_pool_size: '5'

symptoms: too many postgres connections from node pods
FATAL remaining connection slots are reserved postgres
root_cause: Each Node.js pod creates its own pg.Pool with a generous max (e.g. 10-20). With many replicas managed by KEDA autoscaling, total connections = replicas × pool_max, which can saturate PostgreSQL's max_connections (default 100) even before pgbouncer is considered.
fix: Never connect Node.js pods directly to Postgres; always route through PgBouncer Pooler service
Set pg.Pool max to a low value per pod: const pool = new Pool({ max: 3, connectionTimeoutMillis: 5000 })
Calculate safe pool_size: (postgres max_connections - 10 superuser slots) / pgbouncer_instances / expected_concurrent_users_per_pgbouncer
Set Postgres max_connections in CNPG Cluster: spec.postgresql.parameters.max_connections: '200'
Add KEDA ScaledObject maxReplicaCount guard so pod count × pool_max never exceeds PgBouncer max_client_conn
Expose pg_stat_activity count in Prometheus and alert when count > 80% of max_connections

symptoms: how to size node.js pg pool on serverless pods
optimal pool size per pod kubernetes
pg pool settings for short-lived pods
root_cause: Serverless-style pods (scale-to-zero with KEDA) have unpredictable lifetimes and unpredictable concurrency; over-allocating pool connections wastes backend slots while under-allocating causes latency spikes during scale-up.
fix: Set pool max to 2-5 per pod when using PgBouncer in transaction mode; the pooler multiplexes efficiently so large per-pod pools provide no benefit
Set idleTimeoutMillis: 10000 and connectionTimeoutMillis: 3000 in pg.Pool to release idle connections quickly on scale-down
Use allowExitOnIdle: true in pg.Pool so Node.js process can exit cleanly when KEDA scales to zero
Set min: 0 in Prisma datasource: { connection_limit: 3, pool_timeout: 10 } to avoid holding open connections during idle periods
Configure PgBouncer server_idle_timeout: 30 so backend connections are freed when pods are idle: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.server_idle_timeout: '30'
Monitor pool wait queue with custom metric pg_pool_waiting_count via prom-client and expose to KEDA for connection-pressure-based autoscaling

symptoms: how to diagnose pgbouncer connection exhaustion
root_cause: PgBouncer exposes an admin console on port 6432 database 'pgbouncer' that provides SHOW POOLS, SHOW STATS, SHOW CLIENTS, and SHOW SERVERS commands for real-time pooling diagnostics.
fix: Open PgBouncer admin console: kubectl exec -it <pooler-pod> -n <namespace> -- psql -p 6432 pgbouncer
Show pool statistics: SELECT * FROM pgbouncer.pools; or \\c pgbouncer then SHOW POOLS;
Columns to check: cl_active (active clients), cl_waiting (queued clients — nonzero means exhaustion), sv_active (backend connections in use), sv_idle (free backend connections)
Show global stats: SHOW STATS; — look for avg_query_time spikes indicating slow queries holding connections
Show server connections: SHOW SERVERS; — check state column for 'used' vs 'idle' ratio
Export metrics to Prometheus: CNPG Pooler exposes /metrics on port 9187; add PodMonitor targeting label cnpg.io/poolerName
Alert rule: increase(pgbouncer_client_connections_waiting_total[1m]) > 0 for 2m

symptoms: pgbouncer session mode too many postgres connections
when to use transaction vs session pool mode
root_cause: Session mode assigns one backend connection per client for the entire client session lifetime, giving connection counts equal to concurrent clients. Transaction mode multiplexes many clients over far fewer backend connections, dramatically reducing Postgres load but breaking session-level features.
fix: Default to transaction mode (spec.pgbouncer.poolMode: transaction) for Node.js API and worker pods unless session features are needed
Session features incompatible with transaction mode: SET, LISTEN/NOTIFY, advisory locks, cursors, PREPARE/EXECUTE
If LISTEN/NOTIFY is needed (e.g. for real-time features), use a separate dedicated Pooler in session mode or connect directly with a limited pool
Measure connection multiplexing ratio: sv_active/cl_active in SHOW POOLS; transaction mode typically achieves 10:1 to 50:1 client-to-server ratio
Statement mode (spec.pgbouncer.poolMode: statement) provides maximum multiplexing but breaks multi-statement transactions — avoid for general application use
After switching modes: kubectl rollout restart deployment/<pooler-deployment> -n <namespace>

root_cause: Prisma by default opens a connection pool and uses named prepared statements, both of which are incompatible with PgBouncer in transaction mode. Without the pgbouncer=true query parameter, Prisma may also mishandle connection lifecycle on scale events.
fix: Append query parameters to DATABASE_URL: postgresql://user:pass@app-pooler:5432/db?pgbouncer=true&connection_limit=3&pool_timeout=10&connect_timeout=10
The pgbouncer=true flag disables Prisma's prepared statement cache and switches it to simple query protocol
For Prisma v5+: set { datasource: { db: { url: env('DATABASE_URL') } } } and avoid using $queryRaw with named params without casting
Set PRISMA_CLIENT_ENGINE_TYPE=binary if using alpine-based Node images to avoid GLIBC issues unrelated to pooling
Verify connection string resolves to pooler service: kubectl exec -it <app-pod> -- nslookup app-pooler.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
Check Prisma connection pool: add metrics: ['metrics'] to PrismaClient and expose prisma_client_queries_active gauge

symptoms: what to check before deploying pgbouncer kubernetes
root_cause: A PgBouncer Pooler deployed without tuning default parameters will fail under production load; a systematic checklist prevents common misconfigurations.
fix: Set max_client_conn >= expected peak concurrent connections from all pods: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.max_client_conn: '500'
Set default_pool_size based on Postgres max_connections budget: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.default_pool_size: '20'
Set pool mode explicitly (transaction recommended): spec.pgbouncer.poolMode: transaction
Enable TLS for pooler-to-postgres traffic (CNPG does this automatically via client certificates)
Run at least 2 Pooler instances for HA: spec.instances: 2
Configure PodDisruptionBudget for the Pooler deployment to ensure at least 1 replica available during node drain
Add PodMonitor for Prometheus scraping pooler metrics on port 9187
Set server_connect_timeout and client_login_timeout to fail fast on unhealthy backends: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.server_connect_timeout: '10'

symptoms: how pgbouncer pooler crd works cloudnativepg
pooler crd vs standalone pgbouncer deployment
root_cause: The CloudNativePG Pooler CRD creates a Deployment of PgBouncer pods fully managed by the CNPG operator. The operator automatically injects TLS client certificates, rotates them with the cluster, and updates the PgBouncer config when cluster topology changes (e.g. failover).
fix: Pooler pods connect to the Cluster's r/w service using mTLS certificates rotated by the CNPG operator — no manual cert management needed
The Pooler CRD spec.cluster.name must match an existing CNPG Cluster resource in the same namespace
spec.type: rw routes to primary; spec.type: ro routes to replicas — deploy separate Poolers for read/write splitting
CNPG operator creates a Service named <pooler-name> on port 5432 for application pods and port 9187 for metrics
On Cluster failover, the operator updates PgBouncer's target host automatically — no manual intervention needed
Inspect generated config: kubectl get secret <pooler-name>-config -n <namespace> -o jsonpath='{.data.pgbouncer\.ini}' | base64 -d

root_cause: PgBouncer uses a userlist.txt file for client authentication. In CNPG Pooler, this file is managed via Secrets, but mismatches between the pooler's auth_user and the Postgres role credentials cause authentication failures.
fix: CNPG Pooler uses auth_query mode by default — ensure the pgbouncer role exists in Postgres with pg_monitor membership
Create the pgbouncer auth user: kubectl exec -it <cluster-primary-pod> -- psql -c "CREATE ROLE pgbouncer WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'strongpass'; GRANT pg_monitor TO pgbouncer;"
Set auth_query in pooler: spec.pgbouncer.authQuerySecret referencing a Secret with key auth_query containing SELECT usename, passwd FROM pg_shadow WHERE usename=$1
Verify Secret exists: kubectl get secret <auth-query-secret> -n <namespace>
Check pooler logs for specific auth errors: kubectl logs -l cnpg.io/poolerName=<pooler-name> -n <namespace> | grep -i 'auth\|FATAL'
Test connectivity: kubectl exec -it <app-pod> -- psql -h app-pooler -U appuser -d appdb -c 'SELECT 1'

symptoms: how to configure node pg pool for pgbouncer
node postgres pool settings kubernetes pgbouncer
pg.Pool reconnect on connection error kubernetes
root_cause: A pg.Pool configured without proper timeouts and error handling will silently accumulate idle or broken connections in Kubernetes environments where network policies or pgbouncer restarts can sever connections without TCP RST.
fix: Recommended pg.Pool config for PgBouncer transaction mode: { host: process.env.DB_HOST, port: 5432, database: process.env.DB_NAME, user: process.env.DB_USER, password: process.env.DB_PASS, max: 3, idleTimeoutMillis: 10000, connectionTimeoutMillis: 3000, allowExitOnIdle: true }
Attach error handler to prevent unhandled promise rejection: pool.on('error', (err) => { console.error('pg pool error', err); })
Use pool.connect() with explicit client.release() in finally blocks to prevent connection leaks
For health checks: expose a /healthz endpoint that runs pool.query('SELECT 1') with a 2-second timeout
Avoid using pg.Client directly in application code; always use pg.Pool to benefit from automatic reconnection
Set statement_timeout at session level after acquiring connection: await client.query("SET statement_timeout = '30s'")

symptoms: pgbouncer high latency slow queries node.js
database slow after pod scale-up keda
root_cause: When KEDA scales pods rapidly, new pods attempt to acquire PgBouncer connections simultaneously. If default_pool_size is exhausted, clients queue in PgBouncer. Combined with cold query plan caches in Postgres, this causes latency spikes that can cascade into timeouts.
fix: Increase default_pool_size temporarily during scale events: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.reserve_pool_size: '10' and spec.pgbouncer.parameters.reserve_pool_timeout: '3'
Configure Postgres connection pre-warming by running SELECT 1 on pod startup before accepting traffic
Add readinessProbe to Node.js pods that verifies DB connectivity before routing traffic: httpGet /healthz with initialDelaySeconds: 5
Set KEDA ScaledObject cooldownPeriod to prevent thrashing: spec.cooldownPeriod: 60
Use pg_stat_bgwriter to detect checkpoint pressure during scale events: SELECT buffers_checkpoint, buffers_clean FROM pg_stat_bgwriter
Monitor cl_waiting in PgBouncer pools and page on nonzero sustained waiting clients

symptoms: pgbouncer pooler not updating after postgres failover
pgbouncer stuck on old primary after cnpg failover
root_cause: In standalone PgBouncer deployments (not using CNPG Pooler CRD), the host parameter points to a static IP or hostname. After a CNPG failover, the primary changes but PgBouncer still routes to the old primary, which is now a replica and will reject writes.
fix: Always use the CNPG Pooler CRD instead of standalone PgBouncer — the operator automatically updates targets on failover
If using standalone PgBouncer, point host to the CNPG r/w Service (e.g. <cluster-name>-rw.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local) which is updated by the operator
Force PgBouncer config reload after failover: psql -p 6432 pgbouncer -c 'RELOAD;'
Monitor failover events: kubectl get events -n <namespace> --field-selector reason=Failover
Verify current primary: kubectl get cluster <name> -n <namespace> -o jsonpath='{.status.currentPrimary}'
Set server_check_query in PgBouncer to detect stale connections: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.server_check_query: 'SELECT 1'

root_cause: PgBouncer logs key events to stdout but uses non-obvious terminology; understanding log patterns is essential for rapid diagnosis of pooling issues in Kubernetes.
fix: Log 'C-0x...: app/user@addr closing because: client_idle_timeout' — client disconnected cleanly after idle timeout, expected behavior
Log 'S-0x...: app/user@host closing because: server_lifetime' — server connection recycled after server_lifetime seconds, expected
Log 'WARNING: query_wait_timeout expired' — client waited longer than query_wait_timeout for a free server connection; increase pool size or reduce concurrency
Log 'FATAL: max_client_conn reached' — see entry on max_client_conn exhaustion; increase limit or reduce client connections
Log 'ERROR: prepared statement ... does not exist' — application using named prepared statements in transaction mode; see transaction mode entry
Stream pooler logs: kubectl logs -l cnpg.io/poolerName=<name> -n <namespace> -f --prefix
Log 'NOTICE: new connection to server' frequency indicates connection churn; tune server_idle_timeout if too high

symptoms: how to monitor pgbouncer metrics kubernetes
root_cause: Without Prometheus metrics from PgBouncer, connection exhaustion and pool saturation are invisible until they cause application errors. CNPG Pooler exposes metrics natively but requires PodMonitor configuration.
fix: Apply PodMonitor for CNPG Pooler: apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1, kind: PodMonitor, spec.selector.matchLabels: { cnpg.io/poolerName: app-pooler }, spec.podMetricsEndpoints: [{ port: metrics, path: /metrics }]
Key metrics: cnpg_pgbouncer_client_connections_total{state='active'}, cnpg_pgbouncer_client_connections_total{state='waiting'}, cnpg_pgbouncer_server_connections_total{state='active'}
Alert on waiting clients: expr: cnpg_pgbouncer_client_connections_total{state='waiting'} > 0, for: 1m, severity: warning
Import Grafana dashboard ID 14022 for PgBouncer or build custom panels from cnpg_pgbouncer_* metrics
Expose pg_stat_activity from Postgres via postgres_exporter for correlated visibility: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/cloudnative-pg/cloudnative-pg/tree/main/docs/src/samples/monitoring
Set up Loki alert on 'query_wait_timeout' log pattern as a secondary signal

symptoms: using statement pool mode pgbouncer production
root_cause: PgBouncer statement mode releases the server connection after every single SQL statement, not after each transaction. This breaks any application that uses BEGIN/COMMIT blocks, making it unsuitable for virtually all web applications.
fix: Never use spec.pgbouncer.poolMode: statement for applications that issue multi-statement transactions
Statement mode is only appropriate for single-statement tools (pgbench, simple reporting queries) or autocommit-only workloads
Use transaction mode for all Node.js API and worker pods as the default
If you see intermittent rollbacks or 'there is no current transaction' errors, check if poolMode was accidentally set to statement
Verify current mode: kubectl exec -it <pooler-pod> -- psql -p 6432 pgbouncer -c 'SHOW CONFIG;' | grep pool_mode
Change mode with zero-downtime by updating Pooler CRD spec and letting CNPG operator roll out: kubectl patch pooler app-pooler --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"pgbouncer":{"poolMode":"transaction"}}}'

symptoms: how to route reads to replicas pgbouncer kubernetes
root_cause: Deploying separate CNPG Poolers for read/write and read-only traffic allows Node.js pods to distribute read-heavy workloads across replicas while ensuring all writes go to the primary, improving cluster utilization.
fix: Create rw Pooler: spec.type: rw — this routes through the <cluster>-rw Service to the primary
Create ro Pooler: spec.type: ro — this routes through the <cluster>-ro Service load-balanced across all replicas
Expose two environment variables in Node.js pods: DB_WRITE_URL pointing to rw-pooler:5432 and DB_READ_URL pointing to ro-pooler:5432
In Node.js: const writePool = new Pool({ host: process.env.DB_WRITE_URL }); const readPool = new Pool({ host: process.env.DB_READ_URL });
Use writePool for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE and readPool for SELECT-only queries
Set spec.pgbouncer.poolMode: transaction on both Poolers and adjust default_pool_size independently for read vs write workloads

symptoms: pgbouncer idle connections not released kubernetes
postgres backend connections not closing pgbouncer
root_cause: Without server_idle_timeout configured, PgBouncer holds backend (server) connections open indefinitely even when no clients are using them. On Kubernetes with elastic scaling this wastes scarce Postgres connection slots.
fix: Set server_idle_timeout to release idle backend connections: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.server_idle_timeout: '30'
Set server_lifetime to recycle connections periodically (prevents stale connections from reconnecting after Postgres restart): spec.pgbouncer.parameters.server_lifetime: '3600'
Set server_reset_query for transaction mode: spec.pgbouncer.parameters.server_reset_query: 'DISCARD ALL' (optional but ensures clean state when connection is reused)
Monitor sv_idle column in SHOW POOLS — if consistently nonzero, server_idle_timeout is too high
After parameter change: kubectl exec -it <pooler-pod> -- psql -p 6432 pgbouncer -c 'RELOAD;'
Correlate with pg_stat_activity: SELECT count(*), state FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY state — idle connections held by PgBouncer appear as state='idle'

symptoms: how to name pgbouncer pooler resources kubernetes
best practice resource names cloudnativepg pooler
root_cause: Consistent naming conventions for CNPG Pooler resources, Services, and Secrets reduce operational confusion especially in multi-tenant or multi-environment clusters where multiple Clusters and Poolers coexist.
fix: Name Pooler resources with pattern: <cluster-name>-pooler-<type> e.g. appdb-pooler-rw and appdb-pooler-ro
Use namespace-per-environment isolation: namespace appdb-prod, appdb-staging so Services don't collide
Label Poolers consistently: labels: { app.kubernetes.io/part-of: appdb, app.kubernetes.io/component: pgbouncer }
Reference Pooler Service in application ConfigMaps by the Service DNS: appdb-pooler-rw.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
Store connection strings in Secrets (managed by External Secrets Operator pulling from Vault) referencing the Pooler Service hostname, not pod IPs
Document pool mode in Pooler name suffix when mixing modes: appdb-pooler-rw-txn (transaction) vs appdb-pooler-rw-sess (session) for clarity

symptoms: how to deploy Rook-Ceph CephCluster on bare metal
how to bootstrap Rook-Ceph with CephCluster CRD
getting started rook ceph cluster kubernetes
root_cause: Deploying Rook-Ceph requires installing the Rook operator, creating a CephCluster CRD that references raw block devices on nodes, and allowing the operator to automatically provision OSDs.
fix: helm repo add rook-release https://charts.rook.io/release && helm repo update
helm install --create-namespace --namespace rook-ceph rook-ceph rook-release/rook-ceph --version 1.14.x
Create CephCluster CR with spec.storage.useAllDevices: false and spec.storage.nodes listing each node and its raw devices (e.g., /dev/sdb)
kubectl apply -f cluster.yaml -n rook-ceph
kubectl -n rook-ceph get cephcluster && kubectl -n rook-ceph get pods -l app=rook-ceph-osd
Wait for HEALTH_OK: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec -it deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph status

symptoms: OSD pods stuck in Init state
root_cause: OSD provisioning fails when the target block device is not raw/unformatted, has an existing filesystem signature, or LVM metadata, causing the rook-ceph-osd-prepare Job to error out.
fix: kubectl -n rook-ceph logs job/rook-ceph-osd-prepare-<node> -c provision to inspect the error
On the target node: wipefs -a /dev/sdX to remove filesystem signatures
dd if=/dev/zero of=/dev/sdX bs=1M count=10 to clear LVM metadata from the first 10 MB
Restart the OSD prepare job by deleting the ConfigMap: kubectl -n rook-ceph delete cm rook-ceph-osd-<node>-status
Ensure spec.storage.nodes[].devices[].name matches the exact device path visible inside the operator pod
Verify no other process (multipathd, udev) is locking the device: lsblk -f /dev/sdX

symptoms: how to create RBD block storage class rook-ceph
kubernetes RWO PVC rook ceph block
root_cause: RBD (RADOS Block Device) provides ReadWriteOnce block storage for stateful workloads. It requires a CephBlockPool CRD and a matching StorageClass that references the pool and the CSI provisioner.
fix:
Create CephBlockPool: kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: ceph.rook.io/v1
kind: CephBlockPool
metadata:
  name: replicapool
  namespace: rook-ceph
spec:
  replicated:
    size: 3
EOF
Create StorageClass referencing the pool: provisioner: rook-ceph.rbd.csi.ceph.com, parameters.pool: replicapool, parameters.clusterID: <rook-ceph-namespace>
Set reclaimPolicy: Retain for production PVCs to prevent accidental data loss
Annotate as default: kubectl patch storageclass rook-ceph-block -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class":"true"}}}'
Create PVC and verify binding: kubectl get pvc -n <namespace>
Check CSI driver is running: kubectl -n rook-ceph get pods -l app=csi-rbdplugin

symptoms: how to set up CephFS shared filesystem rook-ceph
root_cause: CephFS provides ReadWriteMany (RWX) shared filesystem access via MDS daemons. It requires a CephFilesystem CRD with active and standby MDS counts, and a CSI-backed StorageClass.
fix: kubectl apply -f - with CephFilesystem CRD: spec.metadataPool.replicated.size: 3, spec.dataPools[0].replicated.size: 3, spec.metadataServer.activeCount: 1, spec.metadataServer.activeStandby: true
Wait for MDS pods: kubectl -n rook-ceph get pods -l app=rook-ceph-mds
Create StorageClass with provisioner: rook-ceph.cephfs.csi.ceph.com, parameters.fsName: <filesystem-name>, parameters.pool: <filesystem-name>-data0
Create PVC with accessModes: [ReadWriteMany] and storageClassName matching the CephFS StorageClass
Verify mount in pod: exec into pod and run df -h to confirm CephFS is mounted
Check MDS status: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph fs status

symptoms: how to configure Ceph object storage RGW rook-ceph
root_cause: Ceph Object Gateway (RGW) exposes an S3/Swift-compatible API for object storage. It requires a CephObjectStore CRD with a gateway section, and a matching StorageClass and OBC (ObjectBucketClaim).
fix: kubectl apply -f CephObjectStore with spec.metadataPool.replicated.size: 3, spec.dataPool.replicated.size: 3, spec.gateway.port: 80, spec.gateway.instances: 1
Wait for RGW pod: kubectl -n rook-ceph get pods -l app=rook-ceph-rgw
Create ObjectBucketClaim: storageClassName: rook-ceph-bucket, generateBucketName: my-bucket
Retrieve S3 credentials from Secret: kubectl get secret <obc-name> -n <namespace> -o jsonpath='{.data.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID}' | base64 -d
Get bucket endpoint from ConfigMap: kubectl get cm <obc-name> -n <namespace> -o yaml | grep BUCKET_HOST
Test S3 access: aws s3 ls s3://<bucket-name> --endpoint-url http://<rgw-service-ip>

symptoms: how to diagnose ceph health warnings
root_cause: HEALTH_WARN indicates a non-critical but important condition such as too few PGs, clock skew, slow OSD, or near-full OSDs. Ignoring warnings can lead to HEALTH_ERR and data unavailability.
fix: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec -it deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph health detail to see individual warning codes
Check OSD status: ceph osd stat and ceph osd tree to identify down/out OSDs
Check PG status: ceph pg stat — look for 'degraded', 'undersized', 'peering', 'stale' states
Check clock skew: ceph time-sync-status; ensure NTP is synchronized across all nodes (chronyc tracking)
Check near-full: ceph df — OSDs above nearfull_ratio (default 0.85) trigger HEALTH_WARN
Check slow ops: ceph osd perf and ceph health detail | grep 'slow ops'

symptoms: ceph mon quorum lost health err
root_cause: HEALTH_ERR is a critical state caused by conditions like insufficient monitor quorum, too many OSDs down, PGs stuck inactive/unclean, or pool near-full ratios breached. Writes may be blocked.
fix: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec -it deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph health detail for exact error codes
For MON_CLOCK_SKEW: fix NTP on affected nodes; restart chronyd/systemd-timesyncd
For OSD_DOWN: kubectl -n rook-ceph get pods | grep osd; describe failing OSD pod for container errors
For PG_AVAILABILITY: ceph pg dump_stuck inactive to list stuck PGs and their primary OSDs
For FULL (pool is full): increase OSD count or delete data; writes are blocked at full_ratio (default 0.95)
Force-unpause if cluster was administratively paused: ceph osd unset pause

root_cause: An OSD pod may crash due to disk failure, node eviction, or filesystem corruption. Ceph will mark the OSD out after mon_osd_down_out_interval (default 600s), then begin backfilling to maintain replica count.
fix: Identify the failed OSD: kubectl -n rook-ceph get pods | grep osd | grep -v Running
Get OSD ID from pod name: kubectl -n rook-ceph describe pod rook-ceph-osd-<id>-xxx | grep OSD_ID
Check logs: kubectl -n rook-ceph logs pod/rook-ceph-osd-<id>-xxx --previous
If disk is dead, remove OSD: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph osd out osd.<id> && ceph osd purge osd.<id> --yes-i-really-mean-it
Replace disk and re-trigger OSD discovery by deleting the OSD deployment: kubectl -n rook-ceph delete deploy rook-ceph-osd-<id>
Monitor recovery: ceph -w | grep recovering; check ceph pg stat for undersized PGs returning to active+clean

root_cause: Ceph requires a majority of monitors (quorum) to be reachable for the cluster to function. With 3 monitors, losing 2 breaks quorum. Network partitions, node failures, or clock skew are common causes.
fix: Check quorum status: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph quorum_status -f json-pretty
List monitor pods: kubectl -n rook-ceph get pods -l app=rook-ceph-mon
Check monitor logs for 'outside tolerated range' (clock skew) or 'failed to connect': kubectl -n rook-ceph logs pod/rook-ceph-mon-a-xxx
Verify node-to-node connectivity between mon pods on port 6789/3300
If a monitor is permanently lost, remove it: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph mon remove <mon-id>
Rook will automatically create a replacement monitor; verify: kubectl -n rook-ceph get cephcluster -o jsonpath='{.items[0].status.ceph.details}'

symptoms: ceph PG not balanced across OSDs
how to balance placement groups ceph
root_cause: Placement Group (PG) imbalance occurs when OSDs of different sizes hold disproportionate data, or when OSDs are added without rebalancing. The Ceph balancer module redistributes PGs to optimize data distribution.
fix: Check balance score: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph balancer status
Enable the balancer module if not active: ceph mgr module enable balancer
Set balancer to upmap mode for best results: ceph balancer mode upmap
Enable automatic balancing: ceph balancer on
Check OSD utilization: ceph osd df tree — OSDs should be within 10-15% of each other
After adding new OSDs, force a balance eval: ceph balancer eval and ceph balancer optimize

root_cause: Ceph enforces nearfull_ratio (default 0.85) and full_ratio (default 0.95). When any OSD crosses nearfull_ratio, HEALTH_WARN is raised. Crossing full_ratio blocks all writes to that OSD's PGs.
fix: Monitor capacity: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph df and ceph osd df
Set up Prometheus alert: ceph_osd_utilization > 80 to alert before reaching nearfull
Plan capacity additions when cluster reaches 70% utilization to account for recovery space
Expand cluster by adding new OSD devices via CephCluster spec.storage.nodes or spec.storage.storageClassDeviceSets
Temporarily adjust ratios for emergency space if needed: ceph osd set-nearfull-ratio 0.88 (not recommended long-term)
Enable PG autoscaler to right-size pools: ceph mgr module enable pg_autoscaler && ceph osd pool set <pool> pg_autoscale_mode on

symptoms: ceph high latency NVMe OSD tuning
how to tune ceph for NVMe drives
root_cause: Default Ceph/Bluestore configuration targets HDDs. NVMe drives can handle far higher concurrency and lower latency, requiring tuning of OSD count per device, BlueStore cache, and async messenger threads.
fix: Use one OSD per NVMe device (not multiple OSDs per device) — NVMe is fast enough as a single OSD
Set BlueStore cache size in CephCluster configOverride: osd_memory_target: 4294967296 (4GiB per OSD for NVMe workloads)
Enable BlueStore RocksDB on separate partition for WAL/DB if NVMe has capacity: bluestore_block_db_size and bluestore_block_wal_size
Increase async messenger threads: ms_async_op_threads: 4 in ceph config (ceph config set osd ms_async_op_threads 4)
Set osd_op_num_shards to match NVMe queue depth: ceph config set osd osd_op_num_shards 8
Verify performance: ceph tell osd.* bench and check ceph osd perf output for latency <1ms on NVMe

symptoms: using Rook-Ceph with local SSDs on cloud VMs
root_cause: Running Ceph OSDs on top of network-attached block devices (cloud PVs, iSCSI, NFS) doubles the replication overhead and severely degrades performance and reliability. Ceph is designed for direct-attached local disks.
fix: Use only direct-attached local block devices (SATA SSD, NVMe) as OSD backing storage
On cloud VMs, consider Longhorn instead of Rook-Ceph for simpler operational model on non-bare-metal
If Rook on cloud is required, use local NVMe instance storage (e.g., AWS i3.x, GCP n2d with local SSDs) not EBS/GCE PD
Never specify a StorageClass in CephCluster spec.storage.storageClassDeviceSets pointing to a cloud-backed StorageClass
Validate disk type: lsblk -d -o name,rota (rota=0 means SSD/NVMe, rota=1 means HDD)
If existing cluster uses network disks, migrate by adding local OSDs and draining network-backed OSDs with ceph osd out osd.<id>

root_cause: Rook-Ceph deployments fail silently when prerequisites are unmet. Nodes need specific kernel modules, LVM tools, raw devices, and sufficient resource reservations.
fix: Verify kernel modules: lsmod | grep -E 'rbd|ceph' — load with modprobe rbd ceph if missing
Install lvm2 on all storage nodes: apt-get install -y lvm2 (or equivalent)
Ensure raw devices exist and are unformatted: lsblk -f — target devices must show no FSTYPE
Reserve resources: each OSD needs 2-4 vCPU and 4-8 GiB RAM; reserve via LimitRange or PriorityClass
Label storage nodes: kubectl label node <node> role=storage-node for node affinity in CephCluster
Confirm Rook-Ceph namespace has no pre-existing resources: kubectl -n rook-ceph get all

symptoms: which ceph storage type to use for kubernetes workloads
when to use rbd vs cephfs vs rgw
root_cause: Different Kubernetes workload types require different Ceph storage backends. Choosing the wrong one leads to performance issues or unsupported access modes.
fix: Use RBD (block) for stateful apps needing single-node RWO access: databases (PostgreSQL/CloudNativePG), Redis, Elasticsearch — optimal throughput
Use CephFS (shared filesystem) for workloads needing RWX (multiple pods reading/writing same volume): shared config, media assets, NATS streaming data
Use RGW (object/S3) for large unstructured data, log archives, AI/ML datasets, backups — integrate with AWS SDK via endpoint-url override
Never use CephFS for database data directories — POSIX locking semantics differ from block I/O and cause performance issues
RBD snapshots integrate with VolumeSnapshot API for backup: kubectl apply -f VolumeSnapshotClass with driver: rook-ceph.rbd.csi.ceph.com
Monitor per-pool IOPS and latency: ceph osd pool stats <pool-name> and Prometheus metric ceph_pool_rd_bytes, ceph_pool_wr_bytes

root_cause: PVC provisioning can stall when the CSI provisioner lacks RBAC permissions, the CephCluster is not in HEALTH_OK state, or the clusterID in StorageClass does not match the rook-ceph namespace.
fix: Describe the PVC to see provisioner events: kubectl describe pvc <name> -n <namespace>
Check CSI provisioner logs: kubectl -n rook-ceph logs deploy/csi-rbdplugin-provisioner -c csi-provisioner
Verify clusterID in StorageClass matches the namespace of rook-ceph: kubectl -n rook-ceph get configmap rook-ceph-mon -o jsonpath='{.data.data}'
Confirm CephCluster is HEALTH_OK: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph status | grep health
Check RBAC: kubectl -n rook-ceph get clusterrolebinding | grep csi
If clusterID mismatch, patch StorageClass parameters.clusterID to match the correct namespace value and re-test PVC creation

symptoms: ceph slow write latency bluestore cache
how to optimize bluestore for ceph
root_cause: BlueStore is the default OSD backend. Inadequate cache allocation, missing WAL/DB placement on faster devices, or suboptimal compaction settings reduce throughput and increase latency.
fix: Set osd_memory_target per OSD via Rook CephCluster configOverride: [osd] osd_memory_target = 4294967296
Place BlueStore DB metadata on a faster NVMe partition for HDD OSDs: bluestore_block_db_size = 67108864000 (60GiB) per OSD
Monitor cache hit rate: ceph daemon osd.<id> perf dump | python3 -m json.tool | grep -A5 bluestore_cache
Enable bluestore_cache_autotune: ceph config set global bluestore_cache_autotune true (enabled by default in Ceph 14+)
Check RocksDB compaction backlog: ceph daemon osd.<id> perf dump | grep rocksdb_compact
Use ceph tell osd.all bench --count 1000 --size 4096 to baseline 4K random write IOPS

root_cause: Rook-Ceph issues span operator, CSI, OSD, monitor, and network layers. A structured diagnostic approach prevents chasing symptoms rather than root causes.
fix: Step 1 — Check overall cluster health: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph status
Step 2 — Check Rook operator logs for reconciliation errors: kubectl -n rook-ceph logs deploy/rook-ceph-operator --tail=100
Step 3 — Check CephCluster status conditions: kubectl -n rook-ceph get cephcluster -o jsonpath='{.items[0].status}'
Step 4 — Check all rook-ceph pod states: kubectl -n rook-ceph get pods | grep -v Running | grep -v Completed
Step 5 — Check CSI driver pods: kubectl -n rook-ceph get pods -l app=csi-rbdplugin && kubectl -n rook-ceph get pods -l app=csi-cephfsplugin
Step 6 — Deploy toolbox if not present: kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rook/rook/master/deploy/examples/toolbox.yaml -n rook-ceph

symptoms: how many placement groups per pool ceph
root_cause: Incorrect PG counts cause OSD overload (too many PGs) or data imbalance (too few). Ceph recommends the pg_autoscaler module to dynamically adjust PG counts based on pool data and OSD count.
fix: Enable pg_autoscaler globally: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph mgr module enable pg_autoscaler
Set default mode for new pools: ceph config set global osd_pool_default_pg_autoscale_mode on
For existing pools: ceph osd pool set <pool-name> pg_autoscale_mode on
Check autoscaler recommendations: ceph osd pool autoscale-status
If manually setting PGs, use formula: target_pgs = (OSDs * 100) / pool_replica_size, rounded to nearest power of 2
Monitor pg_num changes: ceph osd pool get <pool> pg_num and ceph -w | grep pg_num

symptoms: ceph slow ops warning in logs
rook-ceph OSD high latency log messages
root_cause: Slow op warnings in OSD logs indicate requests taking longer than osd_op_complaint_time (default 30s). Common causes are disk I/O saturation, network congestion, or memory pressure causing thrashing.
fix: Identify slow op log lines in OSD pods: kubectl -n rook-ceph logs pod/rook-ceph-osd-<id>-xxx | grep 'slow request'
Check OSD I/O stats: kubectl -n rook-ceph exec deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph osd perf | sort -k3 -rn | head -20 (sort by apply latency)
Check node disk I/O: iostat -xz 1 5 on the OSD node to identify saturated devices
Check network: iperf3 between OSD nodes to rule out network bandwidth issues
If memory-related: check node memory pressure with kubectl describe node <node> | grep -A5 Conditions
Reduce OSD client op queue depth if disk is saturated: ceph config set osd osd_max_op_in_flight 32

symptoms: how does Longhorn volume replication work
how many replicas does Longhorn create
root_cause: Longhorn implements distributed block storage by maintaining configurable replica copies across different nodes. Each replica is a separate engine process that synchronously mirrors writes, providing fault tolerance when nodes or disks fail.
fix: Set default replica count globally: kubectl -n longhorn-system edit settings.cattle.io default-replica-count
Override per-volume: annotate PVC with 'longhorn.io/number-of-replicas: "3"' before provisioning
Enforce replica anti-affinity to spread across nodes: set 'Replica Node Level Soft Anti-Affinity' to false for hard enforcement
Check replica placement: kubectl -n longhorn-system get replicas -l longhornvolume=<vol-name> -o wide
Verify replica status in UI: navigate to Longhorn UI -> Volumes -> <volume> -> Replicas tab
Use 'longhorn.io/replica-disk-soft-anti-affinity: false' to enforce one replica per disk

symptoms: Longhorn volume stuck in Attaching state
pod stuck ContainerCreating with Longhorn PVC
root_cause: Longhorn volume attach failures typically occur when the instance manager pod on the target node is not running, the node is cordoned/drained, or there is a stale engine process from a previous attach that was not cleaned up properly.
fix: Check instance manager pods: kubectl -n longhorn-system get pods -l app=instance-manager
Identify the node the volume is trying to attach to: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volume <vol-name> -o jsonpath='{.status.currentNodeID}'
Delete stale engine pod if present: kubectl -n longhorn-system delete pod <engine-pod-name>
Force detach via Longhorn API: kubectl -n longhorn-system patch volume <vol-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"nodeID":"","disableFrontend":false}}'
Check node disk state: kubectl -n longhorn-system get nodes.longhorn.io <node-name> -o jsonpath='{.status.diskStatus}'
Restart the Longhorn manager on the affected node: kubectl -n longhorn-system delete pod -l app=longhorn-manager --field-selector spec.nodeName=<node-name>

root_cause: A volume enters Degraded state when one or more replicas become unavailable or fail health checks. This happens after a node failure, disk error, or network partition. Longhorn will attempt automatic rebuild if a schedulable node with sufficient disk space is available.
fix: Check degraded volume details: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volume <vol-name> -o yaml | grep -A5 'robustness'
List failed replicas: kubectl -n longhorn-system get replicas -l longhornvolume=<vol-name> | grep ERR
Delete failed replica to trigger rebuild: kubectl -n longhorn-system delete replica <replica-name>
Verify sufficient disk space on nodes: kubectl -n longhorn-system get nodes.longhorn.io -o custom-columns='NAME:.metadata.name,STORAGE:.status.diskStatus'
Enable automatic replica rebuild: kubectl -n longhorn-system edit settings.cattle.io replica-replenishment-wait-interval
Monitor rebuild progress: watch kubectl -n longhorn-system get replicas -l longhornvolume=<vol-name>

symptoms: data loss Longhorn all replicas failed
root_cause: A Faulted volume has lost all healthy replicas simultaneously, which typically occurs due to simultaneous multi-node failure, storage backend corruption on all replica nodes, or a Kubernetes upgrade that drained all nodes at once without allowing replica migration.
fix: Identify faulted volume: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volumes | grep Faulted
Check last known good replicas: kubectl -n longhorn-system get replicas -l longhornvolume=<vol-name> -o yaml | grep -E 'currentState|failedAt'
Attempt salvage by activating a replica: In Longhorn UI -> Volumes -> <volume> -> 'Activate' button to try recovering from last replica
As last resort, patch volume to allow recovery from a specific replica: kubectl -n longhorn-system patch volume <vol-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"fromBackup":""}}'
If backup exists, restore from S3 backup: kubectl apply -f - with kind: Volume spec.fromBackup pointing to backup URL
Post-recovery increase replica count and set 'Replica Node Level Soft Anti-Affinity: false' to prevent recurrence

symptoms: backup Longhorn volume snapshot to S3
root_cause: Longhorn supports on-demand and scheduled snapshots via its CRDs. Snapshots capture point-in-time state of a volume and can be used for local recovery or as the basis for remote backups to S3-compatible or NFS targets.
fix: Create on-demand snapshot via kubectl: kubectl -n longhorn-system create -f - with kind: Snapshot, spec.volume: <vol-name>
Configure recurring snapshot job: kubectl apply -f recurringjob.yaml with kind: RecurringJob, spec.task: snapshot, spec.cron: '0 */6 * * *', spec.retain: 4
Label volume to attach recurring job: kubectl -n longhorn-system label volume <vol-name> recurring-job.longhorn.io/snapshot-daily=enabled
List snapshots for a volume: kubectl -n longhorn-system get snapshots -l longhornvolume=<vol-name>
Verify snapshot in UI: Longhorn UI -> Volumes -> <volume> -> Snapshots tab
Delete old snapshot: kubectl -n longhorn-system delete snapshot <snapshot-name>

symptoms: how to configure Longhorn S3 backup target
root_cause: Longhorn requires a backup target (S3-compatible or NFS) to store volume backups externally. Credentials for S3 must be provided via a Kubernetes Secret referenced in the Longhorn backup target settings.
fix: Create S3 credentials secret: kubectl -n longhorn-system create secret generic longhorn-backup-secret --from-literal=AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<key> --from-literal=AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<secret> --from-literal=AWS_ENDPOINTS=https://s3.amazonaws.com
Set backup target URL: kubectl -n longhorn-system edit settings.cattle.io backup-target and set value to 's3://my-bucket@us-east-1/'
Set backup target credential secret: kubectl -n longhorn-system edit settings.cattle.io backup-target-credential-secret and set value to 'longhorn-backup-secret'
Verify connection: In Longhorn UI -> Settings -> General -> Backup Target, click 'Check' button
For NFS target use: nfs://nfs-server.example.com:/export/longhorn-backup as backup-target value
Trigger manual backup: kubectl -n longhorn-system create -f - with kind: Backup, spec.snapshotName: <snapshot-name>

root_cause: Longhorn backup failures to S3 are commonly caused by incorrect IAM permissions (missing s3:PutObject, s3:GetObject, s3:ListBucket, s3:DeleteObject), wrong bucket region in the target URL, or egress network policy blocking Longhorn manager pods from reaching the S3 endpoint.
fix: Verify S3 permissions include: s3:GetObject, s3:PutObject, s3:DeleteObject, s3:ListBucket, s3:GetBucketLocation
Check backup target URL format: must be 's3://<bucket>@<region>/' — note trailing slash is required
Test connectivity from longhorn-manager pod: kubectl -n longhorn-system exec -it <longhorn-manager-pod> -- curl -v https://s3.<region>.amazonaws.com/<bucket>/
For Cilium egress policy, ensure longhorn-system namespace pods are allowed outbound to port 443 for S3 CIDR range
Check Longhorn manager logs for backup errors: kubectl -n longhorn-system logs -l app=longhorn-manager | grep -i backup
For virtual-hosted-style S3 buckets, set AWS_VIRTUAL_HOSTED_STYLE_LINK=true in the backup secret

symptoms: Longhorn no storage available for scheduling
root_cause: Longhorn marks disks as unschedulable when available storage falls below the configured 'Storage Minimum Free Disk Percentage' threshold (default 25%), when disk tags do not match volume requirements, or when the disk or node scheduling is manually disabled.
fix: Check disk scheduling status: kubectl -n longhorn-system get nodes.longhorn.io <node-name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.disks}'
Enable disk scheduling in UI: Longhorn UI -> Node -> Edit -> Disk -> enable scheduling toggle
Or patch via kubectl: kubectl -n longhorn-system patch nodes.longhorn.io <node-name> --type=json -p '[{"op":"replace","path":"/spec/disks/<disk-name>/allowScheduling","value":true}]'
Reduce minimum free disk percentage: kubectl -n longhorn-system edit settings.cattle.io storage-minimum-percentage-of-disk-capacity-for-scheduling
Check disk tags mismatch: volume spec.diskSelector must match disk tags on nodes.longhorn.io
Add disk to node: kubectl -n longhorn-system patch nodes.longhorn.io <node-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"disks":{"extra-disk":{"path":"/mnt/disk2","allowScheduling":true}}}}'

symptoms: how to configure Longhorn data locality
root_cause: Longhorn data locality controls whether the volume engine tries to maintain a local replica on the same node as the consuming pod, reducing read latency and network IO. Without data locality, all reads are served from potentially remote replicas over the network.
fix: Set data locality to 'best-effort': kubectl -n longhorn-system edit settings.cattle.io default-data-locality and set value to 'best-effort'
Override per-StorageClass: add 'dataLocality: best-effort' in StorageClass parameters field
For strict locality (only one local replica guaranteed): set dataLocality to 'strict-local' — note this requires replica count >= 1 and available local disk
Verify data locality setting on existing volume: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volume <vol-name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.dataLocality}'
Monitor local vs remote read ratio via Longhorn metrics: longhorn_volume_data_locality_* in Prometheus
Avoid strict-local for StatefulSets that might be rescheduled to different nodes frequently

root_cause: Longhorn implements ReadWriteMany (RWX) volumes by deploying a share-manager pod per RWX volume. The share-manager exports the Longhorn block volume via NFS using the kernel NFS server (nfsd), and worker pods mount this NFS export. This adds a layer of indirection compared to RWO volumes.
fix: Create RWX PVC with Longhorn StorageClass: set accessModes to ReadWriteMany in PVC spec
Verify share-manager pod is running: kubectl -n longhorn-system get pods -l app=share-manager
Check share-manager logs on failure: kubectl -n longhorn-system logs <share-manager-pod-name>
Ensure nfs-common / nfs-utils is installed on all worker nodes: apt-get install nfs-common or yum install nfs-utils
For Talos nodes, ensure NFS client extension is loaded: check talosctl get extensions | grep nfs
Monitor RWX volume mount: kubectl exec -it <pod> -- df -h | grep nfs

root_cause: RWX volume mount failures in Longhorn are frequently caused by missing NFS client packages on worker nodes, kernel NFS module not loaded, or the share-manager pod failing to start due to the underlying RWO volume being unable to attach.
fix: Check if NFS module is loaded: lsmod | grep nfs on each worker node
For Ubuntu/Debian: apt-get install -y nfs-common on all nodes (use DaemonSet or node config to persist)
For RKE2, add NFS package in node cloud-init or RKE2 node config
For Talos, apply nfs-client system extension via machine config patch
Check share-manager pod events: kubectl -n longhorn-system describe pod <share-manager-pod>
Verify the backing RWO volume is healthy first: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volume <vol-name> before troubleshooting NFS layer

symptoms: how to set up Longhorn DR volume
root_cause: Longhorn Disaster Recovery (DR) volumes are read-only standby volumes in a secondary cluster that continuously poll a backup target and apply incremental backups, enabling near-zero RPO cross-cluster failover by activating the DR volume when the primary cluster becomes unavailable.
fix: Ensure both clusters share the same S3/NFS backup target and credentials
Create backup in primary cluster: kubectl -n longhorn-system create -f backup.yaml with kind: Backup
In secondary cluster, create DR volume: kubectl -n longhorn-system apply -f - with kind: Volume, spec.standby: true, spec.fromBackup: 's3://bucket@region/?backup=<backup-name>&volume=<vol-name>'
Monitor DR volume sync: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volume <dr-vol-name> -o jsonpath='{.status.lastBackup}'
Set backup interval in primary: configure RecurringJob with spec.task: backup to keep DR volume up to date
Activate DR volume for failover: kubectl -n longhorn-system patch volume <dr-vol-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"standby":false}}' then create PVC referencing it

symptoms: how to troubleshoot Longhorn volume issues
root_cause: Longhorn volume problems require a systematic diagnostic approach covering the volume state, replica states, instance manager pods, node/disk conditions, and Longhorn manager logs to isolate whether the issue is at storage, network, scheduling, or Kubernetes layer.
fix: Step 1 - Check volume state: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volumes | grep -v Healthy
Step 2 - Inspect volume details: kubectl -n longhorn-system describe volume <vol-name>
Step 3 - Check replicas: kubectl -n longhorn-system get replicas -l longhornvolume=<vol-name> -o wide
Step 4 - Inspect instance manager: kubectl -n longhorn-system get pods -l app=instance-manager -o wide
Step 5 - Check longhorn-manager logs: kubectl -n longhorn-system logs -l app=longhorn-manager --tail=200 | grep -iE 'error|warn|fatal'

Step 6 - Check node/disk conditions: kubectl -n longhorn-system get nodes.longhorn.io -o custom-columns='NAME:.metadata.name,READY:.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status,SCHEDULABLE:.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Schedulable")].status'

symptoms: Longhorn replica on same node as volume
all replicas on one node Longhorn
Longhorn data loss on single node failure
root_cause: When Longhorn replica anti-affinity is not enforced (soft anti-affinity is the default), all replicas can land on the same node. A single node failure then faults the volume completely, eliminating the fault-tolerance benefit of multiple replicas.
fix: Disable soft anti-affinity: kubectl -n longhorn-system edit settings.cattle.io replica-soft-anti-affinity and set value to 'false'
Also disable zone soft anti-affinity for zone-aware clusters: kubectl -n longhorn-system edit settings.cattle.io replica-zone-soft-anti-affinity
Verify existing replicas are on different nodes: kubectl -n longhorn-system get replicas -o custom-columns='NAME:.metadata.name,NODE:.spec.nodeID,VOL:.metadata.labels.longhornvolume'
For volumes with replicas co-located, manually delete and allow rebuild on different node: kubectl -n longhorn-system delete replica <co-located-replica-name>
Set node anti-affinity at StorageClass level: add 'replicaSoftAntiAffinity: false' in StorageClass parameters
Test failure scenario in staging: cordon node with replicas and verify volume remains healthy with remaining replicas

symptoms: slow disk IO Longhorn replica rebuild
root_cause: Longhorn IO performance degrades during replica rebuilding because rebuild traffic competes with production IO on the same network and storage paths. Additionally, default settings allow concurrent rebuilds that can saturate node bandwidth.
fix: Limit concurrent replica rebuilds: kubectl -n longhorn-system edit settings.cattle.io concurrent-replica-rebuild-per-node-limit and set to 1 or 2
Check if rebuild is causing high IO: kubectl -n longhorn-system get replicas | grep rebuilding
