Monitor volume IO: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volumes <vol-name> -o jsonpath='{.status.actualSize}'
Enable Prometheus scraping for Longhorn metrics and monitor 'longhorn_volume_read_latency_*' and 'longhorn_volume_write_latency_*'
Schedule large rebuilds during low-traffic windows using Longhorn maintenance mode
Use NVMe or SSD-backed disks for Longhorn replicas and separate NIC for storage traffic if possible

symptoms: how to safely remove Longhorn node
root_cause: Draining or replacing a Kubernetes node that hosts Longhorn replicas without following the correct procedure can cause volumes to become degraded or faulted if too many replicas are evicted simultaneously and there is insufficient space to rebuild.
fix: 1. Before draining: verify all volumes have 3 healthy replicas: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volumes | grep -v '3/3'
2. Enable Longhorn node drain: kubectl -n longhorn-system patch nodes.longhorn.io <node-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"allowScheduling":false}}'
3. Cordon the Kubernetes node: kubectl cordon <node-name>
4. Wait for replicas to migrate off the node: watch kubectl -n longhorn-system get replicas | grep <node-name>
5. Drain with Pod Disruption Budget respect: kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --timeout=300s
6. Post-replacement, re-enable scheduling: kubectl -n longhorn-system patch nodes.longhorn.io <new-node-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"allowScheduling":true}}'

symptoms: volume still attached after pod deleted
cannot delete PVC Longhorn volume in use
root_cause: Longhorn volumes can remain attached after a pod is deleted if the kubelet on the node did not properly unmount the volume (e.g., due to node crash or network partition). The Longhorn CSI driver requires a successful unmount before detach, and a stale attachment blocks PVC deletion.
fix: Check if volume attachment exists: kubectl get volumeattachment | grep <pv-name>
Delete stale VolumeAttachment object: kubectl delete volumeattachment <attachment-name>
Check if volume is still attached in Longhorn: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volume <vol-name> -o jsonpath='{.status.state}'
Force detach via Longhorn UI: Volumes -> <volume> -> Detach (Force)
Or via kubectl patch: kubectl -n longhorn-system patch volume <vol-name> --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"nodeID":""}}'
After force detach, delete and recreate PV/PVC if deletion was stuck: kubectl delete pvc <pvc-name> --grace-period=0 --force

symptoms: how to read Longhorn error logs
root_cause: Longhorn manager and instance manager pods emit structured logs that reveal the root cause of volume failures. Key log patterns indicate replica timeouts, engine crashes, iSCSI errors, and disk full conditions that require different remediation actions.
fix: Stream longhorn-manager errors: kubectl -n longhorn-system logs -l app=longhorn-manager -f | grep -iE 'error|fatal|failed'
Check instance-manager for engine crash: kubectl -n longhorn-system logs -l app=instance-manager --tail=500 | grep -i 'crash\|signal\|killed'
Key pattern - replica timeout: 'error syncing replica' indicates network or disk IO issue on replica node
Key pattern - disk full: 'no space left on device' in instance-manager logs means replica disk is exhausted
Key pattern - iSCSI error: 'failed to connect target' means the engine cannot reach replica — check network policy
Key pattern - engine process crash: 'unexpected return code' in longhorn-manager with volume going Faulted — may need replica deletion and rebuild

symptoms: Longhorn NFS backup target connection refused
root_cause: Longhorn NFS backup target failures occur when the NFS server export permissions do not include the Longhorn manager pod CIDR, when NFS v4 is not supported by the server, or when the longhorn-manager pods cannot reach the NFS server due to network policy or firewall rules.
fix: Verify NFS export allows Longhorn pod CIDR: check /etc/exports on NFS server includes pod network CIDR with rw,no_root_squash
Test NFS connectivity from Longhorn manager pod: kubectl -n longhorn-system exec -it <longhorn-manager-pod> -- showmount -e <nfs-server-ip>
Ensure NFS ports 2049, 111 (portmapper) are open between Longhorn pods and NFS server
Set backup target: kubectl -n longhorn-system edit settings.cattle.io backup-target with value 'nfs://<server-ip>:/export/longhorn'
For Cilium network policy, add egress rule allowing longhorn-system namespace to NFS server IP on port 2049
Check backup target credential secret is empty for NFS (NFS uses Kerberos or export-based auth, not AWS keys)

root_cause: Longhorn upgrades require a specific sequence to avoid volume disruption: the engine image must be upgraded before manager pods, volumes should not be in a degraded state before upgrade, and the upgrade must proceed through supported version paths without skipping minor versions.
fix: Before upgrade, verify all volumes are Healthy: kubectl -n longhorn-system get volumes | grep -v Healthy
Check current Longhorn version: kubectl -n longhorn-system get settings.cattle.io longhorn-static-storage-class -o jsonpath='{.value}'
Upgrade via Helm: helm upgrade longhorn longhorn/longhorn -n longhorn-system --version <new-version> --reuse-values
Monitor upgrade progress: watch kubectl -n longhorn-system get pods
After upgrade, verify engine image upgrade: kubectl -n longhorn-system get engineimage

Trigger engine upgrade for existing volumes: In Longhorn UI -> Settings -> General -> 'Upgrade Volume Engine' or run kubectl -n longhorn-system patch volume <vol> --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"engineImage":"<new-engine-image>"}}'

root_cause: When StorageClass uses volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer, the PVC remains Pending until a Pod that references it is scheduled to a node. The scheduler then creates the PV on the node where the Pod lands, avoiding cross-zone topology mismatches.
fix: kubectl describe pvc <pvc-name> -n <namespace>  # look for 'waiting for first consumer to be created before binding'
kubectl get storageclass <sc-name> -o yaml | grep volumeBindingMode
Ensure a Pod referencing the PVC exists and is schedulable; the PVC will bind once the Pod is scheduled
If no Pod references the PVC, the status 'Pending' is expected and correct for WaitForFirstConsumer
kubectl get events -n <namespace> --field-selector involvedObject.name=<pvc-name>

root_cause: A PVC with the finalizer kubernetes.io/pvc-protection cannot be deleted while a Pod is still mounting it. The finalizer is removed only after all Pods using the PVC are fully terminated. If a Pod is also stuck Terminating, the PVC will never be released.
fix: kubectl get pods -n <namespace> -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.spec.volumes[]?.persistentVolumeClaim.claimName=="<pvc-name>") | .metadata.name'
kubectl delete pod <stuck-pod> -n <namespace> --grace-period=0 --force
kubectl get pvc <pvc-name> -n <namespace> -o yaml | grep finalizers
If Pod is already gone but finalizer remains: kubectl patch pvc <pvc-name> -n <namespace> -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":null}}' --type=merge
After finalizer removal the PVC will be garbage-collected immediately

root_cause: Volume expansion requires the StorageClass to have allowVolumeExpansion: true and the underlying CSI driver to support the ControllerExpandVolume and NodeExpandVolume RPCs. Editing the PVC spec.resources.requests.storage without these in place leaves the PVC in a Resizing condition that never resolves.
fix: kubectl get storageclass <sc-name> -o yaml | grep allowVolumeExpansion
If missing: kubectl patch storageclass <sc-name> -p '{"allowVolumeExpansion":true}'
kubectl patch pvc <pvc-name> -n <namespace> -p '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"storage":"20Gi"}}}}' --type=merge
kubectl describe pvc <pvc-name> -n <namespace>  # check Conditions: FileSystemResizePending
For Longhorn: verify node disk health and that the replica expansion did not fail: kubectl get lhvolume -n longhorn-system
For Rook-Ceph: check ceph osd status: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph status

symptoms: how to choose PV reclaim policy
difference between Delete and Retain reclaim policy
root_cause: The reclaimPolicy on a StorageClass determines what happens to the PV (and the backing storage) when the PVC is deleted. Delete removes the PV and the cloud/CSI-provisioned disk automatically; Retain keeps the PV in Released state allowing manual data recovery.
fix: Use reclaimPolicy: Retain for stateful production workloads (databases, NATS streams, CloudNativePG clusters)
Use reclaimPolicy: Delete only for ephemeral or easily reproducible data (caches, build artifacts)
After a PVC is deleted with Retain, the PV enters Released state — rebind it: kubectl patch pv <pv-name> -p '{"spec":{"claimRef":null}}'
Set reclaimPolicy in the StorageClass manifest: spec.reclaimPolicy: Retain
Audit existing PVs: kubectl get pv -o custom-columns='NAME:.metadata.name,RECLAIM:.spec.persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy,STATUS:.status.phase'

symptoms: how to create StatefulSet with volumeClaimTemplates
each pod needs its own PVC
root_cause: StatefulSets use volumeClaimTemplates to automatically provision a dedicated PVC per Pod replica. The PVCs are named <template-name>-<pod-name> and are not deleted when the StatefulSet is scaled down or deleted, preserving data across rescheduling.
fix: Define volumeClaimTemplates in the StatefulSet spec alongside the Pod template
Example snippet: volumeClaimTemplates: [{metadata: {name: data}, spec: {accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce], storageClassName: longhorn, resources: {requests: {storage: 10Gi}}}}]
Mount the volume in the container: volumeMounts: [{name: data, mountPath: /var/lib/data}]
Scale down without losing data: kubectl scale statefulset <name> --replicas=0 -n <namespace>
PVCs persist after scale-down; list them: kubectl get pvc -l app=<statefulset-name> -n <namespace>
To fully clean up, delete PVCs manually after deleting the StatefulSet: kubectl delete pvc -l app=<name> -n <namespace>

root_cause: CSI provisioning failures typically stem from the CSI controller plugin Pod being unavailable, the StorageClass provisioner field not matching the CSI driver name, or missing ClusterRole/ClusterRoleBinding permissions for the CSI provisioner ServiceAccount.
fix: kubectl get pods -n longhorn-system | grep -E 'csi|driver'  # or rook-ceph-csi-*
kubectl describe pvc <pvc-name> -n <namespace> | grep -A20 Events
Verify CSI driver name matches StorageClass provisioner: kubectl get csidrivers && kubectl get storageclass <sc> -o yaml | grep provisioner
Check CSI controller logs: kubectl logs -n longhorn-system deploy/longhorn-csi-plugin -c csi-provisioner --tail=100
Check RBAC: kubectl get clusterrolebindings | grep csi
For Longhorn: kubectl get lhnode -n longhorn-system  # verify all nodes are schedulable

symptoms: RWX on Longhorn block volume fails
root_cause: Block-backed CSI drivers (Longhorn block volumes, Rook-Ceph RBD) only support ReadWriteOnce (RWO) or ReadOnlyMany (ROX). Requesting ReadWriteMany (RWX) on a block PVC will either fail at provisioning or produce data corruption if the driver does not enforce single-writer semantics.
fix: For RWX workloads use a file-system backed driver: Longhorn NFS share, Rook-Ceph CephFS, or an NFS provisioner
Longhorn RWX: set accessModes: [ReadWriteMany] with storageClassName that has nfsOptions enabled in Longhorn settings
Rook-Ceph CephFS StorageClass: provisioner: rook-ceph.cephfs.csi.ceph.com supports RWX natively
Verify actual access mode: kubectl get pv <pv-name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.accessModes}'
For StatefulSets, RWO is usually correct since each Pod gets its own PVC via volumeClaimTemplates

symptoms: how to safely delete a StorageClass
root_cause: Deleting a StorageClass does not delete existing PVs or PVCs but prevents new provisioning. However, PVs created with reclaimPolicy: Delete will still be deleted when their PVC is removed, so operator action is needed to protect existing data before StorageClass removal.
fix: List all PVs using the StorageClass: kubectl get pv -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.spec.storageClassName=="<sc-name>") | {name:.metadata.name,reclaim:.spec.persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy,status:.status.phase}'
Patch reclaimPolicy to Retain on all affected PVs before deleting SC: kubectl patch pv <pv-name> -p '{"spec":{"persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy":"Retain"}}'
Ensure no active workloads are mounting volumes from this StorageClass
Delete the StorageClass: kubectl delete storageclass <sc-name>
Verify new PVCs referencing this SC will fail provisioning as expected
Update Helm values and GitOps manifests to use the new StorageClass before re-deploying workloads

symptoms: slow PVC provisioning with immediate binding
pod scheduling delayed waiting for volume
root_cause: Immediate volume binding mode (volumeBindingMode: Immediate) provisions the PV before Pod scheduling, potentially in a different availability zone than where the Pod lands. This causes the Pod to be unschedulable due to topology constraints and the existing PV cannot be used across zones.
fix: Switch StorageClass to volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer to align volume provisioning with Pod placement
kubectl get storageclass <sc-name> -o yaml > sc-backup.yaml && edit to add volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
StorageClass is immutable after creation: kubectl delete storageclass <sc-name> && kubectl apply -f sc-updated.yaml
Existing PVCs are unaffected; only new PVCs will use the new binding mode
For on-prem single-zone clusters, Immediate is acceptable but WaitForFirstConsumer is still recommended for rack-aware scheduling
Validate topology keys on CSI driver: kubectl get csinode <node-name> -o yaml | grep topology

symptoms: how to manually bind PV to PVC
root_cause: Static provisioning is used when a PV is pre-created (e.g., existing NFS export, Ceph RBD image) and needs to be bound to a specific PVC. This requires matching storageClassName, accessModes, capacity, and optionally a claimRef on the PV to prevent other PVCs from claiming it.
fix: Create PV with matching spec and claimRef: kubectl apply -f pv-static.yaml
PV YAML must include: claimRef.name, claimRef.namespace, claimRef.apiVersion: v1, claimRef.kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
Create PVC with same storageClassName, accessModes, and storage <= PV capacity
To use no StorageClass: set storageClassName: '' on both PV and PVC (empty string, not omitted)
Verify binding: kubectl get pv <pv-name> -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'  # should be Bound
Check claimRef is set: kubectl get pv <pv-name> -o json | jq .spec.claimRef

symptoms: PVC not binding to Released PV
root_cause: After a PVC is deleted, a PV with reclaimPolicy: Retain transitions to Released state. The claimRef on the PV still holds the old PVC UID, preventing automatic rebinding to a new PVC even if the name matches.
fix: kubectl get pv <pv-name> -o yaml | grep -A10 claimRef  # confirm old claimRef UID
Remove the claimRef to make the PV Available again: kubectl patch pv <pv-name> -p '{"spec":{"claimRef":null}}'
Alternatively, patch claimRef to reference the new PVC explicitly: kubectl patch pv <pv-name> -p '{"spec":{"claimRef":{"name":"<new-pvc>","namespace":"<ns>"}}}'
Create or re-create the PVC with matching storageClassName and accessModes
Monitor binding: kubectl get pvc <pvc-name> -n <namespace> -w

symptoms: how to design storage for CloudNativePG on Kubernetes
root_cause: CloudNativePG (CNPG) manages PostgreSQL clusters as StatefulSets where each instance requires a dedicated RWO PVC for the PGDATA directory and optionally a separate PVC for WAL files. Proper StorageClass selection and reclaim policy are critical for data durability and performance.
fix: Use a separate StorageClass with reclaimPolicy: Retain for CNPG clusters to prevent accidental data loss
CNPG Cluster manifest: storage.storageClass: <sc-name> and walStorage.storageClass: <wal-sc-name>
For Longhorn: use a dedicated StorageClass with numberOfReplicas: 3 for HA; avoid default class shared with stateless workloads
Disable volume expansion on CNPG storage SC to force controlled resize procedures: allowVolumeExpansion: false in dev, true in prod with monitoring
Verify CNPG PVC names: kubectl get pvc -n <namespace> -l cnpg.io/cluster=<cluster-name>
Monitor Longhorn volume health for CNPG: kubectl get lhvolume -n longhorn-system | grep <namespace>

symptoms: how to debug PVC not expanding
root_cause: After the CSI controller expands the block device (ControllerExpandVolume), the filesystem inside the volume must also be expanded by the kubelet on the node where the Pod is running (NodeExpandVolume). If the Pod is not running, or the node kubelet has an issue, the PVC stays in FileSystemResizePending condition.
fix: kubectl describe pvc <pvc-name> -n <namespace>  # look for Conditions: FileSystemResizePending
The node expansion happens only while a Pod is running with the PVC mounted — ensure a Pod is scheduled
Check kubelet logs on the node: journalctl -u kubelet -n 100 --no-pager | grep -i resize
Verify the CSI node plugin is running: kubectl get pods -n longhorn-system -l app=longhorn-csi-plugin
For Longhorn: kubectl get lhvolume -n longhorn-system <volume-name> -o yaml | grep -A5 status
After Pod restarts with expanded disk, verify: kubectl exec -it <pod> -- df -h /mount/path

symptoms: how to name StorageClasses in Kubernetes
root_cause: A well-structured set of StorageClasses with consistent naming and a single annotated default reduces misconfiguration. On on-prem clusters with Longhorn and Rook-Ceph, multiple classes serve different workload tiers (bulk, database, backup) and should be clearly differentiated.
fix: Name StorageClasses descriptively: longhorn-retain, longhorn-delete, ceph-rbd-retain, cephfs-rwx
Set exactly one default StorageClass using annotation: storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: 'true'
Check current default: kubectl get storageclass | grep default
Remove default from old class before setting new default to avoid dual-default conflicts
Patch default SC: kubectl patch storageclass <name> -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class":"true"}}}'
Document each StorageClass purpose in its annotations: kubectl annotate storageclass longhorn-retain description='Longhorn 3-replica block, Retain policy, for stateful apps'

root_cause: CSI MountVolume.SetUp failures appear in kubelet logs and Pod events when the CSI node plugin cannot mount the volume due to stale mount paths, a crashed CSI daemonset Pod, or node-level filesystem errors. These errors block Pod startup entirely.
fix: kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n <namespace> | grep -A30 Events  # look for FailedMount
Example log pattern: 'MountVolume.SetUp failed for volume "pvc-abc" : rpc error: code = Internal desc = ...'
Check CSI node DaemonSet Pod on same node: kubectl get pods -n longhorn-system -o wide | grep <node-name>
Restart CSI node plugin on affected node: kubectl rollout restart daemonset/longhorn-csi-plugin -n longhorn-system
Check for stale mount points on node: ssh <node> -- mount | grep <volume-id>
If stale: unmount manually on node: ssh <node> -- umount /var/lib/kubelet/pods/<pod-uid>/volumes/kubernetes.io~csi/<pvc-name>/mount

root_cause: Kubernetes does not enforce PVC capacity limits at the filesystem level for most drivers. A volume can fill up silently until writes fail. Proactive monitoring of kubelet_volume_stats_* metrics in Prometheus is essential to prevent data loss or application crashes.
fix: Prometheus metric for volume usage: kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes
Create Prometheus alert: expr: (kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes) > 0.85, for: 5m, severity: warning
Grafana dashboard: use panel with metric kubelet_volume_stats_available_bytes grouped by persistentvolumeclaim label
Enable Longhorn volume utilization metrics in Grafana: scrape longhorn-backend service on port 9500
kubectl top node  # does not show PVC usage; use Prometheus for per-PVC metrics
For Rook-Ceph: monitor pool usage with ceph df: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph df

symptoms: how to reference PVC in Helm chart
root_cause: Helm charts should expose storageClassName and storage size as values.yaml parameters to enable environment-specific overrides without template changes. Using a hardcoded StorageClass name in chart templates creates portability issues across clusters.
fix: In values.yaml: persistence.storageClass: longhorn-retain and persistence.size: 10Gi
In templates/pvc.yaml: storageClassName: {{ .Values.persistence.storageClass | quote }} and storage: {{ .Values.persistence.size }}
Override per environment in GitOps: helm upgrade ... --set persistence.storageClass=ceph-rbd-retain --set persistence.size=50Gi
For StatefulSets in Helm: use .Values.persistence.storageClass in volumeClaimTemplates[].spec.storageClassName
Validate: helm template . --set persistence.storageClass=test | grep storageClassName
Add schema validation in values.schema.json to enforce string type for storageClass

symptoms: ROX volume not supported by driver
root_cause: ReadOnlyMany (ROX) is supported by very few CSI drivers in practice. Most block-based drivers (Longhorn, Rook-Ceph RBD) do not support ROX. The typical workaround is to provision a RWO PVC, populate it, then create a snapshot or use a pre-provisioned static PV with ReadOnlyMany.
fix: Verify supported access modes for the CSI driver: kubectl get csidriver <driver-name> -o yaml | grep -A10 volumeModes
Longhorn does not natively support ROX for block volumes; use a workaround via NFS share mode
Rook-Ceph CephFS supports ROX: use provisioner rook-ceph.cephfs.csi.ceph.com with accessModes: [ReadOnlyMany]
For static data (ML models, config): use a ConfigMap or a pre-built container image instead of ROX PVC
If ROX is required: provision RWO, populate data, create VolumeSnapshot, restore snapshot as ROX where supported
kubectl get volumesnapshotclasses  # verify snapshot support before using snapshot-based ROX approach

symptoms: sharing single PVC across multiple pods incorrectly
RWO volume mounted by two pods simultaneously
root_cause: A PVC with accessMode ReadWriteOnce can technically be mounted by multiple Pods on the same node simultaneously (Kubernetes allows it per spec), but most block CSI drivers and filesystems are not safe for concurrent writes without coordination. This leads to data corruption, especially for databases.
fix: Never share a RWO PVC between multiple Pods that write data concurrently
For StatefulSets, each Pod gets its own PVC via volumeClaimTemplates — this is the correct pattern
For read-sharing on the same node, use ReadWriteOncePod (k8s 1.22+) to enforce a single Pod binding: accessModes: [ReadWriteOncePod]
kubectl get pvc -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.spec.accessModes[] | contains("ReadWriteOnce")) | {name:.metadata.name,ns:.metadata.namespace}'
For legitimate shared storage, use CephFS with RWX or Longhorn NFS share
Add podAntiAffinity rules to prevent multiple writer Pods from scheduling to the same node when using RWO

symptoms: how to take volume snapshot kubernetes
root_cause: Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot API (v1 stable since 1.20) allows point-in-time snapshots of PVCs through a CSI driver that implements the snapshot interfaces. Snapshots can be used for backup, cloning, or rollback before risky operations like schema migrations on CloudNativePG clusters.
fix: Verify VolumeSnapshotClass exists: kubectl get volumesnapshotclasses

Create snapshot: kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: snapshot.storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: VolumeSnapshot
metadata:
  name: pvc-snapshot-pre-migration
  namespace: <namespace>
spec:
  volumeSnapshotClassName: longhorn
  source:
    persistentVolumeClaimName: <pvc-name>
EOF
Check snapshot readiness: kubectl get volumesnapshot -n <namespace> -w  # ReadyToUse: true
Restore from snapshot by creating PVC with dataSource: kubectl apply -f pvc-from-snapshot.yaml with spec.dataSource.kind: VolumeSnapshot
List snapshots: kubectl get volumesnapshot,volumesnapshotcontent -n <namespace>
Clean up after successful migration: kubectl delete volumesnapshot pvc-snapshot-pre-migration -n <namespace>

symptoms: how to create a NATS JetStream stream on Kubernetes
root_cause: JetStream streams must be created via the NATS CLI or nats.js client after the NATS server is deployed. Streams define subjects, retention policy, storage type, and replication factor that all consumers inherit.
fix: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream add MY_STREAM --subjects 'events.>' --storage file --retention limits --max-msgs 1000000 --max-bytes 10GB --replicas 3
Verify stream: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream info MY_STREAM

In Node.js: const js = await nc.jetstreamManager(); await js.streams.add({ name: 'MY_STREAM', subjects: ['events.>'], storage: StorageType.File, num_replicas: 3, retention: RetentionPolicy.Limits, max_msgs: 1000000 });
Import StorageType and RetentionPolicy from 'nats' package: import { StorageType, RetentionPolicy } from 'nats';
Check stream list: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream ls

symptoms: how to configure NATS JetStream push consumer
root_cause: Push consumers have NATS deliver messages to a subject which a subscriber listens on. They are simpler for low-throughput scenarios but require a dedicated delivery subject per consumer group and are less suitable for horizontal scaling.
fix: Create push consumer via CLI: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer add MY_STREAM PUSH_CONSUMER --deliver-subject deliveries.my-worker --deliver-group workers --ack explicit --max-deliver 5 --filter 'events.>'

In Node.js: const js = nc.jetstream(); const sub = await js.subscribe('events.>', { config: { durable_name: 'PUSH_CONSUMER', deliver_subject: 'deliveries.my-worker', ack_policy: AckPolicy.Explicit, max_deliver: 5 } });
Process messages: for await (const msg of sub) { console.log(msg.data); msg.ack(); }
Import AckPolicy: import { AckPolicy } from 'nats';
Check consumer info: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer info MY_STREAM PUSH_CONSUMER

symptoms: how to configure NATS JetStream pull consumer
JetStream fetch messages pull-based K8s worker pods
root_cause: Pull consumers allow worker pods to explicitly fetch batches of messages, enabling better horizontal scaling and backpressure control. Each pod can pull independently without competing on a delivery subject, making them ideal for Kubernetes worker deployments.
fix: Create pull consumer: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer add MY_STREAM PULL_CONSUMER --pull --ack explicit --max-deliver 5 --filter 'events.>' --max-waiting 512
In Node.js fetch loop: const js = nc.jetstream(); const c = await js.consumers.get('MY_STREAM', 'PULL_CONSUMER');
Fetch batch: const msgs = await c.fetch({ max_messages: 10, expires: 5000 }); for await (const msg of msgs) { await processMsg(msg.data); msg.ack(); }
Use consume() for continuous processing: const sub = await c.consume({ max_messages: 10 }); for await (const msg of sub) { msg.ack(); }
Verify pending messages: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer info MY_STREAM PULL_CONSUMER | grep 'Num Pending'

root_cause: JetStream replication with R=3 requires at least 3 NATS server nodes with JetStream enabled and a properly configured cluster. If the StatefulSet has fewer than 3 replicas or cluster routes are misconfigured, streams fall back to R=1 silently.
fix: Ensure NATS StatefulSet has at least 3 replicas: kubectl scale statefulset nats -n nats --replicas=3
Verify cluster formation: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats server list
Check JetStream is enabled on all nodes: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats server info | grep -i jetstream
Set num_replicas: 3 in stream config and confirm: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream info MY_STREAM | grep 'Replication'
In NATS Helm values: config.cluster.enabled=true, config.cluster.replicas=3, config.jetstream.enabled=true, config.jetstream.fileStore.pvc.size=10Gi
Check stream cluster state: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream info MY_STREAM --json | jq '.cluster'

symptoms: NATS JetStream retention policy best practice
how to configure stream retention limits K8s
JetStream WorkQueue vs Limits vs Interest retention
root_cause: NATS JetStream supports three retention policies: Limits (default, retain messages up to configured limits), Interest (retain only while consumers exist), and WorkQueue (delete after all consumers ack). Choosing the wrong policy causes either data loss or unbounded storage growth.
fix: Use Limits retention for event logs: --retention limits with --max-msgs, --max-bytes, --max-age settings
Use WorkQueue retention for task queues where once-and-only-once processing is needed: --retention workqueue
Use Interest retention for fan-out pub/sub where messages should be dropped once no consumers exist: --retention interest
Set max-age for time-bounded streams: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream add --max-age 24h
Set discard policy to New to reject new messages when limits are reached (vs Old which drops oldest): --discard new
In Node.js: RetentionPolicy.WorkQueue, RetentionPolicy.Interest, RetentionPolicy.Limits from 'nats' package

root_cause: By default NATS JetStream may use memory storage or a non-persistent volume. After pod restarts, all stream data is lost. Persistent storage requires configuring a PVC in the NATS Helm chart or StatefulSet spec.
fix: In nats Helm chart values.yaml: set jetstream.fileStore.enabled=true, jetstream.fileStore.pvc.enabled=true, jetstream.fileStore.pvc.size=10Gi, jetstream.fileStore.pvc.storageClassName=longhorn
Verify PVC is created: kubectl get pvc -n nats
Check stream storage type: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream info MY_STREAM | grep Storage
Mount path for JetStream data in NATS config: jetstream { store_dir: "/data/jetstream" }
Ensure the StorageClass (Longhorn/Rook-Ceph) supports ReadWriteOnce for StatefulSet PVCs: kubectl get storageclass
After adding PVC, recreate stream with storage: file to persist: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream edit MY_STREAM --storage file

root_cause: When a consumer's max_deliver limit is exceeded, messages are moved to a dead-letter stream only if a dead-letter subject is configured. Without it, messages stop being redelivered but remain unacknowledged and block the consumer's progress for WorkQueue streams.
fix: Set nack_backoff for exponential retry: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer edit MY_STREAM MY_CONSUMER --backoff linear
Configure dead-letter via flow_control or a separate stream subscription on '$JS.EVENT.ADVISORY.CONSUMER.MAX_DELIVERIES.MY_STREAM.MY_CONSUMER'
In Node.js, use msg.nak(delay) for delayed redelivery: msg.nak(30000); // redelivery after 30s
Set max_deliver=5 and monitor advisory: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats sub '$JS.EVENT.ADVISORY.CONSUMER.MAX_DELIVERIES.>'
For WorkQueue streams, use term() to permanently discard unprocessable messages: msg.term();
Check num_redelivered counter: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer info MY_STREAM MY_CONSUMER | grep 'Redelivered'

symptoms: how to implement NATS JetStream ack policies
JetStream AckExplicit vs AckNone vs AckAll Node.js
root_cause: NATS JetStream supports three ack policies: Explicit (each message must be individually acked), None (no acks required), and All (acking a message acks all previous unacked messages). Explicit is the safest for at-least-once delivery semantics.
fix: AckExplicit (recommended for reliable processing): msg.ack() after successful processing; msg.nak() to requeue
AckNone (fire-and-forget, at-most-once): no ack call needed, stream will not redeliver
AckAll (cumulative, acks all previous messages): msg.ack() acks all messages with sequence <= current
In Node.js with nats.js: import { AckPolicy } from 'nats'; consumer config: { ack_policy: AckPolicy.Explicit, ack_wait: 30000000000 }
Set ack_wait in nanoseconds: 30s = 30000000000ns
Double-ack prevention: track processed message seq in Redis to avoid duplicate processing on redelivery

symptoms: how to enable NATS JetStream deduplication
root_cause: JetStream supports server-side deduplication using the Nats-Msg-Id header. Messages with the same ID published within the dedup_window are deduplicated automatically, preventing at-least-once publish retries from creating duplicate stream entries.
fix: Set dedup window on stream: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream add MY_STREAM --dupe-window 2m
Or edit existing: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream edit MY_STREAM --dupe-window 2m
In Node.js publish with Nats-Msg-Id header: const js = nc.jetstream(); await js.publish('events.order', payload, { headers: headers() }); — set h.set('Nats-Msg-Id', uniqueId);
Import headers: import { headers } from 'nats'; const h = headers(); h.set('Nats-Msg-Id', `order-${orderId}-${timestamp}`);
Verify dedup window: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream info MY_STREAM | grep 'Duplicate'
Monitor dedup hits via server metrics: nats server report jetstream --all

symptoms: how to set up NATS server cluster K8s StatefulSet
NATS routing between pods headless service
root_cause: NATS clustering in Kubernetes requires each server to know the routes to other cluster members. With a StatefulSet and headless Service, each pod gets a stable DNS name. The NATS config must enumerate these routes or use DNS-based route discovery.
fix: Create headless Service: spec.clusterIP: None with selector matching NATS pods, port 6222 for cluster routes

In nats.conf: cluster { name: nats, listen: 0.0.0.0:6222, routes: [ nats://nats-0.nats.nats.svc.cluster.local:6222, nats://nats-1.nats.nats.svc.cluster.local:6222, nats://nats-2.nats.nats.svc.cluster.local:6222 ] }
Use NATS Helm chart: helm install nats nats/nats -f values.yaml with cluster.enabled=true, cluster.replicas=3
Verify cluster topology: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats server list
Check route connections: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats server info | grep -A5 'Routes'
Use cluster.routes with nats-box for testing: kubectl run -it nats-box --image=natsio/nats-box:latest --rm -- nats server list -s nats://nats.nats.svc.cluster.local:4222

root_cause: JetStream monitoring requires checking both NATS server health endpoints and Prometheus metrics. The NATS server exposes a /jsz endpoint for JetStream-specific stats and the nats-exporter sidecar or standalone deployment exposes Prometheus metrics.
fix: Check JetStream status via HTTP: kubectl port-forward svc/nats 8222:8222 -n nats && curl http://localhost:8222/jsz?all=1 | jq .
View stream stats: curl http://localhost:8222/jsz?streams=1 | jq '.streams[] | {name, messages, bytes, consumer_count}'
Deploy nats-exporter for Prometheus: kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nats-io/prometheus-nats-exporter/main/examples/prometheus-nats-exporter.yaml
Key Prometheus metrics: nats_jetstream_streams_count, nats_jetstream_consumers_count, nats_jetstream_messages_count, nats_jetstream_storage_used_bytes
Check consumer lag: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer report MY_STREAM
Watch advisories for issues: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats sub '$JS.EVENT.ADVISORY.>'  --count=20

symptoms: nats: maximum consumers per stream exceeded
root_cause: JetStream streams have a max_consumers limit (default -1 = unlimited, but can be set explicitly). When the limit is reached, new consumer creation fails. This often occurs when ephemeral consumers from crashed pods are not cleaned up or the limit is too restrictive.
fix: Check current consumer count: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream info MY_STREAM | grep 'Consumers'
List all consumers including ephemeral: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer ls MY_STREAM
Remove stale ephemeral consumers: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer rm MY_STREAM STALE_CONSUMER
Increase max_consumers limit: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream edit MY_STREAM --max-consumers 100
Set unlimited: nats stream edit MY_STREAM --max-consumers -1
Use durable consumers with unique names per pod to avoid unbounded ephemeral consumer accumulation

symptoms: Node.js nats.js connect to NATS JetStream Kubernetes
how to connect nats.js to K8s NATS cluster
root_cause: Connecting to NATS JetStream from Node.js pods requires the nats.js client to connect to the NATS service, optionally with TLS and credentials, then obtain a JetStream context for publishing and consuming messages.
fix: Install nats.js: npm install nats
Connect: import { connect } from 'nats'; const nc = await connect({ servers: ['nats://nats.nats.svc.cluster.local:4222'], maxReconnectAttempts: -1, reconnectTimeWait: 2000 });
Get JetStream context: const js = nc.jetstream(); const jsm = await nc.jetstreamManager();
With credentials (NKey or JWT): const nc = await connect({ servers: ['nats://nats.nats.svc.cluster.local:4222'], authenticator: credsAuthenticator(fs.readFileSync('/var/secrets/nats/user.creds')) });
Graceful shutdown: process.on('SIGTERM', async () => { await nc.drain(); });
Handle connection events: nc.status() for monitoring reconnect/disconnect events in async iterator

symptoms: NATS JetStream consumers not scaling horizontally
multiple pods fighting over same push consumer
push consumer queue group not distributing messages
root_cause: Using push consumers without a queue group (deliver_group) across multiple pod replicas causes each pod to receive all messages, leading to duplicate processing. The correct pattern for horizontal scaling is pull consumers or push consumers with a shared deliver_group.
fix: Anti-pattern: push consumer without queue group — every subscriber gets every message
Fix option 1: Use pull consumers which natively support horizontal scaling — each fetch only returns undelivered messages
Fix option 2: Set deliver_group on push consumer: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer edit MY_STREAM PUSH_CONSUMER --deliver-group workers
In Node.js for pull: const c = await js.consumers.get('MY_STREAM', 'PULL_CONSUMER'); — each pod independently calls c.fetch()
Verify queue group distribution: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer info MY_STREAM PUSH_CONSUMER | grep 'Deliver Group'
For K8s Deployments with multiple replicas, always prefer pull consumers over push consumers

root_cause: Consumer lag in JetStream indicates that messages are being published faster than they are processed. This can be caused by insufficient consumer replicas, slow ack processing, or large batch fetch sizes causing head-of-line blocking.
fix: Monitor lag: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats consumer report MY_STREAM (shows Num Pending and Ack Pending)
Scale consumer pods: kubectl scale deployment worker --replicas=5 -n app
Increase fetch batch size and parallelism in Node.js: const msgs = await c.fetch({ max_messages: 50, expires: 5000 });
Use Promise.all for parallel processing within a batch: await Promise.all(batch.map(msg => processMsg(msg)));
Set max_ack_pending on consumer to control in-flight messages: --max-ack-pending 1000
Use KEDA JetStreamTrigger for autoscaling: scaleTargetRef + trigger type 'nats-jetstream' with lagThreshold

symptoms: NATS JetStream production readiness checklist K8s
best practices before deploying NATS JetStream production
root_cause: A NATS JetStream production deployment on Kubernetes requires careful attention to replication, persistence, resource limits, monitoring, and graceful shutdown to ensure reliability and data durability.
fix: [ ] StatefulSet with 3+ replicas, anti-affinity rules to spread across nodes: podAntiAffinity requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution
[ ] JetStream file storage with PVC on durable StorageClass (Longhorn/Rook-Ceph): jetstream.fileStore.pvc.enabled=true
[ ] All streams created with num_replicas=3 for HA
[ ] Resource requests/limits set: requests cpu:100m,memory:256Mi limits cpu:1,memory:1Gi
[ ] Prometheus scraping enabled via nats-exporter or ServiceMonitor for nats_jetstream_* metrics
[ ] Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM handler with nc.drain() in Node.js workers
[ ] Consumer max_deliver and ack_wait tuned to processing SLA
[ ] Dedup window configured for publish-retry resilient producers

symptoms: NATS JetStream 'no responders available for request'
nats: no responders error publishing to stream
root_cause: The 'no responders' error during JetStream publish occurs when the subject is not covered by any stream, the stream does not exist yet, or the NATS server has JetStream disabled. It can also occur when publishing before the JetStream context is fully established.
fix: Verify stream covers the subject: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream find --subject 'events.order'
Check JetStream is enabled: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats server info | grep -i 'JetStream'
Enable JetStream in nats.conf: jetstream { enabled: true, store_dir: "/data" }
Ensure stream exists before publishing: const jsm = await nc.jetstreamManager(); const streams = await jsm.streams.list().next(); check stream name exists
In Node.js wrap publish with retry: use exponential backoff on NatsError with code NO_RESPONDERS
Check pod DNS resolution: kubectl exec -it worker-pod -- nslookup nats.nats.svc.cluster.local

symptoms: NATS JetStream stream not recovering after pod restart
JetStream stream metadata lost after StatefulSet restart
NATS cluster stream leader election failing
root_cause: JetStream uses a Raft-based consensus for stream metadata in clustered mode. After a cluster restart, leader election must complete before streams become available. With persistent storage, streams recover automatically, but quorum requires 2 of 3 nodes to be healthy.
fix: Check Raft cluster state: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats server report jetstream
Verify all 3 pods are Running: kubectl get pods -n nats -l app=nats
Check stream cluster leader: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats stream info MY_STREAM --json | jq '.cluster'
Force leader step-down if stuck: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats server cluster step-down
Check PVC is mounted correctly: kubectl describe pod nats-0 -n nats | grep -A5 'Mounts'
Review NATS logs for Raft errors: kubectl logs nats-0 -n nats | grep -i 'raft\|leader\|election'

symptoms: best practice for stream and consumer naming K8s
root_cause: Consistent naming conventions for streams, subjects, and consumers improve operability and allow subject-based access control. NATS subjects are hierarchical using dots as separators, and streams should be defined with subject wildcards to allow future extension.
fix: Name streams in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE: ORDERS, USER_EVENTS, PAYMENT_EVENTS
Use hierarchical subjects: <domain>.<entity>.<action> e.g. orders.order.created, payments.invoice.paid
Use wildcard subjects for stream coverage: subjects: ['orders.>'] captures all orders sub-subjects
Name durable consumers with app and worker role: orders-processor, payment-worker
For per-pod consumers in a Deployment, suffix with pod ordinal or UUID: worker-consumer-${POD_NAME}
Use NATS account/user per microservice for isolation: operator > account > users in security config

symptoms: how to use NATS JetStream KeyValue store on Kubernetes
NATS KV store vs Redis for config storage K8s
root_cause: JetStream KeyValue (KV) is a higher-level API built on JetStream streams. It provides a distributed, replicated key-value store suitable for configuration, feature flags, or lightweight state, as an alternative to Redis for simple K/V use cases within the NATS ecosystem.
fix: Create KV bucket: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats kv add CONFIG --replicas 3 --history 5 --ttl 1h
Put/get values: kubectl exec -it nats-0 -n nats -- nats kv put CONFIG feature.dark-mode true && nats kv get CONFIG feature.dark-mode
In Node.js: const js = nc.jetstream(); const kv = await js.views.kv('CONFIG', { replicas: 3 });
Put value: await kv.put('feature.dark-mode', sc.encode('true'));
Watch for changes: const watcher = await kv.watch({ key: 'feature.*' }); for await (const entry of watcher) { console.log(entry.key, entry.value); }
Import StringCodec: import { StringCodec } from 'nats'; const sc = StringCodec();

symptoms: how to deploy Redis Cluster on Kubernetes with Redis Operator
Redis Operator setup for high availability
root_cause: Running Redis in cluster mode on Kubernetes requires a dedicated operator (e.g., Ot-DB Redis Operator or Redis Operator by Opstree) to manage RedisCluster CRDs, handle leader election, and automate slot rebalancing across pods.
fix: helm repo add ot-helm https://ot-container-kit.github.io/helm-charts/
helm install redis-operator ot-helm/redis-operator -n operators --create-namespace

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.redis.opstreelabs.in/v1beta2
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: redis-cluster
  namespace: redis
spec:
  clusterSize: 3
  clusterVersion: v7
  persistenceEnabled: true
  kubernetesConfig:
    image: quay.io/opstree/redis:v7.0.12
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
  redisExporter:
    enabled: true
    image: quay.io/opstree/redis-exporter:v1.44.0
  storage:
    volumeClaimTemplate:
      spec:
        storageClassName: longhorn
        accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: 10Gi
EOF
kubectl get rediscluster -n redis
kubectl get pods -n redis -l app=redis-cluster

symptoms: Redis HA sentinel failover not working
root_cause: Redis Sentinel requires a quorum of sentinel instances to agree before promoting a replica to master. With fewer than 3 sentinel pods or misconfigured quorum value, failover stalls because consensus cannot be reached.
fix: Ensure at least 3 sentinel instances are running: kubectl get pods -n redis -l role=sentinel
Set quorum to majority: sentinel monitor mymaster <redis-master-ip> 6379 2 (for 3 sentinels)
Check sentinel logs: kubectl logs -n redis sentinel-0 | grep -i 'failover\|quorum\|elected'
Verify sentinel can reach master: kubectl exec -n redis sentinel-0 -- redis-cli -p 26379 sentinel masters
For RedisReplication CRD with operator, set spec.redisConfig.sentinel.quorum: '2'
Confirm pods are on different nodes to avoid single-node failure wiping quorum: kubectl get pods -n redis -o wide

symptoms: BullMQ worker ETIMEDOUT connecting to Redis
root_cause: BullMQ uses ioredis and by default ioredis sets maxRetriesPerRequest to a finite number, which causes commands to fail with ETIMEDOUT when Redis is temporarily unavailable (e.g., during failover). BullMQ requires maxRetriesPerRequest=null to block and retry indefinitely.
fix: Set maxRetriesPerRequest: null in ioredis connection options passed to BullMQ:
const connection = new IORedis({ host: 'redis-cluster', port: 6379, maxRetriesPerRequest: null, enableReadyCheck: false });
const queue = new Queue('jobs', { connection });
const worker = new Worker('jobs', processor, { connection });
For Redis Cluster mode use Cluster client: const connection = new IORedis.Cluster([{ host: 'redis-cluster', port: 6379 }], { maxRetriesPerRequest: null });

Verify: kubectl exec -n app worker-pod -- node -e "const {Queue}=require('bullmq');const q=new Queue('t',{connection:{host:'redis-cluster',port:6379,maxRetriesPerRequest:null}});q.getJobCounts().then(console.log)"

symptoms: how to configure Redis AOF persistence on Kubernetes
Redis data loss after pod restart
best practice for Redis persistence in Kubernetes
root_cause: Without persistence enabled, Redis data is lost on pod restart or eviction. AOF (Append-Only File) provides durability by logging every write operation; combined with RDB snapshots it balances durability and recovery speed.
fix: Enable both AOF and RDB in RedisCluster spec via operator:

spec:
  redisConfig:
    data:
      appendonly: 'yes'
      appendfsync: everysec
      save: '3600 1 300 100 60 10000'
      aof-use-rdb-preamble: 'yes'
Mount persistent volume: ensure spec.storage.volumeClaimTemplate uses a StorageClass like longhorn with allowVolumeExpansion: true
Verify AOF is active: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli CONFIG GET appendonly
Check RDB save: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli LASTSAVE
Monitor persistence metrics via redis-exporter: redis_rdb_last_save_timestamp_seconds, redis_aof_enabled

symptoms: Redis out of memory evicting keys unexpectedly
root_cause: Without a maxmemory policy configured, Redis fills memory and starts returning OOM errors for writes. For BullMQ use cases the correct policy is 'noeviction' to prevent silent job loss; for cache-only Redis the policy should be 'allkeys-lru'.
fix: For BullMQ (job queue): set maxmemory-policy noeviction — never evict keys, return error instead

spec:
  redisConfig:
    data:
      maxmemory: '4gb'
      maxmemory-policy: noeviction
For cache use case: maxmemory-policy: allkeys-lru
Apply live (will not persist across restart without config): kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy noeviction
Verify: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli CONFIG GET maxmemory-policy
Alert on memory: set Prometheus alert on redis_memory_used_bytes / redis_memory_max_bytes > 0.85

symptoms: BullMQ stalled jobs not being retried
BullMQ jobs stuck in active state
root_cause: BullMQ moves jobs from 'active' to 'stalled' when a worker locks them but fails to renew the lock within stalledInterval. If no worker is running or the stalledInterval is set too high, jobs remain stuck in active state indefinitely.
fix: Ensure stalledInterval and lockDuration are configured on the Worker:
const worker = new Worker('jobs', processor, { connection, stalledInterval: 30000, lockDuration: 30000 });
Check stalled jobs: kubectl exec -n app worker-pod -- node -e "const {Queue}=require('bullmq');const q=new Queue('jobs',{connection});q.getJobCounts('active','stalled').then(console.log)"
Manually retry stalled jobs with BullMQ's obliterate or moveStalledJobsToWait: await queue.obliterate({ force: false })
Increase worker concurrency to reduce lock contention: new Worker('jobs', processor, { connection, concurrency: 10 })
Monitor active jobs older than lockDuration: track bull_queue_active metric via bullmq-prometheus or custom exporter

symptoms: how to implement graceful BullMQ worker shutdown on Kubernetes
BullMQ worker not finishing jobs before pod terminates
root_cause: Kubernetes sends SIGTERM to pods before termination. Without a graceful shutdown handler, BullMQ workers are killed mid-job, causing jobs to appear stalled or partially processed.
fix: Handle SIGTERM in Node.js worker process:
const worker = new Worker('jobs', processor, { connection });
process.on('SIGTERM', shutdown);
process.on('SIGINT', shutdown);
Set terminationGracePeriodSeconds in Deployment spec to allow jobs to finish: spec.template.spec.terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60
Verify graceful shutdown: kubectl delete pod worker-pod-xxx && kubectl logs worker-pod-xxx | grep 'closing worker'

symptoms: how to create BullMQ repeatable jobs
root_cause: BullMQ supports repeatable jobs using cron expressions or fixed intervals. These jobs are stored in Redis and fire automatically; only one instance of a repeatable job with the same key runs at a time even with multiple workers.
fix: Add a repeatable job with cron expression:
await queue.add('report', { type: 'daily' }, { repeat: { pattern: '0 8 * * *', tz: 'UTC' } });
Add a repeatable job with fixed interval (every 5 minutes):
await queue.add('healthcheck', {}, { repeat: { every: 300000 } });
List repeatable jobs: const repeatableJobs = await queue.getRepeatableJobs(); console.log(repeatableJobs);
Remove a repeatable job: await queue.removeRepeatable('report', { pattern: '0 8 * * *' });
Verify in Redis: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli KEYS 'bull:jobs:repeat:*'

symptoms: how to add BullMQ delayed jobs
BullMQ schedule job for future execution
root_cause: BullMQ supports delayed jobs that are placed in a delayed set in Redis and moved to the wait queue only after the delay has elapsed. This is implemented via a sorted set scored by future timestamp.
fix: Add a delayed job (delay in milliseconds):
await queue.add('email', { to: 'user@example.com' }, { delay: 60000 }); // 1 minute delay
Add a job with specific run-at time: const delay = new Date('2026-07-01T09:00:00Z').getTime() - Date.now(); await queue.add('report', {}, { delay });
Check delayed jobs count: await queue.getJobCounts('delayed')
Inspect delayed jobs: const jobs = await queue.getJobs(['delayed']); jobs.forEach(j => console.log(j.id, j.opts.delay));
Verify in Redis sorted set: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli ZRANGE bull:jobs:delayed 0 -1 WITHSCORES

symptoms: BullMQ worker crashes cause job loss
BullMQ jobs not retried after worker OOM
root_cause: Without explicit retry configuration, failed BullMQ jobs are either immediately removed or pile up in the failed set consuming Redis memory. Similarly, completed jobs without removeOnComplete fill Redis, eventually triggering OOM.
fix: Always configure attempts and backoff on job add:
await queue.add('task', data, { attempts: 5, backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 2000 }, removeOnComplete: { count: 100 }, removeOnFail: { count: 500 } });
Set default job options on Queue initialization:
const queue = new Queue('jobs', { connection, defaultJobOptions: { attempts: 3, backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 1000 }, removeOnComplete: { age: 86400 }, removeOnFail: { age: 604800 } } });
Monitor failed job count: await queue.getJobCounts('failed')
Drain old failed jobs: await queue.clean(7 * 24 * 3600 * 1000, 1000, 'failed')

symptoms: Redis Cluster connection refused from worker pod
root_cause: BullMQ queue processing issues can stem from network policy blocking pod-to-Redis traffic, Redis cluster in failed state, or worker pods not running. Systematic diagnosis narrows the root cause.
fix: Step 1 - Check worker pod status: kubectl get pods -n app -l app=worker
Step 2 - Check Redis cluster health: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli cluster info | grep cluster_state
Step 3 - Test connectivity from worker: kubectl exec -n app worker-pod -- redis-cli -h redis-cluster -p 6379 PING
Step 4 - Check NetworkPolicy: kubectl get networkpolicy -n app && kubectl describe networkpolicy -n app worker-network-policy

Step 5 - Check queue counts: kubectl exec -n app worker-pod -- node -e "const {Queue}=require('bullmq');new Queue('jobs',{connection:{host:'redis-cluster',port:6379,maxRetriesPerRequest:null}}).getJobCounts().then(console.log)"
Step 6 - Check worker logs: kubectl logs -n app -l app=worker --tail=100 | grep -i 'error\|failed\|stalled'

symptoms: Redis Cluster node added but not balanced
root_cause: When adding nodes to a Redis Cluster, slot migration is performed by the operator or manually via redis-cli. Migration is CPU and network intensive and can saturate the Redis node if not rate-limited, causing latency spikes.
fix: Scale RedisCluster via operator: kubectl patch rediscluster redis-cluster -n redis --type merge -p '{"spec":{"clusterSize":4}}'
Monitor slot migration: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli cluster info | grep -E 'cluster_slots|cluster_state'

Rate-limit migration using redis-cli --cluster reshard with --cluster-pipeline: redis-cli --cluster reshard redis-cluster:6379 --cluster-slots 1000 --cluster-from all --cluster-to <new-node-id> --cluster-pipeline 10 --cluster-yes
Watch migration progress: watch -n2 'kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli cluster nodes | awk "{print \$3, \$9}"'
Set lower hz during migration to reduce background work: redis-cli CONFIG SET hz 5
Monitor latency: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli --latency -i 1

symptoms: how to harden Redis on Kubernetes
root_cause: Running Redis Cluster in production on Kubernetes requires validating persistence, security, resource limits, monitoring, and anti-affinity to ensure data durability and high availability.
fix: Persistence: verify AOF enabled and PVC bound: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli CONFIG GET appendonly
Auth: enable requirepass and aclfile via Secret mounted into RedisCluster spec
Resource limits: set requests/limits on all Redis pods — requests.memory: 1Gi, limits.memory: 4Gi to prevent OOM kill
Anti-affinity: set podAntiAffinity topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname so cluster shards land on different nodes
Monitoring: confirm redis-exporter sidecar running and ServiceMonitor scraped by Prometheus: kubectl get servicemonitor -n redis
Backup: schedule redis-dump CronJob or use operator snapshot feature to backup RDB to object storage
NetworkPolicy: restrict Redis port 6379/16379 to only app namespaces: kubectl apply -f redis-network-policy.yaml

symptoms: CROSSSLOT Keys in request don't hash to the same slot
BullMQ multi-key operations fail on Redis Cluster
root_cause: Redis Cluster requires all keys in a multi-key operation (MSET, MGET, Lua scripts, transactions) to hash to the same slot. BullMQ uses hash tags internally to ensure its keys share slots, but custom Lua scripts or pipeline commands without hash tags fail with CROSSSLOT.
fix: Use hash tags in custom keys to force same slot: {queue}:key1 and {queue}:key2 will hash identically
BullMQ handles this internally when connection is IORedis.Cluster — ensure you're not overriding key prefixes
Check if custom scripts use multi-key ops: search codebase for MSET, MGET, EVAL with multiple keys
For IORedis.Cluster, enable natMap if behind a LB: new IORedis.Cluster([...], { natMap: { '10.0.0.1:6379': { host: 'redis-0.redis', port: 6379 } } })
Reproduce error: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli MSET {a}:k1 v1 {b}:k2 v2  # this fails
Correct: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- redis-cli MSET {queue}:k1 v1 {queue}:k2 v2  # same slot

symptoms: how to architect BullMQ with Redis Cluster for high availability
root_cause: BullMQ can operate against Redis Sentinel (via ioredis sentinelRetryStrategy) or Redis Cluster. Redis Cluster provides horizontal write scalability and automatic sharding; Sentinel provides HA for single-shard Redis without sharding. BullMQ's built-in queue-per-key design works well with Cluster hash tags.
fix: For BullMQ with Redis Cluster, use IORedis.Cluster with clusterRetryStrategy:
const connection = new IORedis.Cluster([{ host: 'redis-cluster', port: 6379 }], { redisOptions: { maxRetriesPerRequest: null }, clusterRetryStrategy: (times) => Math.min(times * 100, 3000) });
For Sentinel (single shard HA): const connection = new IORedis({ sentinels: [{host:'redis-sentinel',port:26379}], name:'mymaster', maxRetriesPerRequest: null });
Separate cache Redis (allkeys-lru) from BullMQ Redis (noeviction) — do not share instances
Use separate queues per workload type (email, reports, imports) to isolate failure domains
Deploy worker Deployments (not CronJobs) with HPA or KEDA ScaledObject based on bull_queue_waiting metric

root_cause: Redis AOF rewrite failure (BGREWRITEAOF) typically occurs when Redis cannot fork a child process due to memory overcommit being disabled on the host, or when the data directory runs out of disk space. This log pattern indicates durability risk.
fix: Identify the log pattern: Background AOF rewrite failed: fork: Cannot allocate memory
Fix: enable memory overcommit on node: echo 'vm.overcommit_memory=1' >> /etc/sysctl.conf && sysctl -p
For Kubernetes nodes (RKE2/Talos): apply DaemonSet that sets sysctl or use node config: spec.sysctls on the node
Check disk space: kubectl exec -n redis redis-cluster-0 -- df -h /data
Expand PVC: kubectl patch pvc redis-data-redis-cluster-0 -n redis -p '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"storage":"20Gi"}}}}'
Monitor: alert on redis_aof_last_rewrite_duration_sec > 60 and redis_aof_last_bgrewrite_status != 1

symptoms: how to monitor BullMQ queues with Prometheus
root_cause: Without queue-level metrics (waiting, active, completed, failed counts), operators cannot detect backlogs or stalled processing. BullMQ does not expose Prometheus metrics natively; a separate exporter or custom instrumentation is required.
fix: Use @bull-board/api or bullmq-prometheus library: npm install bullmq-prometheus

Expose metrics endpoint in Node.js worker: const { BullMQPrometheusExporter } = require('bullmq-prometheus'); const exporter = new BullMQPrometheusExporter([queue], { collectDefaultMetrics: true }); app.get('/metrics', async (req,res) => res.send(await exporter.metrics()));
Annotate worker Deployment for Prometheus scrape: annotations: prometheus.io/scrape: 'true', prometheus.io/port: '3001', prometheus.io/path: '/metrics'
Key metrics to alert on: bullmq_queue_waiting > 500 (backlog), bullmq_queue_failed (error rate), bullmq_queue_active (stuck jobs)
Import Grafana dashboard for BullMQ: use dashboard ID 13929 or create panels from bullmq_* metrics
For KEDA autoscaling: configure RedisListsScaler on the BullMQ waiting list key: bull:{queue-name}:wait

symptoms: how to name BullMQ queues in Kubernetes
root_cause: In Kubernetes environments with multiple teams or services, undisciplined BullMQ queue naming leads to accidental cross-service interference, difficult metric aggregation, and unclear ownership. A consistent naming convention prevents these issues.
fix: Adopt naming pattern: {service}-{domain}-{action} e.g. api-email-send, worker-report-generate
For multi-tenant: prefix with tenant ID: {tenantId}:{service}-{domain} — use hash tag {tenantId} to keep related keys in same Redis slot
Set keyPrefix per Queue instance to namespace in Redis: new Queue('email-send', { connection, prefix: 'myapp' });
Document queue names in a shared registry (ConfigMap or Git): kubectl create configmap queue-registry -n app --from-literal=queues='api-email-send,worker-report-generate'
Use the same queue name in Queue (producer) and Worker (consumer) — mismatch silently causes jobs to never process
Label Kubernetes worker Deployments with queue name: labels: queue: api-email-send for easy filtering

symptoms: Redis Cluster pod stuck in Terminating
root_cause: Redis Operator CRDs may have finalizers that prevent deletion if the operator pod is not running or if PVCs are still bound. The CRD resource gets stuck waiting for the operator to process the finalizer.
fix: Check finalizers: kubectl get rediscluster redis-cluster -n redis -o jsonpath='{.metadata.finalizers}'
If operator is down, manually remove finalizer: kubectl patch rediscluster redis-cluster -n redis -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":[]}}' --type=merge
Delete PVCs manually after cluster deletion: kubectl delete pvc -n redis -l app=redis-cluster
Check if operator pod is healthy: kubectl get pods -n operators -l app.kubernetes.io/name=redis-operator
Restart operator if stuck: kubectl rollout restart deployment -n operators redis-operator
Prevent future issues: ensure operator is deployed before creating CRs; use helm upgrade --wait

root_cause: BullMQ job processing latency is affected by worker concurrency, Redis round-trip latency, and job processor CPU/IO blocking. A single worker with concurrency=1 processes jobs serially; under-provisioned workers create visible queue backlog.
fix: Increase worker concurrency for IO-bound jobs: new Worker('jobs', processor, { connection, concurrency: 50 });
For CPU-bound jobs, run multiple worker pods (Deployment replicas) with lower concurrency per pod
Measure Redis RTT from worker pod: kubectl exec -n app worker-pod -- redis-cli -h redis-cluster --latency-history -i 1
Use BullMQ flow producer for chaining jobs to avoid polling: const flow = new FlowProducer({ connection });
Profile job processor with clinic.js or async_hooks to find blocking operations
Scale workers with KEDA based on queue depth: ScaledObject targeting bull:{queue}:wait list length, minReplicaCount: 1, maxReplicaCount: 20, threshold: 10

symptoms: MetalLB L2 vs BGP mode differences
which MetalLB mode should I use
how does MetalLB assign external IPs
root_cause: MetalLB operates in two modes: L2 (ARP/NDP) mode where a single speaker node claims ownership of an IP and responds to ARP requests, providing simple setup but no true load balancing; and BGP mode where all nodes peer with upstream routers and advertise routes, enabling ECMP-based load balancing across multiple nodes.
fix: L2 mode: create L2Advertisement CR referencing your IPAddressPool — speaker on one node answers ARP for the VIP
BGP mode: configure BGPPeer CRs pointing to upstream routers, create BGPAdvertisement CR — all nodes advertise /32 routes via BGP
L2 mode failover relies on gratuitous ARP; BGP mode failover is handled by BGP route withdrawal (faster convergence)
BGP mode requires a router that supports BGP (e.g., Cisco, Juniper, Cumulus, or a software router like Bird/FRR)
Check active mode: kubectl get l2advertisements,bgpadvertisements -n metallb-system
L2 mode has single point of failure per IP; BGP enables true multi-node ECMP load balancing

root_cause: MetalLB v0.13+ uses the IPAddressPool CRD to define the ranges of IPs it can assign to LoadBalancer services, replacing the deprecated legacy configmap approach. Without a properly defined pool, MetalLB cannot assign external IPs.
fix:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: metallb.io/v1beta1
kind: IPAddressPool
metadata:
  name: production-pool
  namespace: metallb-system
spec:
  addresses:
  - 192.168.1.200-192.168.1.250
  - 10.0.0.0/24
  autoAssign: true
EOF
Set autoAssign: false to require explicit annotation: metallb.universe.tf/address-pool: production-pool on Service
Verify pool: kubectl get ipaddresspool -n metallb-system -o yaml
Check assigned IPs: kubectl get svc -A | grep LoadBalancer
Use CIDR notation or range notation (start-end) in the addresses field
Ensure IP ranges do not overlap with existing node IPs or cluster CIDR

root_cause: In MetalLB v0.13+, L2 mode requires both an IPAddressPool and an L2Advertisement CRD. The L2Advertisement tells MetalLB which pools to advertise via ARP/NDP and optionally which nodes act as speakers for those addresses.
fix:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: metallb.io/v1beta1
kind: L2Advertisement
metadata:
  name: l2-advert
  namespace: metallb-system
spec:
  ipAddressPools:
  - production-pool
  nodeSelectors:
  - matchLabels:
      kubernetes.io/os: linux
EOF
Omitting nodeSelectors allows all nodes to become potential speakers; the election picks one per IP
Omitting ipAddressPools makes L2Advertisement apply to all pools
Verify advertisement: kubectl get l2advertisement -n metallb-system -o yaml
Test: kubectl expose deploy nginx --type=LoadBalancer --port=80 and check EXTERNAL-IP appears
Check speaker logs: kubectl logs -n metallb-system -l app=metallb,component=speaker

root_cause: BGP mode in MetalLB requires BGPPeer CRDs defining the upstream router(s) to peer with. Each BGPPeer specifies the router's IP, its ASN, and MetalLB's own ASN. Without valid BGP peers, MetalLB cannot advertise routes.
fix:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: metallb.io/v1beta2
kind: BGPPeer
metadata:
  name: spine-router
  namespace: metallb-system
spec:
  peerAddress: 192.168.0.1
  peerASN: 64512
  myASN: 64513
  routerID: 192.168.1.100
  holdTime: 90s
  keepAliveTime: 30s
EOF
For iBGP (same ASN), set peerASN == myASN and configure allowedCIDRs as needed
Verify peer status: kubectl get bgppeer -n metallb-system -o yaml
Check BGP session state in speaker logs: kubectl logs -n metallb-system -l component=speaker | grep -i bgp
Add BGPAdvertisement CR referencing the pool and peers after BGPPeer is established
For FRR mode, also check FRR container logs: kubectl logs -n metallb-system -l component=speaker -c frr

root_cause: BGPAdvertisement CRD controls which IP pools are advertised to which BGP peers and allows setting BGP attributes like communities and local preference. Without it, IPs from pools will not be advertised even if BGPPeer is configured.
fix:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: metallb.io/v1beta1
kind: BGPAdvertisement
metadata:
  name: bgp-advert
  namespace: metallb-system
spec:
  ipAddressPools:
  - production-pool
  peers:
  - spine-router
  communities:
  - 64512:100
  localPref: 100
  aggregationLength: 32
EOF
aggregationLength: 32 advertises individual /32 host routes; use /24 to aggregate
localPref is only valid for iBGP sessions; omit for eBGP
communities values must match strings accepted by your router
Verify: kubectl get bgpadvertisement -n metallb-system -o yaml
Test route propagation: on the router run 'show bgp ipv4 unicast' or 'ip route show' on a host in the routed network

symptoms: MetalLB speaker and controller pod roles
root_cause: MetalLB has two components: the controller deployment (singleton) handles IP address assignment from pools to Services; the speaker DaemonSet runs on every node and handles the actual traffic announcement — ARP responses in L2 mode or BGP session management in BGP mode.
fix: Check controller: kubectl get deploy -n metallb-system metallb-controller
Check speakers: kubectl get ds -n metallb-system metallb-speaker — should show one pod per node
Controller logs for assignment issues: kubectl logs -n metallb-system -l component=controller
Speaker logs for advertisement issues: kubectl logs -n metallb-system -l component=speaker
In L2 mode, only one speaker per IP will respond to ARP (elected via memberlist protocol)
In BGP mode, all speakers establish BGP sessions and advertise routes independently
Both components require RBAC to watch Services and Endpoints: verify with kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:metallb-system:controller list services

symptoms: MetalLB FRR mode vs native mode
root_cause: MetalLB supports two BGP implementations: the native (built-in) speaker with limited BGP feature support, and FRR mode which runs an FRRouting sidecar container in the speaker pod. FRR mode supports BFD, graceful restart, ECMP, and more advanced routing features unavailable in native mode.
fix: Enable FRR mode via Helm: helm upgrade metallb metallb/metallb -n metallb-system --set speaker.frr.enabled=true
Or set in values.yaml: speaker.frr.enabled: true
FRR mode runs frr container alongside speaker in each speaker pod
Check FRR config: kubectl exec -n metallb-system <speaker-pod> -c frr -- vtysh -c 'show running-config'
Enable BFD for fast failover: add bfdProfile to BGPPeer spec with minTx/minRx/multiplier settings
Check FRR logs: kubectl logs -n metallb-system <speaker-pod> -c frr
FRR mode required for graceful restart: add spec.gracefulRestart: true to BGPPeer

symptoms: MetalLB service stuck in pending external IP
LoadBalancer service no external IP assigned
MetalLB not assigning IP to service
root_cause: MetalLB fails to assign an external IP when: no IPAddressPool exists or matches the service's requested pool, all IPs in the pool are exhausted, the pool's autoAssign is false without the explicit annotation, or the controller pod is not running.
fix: Check controller logs: kubectl logs -n metallb-system -l component=controller | grep -i 'no available IPs\|exhausted\|no pool'
Verify IPAddressPool exists: kubectl get ipaddresspool -n metallb-system
Check pool utilization: kubectl get svc -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.spec.type=="LoadBalancer") | .status.loadBalancer.ingress'
If autoAssign:false, add annotation: kubectl annotate svc <name> metallb.universe.tf/address-pool=<pool-name>
Verify advertisement CR exists (L2Advertisement or BGPAdvertisement) — pool alone is not enough
Check controller is running: kubectl get pod -n metallb-system -l component=controller
Expand pool range: kubectl patch ipaddresspool production-pool -n metallb-system --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"addresses":["192.168.1.200-192.168.1.254"]}}'

symptoms: how many IPs to allocate MetalLB
root_cause: Address pool exhaustion occurs when all IPs in MetalLB's pools are assigned to Services. New LoadBalancer Services remain in Pending state. Proper pool sizing and monitoring prevents service disruption in production environments.
fix: Monitor pool usage: kubectl get svc -A --field-selector spec.type=LoadBalancer | wc -l
Alert on exhaustion: create PrometheusRule on metallb_allocator_addresses_in_use_total / metallb_allocator_addresses_total > 0.8
Use multiple pools with different priorities: spec.avoidBuggyIPs: true for VLAN environments
Reserve IPs: set autoAssign: false on overflow pools, use only via explicit annotation
Use IP sharing (spec.loadBalancerIP + metallb.universe.tf/allow-shared-ip annotation) for services sharing one VIP on different ports
Check metric: kubectl port-forward -n metallb-system svc/metallb-controller-metrics 7472 then curl localhost:7472/metrics | grep metallb_allocator
Plan at least 20% headroom above peak usage

symptoms: MetalLB IP sharing multiple services same IP
share LoadBalancer IP between services MetalLB
root_cause: MetalLB supports IP sharing where multiple Services share the same external IP, provided they use different port numbers. This conserves IP addresses when many Services need external access but on different ports.
fix: Annotate both services with the same sharing key: kubectl annotate svc svc-a metallb.universe.tf/allow-shared-ip=shared-key
kubectl annotate svc svc-b metallb.universe.tf/allow-shared-ip=shared-key
Set same spec.loadBalancerIP on both Services to pin the shared IP address
Services sharing an IP MUST use different port numbers — MetalLB enforces this
Both services must use the same IP protocol (IPv4 or IPv6), not mixed
Verify: kubectl get svc svc-a svc-b — both should show same EXTERNAL-IP
In BGP mode, shared-IP services still get one /32 route to the VIP; traffic is distributed by port at the node level

root_cause: BGP session failures between MetalLB speakers and upstream routers typically stem from ASN mismatches, firewall blocking TCP/179, incorrect router IP, or missing BGPPeer/BGPAdvertisement CRs. Systematic diagnosis narrows the cause quickly.
fix: Check BGPPeer status: kubectl get bgppeer -n metallb-system -o yaml | grep -A5 status
Check speaker logs for BGP errors: kubectl logs -n metallb-system -l component=speaker | grep -iE 'bgp|peer|session|error'
Verify TCP/179 reachability: kubectl exec -n metallb-system <speaker-pod> -- nc -zv <router-ip> 179
Confirm ASN values match router config: peerASN must match router's local ASN, myASN must match router's neighbor remote-as
In FRR mode: kubectl exec -n metallb-system <speaker-pod> -c frr -- vtysh -c 'show bgp summary'
Check router side: 'show bgp neighbors <node-ip>' or equivalent
Ensure speaker pods are not blocked by NetworkPolicy — BGP requires TCP/179 egress to router IP

symptoms: MetalLB L2 mode traffic not reaching service
MetalLB L2 speaker not responding to ARP
root_cause: In L2 mode, MetalLB speaker on the elected node must respond to ARP requests for the VIP. Failures occur when no speaker is elected, the elected node is unreachable on the L2 segment, or the VIP is not in the same L2 broadcast domain as clients.
fix: Find which node owns the VIP: kubectl get events -n metallb-system | grep -i 'announcing\|leader'
Check speaker election: kubectl logs -n metallb-system -l component=speaker | grep -i 'announcing\|elected\|leader'
Verify ARP on client network: arping -I eth0 <vip> from a host in the same L2 segment
Confirm speaker pod is running on the elected node: kubectl get pod -n metallb-system -l component=speaker -o wide
Check L2Advertisement references correct pool: kubectl get l2advertisement -n metallb-system -o yaml
Verify no firewall/iptables blocking gratuitous ARP on the node
Memberlist (UDP/7946) must be reachable between speaker pods for leader election: check NetworkPolicy and firewall rules

symptoms: MetalLB overlapping IP pool with node IPs
MetalLB ARP conflict with existing hosts
root_cause: Defining MetalLB IPAddressPool ranges that overlap with existing node IPs, router IPs, or DHCP ranges causes ARP conflicts, intermittent connectivity loss, and difficult-to-debug network issues because multiple hosts respond to ARP for the same IP.
fix: Always use dedicated, reserved IP ranges not used by DHCP or static assignments
Coordinate with network team to carve out a static IP block exclusively for MetalLB
Set avoidBuggyIPs: true in IPAddressPool to skip .0 and .255 addresses: spec.avoidBuggyIPs: true
Verify no conflicts before applying: nmap -sn 192.168.1.200-250 to check for live hosts in the planned range
After applying, monitor: watch -n1 'kubectl get svc -A | grep LoadBalancer' and test with arping
Document the MetalLB IP range in network IPAM system to prevent reuse
Consider using a VLAN dedicated to Kubernetes service IPs

root_cause: MetalLB speakers use memberlist (gossip protocol on UDP/7946) for cluster membership and L2 leader election. If speakers cannot communicate with each other on UDP/7946 due to firewall rules or NetworkPolicy, they crash or enter a split-brain state.
fix: Check speaker crashes: kubectl describe pod -n metallb-system -l component=speaker | grep -A5 'Last State\|Reason'
Look for memberlist errors: kubectl logs -n metallb-system <speaker-pod> | grep -i memberlist
Ensure UDP/7946 is open between all nodes: kubectl exec -n metallb-system <speaker-pod> -- nc -u -zv <other-node-ip> 7946
If using Calico/Cilium NetworkPolicy, add policy allowing UDP/7946 between speaker pods
Create secret for memberlist encryption if required: kubectl create secret generic -n metallb-system memberlist --from-literal=secretkey=$(openssl rand -base64 128)
Verify secret exists: kubectl get secret -n metallb-system memberlist
After fix, restart speakers: kubectl rollout restart ds -n metallb-system metallb-speaker

symptoms: MetalLB VIP failover takes too long
root_cause: L2 mode has inherent performance limitations: all traffic for a VIP flows through the single elected speaker node, creating a bandwidth bottleneck at that node's NIC. Failover requires ARP re-advertisement which depends on client ARP cache expiry (typically 30-60s on Linux).
fix: Accelerate failover: send gratuitous ARP — MetalLB does this automatically on speaker change, but some clients cache ARP longer
Reduce client ARP cache time: on Linux clients, sysctl -w net.ipv4.neigh.default.gc_stale_time=30
For high-throughput services, switch to BGP mode with ECMP to distribute load across all nodes
Spread VIPs across different speaker nodes using nodeSelectors in L2Advertisement to avoid all VIPs failing over to same node
Monitor bandwidth per node: use node_network_transmit_bytes_total in Prometheus to detect saturation
Consider Kube-VIP as alternative for control-plane HA; MetalLB for data-plane services
L2 mode failover time is ARP cache TTL dependent — BGP convergence is typically <1s with BFD

root_cause: MetalLB installation requires several steps beyond just deploying the operator: configuring strict ARP mode for kube-proxy (in L2 mode), creating the memberlist secret, defining IPAddressPool, and creating advertisement CRs. Missing any step causes silent failures.
fix: 1. If using kube-proxy in IPVS mode, enable strict ARP: kubectl edit configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system and set strictARP: true under ipvs section
2. Install via Helm: helm repo add metallb https://metallb.github.io/metallb && helm install metallb metallb/metallb -n metallb-system --create-namespace
3. Wait for pods ready: kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod -l app=metallb -n metallb-system --timeout=90s
4. Create IPAddressPool CR with your reserved IP range
5. Create L2Advertisement or BGPPeer + BGPAdvertisement CRs based on chosen mode
6. Test: kubectl create deploy test-nginx --image=nginx && kubectl expose deploy test-nginx --type=LoadBalancer --port=80
7. Verify: kubectl get svc test-nginx — EXTERNAL-IP should be assigned within ~10s
