root_cause: MetalLB v0.13+ uses admission webhooks to validate CRD configurations. Common validation errors include invalid ASN ranges (must be 1-4294967295), overlapping IP pools, invalid CIDR notation, or using v1beta1 BGPPeer API (which requires peerAddress as valid IP).
fix: Check webhook errors: kubectl apply -f metallb-config.yaml 2>&1 | grep -i 'admission\|invalid\|webhook'
Verify webhook is running: kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations | grep metallb
ASN valid range: private ASNs are 64512-65534 (2-byte) or 4200000000-4294967294 (4-byte)
Check for overlapping pools: each IP must belong to exactly one IPAddressPool
If webhook pod is down, CRD creation may fail: kubectl get pod -n metallb-system -l component=controller
Use correct API version: BGPPeer is metallb.io/v1beta2 (not v1beta1) in newer versions
Validate YAML before apply: kubectl apply --dry-run=server -f metallb-config.yaml

root_cause: MetalLB exposes Prometheus metrics from both controller and speaker pods. Without monitoring, IP exhaustion, BGP session drops, or speaker failures go undetected until services lose connectivity.
fix: MetalLB controller metrics port: 7472 (configurable); speaker metrics port: 7472
Key metrics: metallb_allocator_addresses_in_use_total, metallb_allocator_addresses_total, metallb_bgp_session_up, metallb_bgp_announced_prefixes_total, metallb_layer2_gratuitous_arp_total
Create ServiceMonitor: kubectl apply -f - with spec.selector matching metallb controller/speaker labels
Alert on BGP session down: expr: metallb_bgp_session_up == 0, severity: critical
Alert on pool exhaustion: expr: metallb_allocator_addresses_in_use_total / metallb_allocator_addresses_total > 0.9
Enable metrics via Helm: helm upgrade metallb metallb/metallb --set prometheus.serviceMonitor.enabled=true
Import Grafana dashboard ID 14127 for MetalLB visualization

symptoms: MetalLB v0.12 config format in newer version
root_cause: MetalLB v0.13 replaced the legacy ConfigMap-based configuration (metallb-config in metallb-system namespace) with CRD-based configuration (IPAddressPool, L2Advertisement, BGPPeer, BGPAdvertisement). Using the old ConfigMap in v0.13+ is silently ignored, leaving MetalLB unconfigured.
fix: Check if legacy configmap exists: kubectl get configmap metallb-config -n metallb-system — if present, it is being ignored in v0.13+
Migrate to CRDs: extract config from configmap and create equivalent IPAddressPool + Advertisement CRs
Delete legacy configmap after migration: kubectl delete configmap metallb-config -n metallb-system
Verify MetalLB version: kubectl get pod -n metallb-system -l component=controller -o jsonpath='{.items[0].spec.containers[0].image}'
Use metallb.universe.tf/v0.13 migration guide for converting config format
After migration, verify: kubectl get ipaddresspool,l2advertisement,bgppeer,bgpadvertisement -n metallb-system
Test service assignment: kubectl get svc -A | grep LoadBalancer — confirm EXTERNAL-IP is populated

symptoms: MetalLB not working with Cilium CNI
root_cause: When Cilium runs in kube-proxy replacement mode (kubeProxyReplacement: true), it handles LoadBalancer service traffic itself and may conflict with MetalLB's L2 ARP responses. Additionally, Cilium's eBPF datapath can interfere with MetalLB's ARP handling unless specific configuration is applied.
fix: Configure Cilium to not handle LoadBalancer IPs assigned by MetalLB: set --enable-l2-announcements=false in Cilium if using MetalLB for L2
Alternatively, use MetalLB BGP mode with Cilium — BGP mode doesn't conflict with Cilium eBPF
If using Cilium kube-proxy replacement, ensure externalTrafficPolicy: Cluster on services (or configure Cilium to handle Local properly)
Cilium + MetalLB L2 recommended config: cilium values: kubeProxyReplacement: true, l2announcements.enabled: false
Test connectivity: kubectl exec -it <test-pod> -- curl http://<metallb-vip>
Check Cilium is not also trying to announce the same IP: cilium service list | grep <vip>
Reference Cilium docs for MetalLB interoperability before deployment

symptoms: how does kube-vip provide control plane HA
control plane high availability with kube-vip
root_cause: Kube-VIP provides a virtual IP (VIP) for the Kubernetes control plane by electing one node as the leader that holds the VIP. All API server traffic is directed to this VIP, and if the leader fails, another node takes over the VIP within seconds, ensuring continuous API server availability.
fix: Deploy kube-vip as a static pod on each control plane node via /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-vip.yaml
Set env KUBE_VIP_VIP=<your-vip-ip> to specify the virtual IP address
Set env cp_enable=true to enable control plane VIP mode
Set env vip_interface=eth0 (or your actual interface) for the VIP to bind to
Verify VIP is active: ping <vip-ip> and curl -k https://<vip-ip>:6443/healthz
Check leader election: kubectl get leases -n kube-system | grep kube-vip

symptoms: how to configure kube-vip ARP mode
root_cause: Kube-VIP supports ARP (Layer 2) mode where the elected leader broadcasts gratuitous ARP announcements to claim the VIP MAC address on the local network. This mode requires all control plane nodes to be on the same L2 segment and is simpler to configure than BGP mode.
fix: Set env vip_arp=true in the kube-vip static pod manifest to enable ARP mode
Set env vip_leaderelection=true to enable Kubernetes lease-based leader election
Ensure all control plane nodes share the same L2 broadcast domain for ARP to work
Generate the manifest: docker run --network host --rm ghcr.io/kube-vip/kube-vip:v0.7.2 manifest pod --interface eth0 --address <vip> --arp --controlplane | tee /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-vip.yaml
Verify ARP announcements with: tcpdump -i eth0 arp host <vip-ip> on any control plane node
Check VIP owner node: kubectl get lease kube-vip-<node> -n kube-system -o yaml

symptoms: how to configure kube-vip BGP mode
root_cause: Kube-VIP BGP mode advertises the VIP as a /32 route to BGP peers (typically ToR switches). Unlike ARP mode, BGP works across L3 boundaries and supports ECMP for multi-path routing. The leader announces the VIP prefix and withdraws it on failover.
fix: Set env bgp_enable=true and cp_enable=true in the kube-vip manifest
Set env bgp_routerinterface=eth0, bgp_as=<your-ASN>, bgp_peeraddress=<router-ip>, bgp_peerpass=<password>
Set env bgp_peeras=<router-ASN> for the upstream peer ASN

Generate manifest: docker run --rm ghcr.io/kube-vip/kube-vip:v0.7.2 manifest pod --interface eth0 --address <vip> --bgp --localAS 65000 --bgpRouterID <node-ip> --bgppeers <router-ip:65001::false> | tee /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-vip.yaml
Verify BGP session: kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-vip-<node> | grep -i bgp
Confirm route advertisement: show ip bgp <vip>/32 on your router

symptoms: kube-vip static pod vs daemonset which to use
root_cause: Static pod deployment is required for control plane VIP because the pod must start before the API server is available (chicken-and-egg). DaemonSet deployment is used for kube-vip in service LoadBalancer mode since it needs the API server running to watch Service objects. Mixing these roles requires careful configuration.
fix: For control plane VIP: place kube-vip manifest in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ as a static pod on each control plane node

For service LoadBalancer mode: deploy kube-vip-cloud-provider as a DaemonSet or Deployment via kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kube-vip/kube-vip-cloud-provider/main/manifest/kube-vip-cloud-controller.yaml
Static pod manifest uses hostNetwork: true and mounts /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf for in-cluster access
DaemonSet uses serviceAccountName with RBAC permissions to watch Service and Endpoints objects
Set env svc_enable=true in kube-vip DaemonSet to enable service LB mode
Validate static pod is running: crictl ps | grep kube-vip on any control plane node

symptoms: assign external IP to kubernetes services with kube-vip
root_cause: Kube-VIP can act as a service LoadBalancer controller (similar to MetalLB) by watching Service objects of type LoadBalancer and assigning VIPs from a configured IP pool. This removes the need for MetalLB when kube-vip is already used for control plane HA.
fix: Deploy kube-vip-cloud-provider: kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kube-vip/kube-vip-cloud-provider/main/manifest/kube-vip-cloud-controller.yaml
Create IP pool ConfigMap: kubectl create configmap -n kube-system kubevip --from-literal range-global=192.168.1.200-192.168.1.220
Enable service mode in kube-vip DaemonSet by setting env svc_enable=true
Create a LoadBalancer service: kubectl expose deployment myapp --type=LoadBalancer --port=80
Check assigned IP: kubectl get svc myapp -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}'
Alternatively annotate service: kubectl annotate svc myapp kube-vip.io/loadbalancerIPs=192.168.1.201 for specific IP assignment

symptoms: kubeadm with virtual IP for control plane
root_cause: When using kubeadm, kube-vip must be installed as a static pod before kubeadm init is run. The controlPlaneEndpoint in kubeadm configuration must point to the kube-vip VIP and port 6443, ensuring all subsequent control plane nodes join via the VIP.
fix: Before kubeadm init, create the kube-vip static pod: mkdir -p /etc/kubernetes/manifests && docker run --network host --rm ghcr.io/kube-vip/kube-vip:v0.7.2 manifest pod --interface eth0 --address <vip> --arp --controlplane --leaderElection | tee /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-vip.yaml
In kubeadm-config.yaml set: controlPlaneEndpoint: '<vip>:6443'
Run: kubeadm init --config kubeadm-config.yaml --upload-certs
On additional control plane nodes, copy /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-vip.yaml before running kubeadm join
Join command: kubeadm join <vip>:6443 --token <token> --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<hash> --control-plane --certificate-key <cert-key>
Verify: kubectl get nodes and curl -k https://<vip>:6443/healthz

symptoms: RKE2 control plane VIP with kube-vip
root_cause: RKE2 does not use kubeadm and manages its own control plane components. Kube-vip must be deployed as a static pod by placing the manifest in /var/lib/rancher/rke2/agent/pod-manifests/ and configuring RKE2's tls-san to include the VIP so TLS certificates are valid for the VIP address.
fix: Create manifest directory: mkdir -p /var/lib/rancher/rke2/agent/pod-manifests/

Generate kube-vip manifest using the RKE2 kubeconfig path: KVVERSION=v0.7.2 && docker run --network host --rm ghcr.io/kube-vip/kube-vip:${KVVERSION} manifest pod --interface eth0 --address <vip> --arp --controlplane --leaderElection | tee /var/lib/rancher/rke2/agent/pod-manifests/kube-vip.yaml
In /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml add: tls-san: ['<vip>'] and set server: https://<vip>:9345 for additional server nodes
Set KUBECONFIG=/etc/rancher/rke2/rke2.yaml in the kube-vip manifest instead of /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf
Start RKE2 server: systemctl enable --now rke2-server
Validate: /var/lib/rancher/rke2/bin/kubectl --kubeconfig /etc/rancher/rke2/rke2.yaml get nodes

root_cause: Talos Linux manages all system components declaratively via machine config. Kube-vip on Talos is deployed as a static pod by injecting the pod manifest via Talos machine configuration extraManifests or by using talosctl to apply the static pod config. The VIP must also be listed in the API server's SANs.
fix: Generate the kube-vip manifest: docker run --rm ghcr.io/kube-vip/kube-vip:v0.7.2 manifest pod --interface eth0 --address <vip> --arp --controlplane --leaderElection > kube-vip.yaml
In Talos machine config controlplane.yaml, add under machine.files: [{content: '<kube-vip-yaml-content>', path: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-vip.yaml, op: create}]
Set cluster.controlPlane.endpoint: https://<vip>:6443 in Talos cluster config
Add VIP to certSANs: cluster.apiServer.certSANs: ['<vip>']
Apply config: talosctl apply-config --insecure --nodes <node-ip> --file controlplane.yaml
Bootstrap: talosctl bootstrap --nodes <first-node-ip> && talosctl kubeconfig --nodes <vip>

root_cause: Kube-VIP uses Kubernetes Lease objects for leader election when vip_leaderelection=true. The election timing parameters (leaseDuration, renewDeadline, retryPeriod) determine how quickly VIP failover occurs. Default values may cause delayed failover or excessive churn in unstable networks.
fix: Set env vip_leaseduration=5 (seconds) to shorten the lease duration for faster failover (default is 15s)
Set env vip_renewdeadline=3 (seconds) for how long the leader retries renewing before giving up (default 10s)
Set env vip_retryperiod=1 (seconds) for how often non-leaders try to acquire the lease (default 2s)
For production stability, use: vip_leaseduration=15, vip_renewdeadline=10, vip_retryperiod=2 (defaults)
For faster failover trade-off: vip_leaseduration=6, vip_renewdeadline=4, vip_retryperiod=1
Monitor lease transitions: kubectl get events -n kube-system --field-selector reason=LeaderElection

root_cause: Kube-VIP issues commonly stem from the static pod not starting due to missing kubeconfig, incorrect interface name, VIP already in use, or RBAC permissions when using lease-based leader election. Systematic log inspection and network validation identify root cause.
fix: Check if kube-vip static pod is running: crictl ps --name kube-vip on control plane node
View kube-vip logs: crictl logs $(crictl ps --name kube-vip -q) or kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-vip-<nodename>
Verify VIP interface exists: ip link show eth0 (replace with your interface)
Check if VIP is assigned on leader: ip addr show dev eth0 | grep <vip-ip>
Test VIP reachability: ping <vip> && curl -k https://<vip>:6443/healthz
Verify lease exists: kubectl get lease -n kube-system | grep kube-vip
Check for IP conflicts: arping -I eth0 <vip-ip> to detect duplicate IP responses
Inspect events: kubectl describe pod kube-vip-<node> -n kube-system

root_cause: VIP flapping occurs when multiple nodes compete for leadership due to network latency causing lease renewals to fail, resulting in multiple nodes believing they should hold the VIP simultaneously. This leads to ARP conflicts and unstable connectivity.
fix: Increase vip_leaseduration to 30s and vip_renewdeadline to 20s to tolerate network jitter
Check system time synchronization on all nodes: chronyc tracking or timedatectl status
Ensure NTP is configured: systemctl status chronyd or systemctl status systemd-timesyncd
Verify etcd health as lease objects are stored in etcd: etcdctl endpoint health --cluster
Check for packet loss between control plane nodes: ping -c 100 <peer-node-ip> | tail -2
Set vip_nodeName env var to enforce node preference for deterministic initial election
Monitor with: kubectl get events -n kube-system -w | grep -i 'kube-vip\|lease'

symptoms: kube-vip error dialing backend connection refused
kube-vip cannot connect to API server
root_cause: Kube-VIP static pod attempts to connect to the Kubernetes API server for leader election but fails because the kubeconfig file path is wrong or the API server is not yet healthy. This is a common bootstrapping chicken-and-egg issue during initial cluster creation.
fix: Verify kubeconfig mount in kube-vip static pod: check volumes section for /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf (kubeadm) or /etc/rancher/rke2/rke2.yaml (RKE2)
Ensure the KUBECONFIG env var matches the mounted path inside the container
For kubeadm clusters, the admin.conf is created after kubeadm init completes — kube-vip will retry automatically
Set env KUBE_VIP_LOG_LEVEL=5 for verbose logging to see exact connection errors
If using in-cluster config, ensure the service account token is properly mounted: check serviceAccountName in the pod spec
For Talos, verify the kubeconfig secret is accessible: talosctl kubeconfig -n <node>
Test manually: kubectl --kubeconfig /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf get lease -n kube-system

symptoms: kube-vip control plane load balancing best practices
how to load balance API server with kube-vip
kube-vip vs haproxy for control plane LB
root_cause: Kube-VIP provides VIP failover but does not load balance across multiple API servers — it always directs traffic to a single leader node. For true load balancing across all control plane nodes, kube-vip can be combined with a local load balancer or used alongside the built-in kube-vip load balancer mode.
fix: Enable kube-vip load balancer mode by setting env lb_enable=true and lb_port=6443 in the static pod manifest
Set env lb_fwdmethod=masquerade for IPVS-based load balancing across all control plane nodes
Configure backend ports: env lb_backendport=6443
List backend nodes: env lb_backends=<node1-ip>:<port>,<node2-ip>:<port>,<node3-ip>:<port>
For truly active-active load balancing, pair kube-vip VIP with kube-proxy or IPVS rules on each node
Health check the API servers: set env lb_healthcheck=true to remove unhealthy backends automatically
Verify LB is distributing: use curl -k https://<vip>:6443/healthz multiple times and check API server access logs

symptoms: kube-vip and metallb at the same time
running kube-vip service lb and metallb together
root_cause: Running both kube-vip service LB mode and MetalLB simultaneously causes both controllers to compete for Service objects of type LoadBalancer. Both may attempt to assign IPs from overlapping pools, resulting in duplicate IP assignments, ARP conflicts, and service instability.
fix: Choose one: either use kube-vip for service LB OR use MetalLB — not both simultaneously
If migrating from MetalLB to kube-vip: kubectl delete -f metallb manifests first, then deploy kube-vip-cloud-provider
If keeping MetalLB for services, disable kube-vip service mode: set svc_enable=false in kube-vip DaemonSet
Use separate IP pools to avoid conflicts if both must coexist: annotate services with metallb.universe.tf/address-pool or kube-vip.io/loadbalancerIPs
Verify no duplicate IP assignments: kubectl get svc -A | grep LoadBalancer | sort -k5
Check for ARP conflicts: arping -D -I eth0 <ip> should return no duplicates

root_cause: Deploying kube-vip without verifying prerequisites leads to failed deployments, VIP conflicts, or ARP/BGP issues. A systematic checklist prevents common setup mistakes.
fix: Verify VIP is not already in use: ping <vip> should be unreachable before deployment
Confirm network interface name is consistent across all nodes: ip link show | grep -E '^[0-9]+'
Ensure correct RBAC exists for lease management: kubectl get clusterrole system:kube-vip-role or create it
For ARP mode: confirm all control plane nodes are on the same L2 subnet
For BGP mode: confirm BGP peering IPs, ASNs, and passwords match router configuration
Verify container runtime is running: systemctl status containerd or systemctl status crio
Pull the kube-vip image on all nodes: crictl pull ghcr.io/kube-vip/kube-vip:v0.7.2
Confirm static pod directory exists: ls /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ on each control plane node

symptoms: VIP takeover delay after control plane node failure
root_cause: VIP failover time is bounded by the lease duration setting. With default values (leaseDuration=15s, renewDeadline=10s), a worst-case failover takes up to 15 seconds. Network validation that the failed node truly stopped can add additional delay before ARP announcements propagate.
fix: Reduce lease duration: set vip_leaseduration=6, vip_renewdeadline=4, vip_retryperiod=1 for ~6s failover
Ensure gratuitous ARP is sent immediately on leader election: vip_gratuitousarp=true (enabled by default in ARP mode)
For BGP mode, tune BGP hold timer on peers to match: set BGP keepalive/holdtime lower (e.g., 3s/9s)
Monitor failover time: time (while ! curl -sk https://<vip>:6443/healthz | grep ok; do sleep 0.1; done) during a node failover test
Use vip_gratuitousarpcount=3 to send multiple gratuitous ARPs reducing switch MAC table update delay
Test failover: kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets && observe VIP movement time

symptoms: kube-vip ARP not working gratuitous ARP not received
VIP not reachable after failover ARP
switch not updating MAC table for kube-vip VIP
root_cause: After a VIP failover in ARP mode, the new leader sends gratuitous ARP announcements, but some network switches may not update their MAC address tables immediately or may have ARP suppression enabled (common in VXLAN/EVPN environments), causing the VIP to remain unreachable.
fix: Increase gratuitous ARP count: set env vip_gratuitousarpcount=5 to send more announcements on election
Set vip_gratuitousarpinterval=1000 (milliseconds) to space out ARP announcements
Check if switch has ARP suppression enabled — disable it for the VLAN containing control plane nodes
For EVPN/VXLAN environments, consider using BGP mode instead of ARP to avoid L2 limitations
Force a switch MAC table flush: on Cisco: clear mac address-table dynamic interface <port> or similar on your switch
Test with arping: arping -U -I eth0 <vip> to send unsolicited ARP requests from the new leader node
Verify ARP sent by kube-vip: tcpdump -i eth0 -nn 'arp and host <vip>' on the new leader node

symptoms: kube-vip RBAC error cannot get leases
root_cause: When using Kubernetes lease-based leader election (vip_leaderelection=true), kube-vip requires RBAC permissions to create, get, update, and watch Lease objects in the kube-system namespace. Missing RBAC causes leader election to fail completely.
fix: Create the required RBAC resources: kubectl apply -f https://kube-vip.io/manifests/rbac.yaml
Or create manually: ClusterRole with rules for coordination.k8s.io/leases (verbs: get, watch, list, create, update)
Create ClusterRoleBinding binding the ClusterRole to ServiceAccount kube-vip in kube-system
Verify permissions: kubectl auth can-i create leases -n kube-system --as=system:serviceaccount:kube-system:kube-vip
Check current RBAC: kubectl get clusterrolebinding | grep kube-vip
For static pods using host-path kubeconfig (not service account), RBAC is less critical — use in-cluster=false and mount /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf instead

root_cause: Kube-VIP releases track Kubernetes API changes, particularly around the coordination.k8s.io/v1 Lease API used for leader election. Using mismatched versions can cause leader election failures or missing features like improved BGP support introduced in later kube-vip versions.
fix: Check latest kube-vip version: curl -sL https://api.github.com/repos/kube-vip/kube-vip/releases/latest | jq -r .tag_name
For Kubernetes 1.24+: use kube-vip v0.5.0+ which supports coordination.k8s.io/v1 Leases
For BGP improvements (VRF, ECMP): use kube-vip v0.6.0+
For service LB improvements: use kube-vip-cloud-provider v0.0.6+
Update static pod by regenerating manifest with new image tag: ghcr.io/kube-vip/kube-vip:v0.7.2
Rolling update control plane kube-vip: update /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-vip.yaml on each node sequentially and verify VIP stability between updates
Release notes: https://github.com/kube-vip/kube-vip/releases

symptoms: kube-vip BGP peer down log message
root_cause: Understanding kube-vip log patterns helps quickly diagnose operational issues. Key log messages indicate leader election state changes, VIP assignment events, ARP announcements, BGP session state, and error conditions that require intervention.
fix: Leader acquired log: 'acquired lease kube-system/kube-vip' — VIP is now active on this node; expected during normal failover
Leader lost log: 'lost lease kube-system/kube-vip' — this node released the VIP; check if intentional
ARP announcement: 'sending ARP announcements' — normal operation, VIP is being claimed via ARP
BGP peer down: 'BGP session to <ip> went down' — check BGP peering config, router availability, and ASN settings
VIP bind failed: 'error binding to interface' — interface name mismatch or permissions issue; verify interface exists
Enable debug logging: set env KUBE_VIP_LOG_LEVEL=5 to get detailed election and network operation logs
Tail logs continuously: kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-vip-<node> -f --since=5m | grep -E 'ERROR|WARN|acquired|lost|BGP'

symptoms: how does Cilium eBPF datapath replace iptables
how cilium handles pod networking without iptables
root_cause: Cilium replaces the traditional iptables/netfilter datapath with eBPF programs attached to network interfaces, enabling in-kernel packet processing at line rate. Each node runs the cilium-agent which compiles and loads BPF programs into the kernel, maintaining BPF maps for endpoint, policy, and NAT state without touching iptables.
fix: Set kubeProxyReplacement: strict in Cilium Helm values to fully replace kube-proxy
helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --set kubeProxyReplacement=strict --set k8sServiceHost=<API_SERVER_IP> --set k8sServicePort=6443
Verify with: cilium status | grep KubeProxyReplacement
Check BPF programs loaded: cilium bpf endpoint list
Validate service handling: cilium service list
Ensure kube-proxy DaemonSet is deleted or never deployed when using strict mode

symptoms: CiliumNetworkPolicy not enforcing L7 HTTP rules
cilium HTTP policy not blocking requests
layer 7 policy has no effect cilium
root_cause: L7 HTTP policies in Cilium require the Envoy proxy sidecar or the Cilium node-local Envoy listener to be enabled. If envoy is not running or the policy lacks the correct toPorts with rules.http specification, L7 filtering is silently skipped and traffic passes as if no L7 policy exists.
fix: Ensure Envoy is enabled: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --set envoy.enabled=true
Verify Envoy DaemonSet is running: kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/name=cilium-envoy
Confirm L7 rule syntax in CiliumNetworkPolicy uses spec.egress[].toPorts[].rules.http[] with method and path fields
Check cilium endpoint list and look for 'policy-verdict' entries: cilium monitor --type policy-verdict
Inspect Envoy listener config: kubectl -n kube-system exec -it <cilium-pod> -- cilium envoy-config list
Use cilium policy get to verify the policy was accepted and parsed correctly

symptoms: how to design CiliumNetworkPolicy for microservices
best practice cilium network policy L3 L4
securing pod communication with cilium identity
root_cause: Cilium enforces security using identity labels derived from Kubernetes pod labels rather than IP addresses, enabling policy that survives pod restarts and scaling. Designing policies around stable label selectors and explicit ingress/egress rules prevents unintended connectivity.
fix: Always pair an ingress CiliumNetworkPolicy on the receiver with an egress policy on the sender for zero-trust enforcement
Use endpointSelector with matchLabels to select pods by app and tier labels rather than podSelector IP blocks
Define explicit toEndpoints/fromEndpoints using label selectors instead of toCIDR for intra-cluster traffic
For external egress, use toFQDNs with matchName or matchPattern to allow DNS-resolved destinations
Deny all by default: deploy a global CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy with empty ingress/egress then add allow rules
Validate with: cilium policy trace --src-endpoint <id> --dst-endpoint <id> --dport 5432/TCP

root_cause: cilium status aggregates health checks from the local agent, including BPF map state, endpoint reachability, Kubernetes connectivity, and cluster mesh status. Failures often stem from BPF compilation errors, map exhaustion, API server reachability issues, or misconfigured CNI configuration files.
fix: Run: cilium status --verbose to see per-component status with error details
Check agent logs: kubectl -n kube-system logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=cilium --tail=200
Verify CNI config is correct: cat /etc/cni/net.d/05-cilium.conflist
Run full connectivity test: cilium connectivity test --test-namespace cilium-test
Check BPF map health: cilium bpf map list and look for errors or 'max entries' warnings
Validate endpoint health: cilium endpoint list and check for 'not-ready' state
Look for kernel version compatibility: uname -r (Cilium 1.14+ requires kernel 5.4+)

symptoms: cilium dropping packets due to map full
root_cause: Cilium BPF maps (CT table, NAT table, endpoints, policy) have fixed pre-allocated sizes. In high-throughput or high-connection-count environments, the connection tracking (CT) and NAT tables fill up, causing packet drops. Default sizes are tuned for modest workloads and must be scaled for production.
fix: Check current map utilization: cilium bpf ct list global | wc -l vs the configured max
Increase CT table size: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --set bpf.ctTcpMax=524288 --set bpf.ctAnyMax=262144
Increase NAT table size: --set bpf.natMax=524288
Increase policy map size: --set bpf.policyMapMax=65536
Monitor map pressure with Prometheus metric: cilium_bpf_map_ops_total and cilium_drop_count_total{reason='CT: Map insertion failed'}
Enable BPF map events in Grafana dashboard ID 16611 (Cilium Agent) to track utilization over time
After resizing, restart cilium-agent pods: kubectl -n kube-system rollout restart daemonset/cilium

symptoms: how to enable Hubble observability in cilium
how to view pod network flows with hubble
root_cause: Hubble is Cilium's built-in observability layer, providing flow visibility via the Hubble API and UI. It must be explicitly enabled, and the Hubble Relay component is needed for multi-node aggregated views from the CLI and UI.
fix: Enable Hubble: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --set hubble.enabled=true --set hubble.relay.enabled=true --set hubble.ui.enabled=true
Wait for rollout: kubectl -n kube-system rollout status daemonset/cilium
Install Hubble CLI: curl -L --remote-name-all https://github.com/cilium/hubble/releases/latest/download/hubble-linux-amd64.tar.gz && tar xzf hubble-linux-amd64.tar.gz && mv hubble /usr/local/bin/
Port-forward Hubble Relay: kubectl -n kube-system port-forward svc/hubble-relay 4245:80 &
Observe flows: hubble observe --follow --namespace my-app
Filter by verdict: hubble observe --verdict DROPPED --last 50
Access Hubble UI: kubectl -n kube-system port-forward svc/hubble-ui 12000:80

symptoms: pods cannot reach other nodes in native routing mode
cilium direct routing mode connectivity failure
root_cause: In native routing mode, Cilium delegates packet forwarding to the underlying network fabric instead of encapsulating in VXLAN/Geneve. This requires the infrastructure routing table to know about pod CIDRs on each node. If the router or cloud VPC does not have routes for pod subnets, inter-node pod traffic is silently dropped.
fix: Set routing mode: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --set routingMode=native --set ipv4NativeRoutingCIDR=10.0.0.0/8
Verify native routing is active: cilium status | grep 'Routing:'
For bare-metal, ensure BGP or static routes are configured: cilium bgpcontrolplane.enabled=true with CiliumBGPPeeringPolicy CRD
Check node routes: ip route show table all | grep <pod-cidr>
Validate inter-node reachability: cilium connectivity test --test=pod-to-pod-no-policy
If using MetalLB for BGP, ensure pod CIDR advertisement is configured alongside service IPs
Fall back to tunnel mode if routing is not configurable: --set routingMode=tunnel --set tunnelProtocol=vxlan

symptoms: how to set up cilium cluster mesh
connect multiple kubernetes clusters with cilium
root_cause: Cilium Cluster Mesh enables cross-cluster pod connectivity and global service load balancing by sharing identity and endpoint state between clusters via a shared KV store (etcd). Each cluster must have a unique cluster name and cluster ID, and the clustermesh-apiserver must be reachable between clusters.
fix: Assign unique cluster IDs: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --set cluster.name=cluster1 --set cluster.id=1
Enable cluster mesh: --set clustermesh.useAPIServer=true --set clustermesh.apiserver.service.type=LoadBalancer
Connect clusters using cilium CLI: cilium clustermesh connect --destination-context cluster2-context
Verify mesh status: cilium clustermesh status --wait
Annotate services for global load balancing: kubectl annotate svc my-service service.cilium.io/global='true'
Check cross-cluster endpoint sync: cilium endpoint list on each cluster and verify remote endpoints appear
Test cross-cluster DNS: use clustermesh-aware DNS with <svc>.<ns>.svc.<cluster>.local format

symptoms: using both cilium and kube-proxy causes conflicts
iptables rules conflicting with cilium BPF
root_cause: Running kube-proxy alongside Cilium in strict kube-proxy replacement mode creates conflicting NAT and packet mangling rules. iptables rules from kube-proxy compete with BPF programs, leading to double-NAT, asymmetric routing, or intermittent connection drops. Cilium's BPF programs assume exclusive ownership of the datapath.
fix: Do not deploy kube-proxy when using Cilium kubeProxyReplacement=strict
If migrating, first set kubeProxyReplacement=partial to handle only specific features, then remove kube-proxy
Delete kube-proxy DaemonSet: kubectl -n kube-system delete daemonset kube-proxy
Clean up residual iptables rules: iptables-save | grep -v KUBE | iptables-restore (with caution on production)
Verify no iptables KUBE-* chains remain: iptables -t nat -L | grep KUBE
Run connectivity tests after removal: cilium connectivity test

symptoms: cilium-test pod cannot reach echo server
root_cause: The cilium connectivity test deploys a set of client and echo-server pods and runs dozens of connectivity scenarios. Failures typically point to specific policy enforcement issues, BPF program loading failures, ICMP/UDP blocking, masquerading misconfiguration, or MTU mismatches between overlay and host networks.
fix: Run with verbose output: cilium connectivity test --test-namespace cilium-test --all-flows 2>&1 | tee /tmp/ct-results.txt
Identify failing test by name in output, e.g., 'no-unexpected-packet-loss', 'pod-to-service-nodeport'
Check for MTU issues: cilium status | grep MTU, then compare with node interface MTU: ip link show eth0
For VXLAN, host MTU must be >= pod MTU + 50 bytes overhead; set --set MTU=1450 if needed
Check masquerade: cilium bpf nat list and verify SNAT entries exist for external traffic
Capture flows during test: hubble observe --follow --namespace cilium-test --verdict DROPPED
For nodeport failures, verify nodePortMode: kubectl -n kube-system exec -it <cilium-pod> -- cilium service list | grep NodePort

root_cause: Cilium upgrades require careful sequencing because BPF programs, CRDs, and the cilium-agent binary must be updated atomically to avoid datapath inconsistencies. Rolling upgrades without following the recommended order can leave nodes with mixed BPF program versions causing policy enforcement gaps.
fix: Read the upgrade notes for the target version: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/operations/upgrade/
Pre-check: ensure all cilium pods are healthy before upgrade: cilium status --all-nodes
Update CRDs first: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --version <new> --set upgradeCompatibility=<old-minor>
Verify CRD update: kubectl get crds | grep cilium.io | awk '{print $1}' | xargs kubectl get crd -o jsonpath='{.spec.versions[*].name}'
Monitor rolling restart of DaemonSet: kubectl -n kube-system rollout status daemonset/cilium --timeout=10m
Run post-upgrade connectivity test: cilium connectivity test
Check for deprecated config keys: cilium-dbg config --help and compare with new defaults
Validate Hubble flows are still visible: hubble observe --last 20

symptoms: cilium DNS policy blocking external DNS lookups
root_cause: Cilium's FQDN-based egress policy (toFQDNs) intercepts DNS responses to build IP allow lists dynamically. If the cilium-agent DNS proxy is not intercepting queries (wrong DNS port, DNS over TCP not handled, or high-churn short-TTL records expiring between refresh cycles), the BPF NAT/policy maps do not contain the resolved IPs, causing connection drops.
fix: Verify DNS proxy is active: cilium status | grep 'DNS Proxy'
Enable DNS proxy explicitly: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --set dnsProxy.enableTransparentMode=true
Check which DNS port is being intercepted: cilium bpf proxy list
For short-TTL records, increase minimum TTL: --set dnsProxy.minTtl=60
Monitor DNS resolutions: cilium fqdn cache list and cilium monitor --type l7 | grep DNS
Ensure egress policy references exact hostnames as returned by DNS, not CNAMEs: use matchPattern for wildcard
Test resolution: kubectl exec -it <pod> -- nslookup api.openai.com and verify IP matches cilium fqdn cache

symptoms: how to configure cilium egress gateway
route pod traffic through dedicated egress node cilium
cilium egress gateway for external API calls
root_cause: Cilium Egress Gateway allows specific pods to exit the cluster through a designated node with a stable IP, enabling allowlisting at external firewalls. This is especially useful when calling external AI APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) that require source IP allowlisting.
fix: Enable egress gateway: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --set egressGateway.enabled=true
Label the egress node: kubectl label node <egress-node> egress-gateway=true
Create CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy resource specifying selectors and egressIP

Example policy spec: selectors: [{podSelector: {matchLabels: {app: ai-worker}}}], destinationCIDRs: ['0.0.0.0/0'], egressGateway: {nodeSelector: {matchLabels: {egress-gateway: 'true'}}, egressIP: '10.0.1.50'}
Assign the egressIP to the egress node interface: ip addr add 10.0.1.50/24 dev eth0 (or use MetalLB to manage it)
Verify gateway policy: kubectl get ciliumeressgatewayPolicy -o yaml
Test by checking source IP from pod: kubectl exec -it <ai-worker-pod> -- curl https://api.openai.com/v1/models -H 'Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY' and inspect source IP via egress node tcpdump

symptoms: how to inspect cilium identity for a pod
cilium endpoint identity not matching policy
root_cause: Cilium assigns each endpoint a numeric security identity derived from its label set. Policy enforcement operates on these identities. If labels are incorrect, missing, or there is a label hash collision, policies may not apply as expected. Verifying endpoint identity is the first diagnostic step for policy mismatch.
fix: List all endpoints and their identities: cilium endpoint list
Inspect a specific endpoint: cilium endpoint get <endpoint-id>
Check the identity labels: cilium identity get <identity-id>
Verify what the policy expects: cilium policy get and compare endpointSelector labels
For namespace isolation, confirm namespace labels are present: kubectl get ns <ns> --show-labels
Trace policy enforcement: cilium policy trace -s <src-endpoint-id> -d <dst-endpoint-id> --dport 443/TCP
Monitor live policy verdicts: cilium monitor --type policy-verdict | grep -E 'DENIED|ALLOWED'

symptoms: using CIDR-based NetworkPolicy instead of cilium identity
ip-based policy breaks when pods reschedule
root_cause: Using IP-based (toCIDR/fromCIDR) rules for intra-cluster traffic defeats Cilium's identity-based security model. Pod IPs change on reschedule, making CIDR policies fragile. Overly broad CIDR blocks can inadvertently allow traffic from unintended pods, violating least-privilege principles.
fix: Replace toCIDR/fromCIDR intra-cluster rules with toEndpoints/fromEndpoints using label selectors
Reserve toCIDR only for external (outside cluster) destinations like database servers or storage endpoints
Use toEntities: ['kube-apiserver'] instead of hardcoding the API server IP in toCIDR
Use toEntities: ['world'] for general internet egress and narrow with toFQDNs for specific services
Audit existing policies: kubectl get ciliumnetworkpolicy -A -o json | jq '.items[].spec | .. | .toCIDR? // empty'
Test replacement: cilium policy trace to validate new label-based rules match intended traffic

symptoms: cilium tunnel mode VXLAN high CPU usage
root_cause: VXLAN encapsulation adds per-packet CPU overhead and reduces effective MTU by 50 bytes. In high-throughput environments, this overhead accumulates significantly. Geneve offers similar overhead but with extensible metadata headers. Native routing mode eliminates encapsulation entirely but requires compatible network infrastructure.
fix: Switch to Geneve if hardware offload is available: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --set tunnelProtocol=geneve
Enable tunnel offload if supported by NIC: --set enableBPFTProxy=true
Measure baseline: iperf3 between pods in VXLAN mode to capture throughput
Switch to native routing if network supports it: --set routingMode=native --set ipv4NativeRoutingCIDR=<pod-cidr>
Tune MTU explicitly: --set MTU=1450 for VXLAN over 1500 MTU links
Enable XDP acceleration if supported: --set bpf.masquerade=true --set loadBalancer.acceleration=native
Monitor CPU impact: prometheus metric cilium_bpf_syscall_duration_seconds and process_cpu_seconds_total for cilium-agent

symptoms: cilium agent log 'map capacity will be exhausted'
cilium bpf map full warning in logs
cilium drops with reason 'BPF map operation failed'
root_cause: Cilium logs capacity warnings when BPF maps approach their configured maximum entries. The log message typically includes the map name, current count, and threshold. If maps fill completely, subsequent insertions fail silently causing packet drops, which appear in drop metrics and Hubble flows with reason 'map insertion failed'.
fix: Search for map pressure logs: kubectl -n kube-system logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=cilium | grep -i 'capacity\|map full\|map exhausted'
Identify which map is pressured from the log entry (e.g., cilium_ct4_global, cilium_nat4, cilium_policy)
Check current utilization: cilium bpf ct list global | wc -l
Increase specific map: for CT table use --set bpf.ctTcpMax=524288, for NAT use --set bpf.natMax=524288
Set up Prometheus alert on: cilium_drop_count_total{reason='CT: Map insertion failed'} > 0
After config change, perform rolling restart: kubectl -n kube-system rollout restart daemonset/cilium
Verify with: cilium bpf map list and check 'current' vs 'max' entries column

symptoms: how to structure cilium helm values for production
root_cause: Production Cilium deployments on bare-metal Kubernetes require explicit configuration of several defaults that are optimized for cloud environments. Missing settings lead to suboptimal performance, insufficient observability, or insecure defaults.
fix: Set kubeProxyReplacement: strict and provide k8sServiceHost/Port explicitly
Enable Hubble with relay and UI: hubble.enabled=true, hubble.relay.enabled=true
Set routingMode based on infrastructure: native for BGP-capable networks, tunnel for layer-2 only
Configure BPF map sizes above defaults: bpf.ctTcpMax=262144, bpf.natMax=262144 minimum
Enable Prometheus metrics: prometheus.enabled=true, operator.prometheus.enabled=true
Set operator.replicas=2 for high availability of the Cilium operator
Pin resource requests/limits: resources.requests.cpu=100m, resources.requests.memory=512Mi for agent pods
Enable IPAM mode: ipam.mode=kubernetes for kubeadm/RKE2 or ipam.mode=cluster-pool for manual CIDR management

root_cause: Hubble requires the cilium-agent to export flow events via a Unix socket, and Hubble Relay to aggregate them. Common failure modes include Hubble disabled in the Helm release, Relay pod in CrashLoopBackOff due to agent connectivity issues, or TLS certificate misconfiguration between Relay and agent.
fix: Verify Hubble is enabled: kubectl -n kube-system get configmap cilium-config -o jsonpath='{.data.enable-hubble}'
Check Relay pod status: kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/name=hubble-relay
Check Relay logs: kubectl -n kube-system logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=hubble-relay --tail=100
Verify agent listens on Hubble socket: kubectl -n kube-system exec -it <cilium-pod> -- ss -xlp | grep hubble
If TLS is misconfigured, regenerate certificates: cilium hubble enable --ui
Test Relay connectivity directly: kubectl -n kube-system port-forward svc/hubble-relay 4245:80 && hubble observe --server localhost:4245
Enable Hubble via Helm if missing: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --set hubble.enabled=true --set hubble.relay.enabled=true --reuse-values

symptoms: how to write CiliumNetworkPolicy with namespace isolation
root_cause: Namespace-level isolation in Cilium is achieved by combining endpoint selectors for intra-namespace traffic with explicit cross-namespace allow rules. A common pattern is to deny all by default using an empty policy and then add specific allow rules per service, leveraging Kubernetes namespace labels for selector matching.
fix: Apply default-deny using CiliumNetworkPolicy with empty ingress/egress to select all pods in namespace
Example deny-all spec: endpointSelector: {} with policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress] and no rules
Allow intra-namespace traffic: fromEndpoints: [{namespaceSelector: {matchLabels: {kubernetes.io/metadata.name: my-ns}}}]
Allow DNS egress explicitly: toEndpoints: [{matchLabels: {k8s-app: kube-dns}}] with port 53 UDP/TCP
Cross-namespace allow pattern: fromEndpoints with both namespaceSelector and podSelector combined
Apply CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy for cluster-wide defaults that apply before namespace policies
Validate: cilium policy trace --src-endpoint <id> --dst-k8s-pod my-ns/my-pod --dport 80

symptoms: how to install Cilium Gateway API support
Cilium Gateway API setup from scratch
root_cause: Cilium must be installed with the `gatewayAPI.enabled=true` Helm value and the Gateway API CRDs must be applied separately before Cilium starts, because Cilium does not bundle the upstream Gateway API CRDs.
fix: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/gateway-api/releases/download/v1.1.0/standard-install.yaml
helm upgrade --install cilium cilium/cilium --version 1.15.5 --namespace kube-system --set gatewayAPI.enabled=true --set kubeProxyReplacement=true
kubectl get gatewayclass cilium -o yaml  # verify GatewayClass is Accepted
kubectl get crd gateways.gateway.networking.k8s.io httproutes.gateway.networking.k8s.io  # verify CRDs exist
kubectl rollout restart daemonset/cilium -n kube-system  # if upgrading existing install

root_cause: The Cilium operator watches for GatewayClass resources with `spec.controllerName: io.cilium/gateway-controller`. If `gatewayAPI.enabled` was not set at install time or the Cilium operator pod is not running, the GatewayClass remains unaccepted.
fix: kubectl describe gatewayclass cilium  # check status.conditions for reason
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=cilium-operator  # verify operator is running
helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium -n kube-system --reuse-values --set gatewayAPI.enabled=true
kubectl rollout restart deployment/cilium-operator -n kube-system
kubectl get gatewayclass cilium -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Accepted")].status}'

symptoms: how to create Gateway with HTTPS listener
root_cause: A Gateway resource defines the infrastructure entry point. For TLS termination, the listener must reference a Kubernetes Secret (or cert-manager Certificate) via `tls.certificateRefs`. Cilium creates an Envoy listener and handles the TLS handshake.
fix: kubectl create secret tls my-tls-secret --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key -n infra

Apply Gateway YAML: apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1, kind: Gateway, metadata.name: main-gateway, spec.gatewayClassName: cilium, spec.listeners: [{name: https, port: 443, protocol: HTTPS, tls: {mode: Terminate, certificateRefs: [{name: my-tls-secret}]}}]
kubectl describe gateway main-gateway -n infra  # check status.conditions Programmed=True
kubectl get gateway main-gateway -n infra -o jsonpath='{.status.addresses}'  # get assigned IP

symptoms: how to configure HTTPRoute for hostname routing
route traffic by hostname with Gateway API
root_cause: HTTPRoute `spec.hostnames` filters requests by the HTTP Host header so a single Gateway can serve multiple virtual hosts without additional Gateways. Each HTTPRoute references one or more parent Gateways via `spec.parentRefs`.
fix: Apply HTTPRoute: apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1, kind: HTTPRoute, spec.hostnames: [api.example.com], spec.parentRefs: [{name: main-gateway, namespace: infra}], spec.rules: [{backendRefs: [{name: api-svc, port: 8080}]}]
kubectl get httproute -A  # list all routes
kubectl describe httproute api-route  # verify status.parents[0].conditions Accepted=True and ResolvedRefs=True
curl -H 'Host: api.example.com' http://<gateway-ip>/healthz  # smoke test

symptoms: how to do path matching in HTTPRoute
root_cause: HTTPRoute supports three path match types: `Exact`, `PathPrefix`, and `RegularExpression`. Cilium translates these into Envoy route matchers. More-specific matches (Exact > PathPrefix > RegularExpression) take precedence per the Gateway API specification.
fix: Define path match: spec.rules[0].matches: [{path: {type: PathPrefix, value: /api/v1}}]
For exact match: spec.rules[0].matches: [{path: {type: Exact, value: /health}}]
Combine path + method: spec.rules[0].matches: [{path: {type: PathPrefix, value: /admin}, method: GET}]
kubectl describe httproute <name>  # inspect status.parents for route acceptance
Check Envoy config: kubectl exec -n kube-system ds/cilium -- cilium-dbg envoy config routes  # verify route entries

symptoms: route by request header Gateway API
root_cause: HTTPRoute `spec.rules[].matches[].headers` allows routing decisions based on request headers, supporting `Exact` and `RegularExpression` header matching. This enables canary releases, A/B tests, or tenant routing without path changes.
fix: Add header match: spec.rules[0].matches: [{headers: [{name: X-Canary, value: 'true', type: Exact}]}]
Route canary header to new backend: spec.rules[0].backendRefs: [{name: app-canary, port: 8080}]
Fallback rule (no match): spec.rules[1].backendRefs: [{name: app-stable, port: 8080}]
kubectl apply -f httproute-canary.yaml && kubectl describe httproute canary-route
curl -H 'X-Canary: true' http://<gw-ip>/app  # verify canary path

root_cause: HTTPRoute `spec.rules[].backendRefs[].weight` enables weighted traffic splitting across multiple backends, allowing progressive canary rollouts without external traffic management tools.
fix: Apply split rule: spec.rules[0].backendRefs: [{name: app-stable, port: 8080, weight: 90}, {name: app-canary, port: 8080, weight: 10}]
kubectl apply -f httproute-split.yaml
kubectl describe httproute traffic-split  # confirm ResolvedRefs=True for both backends
Monitor: kubectl top pods -l app=app-canary  # check canary pod receives ~10% load
Promote canary: update weight to 100/0 then remove canary backendRef after validation

symptoms: how to configure TLSRoute with Cilium
root_cause: TLSRoute enables TLS passthrough (SNI-based routing) where the Gateway forwards encrypted traffic to the backend without terminating TLS. The Gateway listener must use protocol `TLS` with `mode: Passthrough` and Cilium handles SNI inspection at L4.
fix: Configure listener: spec.listeners: [{name: tls-passthrough, port: 443, protocol: TLS, tls: {mode: Passthrough}}]

Apply TLSRoute: apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1alpha2, kind: TLSRoute, spec.parentRefs: [{name: main-gateway}], spec.hostnames: [db.example.com], spec.rules: [{backendRefs: [{name: postgres-svc, port: 5432}]}]
kubectl get tlsroute -A  # list TLS routes
kubectl describe tlsroute db-tls-route  # verify Accepted condition
openssl s_client -connect <gw-ip>:443 -servername db.example.com  # verify SNI passthrough

symptoms: route gRPC services with Gateway API
root_cause: GRPCRoute (graduated to GA in Gateway API v1.1) allows routing gRPC traffic by service name and method. Cilium uses HTTP/2 framing to match gRPC requests; the Gateway listener must use HTTPS/HTTP2 protocol.
fix: Apply Gateway with HTTP2 listener: spec.listeners: [{name: grpc, port: 443, protocol: HTTPS, tls: {mode: Terminate, certificateRefs: [{name: tls-secret}]}}]

Apply GRPCRoute: apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1, kind: GRPCRoute, spec.rules: [{matches: [{method: {service: helloworld.Greeter, method: SayHello}}], backendRefs: [{name: grpc-svc, port: 50051}]}]
kubectl get grpcroute -A
grpcurl -authority grpc.example.com <gw-ip>:443 helloworld.Greeter/SayHello  # test gRPC call
kubectl describe grpcroute <name>  # check Accepted and ResolvedRefs conditions

symptoms: how to use cert-manager Gateway shim
root_cause: cert-manager's Gateway API integration (the `gateway` issuer/shim) watches Gateway resources and automatically provisions certificates via Let's Encrypt or internal CAs, storing them as Secrets referenced by the Gateway listener.
fix: Install cert-manager: helm install cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager -n cert-manager --create-namespace --set installCRDs=true --set featureGates='ExperimentalGatewayAPISupport=true'
Create ClusterIssuer for Let's Encrypt: kubectl apply -f cluster-issuer-letsencrypt.yaml
Annotate Gateway: kubectl annotate gateway main-gateway cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer=letsencrypt-prod -n infra
Add tls.certificateRefs to listener pointing to auto-managed secret name
kubectl get certificate -n infra  # cert-manager creates Certificate object automatically
kubectl describe certificate main-gateway-tls -n infra  # verify Ready=True

symptoms: HTTPRoute in different namespace than Gateway
root_cause: By default, Gateway API resources can only reference resources in the same namespace. ReferenceGrant explicitly permits a resource in namespace A to be referenced by a resource in namespace B, enabling cross-namespace Gateway-to-HTTPRoute and HTTPRoute-to-Service references.
fix: Apply ReferenceGrant in the target namespace: apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1beta1, kind: ReferenceGrant, metadata.namespace: app-ns, spec.from: [{group: gateway.networking.k8s.io, kind: HTTPRoute, namespace: infra}], spec.to: [{group: '', kind: Service}]
Reference backend from HTTPRoute: spec.rules[0].backendRefs: [{name: app-svc, namespace: app-ns, port: 8080}]
kubectl get referencegrant -A  # list all grants
kubectl describe httproute cross-ns-route  # ResolvedRefs condition shows RefNotPermitted if grant is missing

root_cause: When an HTTPRoute references a Service in a different namespace without a corresponding ReferenceGrant, the Cilium controller sets the `ResolvedRefs` condition to `False` with reason `RefNotPermitted`, and traffic is not forwarded.
fix: kubectl describe httproute <name> -n <ns>  # look for RefNotPermitted in status.parents[].conditions
Check if ReferenceGrant exists: kubectl get referencegrant -n <backend-namespace>

Create ReferenceGrant: kind: ReferenceGrant, metadata.namespace: <backend-ns>, spec.from: [{group: gateway.networking.k8s.io, kind: HTTPRoute, namespace: <route-ns>}], spec.to: [{group: '', kind: Service}]
kubectl apply -f referencegrant.yaml && kubectl describe httproute <name>  # ResolvedRefs should become True within seconds

symptoms: migrate from Ingress to Gateway API
Ingress to Gateway API migration steps
root_cause: Gateway API supersedes Ingress with a richer, role-oriented model. Migrating involves creating a GatewayClass and Gateway to replace IngressClass and Ingress controller deployment, then converting Ingress rules to HTTPRoute resources.
fix: Map Ingress annotations to HTTPRoute filters: e.g., nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target becomes HTTPRoute URLRewrite filter
Convert each Ingress rule: spec.rules[].host -> HTTPRoute spec.hostnames[], spec.rules[].http.paths[] -> HTTPRoute spec.rules[].matches[].path
Apply Gateway: kubectl apply -f gateway.yaml  # replace IngressClass controller
Apply HTTPRoutes: kubectl apply -f httproutes/  # one per former Ingress or grouped by namespace
Run both Ingress and HTTPRoute in parallel during cutover, verify with curl tests
Delete old Ingress resources after smoke tests pass: kubectl delete ingress --all -n <ns>
Remove nginx/traefik ingress controller once migration is complete

root_cause: Gateway API issues can occur at multiple layers: CRD installation, GatewayClass acceptance, Gateway programming, HTTPRoute route acceptance, ReferenceGrant permissions, or Cilium Envoy proxy configuration. Systematic layer-by-layer diagnosis is required.
fix: Layer 1 - CRDs: kubectl get crd | grep gateway.networking.k8s.io  # must show 6+ CRDs
Layer 2 - GatewayClass: kubectl describe gatewayclass cilium | grep -A5 Conditions  # must be Accepted=True
Layer 3 - Gateway: kubectl describe gateway <name> -n <ns> | grep -A10 Conditions  # must be Programmed=True
Layer 4 - HTTPRoute: kubectl describe httproute <name> -n <ns>  # Accepted=True and ResolvedRefs=True
Layer 5 - Envoy: kubectl exec -n kube-system ds/cilium -- cilium-dbg proxy status
Layer 6 - Logs: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=cilium-operator --since=5m | grep -i gateway
Layer 7 - Connectivity: kubectl run debug --rm -it --image=curlimages/curl -- curl -v http://<gateway-ip>/<path> -H 'Host: <hostname>'

symptoms: all namespaces can attach routes to Gateway
root_cause: A Gateway without `spec.listeners[].allowedRoutes.namespaces` restrictions defaults to `Same` namespace only in newer versions, but explicitly setting it prevents accidental cross-namespace route attachment and enforces the principle of least privilege.
fix: Restrict to same namespace (default): spec.listeners[0].allowedRoutes.namespaces.from: Same
Restrict to labeled namespaces: spec.listeners[0].allowedRoutes.namespaces.from: Selector, spec.listeners[0].allowedRoutes.namespaces.selector.matchLabels: {gateway-access: allowed}
Label namespaces that should attach: kubectl label namespace app-ns gateway-access=allowed
Avoid `from: All` in production — it allows any namespace to attach routes to the Gateway
kubectl describe gateway <name>  # verify attachedRoutes count matches expectation

symptoms: Cilium Gateway no LoadBalancer IP assigned
root_cause: Cilium Gateway API creates a LoadBalancer Service for each Gateway. If MetalLB or Kube-VIP is not configured, or if the IP address pool is exhausted, the Service will stay in `<pending>` state and the Gateway will have no assigned address.
fix: kubectl get svc -n infra | grep cilium-gateway  # find the auto-created LoadBalancer service
kubectl describe svc cilium-gateway-main-gateway -n infra  # look for 'no available IPs' event
kubectl get ipaddresspool -A  # verify MetalLB pool has available addresses
kubectl get l2advertisement -A  # verify MetalLB L2 advertisement is configured
Add IP pool: kubectl apply -f metallb-ippool.yaml  # extend or create new pool
kubectl describe gateway <name>  # status.addresses should populate within 30s after LB IP assigned

symptoms: how to structure Gateways per team
organize Gateway API resources for multiple teams
root_cause: Gateway API's role-oriented design separates infrastructure owners (GatewayClass/Gateway) from application developers (HTTPRoute). Best practice is to have platform teams own Gateways in a shared namespace and application teams own HTTPRoutes in their own namespaces.
fix: Platform team manages: GatewayClass (cluster-scoped) and Gateway resources in `infra` namespace
Application teams manage: HTTPRoute in their own namespace referencing the shared Gateway via parentRefs
Use allowedRoutes.namespaces.selector to control which app namespaces can attach to each Gateway
Use separate Gateways for internet-facing vs internal traffic (e.g., `public-gateway` and `internal-gateway`)
Use ReferenceGrant to grant specific cross-namespace service access rather than blanket permissions
Apply RBAC: platform team has write on Gateway, app teams have write on HTTPRoute in their namespace only

root_cause: Cilium Gateway API uses Envoy as the data-plane proxy. Default settings may not be tuned for high-throughput workloads. Enabling HTTP/2 multiplexing, connection pooling, and setting appropriate timeout values significantly reduces latency under load.
fix: Enable HTTP/2 on backend connections by ensuring backend services advertise h2 via ALPN or use gRPC protocol
Set explicit timeouts in HTTPRoute: add BackendTimeout filter to avoid hanging connections (spec.rules[0].timeouts.backendRequest: 30s)
Monitor Envoy metrics: kubectl exec -n kube-system ds/cilium -- cilium-dbg metrics list | grep envoy
Check connection pool stats: kubectl exec -n kube-system ds/cilium -- curl -s localhost:9964/metrics | grep upstream_cx
Tune Cilium Envoy thread count: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium -n kube-system --set envoy.enabled=true --set envoy.resources.requests.cpu=500m
Use Prometheus to track: cilium_proxy_upstream_cx_total, cilium_proxy_upstream_rq_total metrics

root_cause: Deploying Gateway API to production requires validating CRD versions, security controls, TLS configuration, observability, and high-availability settings before going live.
fix: CRDs: verify standard channel CRDs installed (not experimental unless needed): kubectl get crd | grep networking.k8s.io
GatewayClass: confirm Accepted=True: kubectl get gatewayclass cilium -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Accepted")].status}'
TLS: all HTTPS listeners use valid certificates (cert-manager or pre-provisioned): kubectl get certificate -A
allowedRoutes: no Gateway uses `from: All` without explicit justification
ReferenceGrants: audit all cross-namespace grants: kubectl get referencegrant -A
Observability: Cilium Envoy metrics scraped by Prometheus, HTTPRoute request rates visible in Grafana
HA: Cilium operator has replicas>=2: kubectl get deploy/cilium-operator -n kube-system -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}'
Smoke test all HTTPRoutes with curl before DNS cutover

symptoms: Gateway routes configured but 404 from backend
root_cause: A common cause is a mismatch between the HTTPRoute `spec.hostnames` and the actual HTTP Host header sent by the client, or a missing trailing-slash handling. Another cause is the backend Service selector not matching any pods, resulting in an empty endpoint set.
fix: Verify hostname match: curl -v -H 'Host: exact.hostname.from.route' http://<gw-ip>/path  # must match spec.hostnames exactly
Check backend endpoints: kubectl get endpoints <svc-name> -n <ns>  # must be non-empty
kubectl describe httproute <name>  # confirm ResolvedRefs=True, not just Accepted=True
Check pod readiness: kubectl get pods -l <selector> -n <ns>  # all pods must be Running/Ready
Check Service targetPort matches container port: kubectl describe svc <name> -n <ns>
Enable Cilium debug: kubectl exec -n kube-system ds/cilium -- cilium-dbg envoy config routes  # inspect actual Envoy route table

symptoms: how to configure KEDA ScaledObject for Redis list queue
autoscale workers based on Redis queue length
root_cause: KEDA ScaledObject with the Redis list trigger polls a Redis key's list length and scales a Deployment proportionally. The trigger compares the list length against a threshold to determine the desired replica count.
fix: Create a ScaledObject referencing your Deployment and the Redis trigger: apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1, kind: ScaledObject
Set spec.scaleTargetRef.name to your worker Deployment name
Add trigger: type: redis, metadata: address: redis-cluster:6379, listName: bull:<queue-name>:wait, listLength: '10'
Set spec.minReplicaCount: 0 for scale-to-zero and spec.maxReplicaCount: 20
Apply with: kubectl apply -f scaledobject.yaml -n <namespace>
Verify with: kubectl get scaledobject -n <namespace> and kubectl describe hpa keda-hpa-<name> -n <namespace>

symptoms: how to configure KEDA ScaledJob for BullMQ jobs
scale Kubernetes Jobs with BullMQ queue
root_cause: ScaledJob creates individual Kubernetes Jobs per batch of messages rather than scaling long-running Deployment replicas. This is ideal for BullMQ tasks where each job should run to completion independently and the queue may have bursty traffic.
fix: Define apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1, kind: ScaledJob with spec.jobTargetRef containing your Job template
Set spec.triggers[0].type: redis and metadata.listName: bull:<queue>:wait, metadata.listLength: '1'
Configure spec.scalingStrategy.strategy: accurate for precise job-to-message mapping
Set spec.maxReplicaCount and spec.successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3, spec.failedJobsHistoryLimit: 3
Apply and verify: kubectl get scaledjob -n <namespace>
Monitor job creation: kubectl get jobs -n <namespace> -w

symptoms: minReplicaCount 0 but pods never scale down
root_cause: Scale-to-zero requires minReplicaCount: 0 in the ScaledObject spec AND the trigger metric must return zero. If the trigger cannot connect to the external system or the metric endpoint returns an error, KEDA defaults to keeping current replicas rather than scaling to zero, as a safety measure.
fix: Verify minReplicaCount: 0 is set in spec: kubectl get scaledobject <name> -n <ns> -o yaml | grep minReplicaCount
Check KEDA operator logs for trigger errors: kubectl logs -n keda deployment/keda-operator --tail=100
Confirm the external scaler can reach Redis/NATS: kubectl exec -n keda deploy/keda-operator -- nc -zv redis-cluster 6379
Inspect HPA managed by KEDA: kubectl describe hpa keda-hpa-<name> -n <ns> and look for ScalingLimited or metric fetch errors
Check if cooldownPeriod is too long: default is 300s, reduce with spec.cooldownPeriod: 60 in ScaledObject
Ensure KEDA metrics-apiserver is healthy: kubectl get apiservice v1beta1.external.metrics.k8s.io

symptoms: how to set KEDA cooldownPeriod and pollingInterval
best practice for KEDA polling configuration
KEDA scaling too aggressive or too slow
root_cause: pollingInterval controls how frequently KEDA checks the external metric source (default 30s). cooldownPeriod controls how long KEDA waits after the last active trigger before scaling to zero (default 300s). Tuning these values prevents thrashing and reduces load on external systems.
fix: Set pollingInterval based on acceptable lag: spec.pollingInterval: 15 for near-real-time queues, 60 for batch workloads
Set cooldownPeriod to at least 2x the expected job processing time to avoid premature scale-down: spec.cooldownPeriod: 120
For Redis/BullMQ queues with bursty traffic, use pollingInterval: 10 and cooldownPeriod: 180
Avoid pollingInterval below 5s to prevent excessive load on Redis or NATS
Combine with HPA stabilizationWindowSeconds to smooth scaling decisions: set scaleDown.stabilizationWindowSeconds: 120 in the HPA behavior
Monitor KEDA polling overhead in Prometheus: keda_scaler_metrics_latency metric

symptoms: KEDA activation threshold vs scaling threshold confusion
pods scale from 0 but only after long delay
root_cause: KEDA has two separate thresholds: activationThreshold (triggers scale FROM zero to minReplicaCount when metric exceeds it) and the regular threshold (used for normal HPA scaling). If activationThreshold is set too high, the queue must accumulate many messages before any pods start.
fix: In trigger metadata, set activationListLength separately from listLength: activationListLength: '1', listLength: '10'
activationListLength: '1' means the first message triggers scale-from-zero; listLength: '10' means one pod per 10 queued items
Verify both thresholds in ScaledObject: kubectl get scaledobject <name> -n <ns> -o jsonpath='{.spec.triggers}'
For NATS JetStream trigger: use activationLagThreshold: '1' alongside lagThreshold for equivalent behavior
For Prometheus trigger: use activationThreshold: '0' to activate on any non-zero metric value
Test threshold behavior by publishing a single message and watching: kubectl get pods -n <ns> -w

symptoms: how to use KEDA NATS JetStream trigger
autoscale with NATS JetStream consumer lag
root_cause: The KEDA NATS JetStream scaler reads consumer pending message count from a JetStream consumer and scales pods based on the lagThreshold. It requires a durable consumer to be created on the stream before the ScaledObject is applied.
fix: Pre-create a durable pull consumer on your NATS stream: nats consumer add <stream> <consumer> --pull --durable <consumer> -s nats://nats:4222
Define trigger: type: nats-jetstream with metadata: natsServerMonitoringEndpoint: nats:8222, account: '$G', stream: <stream-name>, consumer: <consumer-name>, lagThreshold: '10'
Set activationLagThreshold: '1' to scale from zero on first pending message
Ensure NATS monitoring port 8222 is accessible from the KEDA operator pod
Verify with: kubectl describe scaledobject <name> -n <ns> and check External Metric Value in events
Monitor consumer lag: nats consumer info <stream> <consumer> -s nats://nats:4222

symptoms: how to configure KEDA Prometheus trigger
autoscale based on custom Prometheus metric
root_cause: The KEDA Prometheus trigger queries a Prometheus endpoint with a PromQL expression and scales the target based on the returned value. This enables autoscaling on any custom metric exposed to Prometheus, such as request queue depth, error rates, or business metrics.
fix: Define trigger: type: prometheus with metadata: serverAddress: http://prometheus-operated.monitoring:9090, query: 'sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-api"}[1m]))', threshold: '100'
Set activationThreshold: '5' to scale from zero only when RPS exceeds 5
Ensure the Prometheus server is reachable from the KEDA namespace: kubectl exec -n keda deploy/keda-operator -- wget -qO- http://prometheus-operated.monitoring:9090/api/v1/query?query=up
Use namespace scoping if needed: add namespace: monitoring in trigger metadata
For authenticated Prometheus, create a TriggerAuthentication with bearer token: kubectl create secret generic prom-token -n keda --from-literal=token=<token>
Validate PromQL query returns a scalar: curl 'http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query?query=<encoded-query>'

symptoms: how to use KEDA Redis Streams trigger
autoscale with Redis XLEN consumer group lag
root_cause: The KEDA Redis Streams scaler measures the pending entry count for a consumer group on a Redis stream (XPENDING), enabling autoscaling of workers that consume from Redis Streams rather than lists. This is the correct trigger for BullMQ v4+ which uses streams internally.
fix: Use trigger type: redis-streams with metadata: address: redis-cluster:6379, stream: <stream-key>, consumerGroup: <group-name>, pendingEntriesCount: '10'
Create the consumer group if it doesn't exist: redis-cli XGROUP CREATE <stream> <group> $ MKSTREAM
Set activationLagThreshold: '1' to scale from zero when first message arrives
For Redis Cluster mode, use addresses: redis-node-0:6379,redis-node-1:6379,redis-node-2:6379 instead of address
Create TriggerAuthentication for Redis password: kubectl create secret generic redis-auth -n <ns> --from-literal=password=<pass>
Reference in ScaledObject: spec.triggers[0].authenticationRef.name: redis-auth

symptoms: external metrics not available for HPA
root_cause: KEDA exposes external metrics through its own metrics-apiserver which registers as an API service extension. If the KEDA metrics-apiserver pod is unhealthy or the APIService object is misconfigured, the HPA cannot fetch external metrics and shows 'unknown' for metric values.
fix: Check KEDA metrics server pod: kubectl get pods -n keda -l app=keda-metrics-apiserver
Check APIService registration: kubectl get apiservice v1beta1.external.metrics.k8s.io -o yaml
Verify APIService shows Available=True: kubectl get apiservice v1beta1.external.metrics.k8s.io -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions}'
If unavailable, restart metrics server: kubectl rollout restart deployment/keda-metrics-apiserver -n keda
Test external metrics endpoint: kubectl get --raw /apis/external.metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1
Check for certificate issues: kubectl logs -n keda deployment/keda-metrics-apiserver --tail=50 | grep -i cert

symptoms: how to configure HPA stabilization window
HPA scaling too fast on scale down
prevent HPA thrashing during traffic spikes
root_cause: HPA without stabilization windows can scale down too aggressively after a traffic spike, causing subsequent traffic to find insufficient capacity. The stabilizationWindowSeconds in HPA behavior smooths scaling decisions by looking back over a time window and using the maximum or minimum desired replica count.
fix: Add behavior to HPA or ScaledObject's advanced.horizontalPodAutoscalerConfig: scaleDown.stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
For KEDA ScaledObjects, set via spec.advanced.horizontalPodAutoscalerConfig.behavior: scaleDown: stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
Also set scaleDown.policies: [{type: Percent, value: 25, periodSeconds: 60}] to limit scale-down rate to 25% per minute
For scale-up, set scaleUp.stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0 and scaleUp.policies: [{type: Pods, value: 4, periodSeconds: 15}] to allow fast scale-up
Verify HPA behavior is applied: kubectl get hpa keda-hpa-<name> -n <ns> -o yaml | grep -A 20 behavior
Monitor HPA decisions: kubectl describe hpa keda-hpa-<name> -n <ns> | grep -A 10 Events

symptoms: how does VPA work with KEDA
VPA recommender setup for Node.js pods
root_cause: VPA (Vertical Pod Autoscaler) and KEDA can coexist when VPA is set to Off or Initial mode for the same Deployment, or when targeting different resource attributes. VPA recommender analyzes historical CPU/memory usage and suggests right-sized resource requests, while KEDA handles horizontal scaling.
fix: Install VPA: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/releases/download/vertical-pod-autoscaler-1.1.0/vpa-v1-crd-gen.yaml && helm upgrade --install vpa fairwinds-stable/vpa -n kube-system
Create VPA in Recommendation-only mode: apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1, kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler, spec.updatePolicy.updateMode: Off
Target the same Deployment as KEDA ScaledObject: spec.targetRef.name: <deployment-name>
View recommendations: kubectl get vpa <name> -n <ns> -o jsonpath='{.status.recommendation}'
Do NOT use updateMode: Auto when KEDA is also active — VPA pod evictions conflict with KEDA's HPA replica management
Use VPA Off mode to gather recommendations, then manually update resource requests in the Deployment

symptoms: HPA and VPA both in Auto mode causing pod evictions
VPA evicting pods that KEDA just scaled up
autoscaling conflicts between VPA and HPA
root_cause: Running VPA in Auto or Recreate updateMode simultaneously with HPA (or KEDA-managed HPA) causes conflicts. VPA evicts pods to resize them while HPA is simultaneously trying to maintain a certain replica count, leading to thrashing, availability gaps, and unpredictable behavior.
fix: Never set VPA updateMode: Auto or Recreate on a Deployment also managed by KEDA or HPA
Use VPA updateMode: Off for recommendation collection only, then apply recommendations manually
Alternatively use VPA updateMode: Initial to set resources only at pod creation, not after
If using Goldilocks for VPA recommendations, ensure it runs in Off mode: kubectl get vpa -A -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}: {.spec.updatePolicy.updateMode}{"\n"}{end}'
Separate concerns: use KEDA for horizontal scaling based on queue depth, use manual resource tuning from VPA recommendations
Monitor for unexpected pod evictions: kubectl get events -n <ns> --field-selector reason=EvictedByVPA

symptoms: KEDA not scaling up even with messages in queue
ScaledObject shows ready but no scaling
KEDA trigger returning wrong metric value
root_cause: KEDA may fail to scale due to authentication issues with the trigger source, metric value below activationThreshold, misconfigured trigger metadata, or the HPA being manually paused. Systematic diagnosis requires checking the ScaledObject status, KEDA operator logs, and the raw metric value.
fix: Check ScaledObject status: kubectl get scaledobject <name> -n <ns> -o yaml | grep -A 20 status
Look for Active=True and ReadyCondition in status.conditions
Get raw metric value from KEDA: kubectl get --raw '/apis/external.metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/<ns>/s<scaledobject-name>-<trigger-index>-<trigger-type>'
Check KEDA operator for authentication errors: kubectl logs -n keda deployment/keda-operator | grep -i 'error\|auth\|connect' | tail -50
Verify TriggerAuthentication secret exists and has correct keys: kubectl get triggerauthentication -n <ns> -o yaml
Check if HPA is paused: kubectl get hpa keda-hpa-<name> -n <ns> -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations.autoscaling\.alpha\.kubernetes\.io/pause-triggers}'

symptoms: KEDA ScaledObject error: no object references for scaler
keda operator error: unable to get external metric
root_cause: KEDA ScaledObject enters an error state when the scaler cannot initialize, typically due to a missing or unreachable TriggerAuthentication secret, wrong Redis/NATS address, or an invalid trigger configuration field. The ScaledObject condition ScalerReady=False is set and no scaling occurs.
fix: Describe the ScaledObject to see condition messages: kubectl describe scaledobject <name> -n <ns>
Check KEDA operator logs filtered to the specific ScaledObject: kubectl logs -n keda deploy/keda-operator | grep <scaledobject-name>
Verify the TriggerAuthentication references a valid secret: kubectl get secret <secret-name> -n <ns>
For Redis trigger, test connectivity: kubectl run redis-test --rm -it --image=redis:7 -n <ns> -- redis-cli -h redis-cluster -p 6379 ping
Validate trigger metadata fields match the scaler's required parameters exactly (case-sensitive): check keda.sh/docs for the specific scaler
After fixing, force KEDA to re-evaluate by annotating: kubectl annotate scaledobject <name> -n <ns> keda.sh/restore-webhook-rollback=true --overwrite

symptoms: KEDA scaling lag too slow for spike traffic
queue depth grows faster than pods scale up
how to tune KEDA for fast scale-up response
root_cause: Default KEDA pollingInterval of 30s combined with Kubernetes pod startup time (image pull + init containers) creates an end-to-end scaling lag. For time-sensitive queues, this means messages accumulate before workers can process them. Reducing pollingInterval and optimizing pod startup minimizes this lag.
fix: Reduce pollingInterval: spec.pollingInterval: 5 for latency-sensitive workloads (monitor Redis load impact)
Pre-pull images on all nodes using a DaemonSet or ImagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent with warm nodes
Use minReplicaCount: 1 instead of 0 to keep at least one worker always running for instant processing
Set HPA scaleUp behavior for fast scale-up: scaleUp.stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0, scaleUp.policies: [{type: Pods, value: 10, periodSeconds: 15}]
Use init containers sparingly and precompile Node.js assets into the image to minimize startup time
Monitor scale-up latency with Prometheus: keda_scaler_metrics_latency and measure time from queue message publish to first pod ready

symptoms: how to verify KEDA is working correctly
root_cause: After installing KEDA via Helm, several components must be healthy and properly registered before ScaledObjects will function. A systematic checklist prevents common misconfigurations from causing silent failures.
fix: Verify all KEDA pods running: kubectl get pods -n keda -l app.kubernetes.io/name=keda
Check keda-operator, keda-metrics-apiserver, and keda-admission-webhooks are all Running
Confirm APIService registered: kubectl get apiservice v1beta1.external.metrics.k8s.io -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0].status}'  # should be True
Test a sample ScaledObject with a simple Prometheus trigger to validate end-to-end metric flow
Verify RBAC: kubectl auth can-i list scaledobjects --as=system:serviceaccount:keda:keda-operator -n default
Check webhook: kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations | grep keda and kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations | grep keda

symptoms: how to pause KEDA autoscaling during deployment
stop KEDA from scaling during rollout
root_cause: During rolling deployments or maintenance windows, KEDA should be paused to prevent it from scaling the Deployment while it is being updated. KEDA supports a pause annotation that suspends all scaling activity without deleting the ScaledObject.
fix: Pause KEDA scaling: kubectl annotate scaledobject <name> -n <ns> autoscaling.keda.sh/paused=true
Optionally set paused replica count: kubectl annotate scaledobject <name> -n <ns> autoscaling.keda.sh/paused-replicas='2'
Perform your deployment: kubectl set image deployment/<name> app=<new-image> -n <ns>
