Wait for rollout: kubectl rollout status deployment/<name> -n <ns>
Resume KEDA scaling: kubectl annotate scaledobject <name> -n <ns> autoscaling.keda.sh/paused-
Verify KEDA resumes normal operation: kubectl describe scaledobject <name> -n <ns> | grep -A 5 Conditions

root_cause: VPA recommender requires at least 24 hours of Prometheus metrics (or in-cluster metrics-server data) to generate meaningful recommendations. If metrics-server is not installed, or if pods have been running for less than the minimum observation window, the VPA recommendation will remain empty.
fix: Verify metrics-server is installed and healthy: kubectl top nodes && kubectl top pods -n <ns>
If metrics-server missing: helm upgrade --install metrics-server metrics-server/metrics-server -n kube-system
Check VPA recommender logs: kubectl logs -n kube-system deployment/vpa-recommender --tail=50 | grep -i error
Confirm VPA is watching the correct target: kubectl get vpa <name> -n <ns> -o yaml | grep -A 5 targetRef
Wait at least 24h of stable pod operation; check again: kubectl get vpa <name> -n <ns> -o jsonpath='{.status.recommendation.containerRecommendations}'
For immediate recommendations in test environments, use Goldilocks: kubectl label namespace <ns> goldilocks.fairwinds.com/enabled=true

symptoms: how to name KEDA ScaledObjects consistently
organize KEDA autoscaling resources in multi-tenant cluster
root_cause: Consistent naming and labeling of KEDA ScaledObjects, TriggerAuthentications, and managed HPAs simplifies operations, monitoring, and troubleshooting in clusters with many autoscaled workloads. KEDA creates HPA objects with a keda-hpa- prefix that must be anticipated.
fix: Name ScaledObjects after the Deployment they scale: <deployment-name>-scaledobject (e.g., worker-api-scaledobject)
KEDA auto-creates HPA named keda-hpa-<scaledobject-name>; account for this in RBAC and monitoring queries
Name TriggerAuthentications after the external system: redis-triggerauth, nats-triggerauth, prometheus-triggerauth
Label all ScaledObjects with app, component, and team labels for filtering: kubectl label scaledobject <name> -n <ns> team=platform component=autoscaling
Store ScaledObjects in the same namespace as the target Deployment; TriggerAuthentications can be in a shared namespace using ClusterTriggerAuthentication for cross-namespace use
Use kubectl get scaledobject,scaledjob -A -o wide to get a cluster-wide overview

symptoms: HPA not scaling even with high CPU
HPA current replicas stuck below max
HPA shows DesiredReplicas lower than expected
root_cause: HPA may be constrained by stabilization windows, scaling policies, or resource quota limits. When using both CPU-based HPA and KEDA external metrics, HPA takes the maximum of all metric recommendations. If one metric driver is failing, it can suppress the scaling decision.
fix: Describe the HPA to see all metric values and conditions: kubectl describe hpa <name> -n <ns>
Check for AbleToScale=False conditions indicating resource quota or limit range blocks
Review scaling events: kubectl get events -n <ns> --field-selector involvedObject.kind=HorizontalPodAutoscaler --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
Verify current metric values: kubectl get --raw '/apis/metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/<ns>/pods' | jq .
Check if namespace ResourceQuota is blocking new pods: kubectl describe resourcequota -n <ns>
For KEDA-managed HPAs, verify KEDA operator is not throttling: kubectl logs -n keda deploy/keda-operator | grep <hpa-name> | grep -i throttl

symptoms: how to create a CronJob in Kubernetes
root_cause: Kubernetes CronJobs run Jobs on a time-based schedule using standard cron syntax. The spec.schedule field accepts five-field cron expressions (minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week) and determines when new Job objects are spawned.
fix: Define spec.schedule using five-field cron syntax: '0 2 * * *' for daily at 02:00 UTC
Set spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.restartPolicy to Never or OnFailure (required for Jobs)
Apply with: kubectl apply -f cronjob.yaml
Verify schedule: kubectl get cronjob <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.schedule}'
Manually trigger a run: kubectl create job --from=cronjob/<name> <job-name>
List generated Jobs: kubectl get jobs --selector=job-name -l 'cronjob-name=<name>'

symptoms: multiple job instances running at same time
root_cause: The spec.concurrencyPolicy field controls whether concurrent Job executions are permitted. Without setting it, it defaults to Allow, which can cause multiple overlapping Job instances if a Job runs longer than its schedule interval, leading to resource exhaustion.
fix: Set spec.concurrencyPolicy: Forbid to skip new runs if a Job is still active
Set spec.concurrencyPolicy: Replace to cancel the running Job and start a fresh one
Set spec.concurrencyPolicy: Allow (default) only when Jobs are truly independent and short-lived
Check current active Jobs: kubectl get jobs -l 'cronjob-name=<name>' --field-selector=status.active=1
Monitor with: kubectl describe cronjob <name> | grep -i concurrency

symptoms: job not created at expected time kubernetes
root_cause: If the CronJob controller was down or the cluster was unavailable, Kubernetes counts missed schedule slots. If more than 100 schedules were missed within the startingDeadlineSeconds window (or since last scheduled time), Kubernetes stops scheduling new Jobs and logs 'Cannot determine if job needs to be started'.
fix: Check missed schedule count: kubectl describe cronjob <name> | grep -A5 'Events'
Set spec.startingDeadlineSeconds to a value smaller than your schedule interval to limit the missed-schedule window (e.g., 60 for a minutely job)
If >100 missed schedules occurred, delete and recreate the CronJob to reset the counter
Ensure the kube-controller-manager is healthy: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-controller-manager
Review controller logs: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l component=kube-controller-manager --tail=100 | grep cronjob

symptoms: how many completed jobs to keep kubernetes
root_cause: By default, Kubernetes keeps the last 3 successful and 1 failed Job from a CronJob. In high-frequency CronJobs this can accumulate many completed Job and Pod objects, consuming etcd space and cluttering kubectl get jobs output.
fix: Set spec.successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3 for most workloads (default is 3, reduce to 1 for high-frequency jobs)
Set spec.failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1 to retain only the most recent failure for debugging
For audit trails, set both limits to 0 and rely on external log aggregation (Loki/Fluentbit) instead
Check current Job count: kubectl get jobs --selector='app=<name>' | wc -l
Manually clean old Jobs: kubectl delete jobs --selector='app=<name>' --field-selector=status.conditions[0].type=Complete

symptoms: how to suspend a CronJob temporarily
root_cause: The spec.suspend field allows pausing CronJob scheduling without deleting the resource. When set to true, no new Jobs are created. Jobs already running are not affected.
fix: Suspend a CronJob: kubectl patch cronjob <name> -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'
Resume a CronJob: kubectl patch cronjob <name> -p '{"spec":{"suspend":false}}'
Verify status: kubectl get cronjob <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.suspend}'
In GitOps (Helm/ArgoCD), set spec.suspend: true in the values.yaml and sync — removes need for manual patching
Note: in-flight Jobs continue to completion; suspend only stops future schedule triggers

root_cause: Before k8s 1.25, CronJob schedules always used UTC. The spec.timeZone field (stable in 1.27) allows specifying an IANA timezone (e.g., 'America/New_York'), so cron expressions are evaluated in local time rather than UTC.
fix: Add spec.timeZone: 'Asia/Jakarta' (or any IANA tz name) to the CronJob spec
Verify the feature gate is enabled (stable since 1.27, no gate needed): kubectl version --short
Confirm resolved schedule: kubectl describe cronjob <name> | grep -i 'last schedule'
List valid IANA timezone names: timedatectl list-timezones (on any Linux node)
For clusters <1.25: encode timezone offset directly into cron expression or use a wrapper Job that checks local time

root_cause: The spec.backoffLimit on a Job controls how many times Kubernetes retries a failed Pod before marking the Job as Failed. The default is 6 retries with exponential backoff. If the underlying error is permanent (bad config, missing secret), retries waste resources indefinitely.
fix: Set spec.backoffLimit: 3 for most batch jobs (reduces wasted retries on permanent failures)
Set spec.backoffLimit: 0 for jobs where a single failure should immediately surface (zero-tolerance mode)
Monitor retry count: kubectl get job <name> -o jsonpath='{.status.failed}'
Check pod logs from latest failed attempt: kubectl logs $(kubectl get pods --selector=job-name=<name> --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp -o jsonpath='{.items[-1].metadata.name}')
Fix root cause (secret, config, image) before clearing: kubectl delete job <name> && kubectl apply -f job.yaml

root_cause: Without spec.activeDeadlineSeconds, a Job's Pods can run indefinitely. This is dangerous for batch workloads where a hung or deadlocked process would never be terminated, consuming node resources and blocking dependent workflows.
fix: Set spec.activeDeadlineSeconds: 3600 (1 hour) as a hard wall-clock timeout for the entire Job
Note: activeDeadlineSeconds counts from Job creation, not individual Pod start times
When deadline is exceeded, all running Pods are terminated and the Job status becomes Failed with reason DeadlineExceeded
Verify: kubectl get job <name> -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Failed")].reason}'
Combine with backoffLimit to control both retry count and total time budget
Alert on stuck Jobs with PromQL: kube_job_status_active > 0 and kube_job_created < (time() - 3600)

symptoms: how to auto-delete completed jobs kubernetes
root_cause: Completed Jobs and their Pods are not automatically deleted unless spec.ttlSecondsAfterFinished is set. Accumulating thousands of completed Jobs degrades etcd performance and slows kubectl list operations.
fix: Set spec.ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 86400 to delete successful/failed Jobs 24 hours after completion
Set to 0 for immediate deletion after completion (useful in high-frequency CronJobs)
Apply cluster-wide via a ValidatingWebhookConfiguration or OPA Gatekeeper policy that enforces ttlSecondsAfterFinished on all Jobs
Verify TTL controller is running: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=ttl-controller 2>/dev/null || echo 'Included in kube-controller-manager'
Check deletable Jobs now: kubectl get jobs -A --field-selector=status.conditions[0].type=Complete -o name | head -20

symptoms: how to debug stuck kubernetes job
root_cause: Stuck Jobs may result from image pull errors, resource quota exhaustion, missing PVCs, node selectors matching no nodes, or the Job controller not being able to create Pods. Diagnosis requires checking multiple layers.
fix: Check Job status: kubectl describe job <name> | grep -A10 'Conditions'
List job pods and their phases: kubectl get pods --selector=job-name=<name> -o wide
Inspect pod events for scheduling failures: kubectl describe pod <pod-name> | grep -A10 'Events'
Check resource quotas: kubectl describe resourcequota -n <namespace>
Inspect controller logs: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l component=kube-controller-manager --tail=200 | grep <job-name>
Check for node pressure: kubectl get nodes -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,READY:.status.conditions[-1].status,PRESSURE:.status.conditions[*].type
Verify image pull secret exists: kubectl get secret regcred -n <namespace>

symptoms: using CronJob for every recurring task
when to use BullMQ instead of CronJob
kubernetes cronjob vs queue repeatable job
root_cause: Kubernetes CronJobs are heavy-weight: each trigger creates a new Pod with full startup overhead (image pull, kubelet scheduling, container init). For sub-minute intervals, per-tenant scheduling, dynamic schedule changes, or jobs needing retry queues with back-pressure, BullMQ repeatable jobs on Redis Cluster are far more appropriate.
fix: Use Kubernetes CronJob when: schedule is coarse (≥1 min), job is stateless, runs infrequently, and requires cluster-level isolation
Use BullMQ repeatable jobs when: interval is <1 minute, schedules are tenant-specific or dynamic, you need fine-grained retry/delay/priority control
BullMQ repeatable job example: queue.add('sync', data, { repeat: { pattern: '*/30 * * * * *' } })
Avoid using CronJob for per-user or per-tenant scheduling — it would create thousands of CronJob objects
For mixed use: trigger BullMQ jobs from a CronJob (one CronJob enqueues many BullMQ tasks) to combine scheduling with queue semantics
Monitor BullMQ lag with: redis-cli -h <redis-host> LLEN bull:<queuename>:wait

symptoms: when to use BullMQ repeatable vs kubernetes cronjob
root_cause: CronJobs and BullMQ repeatable jobs serve overlapping but distinct roles. CronJobs provide cluster-native, declarative, GitOps-managed scheduling with Pod isolation. BullMQ provides sub-second scheduling, concurrency limits, rate limiting, prioritization, and fan-out within a Node.js worker fleet.
fix: Prefer CronJob for: database backups, report generation, certificate rotation, image cleanup — infrequent, long-running, cluster-scoped
Prefer BullMQ for: sending emails, webhook dispatches, AI inference batches — high-frequency, short-lived, per-tenant workloads
Combine: use CronJob to call an internal API endpoint that enqueues BullMQ jobs for distributed execution across worker pods
BullMQ worker scaling: pair with KEDA ScaledObject using redis-streams or custom triggers to autoscale Node.js workers
CronJob resource isolation: set resource requests/limits on spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers[].resources
Reference KEDA Redis queue trigger: kubectl get scaledobject <name> -o yaml | grep -A5 triggers

symptoms: cronjob not running after cluster downtime
cannot determine if job needs to be started
root_cause: Kubernetes CronJob controller logs 'Cannot determine if job needs to be started' and stops scheduling when more than 100 missed start times are detected since the last successful schedule. This typically occurs after extended cluster downtime or kube-controller-manager restart.
fix: Check CronJob events: kubectl describe cronjob <name> | tail -20
Confirm the condition: kubectl get cronjob <name> -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions}'
Set spec.startingDeadlineSeconds to a value less than 100x the schedule interval (e.g., for a 1-minute cron, set startingDeadlineSeconds: 60 to cap the window at 1 missed slot)
To reset: kubectl delete cronjob <name> && kubectl apply -f cronjob.yaml (recreate resets the missed-schedule counter)
Monitor: alert on kube_cronjob_status_active == 0 and time() - kube_cronjob_status_last_schedule_time > <expected_interval>

symptoms: why is my cronjob not working
root_cause: CronJob failures are multi-layered: the CronJob itself, the spawned Job, the Pod, and the container all have independent failure modes. A systematic checklist prevents spending time at the wrong layer.
fix: 1. Check CronJob spec: kubectl get cronjob <name> -o yaml | grep -E 'schedule|suspend|concurrencyPolicy'
2. Check if suspended: kubectl get cronjob <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.suspend}' (must be false)
3. Check last schedule time: kubectl get cronjob <name> -o jsonpath='{.status.lastScheduleTime}'
4. Check spawned Jobs: kubectl get jobs -l 'cronjob-name=<name>' --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
5. Check Job conditions: kubectl describe job <job-name> | grep -A5 Conditions
6. Check Pod logs: kubectl logs -l job-name=<job-name> --tail=100
7. Check kube-controller-manager logs for scheduling errors: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l component=kube-controller-manager --tail=200 | grep -i cron
8. Confirm RBAC: kubectl auth can-i create jobs --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa>

symptoms: many cronjob pods slow down cluster
root_cause: High-frequency CronJobs (every minute or more frequently) generate significant Pod churn: Pod creation, scheduling, execution, and deletion events all write to etcd. At scale this degrades etcd write throughput and increases API server load.
fix: Reduce CronJob frequency: move sub-minute work to BullMQ repeatable jobs
Set spec.ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 300 on spawned Jobs to auto-delete completed Pods quickly
Minimize successfulJobsHistoryLimit and failedJobsHistoryLimit (set to 1 each for high-frequency jobs)
Profile etcd write load: etcdctl --endpoints=<ep> endpoint status --write-out=table
Monitor API server request rate: rate(apiserver_request_total{verb='CREATE',resource='pods'}[5m])
Use indexed Jobs with spec.completionMode: Indexed for parallelism rather than spawning many small Jobs

root_cause: Job Pods without memory limits can be OOMKilled by the Linux kernel when node memory pressure occurs, causing the Job to retry (up to backoffLimit). Without limits, jobs compete with production workloads for memory.
fix: Always set resources.requests and resources.limits on CronJob/Job pod templates
Identify OOMKilled pods: kubectl get pods --selector=job-name=<name> -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}: {.status.containerStatuses[0].lastState.terminated.reason}{"\n"}{end}'
Check node memory pressure events: kubectl describe node <node> | grep -A5 'MemoryPressure'
Tune memory limit: start 20% above the p99 RSS observed in previous runs (query: container_memory_rss{pod=~'<name>.*'})
For memory-intensive batch jobs, add nodeSelector or tolerations to schedule on high-memory dedicated nodes
Set LimitRange in namespace to enforce default resource limits: kubectl apply -f limitrange.yaml

symptoms: how to write idempotent kubernetes job
job runs twice due to retry
root_cause: Kubernetes Jobs can run a Pod more than once due to backoff retries, node failures, or kubelet restarts. Job workloads must be idempotent: running the same task twice should produce the same result without data corruption.
fix: Use database upserts instead of inserts: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE SET ... (PostgreSQL/CloudNativePG)
Store a processing token (job-id + run-id) in the target table and skip if already present
Pass the Job name as an env var via fieldRef: fieldPath: metadata.labels['job-name'] for unique run identification
For file uploads: write to a temp path then atomic rename to the final path (mv is atomic on same filesystem)
In Node.js workers: wrap the handler in a distributed lock via Redis (redlock) before processing
Test idempotency: kubectl create job --from=cronjob/<name> test-idempotency-run1 && sleep 5 && kubectl create job --from=cronjob/<name> test-idempotency-run2

root_cause: Without naming and labeling conventions, CronJobs and their spawned Jobs become hard to identify, filter, and monitor at scale. Consistent labels enable Prometheus metrics correlation, Loki log queries, and GitOps drift detection.
fix: Name CronJobs descriptively: <team>-<function>-<interval> e.g., 'data-export-daily', 'cache-warmup-hourly'
Always set labels: app, team, component: cronjob, and tier (batch/maintenance)
Propagate labels to JobTemplate: spec.jobTemplate.metadata.labels must mirror CronJob labels for metric cardinality
Add annotation: 'batch.kubernetes.io/owner': '<team>' for ownership tracking
Use consistent selector in Prometheus: kube_cronjob_info{namespace='production',cronjob=~'data-.*'}
In Helm charts: use {{ include 'chart.labels' . }} helper to enforce label consistency across all CronJob templates

root_cause: Jobs may complete with exit code 0 (appearing successful) while failing to produce expected output due to caught exceptions, partial database writes, or silent errors in Node.js worker code. The Job status shows Complete but business logic failed.
fix: Instrument job with explicit exit codes: process.exit(1) on business logic failure, not just unhandled exceptions
Emit a structured log line at job end: logger.info({ status: 'completed', rowsProcessed: n, errors: m }) for Loki querying
Create a custom Prometheus gauge pushed to Pushgateway at job end: job_rows_processed{job='export'} <count>
Query Loki for silent failures: {job='batch'} |= 'completed' | json | rowsProcessed < 1
Add a post-job validation step as a separate container in the Job Pod spec (init containers run before; a sidecar or second container can validate output)
Set up alert: increase(job_rows_processed_total[1h]) == 0 for jobs that should always produce output

symptoms: job pod environment variables from vault
root_cause: Hardcoding secrets in CronJob environment variables or ConfigMaps is insecure. The recommended pattern for the target stack is to inject secrets from HashiCorp Vault via External Secrets Operator (ESO), which syncs secrets into Kubernetes Secrets that are mounted into Job Pods.
fix: Create an ExternalSecret referencing Vault: kubectl apply -f externalsecret-cronjob-creds.yaml with spec.refreshInterval: 1h
Mount the synced Secret as env vars in the CronJob jobTemplate: envFrom: [{secretRef: {name: <eso-synced-secret>}}]
Never use spec.env[].value for secrets — use secretKeyRef instead
Verify ESO sync status: kubectl get externalsecret <name> -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions}'
For short-lived Jobs, set ESO refreshInterval lower than the Job's activeDeadlineSeconds to ensure fresh credentials
Audit mounted secrets: kubectl get pods --selector=job-name=<name> -o jsonpath='{.items[0].spec.containers[0].env}' | jq

symptoms: how to assign static egress IP for OpenAI API calls
cilium egress gateway static IP setup
pods using different source IPs for external AI APIs
root_cause: Without a Cilium Egress Gateway, pods use the node IP as source when reaching external AI APIs, making it impossible to safelist a stable IP at the provider firewall. The Egress Gateway assigns a dedicated static IP to matched egress traffic.
fix: Label the node that will hold the egress IP: kubectl label node <node> egress-gateway=ai-apis

Create a CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy: kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: cilium.io/v2
kind: CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy
metadata:
  name: ai-api-egress
spec:
  selectors:
  - podSelector:
      matchLabels:
        egress-group: ai-consumers
  destinationCIDRs:
  - 0.0.0.0/0
  egressGateway:
    nodeSelector:
      matchLabels:
        egress-gateway: ai-apis
    egressIP: 10.0.1.50
EOF
Assign the static IP 10.0.1.50 to the egress node NIC: ip addr add 10.0.1.50/32 dev eth0 (or via MachineConfig on Talos)
Label AI-consumer pods: kubectl label pod <pod> egress-group=ai-consumers
Verify egress IP is used: kubectl exec -n <ns> <pod> -- curl -s https://api.ipify.org (should return 10.0.1.50)
Add 10.0.1.50 to OpenAI / Anthropic / Gemini IP safelist in provider console

symptoms: cilium egress gateway not applying static IP
source IP still shows node IP after CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy
root_cause: The Cilium Egress Gateway feature must be explicitly enabled at install time via the `egressGateway.enabled=true` Helm value. If omitted, CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy objects are accepted by the API server but silently have no effect.
fix: Check if feature is enabled: kubectl -n kube-system get configmap cilium-config -o jsonpath='{.data.enable-egress-gateway}'
Enable via Helm upgrade: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium -n kube-system --reuse-values --set egressGateway.enabled=true
Restart Cilium DS to pick up config: kubectl -n kube-system rollout restart daemonset/cilium
Confirm feature flag: cilium config view | grep egress-gateway
Re-apply CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy and re-test egress IP with curl https://api.ipify.org from a consumer pod

symptoms: how to restrict pods to only reach api.openai.com via CiliumNetworkPolicy
FQDN based network policy for AI APIs
block all outbound except specific AI hostnames
root_cause: FQDN-based CiliumNetworkPolicy uses Cilium's DNS proxy to resolve allowed hostnames at runtime, letting you restrict pods to specific AI API FQDNs (api.openai.com, api.anthropic.com, generativelanguage.googleapis.com) without hardcoding IPs that change.
fix: Enable DNS proxy in Cilium: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium -n kube-system --reuse-values --set dnsPolicyUnloadOnShutdown=false

Apply FQDN policy:
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: cilium.io/v2
kind: CiliumNetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-ai-apis-fqdn
  namespace: ai-workers
spec:
  endpointSelector:
    matchLabels:
      egress-group: ai-consumers
  egress:
  - toFQDNs:
    - matchName: api.openai.com
    - matchName: api.anthropic.com
    - matchName: generativelanguage.googleapis.com
    toPorts:
    - ports:
      - port: "443"
        protocol: TCP
  - toEndpoints:
    - matchLabels:
        k8s:io.kubernetes.pod.namespace: kube-system
        k8s-app: kube-dns
    toPorts:
    - ports:
      - port: "53"
        protocol: UDP
EOF
Verify DNS interception: kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium fqdn cache list
Test denied host: kubectl exec -n ai-workers <pod> -- curl -v https://evil.com (should timeout/refuse)
Test allowed host: kubectl exec -n ai-workers <pod> -- curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://api.openai.com/v1/models (should return 200 or 401)

symptoms: FQDN CiliumNetworkPolicy blocks AI API after pod restart
DNS resolution fails for api.openai.com under Cilium policy
cilium fqdn cache miss after new pod starts
root_cause: Cilium's FQDN cache is per-endpoint and is populated lazily on the first DNS query. If a pod restarts before the cache is warm, the first outgoing TLS connection is dropped because Cilium has no IPs mapped for the FQDN yet.
fix: Add a DNS pre-warm init container or startup probe that performs a DNS lookup before main container starts: command: ['sh', '-c', 'until nslookup api.openai.com; do sleep 1; done']
Increase FQDN cache min-ttl so cached IPs survive short pod restarts: --dns-policy-unload-on-shutdown=false and --tofqdns-min-ttl=3600 in cilium-config
Enable toFQDNs pre-cache via CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy with matchPattern: '*.openai.com' so the cache is populated cluster-wide regardless of pod lifecycle
Check current cache: kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium fqdn cache list | grep openai
Monitor FQDN policy drops: kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium monitor --type drop | grep FQDN

symptoms: how to audit all outbound HTTP requests to AI APIs from Node.js pods
log every OpenAI API call with latency and tokens
root_cause: Without structured outbound request logging, diagnosing cost spikes, latency regressions, or unexpected model calls requires guesswork. A combination of OpenTelemetry instrumentation in Node.js and Cilium Hubble network observability gives full visibility.
fix: Instrument Node.js with OpenTelemetry HTTP instrumentation: npm install @opentelemetry/instrumentation-http @opentelemetry/sdk-node
Add span attributes for AI calls in LangChain.js callback: new CallbackHandler({ onLLMEnd: (output, runId) => span.setAttributes({ 'ai.tokens': output.llmOutput?.tokenUsage?.totalTokens }) })
Enable Hubble in Cilium: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium -n kube-system --reuse-values --set hubble.enabled=true --set hubble.relay.enabled=true
Observe egress flows to AI APIs: hubble observe --namespace ai-workers --to-fqdn api.openai.com --follow
Forward OTel traces to Tempo and create Grafana dashboard filtering traces by span attribute ai.tokens > 0
Set up Loki alert for HTTP 429 responses from api.openai.com: logql '{namespace="ai-workers"} |= "429" |= "openai"'

symptoms: how to implement retry with exponential backoff for OpenAI API in Node.js
429 rate limit errors from OpenAI not retried automatically
root_cause: AI API providers enforce per-minute and per-day token/request rate limits. Without explicit retry-with-backoff logic, a transient 429 or 503 causes the entire request chain to fail, degrading user experience unnecessarily.
fix: Use the official OpenAI Node SDK built-in retry: const openai = new OpenAI({ maxRetries: 5 }); // retries 429, 500, 503 with exponential backoff automatically
For LangChain.js ChatOpenAI, set maxRetries: const llm = new ChatOpenAI({ maxRetries: 3, timeout: 30000 });
Honour the Retry-After header from 429 responses: const retryAfter = parseInt(err.response?.headers?.['retry-after'] ?? '1', 10);
Emit OTEL span events on each retry attempt for traceability: span.addEvent('ai.retry', { 'retry.attempt': attemptNumber })

symptoms: how to add circuit breaker for Anthropic API calls in Node.js
prevent cascade failure when AI API is down
circuit breaking for external AI service
root_cause: When an AI API endpoint is degraded, unbounded retries exhaust connection pools and memory. A circuit breaker opens after a threshold of failures, fast-failing requests and allowing the downstream service to recover.
fix: Install opossum circuit breaker: npm install opossum
Expose circuit state as a Prometheus metric via prom-client: gauge.set({ state: breaker.status.state }, 1)
Create Grafana alert: circuit state == open for > 2 minutes triggers PagerDuty
Test circuit: kubectl exec -n ai-workers <pod> -- node -e "require('./breaker').getState()" (should return 'halfOpen' during recovery)

symptoms: how to set request timeout for Google Gemini API calls
LlamaIndex TS timeout configuration for AI API
AI API call hangs indefinitely in Node.js pod
root_cause: AI inference endpoints can stall during model loading or upstream queue saturation. Without explicit timeouts, a single hung request holds an async context open until the pod OOMKills or the event loop saturates, blocking all other work.
fix: Set per-request timeout in Google Generative AI SDK: const model = genAI.getGenerativeModel({ model: 'gemini-1.5-pro' }); const result = await Promise.race([model.generateContent(prompt), timeout(30_000)]);
Configure LlamaIndex TS timeout: const llm = new Gemini({ model: 'models/gemini-1.5-pro', timeout: 30000 });
Set global axios/fetch timeout at Node.js bootstrap: import { setGlobalDispatcher, Agent } from 'undici'; setGlobalDispatcher(new Agent({ connectTimeout: 5000, bodyTimeout: 30000 }));
Emit a timeout metric when AbortError is caught: metrics.increment('ai.request.timeout', { provider, model })

symptoms: how to route AI API egress through an outbound HTTP proxy in Kubernetes
force pods to use squid proxy for external AI calls
corporate proxy for OpenAI egress traffic
root_cause: Enterprise environments often require all external traffic to traverse an audited outbound proxy (Squid, Mitmproxy, Nginx). Node.js respects HTTP_PROXY / HTTPS_PROXY env vars, which can be injected at the Deployment level to route AI API traffic through the proxy without code changes.
fix: Deploy Squid in-cluster: helm install squid chart/squid --set service.type=ClusterIP --set service.port=3128
Configure Squid ACL to allow AI FQDNs only: echo 'acl ai_apis dstdomain .openai.com .anthropic.com .googleapis.com\nhttp_access allow ai_apis\nhttp_access deny all' >> /etc/squid/squid.conf

Inject proxy env vars in AI worker Deployment:
env:
- name: HTTPS_PROXY
  value: http://squid.proxy.svc.cluster.local:3128
- name: NO_PROXY
  value: localhost,127.0.0.1,10.0.0.0/8,.svc.cluster.local
Force Node.js undici (fetch) to use proxy: npm install undici-proxy-agent; import { ProxyAgent } from 'undici'; const dispatcher = new ProxyAgent(process.env.HTTPS_PROXY!);
Verify requests appear in Squid access log: kubectl logs -n proxy deploy/squid | grep openai

symptoms: AI API calls failing from pod but curl from node works
egress to api.openai.com blocked inside cluster
how to troubleshoot Cilium egress drops
root_cause: Cilium policy drops are invisible to standard kubectl unless Hubble or the cilium monitor is used. Network-layer drops leave no application log, making egress issues appear as connect timeouts rather than policy rejections.
fix: Check Cilium policy verdict for the pod: kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium monitor --type drop --from-endpoint <endpoint-id>
Find endpoint ID: kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium endpoint list | grep <pod-ip>
Use Hubble CLI to trace flows: hubble observe --namespace ai-workers --verdict DROPPED --follow
Check if FQDN policy has cached IPs: kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium fqdn cache list | grep openai
Verify CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy is matched: kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium policy get | grep -A5 ai-api-egress
Test TCP reachability bypassing policy temporarily: kubectl run tmp --image=nicolaka/netshoot --rm -it -- curl -v https://api.openai.com/v1/models -H 'Authorization: Bearer test'

symptoms: hardcoding OpenAI IP CIDR ranges in CiliumNetworkPolicy
network policy breaks when OpenAI changes IPs
using IP-based egress policy for AI APIs instead of FQDN
root_cause: AI API providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) use CDN-backed or dynamically-assigned IP ranges that change without notice. Hardcoding CIDR blocks causes policy mismatches and silent egress failures when IPs rotate.
fix: Replace `toCIDRSet` with `toFQDNs` in CiliumNetworkPolicy for all AI API destinations
Use matchName for exact hostnames: matchName: api.openai.com (not matchPattern: '*.openai.com' unless wildcard is truly needed)
If a firewall upstream requires IPs, use the Cilium Egress Gateway static IP and configure the upstream firewall to allow that single egress IP to reach the provider
Subscribe to provider IP change notifications or status pages to avoid surprise outages: https://status.openai.com
Validate FQDN policy is enforced: cilium fqdn cache list should show resolved IPs that match nslookup output

symptoms: high latency on AI API calls from Kubernetes pods
p99 latency spikes when calling Gemini from Node.js
AI API response time degrades under load
root_cause: Multiple factors compound latency for AI egress: DNS resolution on each request (no connection reuse), per-request TLS handshake overhead, and insufficient connection pool size causing queuing inside Node.js HTTP agent.
fix: Enable HTTP keep-alive in Node.js fetch/undici: import { Agent } from 'undici'; const agent = new Agent({ keepAliveTimeout: 60000, connections: 20 }); setGlobalDispatcher(agent);
Reuse the OpenAI client singleton across requests — do NOT instantiate new OpenAI() per request
Pre-resolve AI API hostnames at pod startup using dns.resolve4('api.openai.com') to warm the DNS cache before first user request
Measure p50/p95/p99 latency per AI provider via OTEL histogram: histogram.record(durationMs, { 'ai.provider': 'openai', 'ai.model': model })
Profile connection establishment time: enable NODE_DEBUG=http2 to see if TLS handshakes are repeated
Set Cilium conntrack GC tuning to keep long-lived AI connections alive: --conntrack-gc-interval=30s

symptoms: egress security checklist for AI API integration
what to verify before deploying AI API consumers to production
security review for outbound AI API calls
root_cause: Shipping AI API consumers without a structured egress security review risks data exfiltration via unintended endpoints, unbounded API spend due to missing rate limits, and compliance failures from unaudited external data transfers.
fix: [ ] CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy configured with static egressIP; IP added to provider safelist
[ ] CiliumNetworkPolicy with toFQDNs restricts pods to only api.openai.com / api.anthropic.com / generativelanguage.googleapis.com on port 443
[ ] Default-deny egress policy applied to namespace; DNS (UDP 53 to kube-dns) explicitly allowed
[ ] API keys stored in HashiCorp Vault; injected via External Secrets Operator as Kubernetes Secrets; not present in image or ConfigMap
[ ] KEDA ScaledObject with maxReplicaCount bounded to prevent runaway API spend scaling
[ ] Circuit breaker (opossum) and retry-with-backoff configured; Prometheus metrics emitted on breaker state changes
[ ] Hubble network flow logging enabled; Grafana dashboard shows egress bytes/flows per pod to each AI FQDN
[ ] Request timeout <= 30s enforced via AbortController; hung request metric alerted on

symptoms: kubectl get ciliusegressgatewaypolicies returns No resources found
egress gateway CRD missing in cluster
root_cause: CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy is a CRD introduced in Cilium 1.13 and requires the egress gateway feature flag. On clusters running Cilium < 1.13 or without the CRD installed, kubectl apply silently succeeds (if CRD exists) but gateway feature is inactive.
fix: Check Cilium version: kubectl -n kube-system get ds/cilium -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
Verify CRD exists: kubectl get crd ciliusegressgatewaypolicies.cilium.io
If missing, upgrade Cilium: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium -n kube-system --version 1.15.x --reuse-values --set egressGateway.enabled=true
After upgrade confirm CRD: kubectl get crd | grep egress
Re-apply CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy and verify: kubectl get cegp -A

symptoms: how to design resilient AI API egress in Kubernetes
architecture for OpenAI integration with static IP and policy
multi-layer egress control for AI providers
root_cause: A multi-layered egress architecture combines static IP assignment (Cilium Egress Gateway), FQDN-level enforcement (CiliumNetworkPolicy), application-level resilience (retry/backoff/circuit breaker), and observability (Hubble + OTel) to make AI API integration both secure and observable.
fix: Layer 1 - Identity: AI consumer pods labeled egress-group=ai-consumers; API keys injected from Vault via ESO
Layer 2 - Routing: CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy SNATs all labeled pod egress to static IP 10.0.1.50
Layer 3 - Policy: CiliumNetworkPolicy with toFQDNs allows ONLY api.openai.com, api.anthropic.com, generativelanguage.googleapis.com on TCP 443 + kube-dns on UDP 53; default deny all other egress
Layer 4 - Resilience: Node.js SDK with maxRetries=3, opossum circuit breaker (50% error threshold, 60s reset), AbortController timeout 30s
Layer 5 - Observability: Hubble relay exports flows to Loki; OTel spans with ai.tokens attribute sent to Tempo; Grafana dashboards per provider
Layer 6 - Cost control: KEDA ScaledObject maxReplicaCount=10 prevents runaway scaling; per-pod rate limiter using Redis + BullMQ token bucket

symptoms: how to implement token bucket rate limiter for OpenAI API in Node.js with BullMQ
per-model rate limiting for AI API calls
queue AI API requests with Redis and BullMQ
root_cause: OpenAI and Anthropic enforce per-minute TPM (tokens per minute) and RPM (requests per minute) limits per API key. Distributing rate-limit enforcement across Node.js pods without a shared counter causes all pods to independently hit the limit simultaneously.
fix: Configure rate limiter on Worker: new Worker('ai-requests', processor, { connection: redisCluster, limiter: { max: 50, duration: 60000 } }); // 50 RPM
Track TPM usage by emitting token counts after each response: await redisCluster.incrby('ai:tpm:openai', tokensUsed); await redisCluster.expire('ai:tpm:openai', 60);
Alert when approaching limit: if (await redis.get('ai:tpm:openai') > 80000) { logger.warn('TPM at 80% of limit'); }
Set BullMQ job priority per request type: high-priority for user-facing, low for batch/background jobs

symptoms: how to verify Cilium egress gateway is actually forwarding traffic
egress gateway node not routing AI API traffic
CiliumEgressGatewayPolicy applied but traffic not going through gateway node
root_cause: The Egress Gateway requires the selected gateway node to have the egressIP assigned to an interface and IP forwarding enabled. Misconfigured nodes silently pass traffic through normal routing instead of the gateway, defeating IP safelisting.
fix: SSH to gateway node and confirm IP is assigned: ip addr show | grep 10.0.1.50
Check IP forwarding is enabled on gateway node: sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward (should be 1)
Verify Cilium selects correct node as gateway: kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium bpf egress list
Check BPF masquerade map: kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium bpf nat list | grep <pod-cidr>
From a consumer pod, trace the actual source IP: kubectl exec -n ai-workers <pod> -- curl -s https://api.ipify.org (must return 10.0.1.50)
Check Cilium metrics: kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium metrics list | grep egress_gateway

symptoms: how to structure Node.js AI client module for testability and observability
best practices for LangChain.js provider abstraction
AI provider interface convention in Node.js microservices
root_cause: Scattering direct provider SDK calls throughout the codebase makes it hard to swap providers, inject mocks in tests, enforce consistent timeout/retry/logging policies, and centrally manage API key rotation.
fix: Define an AIProvider interface: interface AIProvider { complete(prompt: string, opts?: CompletionOpts): Promise<CompletionResult>; }

Implement per-provider class wrapping the SDK with consistent retry/timeout/circuit breaker: class OpenAIProvider implements AIProvider { private breaker: CircuitBreaker; constructor(private client: OpenAI) { this.breaker = new CircuitBreaker(this.callAPI.bind(this), CB_OPTS); } }
Register providers in DI container (e.g., tsyringe): container.register<AIProvider>('AIProvider', { useClass: OpenAIProvider });
Emit standard OTEL spans in the base class: span = tracer.startSpan('ai.complete', { attributes: { 'ai.provider': this.name, 'ai.model': opts.model } });
Use environment variable AI_PROVIDER=openai|anthropic|gemini to switch implementations without code changes
Write provider-agnostic tests using the interface: const mockProvider: AIProvider = { complete: jest.fn().mockResolvedValue({ text: 'mocked' }) };

symptoms: how to set up Hubble UI dashboard for AI API egress monitoring
visualize outbound traffic to OpenAI from Kubernetes
Grafana dashboard for AI API egress flows
root_cause: Hubble provides real-time L3/L4/L7 flow visibility for Cilium-managed traffic. Enabling the Hubble relay and UI gives instant network-level observability for AI API egress without modifying application code.
fix: Enable Hubble with metrics: helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium -n kube-system --reuse-values --set hubble.enabled=true --set hubble.relay.enabled=true --set hubble.ui.enabled=true --set hubble.metrics.enabled='{dns,drop,tcp,flow,port-distribution,icmp,http}'
Port-forward Hubble UI: kubectl port-forward -n kube-system svc/hubble-ui 12000:80
Filter AI API flows in Hubble CLI: hubble observe --namespace ai-workers --to-fqdn api.openai.com --protocol TCP --verdict FORWARDED
Export Hubble metrics to Prometheus: kubectl -n kube-system get svc hubble-peer -o jsonpath='{.spec.clusterIP}'; add scrape job in prometheus-values.yaml
Import Grafana dashboard ID 16611 (Cilium v1.12 Hubble) and filter by destination FQDN containing openai or anthropic
Set Grafana alert: hubble_drop_total{reason='POLICY_DENIED'} > 0 for 5m in namespace ai-workers

symptoms: LlamaIndex TS Gemini calls fail with ECONNREFUSED when HTTPS_PROXY is set
Node.js fetch does not respect HTTPS_PROXY env var for AI SDK calls
proxy env var ignored by Google Generative AI SDK in Node.js
root_cause: Node.js native fetch (and undici, which backs it) does NOT automatically read HTTP_PROXY / HTTPS_PROXY environment variables. Only legacy http/https modules (via global-agent or http-proxy-agent) do. AI SDKs using native fetch silently bypass the proxy.
fix: Install global-agent: npm install global-agent
Bootstrap it before any SDK imports in the main entrypoint: import 'global-agent/bootstrap'; process.env.GLOBAL_AGENT_HTTPS_PROXY = process.env.HTTPS_PROXY!;
For undici-based SDKs, set dispatcher explicitly: import { ProxyAgent, setGlobalDispatcher } from 'undici'; setGlobalDispatcher(new ProxyAgent(process.env.HTTPS_PROXY!));
Verify proxy is used: HTTPS_PROXY=http://squid:3128 node -e "fetch('https://api.openai.com/v1/models')" and check Squid access.log
For Google AI SDK specifically, pass a custom fetch with proxy agent: new GoogleGenerativeAI(key, { fetchImplementation: proxyFetch })

symptoms: how to configure Vault Kubernetes auth method
root_cause: The Vault Kubernetes auth method allows pods to authenticate using their ServiceAccount JWT tokens, enabling workloads to retrieve secrets without hardcoding credentials. It must be enabled and configured with the Kubernetes API server CA and endpoint.
fix: vault auth enable kubernetes

vault write auth/kubernetes/config kubernetes_host='https://$(kubectl get svc kubernetes -o jsonpath="{.spec.clusterIP}"):443' kubernetes_ca_cert=@/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt token_reviewer_jwt=$(cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token)
vault write auth/kubernetes/role/my-app bound_service_account_names=my-app-sa bound_service_account_namespaces=production policies=my-app-policy ttl=1h
kubectl create serviceaccount my-app-sa -n production
kubectl annotate serviceaccount my-app-sa -n production vault.hashicorp.com/role=my-app

symptoms: how to use Vault dynamic database secrets
root_cause: Vault's database secrets engine generates short-lived, dynamic credentials for PostgreSQL (including CloudNativePG clusters), eliminating static passwords and enabling automatic rotation with configurable TTLs.
fix: vault secrets enable database

vault write database/config/cnpg-db plugin_name=postgresql-database-plugin allowed_roles='app-role' connection_url='postgresql://{{username}}:{{password}}@cnpg-cluster-rw.db.svc:5432/appdb' username='vault_admin' password='<static-admin-pw>'

vault write database/roles/app-role db_name=cnpg-db creation_statements="CREATE ROLE \"{{name}}\" WITH LOGIN PASSWORD '{{password}}' VALID UNTIL '{{expiration}}'; GRANT SELECT,INSERT,UPDATE,DELETE ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO \"{{name}}\";" default_ttl=1h max_ttl=24h
vault read database/creds/app-role
Configure External Secrets Operator to sync vault database/creds/app-role into a Kubernetes Secret for Node.js pods

symptoms: how to use Vault KV v2 secrets
root_cause: Vault KV v2 is a versioned key-value secrets engine that stores arbitrary secrets with metadata, supporting up to a configurable number of versions. It is the recommended engine for storing static secrets like API keys and TLS certificates.
fix: vault secrets enable -path=secret kv-v2
vault kv put secret/myapp/config openai_api_key='sk-...' anthropic_api_key='ant-...'
vault kv get -version=2 secret/myapp/config
vault kv metadata get secret/myapp/config
vault kv rollback -version=1 secret/myapp/config

symptoms: how to use Vault agent injector
inject vault secrets into pod environment
root_cause: The Vault Agent Injector is a mutating admission webhook that automatically injects a Vault Agent sidecar into annotated pods. The agent authenticates to Vault and renders secrets as files on a shared in-memory volume, enabling zero-code-change secret retrieval.
fix: helm install vault hashicorp/vault --set injector.enabled=true -n vault
Annotate pod spec: vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject: 'true'
Annotate pod spec: vault.hashicorp.com/role: 'my-app'
Annotate pod spec: vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-secret-config: 'secret/data/myapp/config'
Annotate pod spec: vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-template-config: '{{ with secret "secret/data/myapp/config" }}{{ range $k,$v := .Data.data }}export {{ $k }}={{ $v }}{{ end }}{{ end }}'
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- cat /vault/secrets/config
kubectl logs <pod> -c vault-agent-init  # check init container logs

symptoms: how to use Vault transit secrets engine
root_cause: Vault's Transit secrets engine provides encryption-as-a-service, allowing applications to encrypt/decrypt data without managing keys. Keys never leave Vault; applications send plaintext and receive ciphertext, enabling BYOK-style encryption for Node.js workloads.
fix: vault secrets enable transit
vault write -f transit/keys/my-app-key type=aes256-gcm96
vault write transit/encrypt/my-app-key plaintext=$(echo -n 'sensitive-data' | base64)
vault write transit/decrypt/my-app-key ciphertext='vault:v1:...'
vault write transit/keys/my-app-key/rotate  # rotate encryption key
vault write transit/rewrap/my-app-key ciphertext='vault:v1:...'  # rewrap old ciphertext to new key version
In Node.js: use node-vault or @hashicorp/vault-client to call transit/encrypt|decrypt endpoints

symptoms: how to set up External Secrets Operator SecretStore
root_cause: External Secrets Operator (ESO) SecretStore is a namespaced CRD that configures the connection to an external secret backend (e.g., Vault). It must reference a valid auth credential within the same namespace and is the prerequisite for ExternalSecret resources.
fix: helm install external-secrets external-secrets/external-secrets -n external-secrets --create-namespace

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: SecretStore
metadata:
  name: vault-backend
  namespace: production
spec:
  provider:
    vault:
      server: 'http://vault.vault.svc:8200'
      path: 'secret'
      version: v2
      auth:
        kubernetes:
          mountPath: kubernetes
          role: my-app
          serviceAccountRef:
            name: my-app-sa
EOF
kubectl get secretstore vault-backend -n production -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions}'
kubectl describe secretstore vault-backend -n production  # check Ready condition

root_cause: ClusterSecretStore is the cluster-scoped variant of SecretStore in External Secrets Operator. It allows all namespaces to reference a single backend configuration, avoiding per-namespace SecretStore duplication in multi-tenant clusters.
fix:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ClusterSecretStore
metadata:
  name: vault-cluster-store
spec:
  provider:
    vault:
      server: 'http://vault.vault.svc:8200'
      path: 'secret'
      version: v2
      auth:
        kubernetes:
          mountPath: kubernetes
          role: cluster-reader
          serviceAccountRef:
            name: eso-cluster-sa
            namespace: external-secrets
EOF
kubectl get clustersecretstore vault-cluster-store
Reference from ExternalSecret: spec.secretStoreRef.kind: ClusterSecretStore
kubectl describe clustersecretstore vault-cluster-store  # verify status.conditions Ready=True

symptoms: sync vault secret to kubernetes secret
root_cause: ExternalSecret is the ESO CRD that defines which keys to pull from an external store and how to map them into a native Kubernetes Secret. It supports refresh intervals, templating, and merging multiple sources into a single Secret.
fix:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: myapp-secrets
  namespace: production
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h
  secretStoreRef:
    name: vault-cluster-store
    kind: ClusterSecretStore
  target:
    name: myapp-secrets
    creationPolicy: Owner
  data:
  - secretKey: OPENAI_API_KEY
    remoteRef:
      key: secret/myapp/config
      property: openai_api_key
  - secretKey: DATABASE_URL
    remoteRef:
      key: secret/myapp/db
      property: url
EOF
kubectl get externalsecret myapp-secrets -n production
kubectl get externalsecret myapp-secrets -n production -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions}'
kubectl get secret myapp-secrets -n production -o jsonpath='{.data}' | base64 -d

symptoms: how to implement secret rotation in kubernetes
root_cause: Secrets should be rotated regularly to limit blast radius from credential compromise. In Kubernetes, ESO's refreshInterval + Vault's dynamic secrets enable automatic rotation; pods consume updated secrets via volume mounts (auto-refreshed) or via a Reloader controller that triggers rolling restarts when Secrets change.
fix: Set ExternalSecret refreshInterval to match your rotation policy (e.g., 15m for DB creds)
Deploy Reloader: helm install reloader stakater/reloader -n kube-system
Annotate Deployment: reloader.stakater.com/auto: 'true'  # triggers rolling restart on Secret change
For Vault dynamic secrets: set max_ttl <= refresh interval to ensure new creds before expiry

vault write database/config/mydb -<<EOF
..."max_ttl": "24h"
EOF
Monitor rotation: kubectl get externalsecret -A -o wide  # check READY and SYNCED columns
Alert on ESO sync failures: ExternalSecretSyncError metric in Prometheus (external_secrets_sync_calls_error_total)

symptoms: kubernetes secret yaml in git repo
root_cause: Storing Kubernetes Secret manifests (base64-encoded, not encrypted) in Git repositories exposes credentials in version history, violating the no-secrets-in-Git principle. Even private repos pose risk via leaked tokens, third-party CI access, or audit trail requirements.
fix: Never commit raw Secret YAML to Git — use ExternalSecret, SealedSecret, or SOPS-encrypted files instead
Scan repo for secrets: git log --all --full-history -- '*.yaml' | grep -i 'kind: Secret'
Use trufflehog or gitleaks in CI: gitleaks detect --source . --verbose
Add to .gitignore: *-secret.yaml, *-secrets.yaml, *.env
Migrate existing secrets: kubectl delete secret <name> && create via ESO ExternalSecret
If secrets were committed: git filter-branch or BFG Repo Cleaner to purge history, then rotate all exposed credentials immediately
Enforce: add pre-commit hook with detect-secrets or gitleaks

symptoms: how to use sealed secrets kubernetes
root_cause: Sealed Secrets (Bitnami) allows encrypting Kubernetes Secrets into SealedSecret CRDs that are safe to store in Git. Only the in-cluster controller can decrypt them using its private key, enabling GitOps workflows without exposing secret values.
fix: helm install sealed-secrets sealed-secrets/sealed-secrets -n kube-system --set fullnameOverride=sealed-secrets-controller
kubeseal --fetch-cert --controller-name=sealed-secrets-controller --controller-namespace=kube-system > pub-cert.pem
kubectl create secret generic myapp-secret --dry-run=client --from-literal=api-key=secret123 -o yaml | kubeseal --cert pub-cert.pem -o yaml > myapp-sealedsecret.yaml
git add myapp-sealedsecret.yaml && git commit -m 'add sealed secret'
kubectl apply -f myapp-sealedsecret.yaml  # controller decrypts and creates Secret
kubectl get sealedsecret myapp-secret -n production
kubeseal --re-encrypt < myapp-sealedsecret.yaml > myapp-sealedsecret-new.yaml  # re-encrypt after key rotation

root_cause: Vault uses Shamir's Secret Sharing to seal/unseal its encryption key. After every restart or pod eviction, Vault enters a sealed state and cannot serve requests until enough unseal key shards are provided. Auto-unseal with Kubernetes secrets or cloud KMS eliminates manual intervention.
fix: vault status  # check Sealed: true/false
vault operator unseal <key-shard-1>  # repeat for threshold shards (default 3 of 5)
Configure auto-unseal with Kubernetes transit: in vault.hcl: seal "transit" { address = "https://vault-ha.vault.svc:8200" token = "..." key_name = "autounseal" mount_path = "transit/" }
For HA Vault on K8s: helm install vault hashicorp/vault --set server.ha.enabled=true --set server.ha.replicas=3
kubectl exec -it vault-0 -n vault -- vault operator init -key-shares=5 -key-threshold=3 > vault-init.txt
Store unseal keys in a separate secure system (1Password, AWS Secrets Manager) — never in same cluster
Monitor: vault_core_unsealed gauge in Prometheus via Vault telemetry

root_cause: Vault running in Kubernetes without persistent storage or an HA backend (Raft/Consul) loses all data on pod restart. Additionally, if auto-unseal is not configured, Vault enters sealed state after each restart requiring manual intervention.
fix: Use Vault with integrated Raft storage and persistent volumes: helm install vault hashicorp/vault --set server.ha.enabled=true --set server.ha.raft.enabled=true
Ensure PVC is bound: kubectl get pvc -n vault  # vault-data-vault-0 should be Bound
Set storage class with retain policy: storageClassName: longhorn, reclaimPolicy: Retain
Enable auto-unseal to avoid manual unseal after restart
kubectl rollout status statefulset/vault -n vault  # check all replicas ready
vault operator raft list-peers  # verify raft cluster membership after restart
Test: kubectl delete pod vault-0 -n vault && watch kubectl get pods -n vault

symptoms: who can read secrets in kubernetes
root_cause: Kubernetes Secrets are accessible to anyone with get/list/watch permissions on the secrets resource in a namespace, including broad RBAC roles like cluster-admin or edit. Least-privilege RBAC combined with namespace isolation limits blast radius from compromised service accounts.
fix: Audit who can read secrets: kubectl auth can-i --list --namespace=production | grep secrets
Never use cluster-admin for application service accounts
Create tight Role: rules: [{apiGroups: [""], resources: ["secrets"], resourceNames: ["myapp-secrets"], verbs: ["get"]}]
Avoid list/watch on secrets — it allows enumerating all secret names
Use resource name restrictions (resourceNames field) to limit to specific secrets
Audit existing ClusterRoleBindings: kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.roleRef.name == "cluster-admin")'
Enable Kubernetes audit logging for secrets: add audit policy with level: RequestResponse for secrets resource
Separate namespaces per application team to enforce namespace-level RBAC boundaries

symptoms: ESO cannot fetch secret from vault
root_cause: ExternalSecret sync failures typically stem from: incorrect Vault path or property name, expired or missing ServiceAccount token, misconfigured SecretStore auth, Vault policy not granting read access, or network connectivity issues between ESO and Vault.
fix: kubectl describe externalsecret <name> -n <ns>  # read status.conditions for error message
kubectl get externalsecret <name> -n <ns> -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*].message}'
kubectl logs -n external-secrets -l app.kubernetes.io/name=external-secrets --tail=100 | grep ERROR
Test Vault connectivity from ESO pod: kubectl exec -it -n external-secrets <eso-pod> -- curl -s http://vault.vault.svc:8200/v1/sys/health
Verify Vault policy: vault policy read <policy-name>  # ensure path secret/data/<path> has read capability
Check KV path format: KV v2 secrets are at secret/data/<path> not secret/<path>
Manually test auth: vault write auth/kubernetes/login role=<role> jwt=$(kubectl create token <sa-name> -n <ns>)
Force resync: kubectl annotate externalsecret <name> -n <ns> force-sync=$(date +%s)

symptoms: vault agent injector pod stuck init container
root_cause: The vault-agent-init container fails when: the Vault role is not bound to the pod's ServiceAccount, the Vault address is unreachable, the secret path does not exist, or the agent template syntax is invalid. The pod stays in Init:0/1 state indefinitely.
fix: kubectl logs <pod> -c vault-agent-init  # look for 'Error logging in' or 'no such file or directory'
Verify pod ServiceAccount matches the bound_service_account_names in the Vault role: kubectl get pod <pod> -o jsonpath='{.spec.serviceAccountName}'
Check Vault role: vault read auth/kubernetes/role/<role-name>
Test JWT auth manually: JWT=$(kubectl exec <pod> -c vault-agent-init -- cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token) && vault write auth/kubernetes/login role=<role> jwt=$JWT
Verify secret path exists: vault kv get secret/myapp/config
Check agent annotation template: vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-template-<filename>  # must be valid Go template
If Vault uses TLS: set vault.hashicorp.com/tls-skip-verify: 'true' for testing or provide CA via vault.hashicorp.com/ca-cert annotation

root_cause: High secret fetch latency in ESO or Vault Agent can stem from: excessive refreshInterval causing thundering herd, Vault raft leader elections under load, missing Vault response caching in the agent, or ESO making individual API calls per ExternalSecret without batching.
fix: Enable Vault Agent caching: vault.hashicorp.com/agent-cache-enable: 'true' and vault.hashicorp.com/agent-cache-use-auto-auth-token: 'true'
Tune ESO refreshInterval based on secret volatility — use 1h for static API keys, 15m for dynamic DB creds
Scale Vault: ensure 3+ Raft replicas to avoid leader bottleneck: kubectl get pods -n vault  # vault-0,1,2 all Running
Enable Vault performance standby nodes for read scaling (requires Vault Enterprise or OSS read forwarding)
Monitor: vault_core_handle_request latency histogram, vault_raft_apply duration in Prometheus
Check ESO queue depth: kubectl top pod -n external-secrets  # high CPU = sync backlog
Use ClusterSecretStore instead of per-namespace SecretStore to reduce auth overhead

root_cause: Vault returns a 403 permission denied error when the token's attached policies do not include a matching path with read capability. For KV v2, a common mistake is writing policy for secret/<path> instead of secret/data/<path>.
fix: vault token capabilities <token> secret/data/myapp/config  # check actual capabilities
vault policy read <policy-name>  # review current policy
Fix KV v2 policy path: use 'secret/data/myapp/*' not 'secret/myapp/*'
Verify role has correct policy attached: vault read auth/kubernetes/role/my-app  # check policies field
Update role with fixed policy: vault write auth/kubernetes/role/my-app policies=myapp-policy bound_service_account_names=my-app-sa bound_service_account_namespaces=production
Retest: vault kv get -mount=secret myapp/config  # using token with new policy

symptoms: how to structure vault secrets paths
organizing secrets in vault for kubernetes
root_cause: A consistent Vault path naming convention prevents path collisions, simplifies policy management, and maps cleanly to Kubernetes namespaces and workloads. The recommended pattern mirrors the cluster topology: <env>/<namespace>/<app>/<secret-type>.
fix: Follow path pattern: secret/<env>/<namespace>/<app>/config (e.g., secret/production/orders/api-keys)
Separate secret types: config (static env vars), db (dynamic DB creds), tls (certificates), ssh (keys)
Write wildcard policies per namespace: path "secret/data/production/<namespace>/*" to match Kubernetes RBAC namespace boundaries
Use metadata tags in KV v2 for additional context: vault kv metadata put -custom-metadata=team=backend secret/production/orders/api-keys
Document path schema in a CLAUDE.md or team runbook
Enforce naming via Vault Sentinel policies (Enterprise) or admission controller that validates ESO ExternalSecret paths
Avoid deep nesting beyond 4 levels — vault list becomes unwieldy

symptoms: kubernetes secret not updated after vault rotation
pod still using old secret value
secret rotation not reflected in app
root_cause: After ESO syncs a new secret value from Vault into a Kubernetes Secret, pods that mount the secret as environment variables (envFrom/env) are not automatically updated — environment variables are set at pod start time. Only volume-mounted secrets get live updates (with up to ~1 minute kubelet sync delay).
fix: Check if secret was updated: kubectl get secret <name> -n <ns> -o jsonpath='{.metadata.resourceVersion}'  # changes on update
Verify ESO sync: kubectl get externalsecret <name> -n <ns> -o jsonpath='{.status.refreshTime}'
For env var secrets: deploy Reloader (stakater/reloader) to trigger rolling restart on Secret change
For volume-mounted secrets: kubelet auto-refreshes within syncing period (default 60s) — check: kubectl describe pod <pod> | grep SecretVolume
Force pod restart after rotation: kubectl rollout restart deployment/<name> -n <ns>
Verify new value in pod: kubectl exec <pod> -- printenv DATABASE_URL  # after restart
Set Reloader annotation: reloader.stakater.com/auto: 'true' on Deployment to automate restarts

symptoms: how to install kube-prometheus-stack with Helm
deploy Prometheus and Grafana on Kubernetes
root_cause: The kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart bundles Prometheus Operator, Prometheus, Alertmanager, Grafana, and a set of default ServiceMonitors and PrometheusRules. Installing it correctly requires setting storage, retention, and resource values appropriate for on-premise cluster size.
fix: helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts && helm repo update
Create values file: prometheus.prometheusSpec.retention=30d, prometheus.prometheusSpec.storageSpec.volumeClaimTemplate.spec.storageClassName=longhorn, resources.requests.memory=4Gi
helm upgrade --install kube-prometheus-stack prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack -n monitoring --create-namespace -f values.yaml
Verify: kubectl -n monitoring get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=kube-prometheus-stack
Access Grafana: kubectl -n monitoring port-forward svc/kube-prometheus-stack-grafana 3000:80
Default Grafana admin password is in secret kube-prometheus-stack-grafana under key admin-password

symptoms: ServiceMonitor not picked up by Prometheus Operator
root_cause: Prometheus Operator selects ServiceMonitors using the prometheusSpec.serviceMonitorSelector label selector. If the ServiceMonitor's labels do not match this selector, or if it is in a namespace not covered by serviceMonitorNamespaceSelector, the Operator ignores it entirely.
fix: Check Prometheus selector: kubectl -n monitoring get prometheus kube-prometheus-stack-prometheus -o jsonpath='{.spec.serviceMonitorSelector}'
Check ServiceMonitor labels: kubectl -n <app-ns> get servicemonitor <name> --show-labels
Add matching label to ServiceMonitor: metadata.labels.release=kube-prometheus-stack (or whatever the selector key is)
If cross-namespace: ensure prometheusSpec.serviceMonitorNamespaceSelector covers the app namespace
After fixing labels, Operator reconciles within ~30s; verify: kubectl -n monitoring logs deployment/kube-prometheus-stack-operator | grep ServiceMonitor
Confirm target appears: open Prometheus UI -> Status -> Targets

symptoms: how to create ServiceMonitor for Node.js app
scrape custom Node.js metrics with Prometheus
root_cause: Node.js applications using prom-client expose a /metrics endpoint that Prometheus must scrape via a ServiceMonitor CRD pointing to the correct port and path on the app's Kubernetes Service.
fix: Add prom-client to Node.js app: npm install prom-client

Register default metrics and expose endpoint: const client = require('prom-client'); client.collectDefaultMetrics(); app.get('/metrics', async (req, res) => { res.set('Content-Type', client.register.contentType); res.end(await client.register.metrics()); });
Expose a named port on the Kubernetes Service: ports: [{name: metrics, port: 9090, targetPort: 9090}]
Create ServiceMonitor: apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1, kind: ServiceMonitor, spec.endpoints[0].port=metrics, spec.endpoints[0].path=/metrics, spec.selector.matchLabels matching the Service
Label the ServiceMonitor with release: kube-prometheus-stack to satisfy Prometheus selector
Verify scrape: curl http://<pod-ip>:9090/metrics | grep nodejs

symptoms: how to create custom counter histogram in Node.js
track HTTP request duration in Prometheus Node.js
root_cause: Custom business metrics in Node.js require explicitly registering Histogram or Counter instances with prom-client using specific label dimensions. Poorly defined label cardinality causes TSDB growth issues.
fix: Define Histogram with bounded labels only: const httpDuration = new client.Histogram({ name: 'http_request_duration_seconds', help: 'HTTP request duration', labelNames: ['method', 'route', 'status_code'], buckets: [0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2.5] });

Instrument Express middleware: app.use((req, res, next) => { const end = httpDuration.startTimer(); res.on('finish', () => end({ method: req.method, route: req.route?.path ?? 'unknown', status_code: res.statusCode })); next(); });
Never use user-specific values (user IDs, UUIDs) as label values — use bounded enums only
Register business counter: const jobsProcessed = new client.Counter({ name: 'worker_jobs_processed_total', help: 'Total jobs processed', labelNames: ['queue', 'status'] });
Increment: jobsProcessed.inc({ queue: 'email', status: 'success' });
Expose via /metrics endpoint and scrape with ServiceMonitor

symptoms: how to create PrometheusRule for alert
set up alerting rule Kubernetes Prometheus
create custom alert with PrometheusRule CRD
root_cause: PrometheusRule is a CRD managed by Prometheus Operator that defines alerting and recording rules. It must be labelled to match the Prometheus instance's ruleSelector to be loaded.
fix: Create PrometheusRule manifest: apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1, kind: PrometheusRule, metadata.labels.release=kube-prometheus-stack

Define alert group: spec.groups[0].name=node-js-alerts, rules[0].alert=HighErrorRate, expr='rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{status_code=~"5.."}[5m]) / rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count[5m]) > 0.05', for: 5m
Add annotations: summary='High error rate on {{ $labels.route }}', description='Error rate is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}'
Apply: kubectl apply -f prometheusrule.yaml -n monitoring
Verify rule is loaded: kubectl -n monitoring exec -it prometheus-kube-prometheus-stack-prometheus-0 -- promtool check rules /etc/prometheus/rules/**/*.yaml
Check in Prometheus UI -> Alerts to confirm rule appears and evaluates

symptoms: how to create recording rules Prometheus
reduce query load with recording rules
root_cause: Expensive PromQL queries executed by multiple Grafana dashboards and alerts cause high Prometheus CPU usage. Recording rules precompute these expressions on an interval and store them as new time series, dramatically reducing query cost.
fix: Identify expensive queries via Prometheus UI -> Status -> Runtime & Build Info -> TSDB Head Chunks or query profiling
Create a recording rule in PrometheusRule: name: 'job:http_request_duration_seconds:rate5m', expr: 'rate(http_request_duration_seconds_sum[5m]) / rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count[5m])'
Naming convention: <level>:<metric>:<operation> e.g. job:http_errors:rate5m
Add labels to recording rules to preserve job/namespace dimensions: record: job:http_errors:rate5m
Reference recording rule metric in dashboard panels instead of raw rate() queries
Set evaluation interval matching scrape interval or coarser (e.g. 1m) to balance freshness and load

symptoms: how to deploy PostgreSQL exporter on Kubernetes
root_cause: CloudNativePG exposes Prometheus metrics natively on port 9187 via its built-in metrics endpoint, but a ServiceMonitor must be created to have Prometheus Operator scrape it. Additional postgres_exporter can be used for custom query metrics.
fix: CloudNativePG built-in metrics: enable in Cluster spec: monitoring.enablePodMonitor: true — this creates a PodMonitor automatically
Verify PodMonitor created: kubectl -n postgres get podmonitor cnpg-<cluster-name>
For custom queries, deploy prometheus-community/prometheus-postgres-exporter chart with a DSN secret
Create DSN secret: kubectl create secret generic pg-exporter-dsn --from-literal=datasource='postgresql://user:pass@host:5432/db?sslmode=disable'
helm upgrade --install pg-exporter prometheus-community/prometheus-postgres-exporter -n monitoring --set config.datasource.existingSecret.name=pg-exporter-dsn
Key metrics to alert on: pg_up, pg_stat_activity_count, pg_replication_lag, pg_database_size_bytes

symptoms: how to monitor Redis with Prometheus
root_cause: Redis does not expose Prometheus metrics natively. The oliver006/redis_exporter sidecar or standalone deployment scrapes Redis INFO and converts it to Prometheus format. For Redis Cluster, each node needs to be scraped separately.
fix: Deploy redis-exporter as sidecar in Redis pod or standalone: image: oliver006/redis_exporter:latest
Set environment: REDIS_ADDR=redis://localhost:6379, REDIS_PASSWORD from secret
For Redis Cluster, use REDIS_ADDR pointing to each node and set --is-cluster flag
Create ServiceMonitor targeting port redis-exporter (9121) with path /metrics
Key metrics: redis_up, redis_connected_clients, redis_memory_used_bytes, redis_commands_total, redis_keyspace_hits_total, redis_keyspace_misses_total
Alert on redis_up == 0 and redis_memory_used_bytes / redis_memory_max_bytes > 0.9

symptoms: target shows red in Prometheus UI
root_cause: A Prometheus target showing as DOWN indicates the scrape endpoint is unreachable. Common causes are: wrong port in ServiceMonitor, missing network policy allowing Prometheus to reach pods, pod not ready, or wrong scheme (http vs https).
fix: Check target error in Prometheus UI -> Status -> Targets, note the exact error message
Verify pod is running and port is open: kubectl -n <ns> get pod <pod> -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].ports}'
Test connectivity from Prometheus pod: kubectl -n monitoring exec -it prometheus-0 -- wget -qO- http://<pod-ip>:<port>/metrics
If Cilium NetworkPolicy is enforcing: ensure policy allows ingress from namespace monitoring on metrics port
Check ServiceMonitor port name matches Service port name exactly (case-sensitive)
If TLS: set servicemonitor.spec.endpoints[0].scheme=https and configure tlsConfig with insecureSkipVerify: true or proper CA cert
Restart Prometheus Operator if config not refreshing: kubectl -n monitoring rollout restart deployment kube-prometheus-stack-operator

symptoms: TSDB out of memory high cardinality
root_cause: High cardinality occurs when label dimensions have many unique values (e.g., user IDs, request IDs, pod IPs as labels). Each unique label value combination creates a separate time series. At scale, this exhausts Prometheus memory and slows queries.
fix: Identify high-cardinality metrics: curl -s http://localhost:9090/api/v1/query?query=topk(20,count+by(__name__)({__name__=~".+"})) | jq '.data.result[] | {metric: .metric.__name__, count: .value[1]}'
Drop high-cardinality labels at scrape time via metric_relabel_configs in ServiceMonitor: action: labeldrop, regex: 'user_id|request_id|pod_ip'
Use metric_relabel_configs action: drop to discard unnecessary metrics entirely: sourceLabels: [__name__], regex: 'go_gc_.*', action: drop
Enable Prometheus cardinality analysis: kubectl -n monitoring exec prometheus-0 -- promtool tsdb analyze /prometheus
Set per-scrape sample limit in ServiceMonitor: spec.endpoints[0].sampleLimit: 10000
Consider Prometheus recording rules to aggregate away high-cardinality labels before storing

root_cause: Prometheus TSDB storage consumption depends on number of active time series, scrape interval, and retention period. Under-provisioning leads to OOM kills or disk exhaustion. The formula is: bytes = samples_per_second * bytes_per_sample * retention_seconds.
fix: Estimate samples/sec: (number_of_time_series * scrape_frequency) e.g. 100k series * (1/15s) = 6667 samples/sec
Prometheus uses ~1-2 bytes per sample after compression; use 2 bytes for conservative estimate
Formula: storage_GB = (samples_per_sec * 2 * retention_seconds) / 1e9
For 100k series, 15s interval, 30d retention: 6667 * 2 * 2592000 / 1e9 ≈ 34 GB
Add 20% headroom; set in values: prometheus.prometheusSpec.storageSpec.volumeClaimTemplate.spec.resources.requests.storage=50Gi
Set retention: prometheusSpec.retention=30d and optionally retentionSize=45GB to cap disk usage
Monitor TSDB: prometheus_tsdb_head_series, prometheus_tsdb_storage_blocks_bytes

symptoms: how to configure Prometheus remote write
send metrics to Thanos or Mimir
root_cause: Remote write allows Prometheus to forward all scraped samples to a remote storage backend (Thanos Receiver, Grafana Mimir, VictoriaMetrics) for long-term retention and global querying across clusters, addressing single-Prometheus scalability limits.
fix: Add to kube-prometheus-stack values: prometheus.prometheusSpec.remoteWrite[0].url=http://thanos-receive.monitoring:19291/api/v1/receive
For authenticated remote write (Mimir): add remoteWrite[0].basicAuth.username.name=mimir-creds, key=username and password secret reference
Add queue tuning to handle backpressure: remoteWrite[0].queueConfig.maxSamplesPerSend=10000, capacity=100000, maxShards=30
Add relabelConfigs to filter what gets remote-written: only forward production namespace metrics
Monitor remote write health: prometheus_remote_storage_samples_failed_total, prometheus_remote_storage_queue_highest_sent_timestamp_seconds
Alert if lagging: rate(prometheus_remote_storage_samples_failed_total[5m]) > 0

symptoms: how to use PodMonitor instead of ServiceMonitor
scrape pods without a Service in Prometheus
root_cause: PodMonitor is the pod-level equivalent of ServiceMonitor and is needed when pods expose metrics but do not have a backing Kubernetes Service, or when multiple pods need individual scraping (e.g., CloudNativePG cluster nodes, Kafka brokers).
fix: Create PodMonitor: apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1, kind: PodMonitor, spec.podMetricsEndpoints[0].port=metrics, spec.podMetricsEndpoints[0].path=/metrics
Set spec.selector.matchLabels to match target pod labels, spec.namespaceSelector.matchNames to restrict namespace
Label PodMonitor with release: kube-prometheus-stack to match Prometheus podMonitorSelector
Verify Prometheus picks it up: kubectl -n monitoring get prometheus -o jsonpath='{.items[0].spec.podMonitorSelector}'
Check Prometheus UI -> Status -> Targets, filter by job label from PodMonitor
PodMonitor scrapes each pod IP directly, so ensure NetworkPolicy allows ingress from prometheus namespace on metrics port

symptoms: how to provision Grafana dashboards as code
root_cause: Grafana supports provisioning dashboards from ConfigMaps at startup, allowing dashboards to be version-controlled and deployed via GitOps. The kube-prometheus-stack chart watches ConfigMaps with a specific label for automatic dashboard loading.
fix: Create ConfigMap with dashboard JSON: apiVersion: v1, kind: ConfigMap, metadata.labels.grafana_dashboard=1, data.dashboard.json=<grafana-json-content>
Apply to the monitoring namespace or configure grafana.sidecar.dashboards.searchNamespace=ALL in values to watch all namespaces
Verify sidecar is enabled: kubectl -n monitoring get deployment kube-prometheus-stack-grafana -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[*].name}' — should include grafana-sc-dashboard
For folder organization: add annotation grafana_folder: MyApp to the ConfigMap
Export existing dashboard JSON from Grafana UI: Dashboard Settings -> JSON Model -> Copy
Store dashboard JSON in Helm chart templates/dashboards/ and reference in chart values; use helm upgrade to apply

symptoms: Grafana dashboard using high-cost PromQL in every panel
too many instant queries hitting Prometheus
root_cause: Grafana dashboards that use expensive PromQL expressions (e.g., rate() over long windows with many label matchers) in every panel without recording rules cause repeated costly Prometheus queries, leading to slow dashboard load times and high Prometheus CPU.
fix: Identify slow queries: Grafana -> Explore -> Inspector on a panel to see query time
Move expensive expressions to Prometheus recording rules and reference the pre-computed metric in Grafana
Use Grafana variables with label_values() wisely — avoid label_values(huge_metric, label) which full-scans TSDB; prefer label_values(up{job='myapp'}, namespace)
Enable Grafana query caching: in grafana.ini set [caching] enabled=true with Redis backend
Limit panel refresh rate; not every panel needs 10s refresh — set appropriate per-panel min interval
Use $__rate_interval variable instead of hardcoded [5m] in rate() to avoid undersampling on variable scrape intervals

symptoms: alert in Prometheus UI shows inactive
root_cause: Alerts defined in PrometheusRule may not fire due to incorrect PromQL expressions that return no results, the rule not being loaded by the Prometheus Operator due to label mismatch, or the for duration not having elapsed yet.
fix: Step 1: Confirm rule is loaded — kubectl -n monitoring exec prometheus-kube-prometheus-stack-prometheus-0 -- wget -qO- http://localhost:9090/api/v1/rules | jq '.data.groups[].rules[] | select(.name=="<AlertName>")'
Step 2: Test expression directly in Prometheus UI -> Graph; if it returns empty, fix the PromQL
