Step 3: Check for label mismatch — verify PrometheusRule has label matching prometheusSpec.ruleSelector (e.g. release: kube-prometheus-stack)
Step 4: Check Operator logs for rule load errors: kubectl -n monitoring logs deployment/kube-prometheus-stack-operator --since=5m | grep -i rule
Step 5: Check for syntax errors: promtool check rules prometheusrule.yaml
Step 6: If alert is pending but not firing, the for duration hasn't elapsed; reduce for value temporarily for testing

root_cause: Debugging a missing metric in Grafana requires systematically validating each layer: app exposes metric -> Kubernetes Service/port correct -> ServiceMonitor/PodMonitor exists and is selected -> Prometheus scrapes target -> metric exists in Prometheus -> Grafana data source and query correct.
fix: 1. App layer: curl http://<pod-ip>:<port>/metrics | grep <metric_name> — confirm metric is emitted
2. Service layer: kubectl get svc -n <ns> -o yaml — confirm named port matches ServiceMonitor endpoint port
3. ServiceMonitor layer: kubectl get servicemonitor -n <ns> -o yaml — check selector, namespaceSelector, labels match Prometheus ruleSelector
4. Prometheus targets: open Prometheus UI /targets — target should show state=UP
5. Metric exists: query in Prometheus UI /graph — run <metric_name>{job='<job>'} to confirm data
6. Grafana data source: Grafana -> Configuration -> Data Sources -> Test — ensure Prometheus URL is reachable
7. Grafana panel: open panel inspector -> Query -> run query manually, check for correct label selectors

symptoms: Grafana Prometheus data source not working after kube-prometheus-stack install
Grafana shows no data source configured
root_cause: kube-prometheus-stack provisions a default Grafana data source pointing to the internal Prometheus Service URL. If the chart release name differs from the default, the URL in the provisioned data source may be incorrect.
fix: Check provisioned data source: kubectl -n monitoring get configmap kube-prometheus-stack-grafana -o yaml | grep datasource
Default URL is http://kube-prometheus-stack-prometheus.monitoring:9090 — if release name differs, URL changes
Override in values: grafana.additionalDataSources or grafana.datasources."datasources.yaml".datasources[0].url=http://<release-name>-prometheus.<ns>:9090
After updating values: helm upgrade kube-prometheus-stack ... -f values.yaml
Verify in Grafana UI -> Configuration -> Data Sources -> Prometheus -> Save & Test
If behind auth: set grafana.datasources."datasources.yaml".datasources[0].basicAuthUser and basicAuthPassword

symptoms: how to structure observability with kube-prometheus-stack
root_cause: A production on-premise observability stack requires Prometheus for metrics, Grafana for visualization, Loki for logs, and Tempo for traces, all integrated with OpenTelemetry. The kube-prometheus-stack provides the metrics foundation but must be configured for cross-namespace collection and long-term storage.
fix: Deploy kube-prometheus-stack in dedicated monitoring namespace with serviceMonitorNamespaceSelector: {} (all namespaces) and podMonitorNamespaceSelector: {} (all namespaces)
Deploy Loki stack for log aggregation: helm upgrade --install loki grafana/loki-stack -n monitoring
Deploy Tempo for distributed tracing: helm upgrade --install tempo grafana/tempo -n monitoring
Configure Grafana with Loki, Tempo, Prometheus data sources and enable trace-to-log correlations using TraceID label
Deploy OpenTelemetry Collector (otel-collector) to receive OTLP spans from Node.js apps and forward to Tempo; use Vector/Fluent Bit for log aggregation to Loki
Node.js apps: instrument with @opentelemetry/sdk-node, export metrics to OTLP collector, use prom-client for Prometheus metrics, structured JSON logs to stdout

symptoms: prom-client metrics reset after pod restart
Node.js worker metrics lost on crash
root_cause: prom-client stores all metrics in-process memory. When a Node.js pod crashes or restarts, all counter values reset to zero, causing visible counter resets in Prometheus graphs. This is expected behavior but must be accounted for in PromQL with rate() and handled in dashboards.
fix: Use rate() and increase() in PromQL instead of raw counter values — these functions automatically handle counter resets
Alert on rate(counter[5m]) > threshold rather than counter > threshold to avoid false alerts from resets
For Gauge metrics that represent in-flight state, reset them explicitly on graceful shutdown: process.on('SIGTERM', () => inFlightGauge.set(0))
To persist counters across restarts, push to Prometheus Pushgateway: use prom-client pushgateway client, but only for batch jobs (not long-running services)
Add pod restart rate alert: rate(kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total[15m]) * 60 > 1 to detect frequent restarts that cause reset artifacts
In Grafana, add annotation for pod restarts to visually correlate metric drops with restart events

symptoms: loki monolithic vs microservices vs SSD
how to choose loki deployment mode
root_cause: Loki supports three deployment modes: monolithic (single binary, all components in one process), Simple Scalable Deployment (SSD, read/write path split into two targets), and microservices (each component deployed independently). Choosing the wrong mode for workload size leads to either wasted resources or scalability bottlenecks.
fix: Monolithic: set target: all in loki config; suitable for <100GB/day ingestion
SSD: deploy two Deployments with target: read and target: write; scales read/write independently
Microservices: deploy ingester, distributor, querier, query-frontend, compactor, ruler separately via Helm values
Helm install: helm upgrade --install loki grafana/loki -f values.yaml --set deploymentMode=SimpleScalable
Check mode at runtime: kubectl exec -n loki deploy/loki -- /bin/loki --config.file=/etc/loki/config.yaml -target=list
SSD minimum replicas: loki.write.replicas=3, loki.read.replicas=2 for HA

symptoms: loki ingestion slow due to labels
root_cause: Loki indexes only labels, not log content. High-cardinality label values (e.g., pod IP, request ID, user ID) create millions of unique streams, causing excessive memory use in ingesters and slow query performance. Each unique label combination is a separate stream.
fix: Limit labels to static, low-cardinality values: namespace, app, pod (template name), container, node
Never use dynamic values as labels: avoid request_id, user_id, pod_ip
Use structured log fields for high-cardinality data and query with LogQL |= or | json parser
Validate stream count: curl -s http://loki:3100/metrics | grep loki_ingester_streams_created_total
Set ingester.max_streams_per_user in loki config to enforce per-tenant stream limits
Run logcli series '{namespace="prod"}' --analyze-labels to detect cardinality offenders

root_cause: LogQL is Loki's query language with two query types: log queries (return log lines) and metric queries (return numeric values over time). Knowing the correct syntax for filtering, parsing, and aggregating is essential for effective log analysis.
fix: Basic filter: {namespace="prod", app="api"} |= "ERROR"
JSON parser: {app="api"} | json | level="error" | line_format "{{.msg}}"
Rate metric: rate({app="api"} |= "ERROR" [5m])
Top talkers by pod: topk(5, sum by (pod) (rate({namespace="prod"} [5m])))
Regex filter: {app="worker"} |~ "job_id=\d+ failed"
Unwrap numeric field: sum_over_time({app="api"} | json | unwrap duration_ms [5m])
Count distinct errors: count_over_time({app="api"} |= "unhandled exception" [1h])

root_cause: Loki requires an object storage backend (S3, GCS, Azure Blob, or S3-compatible like MinIO) for chunk storage in scalable deployments. Misconfigured credentials, bucket names, or endpoint URLs cause ingesters to fail writing chunks and data loss on restart.
fix: Configure S3-compatible (MinIO) in loki config: storage_config.aws.s3=s3://access:secret@minio:9000/loki-chunks
Set schema_config.configs[].store=tsdb and object_store=s3 for Loki 2.8+
For in-cluster MinIO: aws.endpoint=http://minio.minio.svc:9000, aws.s3forcepathstyle=true
Store credentials via Kubernetes Secret and reference with envFrom in Loki Deployment
Test connectivity: kubectl exec -n loki deploy/loki -- curl -s http://minio.minio.svc:9000/loki-chunks
Check chunk upload errors: kubectl logs -n loki deploy/loki | grep -i 'failed to put object'

symptoms: how to delete old loki logs
root_cause: Loki's compactor component manages TSDB index compaction and log retention/deletion. Without retention configuration, log data accumulates indefinitely in object storage. Compactor must be enabled and retention_enabled set to true for automatic cleanup.
fix: Enable compactor in loki config: compactor.working_directory=/loki/compactor, compactor.shared_store=s3
Set global retention: limits_config.retention_period=30d
Enable retention: compactor.retention_enabled=true
Per-tenant override in ruler_config or overrides: overrides.<tenant>.retention_period=7d
Verify compaction: kubectl logs -n loki deploy/loki-compactor | grep -i compaction
Check compactor metrics: loki_compactor_runs_completed_total, loki_compactor_blocks_cleaned_total
Helm values: loki.compactor.retention_enabled=true, loki.limits_config.retention_period=720h

symptoms: how to ship logs with vector to loki
root_cause: Vector is a high-performance log collector that can collect Kubernetes pod logs via the kubernetes_logs source and forward them to Loki. Proper configuration of Vector's sinks and label enrichment ensures logs are queryable by namespace, pod, and container.
fix: Deploy Vector as DaemonSet: helm upgrade --install vector vector/vector -f vector-values.yaml
Configure kubernetes_logs source in vector.yaml: sources.k8s_logs.type=kubernetes_logs
Add Loki sink: sinks.loki.type=loki, sinks.loki.endpoint=http://loki-gateway.loki.svc:80
Map labels: sinks.loki.labels.namespace={{ kubernetes.pod_namespace }}, labels.pod={{ kubernetes.pod_name }}
Enable pod metadata enrichment: sources.k8s_logs.extra_label_selector=app
Parse JSON logs: transforms.parse_json.type=remap, transforms.parse_json.source='. = parse_json!(.message)'
Verify with: kubectl exec -n vector ds/vector -- vector top

symptoms: how to configure fluent bit for loki
root_cause: Fluent Bit is a lightweight log processor and forwarder commonly deployed as a DaemonSet in Kubernetes. It must be configured with the Kubernetes filter for metadata enrichment and the Loki output plugin to ship logs. Multiline support requires specific parser configuration.
fix: Install via Helm: helm upgrade --install fluent-bit fluent/fluent-bit -f fb-values.yaml
Configure Kubernetes filter: [FILTER] Name kubernetes, Kube_URL https://kubernetes.default.svc:443
Set Loki output: [OUTPUT] Name loki, host loki-gateway.loki.svc, port 80, labels job=fluentbit, namespace=$kubernetes['namespace_name'], pod=$kubernetes['pod_name']
Multiline for Node.js stack traces: [FILTER] Name multiline, multiline.key_content log, multiline.parser go (or custom regex parser)
Custom multiline parser: [MULTILINE_PARSER] Name nodejs_stack, type regex, flush_timeout 1000, rule 'start_state' '/^\{/' 'cont_state', rule 'cont_state' '/^\s/' 'cont_state'
Verify output: kubectl logs -n logging ds/fluent-bit | grep -i loki

symptoms: how to configure pino for loki
root_cause: Node.js applications should emit structured JSON logs so that Loki's JSON parser can extract fields for filtering and alerting. Pino is the recommended logger for Node.js due to its low overhead and built-in JSON output. Proper configuration ensures level, timestamp, and context fields are consistently present.
fix: Install: npm install pino pino-pretty
Create logger: const logger = pino({ level: process.env.LOG_LEVEL || 'info', timestamp: pino.stdTimeFunctions.isoTime })
Add request context: logger.child({ requestId: req.id, userId: req.user?.id }).info({ msg: 'request handled', durationMs: elapsed })
Never log at DEBUG in production; set LOG_LEVEL via Kubernetes ConfigMap/env
Use pino-http for automatic request logging: app.use(pinoHttp({ logger }))
Ensure no circular refs: avoid logging entire request/response objects
In Loki, query with: {app="api"} | json | level="error" | line_format "{{.msg}} traceId={{.traceId}}"

root_cause: Querying Loki without a stream selector that narrows to a small set of streams forces a full scan across all stored chunks. This is the most common performance anti-pattern: using broad selectors like {namespace="prod"} without additional label filters.
fix: Always start with the most specific stream selector possible: {namespace="prod", app="api", container="api"}
Add label matchers before log pipeline filters to reduce chunk reads
Avoid {namespace=~".+"} which matches all streams
Use --since flag in logcli to limit time range: logcli query '{app="api"}' --since=1h
Set query_timeout in loki config to prevent runaway queries: querier.query_timeout=2m
Enable query sharding for large queries: query_scheduler.max_outstanding_requests_per_tenant=200
Monitor slow queries: loki_request_duration_seconds_bucket metric in Prometheus

symptoms: loki logs not appearing in grafana
root_cause: Logs may be missing in Loki due to issues at any point in the pipeline: collector not running, push failing to Loki distributor, ingester not flushing chunks, or query targeting wrong labels. Systematic diagnosis requires checking each stage.
fix: 1. Verify collector is running: kubectl get pods -n logging -l app=vector (or fluent-bit)
2. Check collector logs for push errors: kubectl logs -n logging ds/vector | grep -i 'error\|failed'

3. Test direct push to Loki: curl -XPOST http://loki-gateway.loki.svc/loki/api/v1/push -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"streams":[{"stream":{"job":"test"},"values":[["$(date +%s)000000000","test log line"]]}]}'
4. Query via logcli: logcli query '{job="test"}' --addr=http://loki-gateway.loki.svc
5. Check Loki distributor metrics: loki_distributor_lines_received_total
6. Check ingester WAL: kubectl exec -n loki deploy/loki-ingester -- ls /loki/wal
7. Validate Grafana datasource URL points to loki-gateway service, not individual pods

symptoms: loki out of order ingestion error
logs arriving out of order loki
root_cause: Loki requires that log entries within a single stream are pushed in chronological order. When multiple collectors or application instances push logs for the same stream with timestamps that arrive out of order, Loki rejects entries with HTTP 400 'entry out of order'. This commonly happens with buffered log collectors flushing stale entries.
fix: Enable out-of-order ingestion in loki config: ingester.unordered_writes=true (Loki 2.6+)
Set max_chunk_age to tolerate late arrivals: ingester.max_chunk_age=2h
Configure chunk_idle_period longer than collector flush interval: ingester.chunk_idle_period=30m
In Vector: set buffer.type=disk and buffer.max_size to avoid drops on restart
In Fluent Bit: set Retry_Limit False and storage.type filesystem for durability
Monitor rejected entries: loki_distributor_ingester_append_failures_total metric

root_cause: Loki ingesters hold active chunks in memory until flush. High stream count, long chunk_idle_period, or excessive chunk_target_size cause memory to grow beyond pod limits. In Kubernetes, ingesters are commonly OOMKilled under high log volume.
fix: Tune chunk_idle_period: ingester.chunk_idle_period=15m (shorter = more frequent flushes, less memory)
Set chunk_target_size: ingester.chunk_target_size=1572864 (1.5MB compressed)
Limit concurrent flushes: ingester.concurrent_flushes=16
Set Kubernetes resource requests/limits: requests.memory=2Gi, limits.memory=4Gi per ingester pod
Scale ingesters horizontally and enable WAL: ingester.wal.enabled=true, ingester.wal.dir=/loki/wal
Monitor: loki_ingester_memory_chunks, loki_ingester_chunks_flushed_total in Prometheus
Enable zone-aware replication for HA ingesters: ingester.availability_zone per pod

symptoms: vector fluent bit kubernetes metadata missing
root_cause: Log collectors need RBAC permissions to query the Kubernetes API for pod metadata enrichment. Without a ClusterRole granting get/list/watch on pods, the Kubernetes filter/source cannot attach namespace, pod name, labels, and annotations to log records.
fix: Create ClusterRole for Vector/Fluent Bit: rules: [{apiGroups:[""], resources:["pods","namespaces"], verbs:["get","list","watch"]}]
Bind to ServiceAccount: kubectl create clusterrolebinding vector-logs --clusterrole=vector-k8s-reader --serviceaccount=logging:vector
In Fluent Bit Helm values: rbac.create=true, serviceAccount.create=true
In Vector Helm values: rbac.create=true
Verify annotation enrichment: kubectl logs -n logging ds/vector | grep -i 'metadata'
Test metadata is present in Loki: {namespace="prod", app="api"} | json | pod != ""

symptoms: fluent bit multiline nodejs stack trace
stack trace split across lines loki
root_cause: Node.js stack traces span multiple lines. Without multiline parsing configuration, each line is ingested as a separate log entry in Loki, making stack traces impossible to correlate. Fluent Bit's multiline filter must be configured to detect and concatenate continuation lines.
fix: Define multiline parser in parsers.conf: [MULTILINE_PARSER] Name nodejs_exception, Type regex, flush_timeout 1000
Add start rule: rule 'start_state' '/Error:|UnhandledPromiseRejection/' 'stack_state'
Add continuation rule: rule 'stack_state' '/^\s+at /' 'stack_state'
Reference in fluent-bit.conf: [FILTER] Name multiline, match *, multiline.key_content log, multiline.parser nodejs_exception
Mount parsers.conf as ConfigMap volume: volumeMounts: [{name: parsers, mountPath: /fluent-bit/etc/parsers_multiline.conf}]
Pass parsers file: [SERVICE] Parsers_File /fluent-bit/etc/parsers_multiline.conf
Verify: kubectl logs -n logging ds/fluent-bit -f | grep -A5 'Error:'

symptoms: nodejs pino log fields best practice
log correlation with traces loki tempo
what fields to include in structured logs
root_cause: Consistent log field naming across all Node.js services enables cross-service correlation in Grafana using Loki and Tempo. Without standard fields like traceId, spanId, and requestId, correlating logs to traces requires manual effort.
fix: Always include: { level, time (ISO8601), msg, traceId, spanId, requestId, service, version }
Inject OpenTelemetry trace context: import { trace } from '@opentelemetry/api'; const span = trace.getActiveSpan(); const { traceId, spanId } = span?.spanContext() ?? {}
Add traceId to pino child logger: req.log = logger.child({ traceId, spanId, requestId: req.id })
Configure Grafana Loki derived fields: Field name=traceId, Regex=traceId=(\w+), Internal link to Tempo datasource
Standardize error logging: logger.error({ err, traceId, msg: 'operation failed' }) — pino serializes Error objects via err serializer
Use pino.destination for async logging to avoid blocking event loop in high-throughput services

root_cause: Deploying Loki to production without verifying HA, retention, resource limits, and access control commonly leads to data loss, runaway storage growth, or query outages. A systematic checklist prevents these issues.
fix: [ ] Replication factor >= 3 for ingesters: ingester.lifecycler.ring.replication_factor=3
[ ] WAL enabled for ingesters: ingester.wal.enabled=true with persistent volume
[ ] Object storage backend configured (not local filesystem) for multi-replica deployments
[ ] Compactor enabled with retention_enabled=true and retention_period set
[ ] Resource requests and limits set on all Loki components
[ ] Loki alert rules deployed for ingestion errors and query latency
[ ] Grafana datasource verified with test query returning data
[ ] logcli available for CLI-based troubleshooting: brew install logcli or docker run grafana/logcli

symptoms: loki compactor not deleting old data
loki storage not decreasing after retention period
root_cause: Loki's compactor only performs retention deletes when both compactor.retention_enabled=true AND the compactor has successfully completed at least one full compaction cycle. A common mistake is setting retention_period without enabling the retention flag, or deploying compactor without write access to the object store.
fix: Verify retention is enabled: grep -A5 'compactor:' /etc/loki/config.yaml | grep retention_enabled
Ensure compactor has object store write permissions (bucket IAM policy or MinIO policy)
Check compactor completed compaction: kubectl logs -n loki deploy/loki-compactor | grep 'compaction cycle'
Verify retention_period is set under limits_config: limits_config.retention_period=720h
For per-stream retention, use ruler_config.storage or overrides file (Loki 2.8+)
Force immediate compaction check: kubectl delete pod -n loki -l app=loki-compactor (compactor restarts and re-evaluates)
Monitor: loki_compactor_apply_retention_last_successful_run_at metric

root_cause: Loki enforces per-tenant ingestion rate limits via limits_config.ingestion_rate_mb and ingestion_burst_size_mb. When a burst of logs from a collector or application restart exceeds these limits, the distributor returns HTTP 429, causing collectors to retry and potentially drop logs.
fix: Increase ingestion limits in loki config: limits_config.ingestion_rate_mb=32, limits_config.ingestion_burst_size_mb=64
Per-tenant override: overrides.<tenant-id>.ingestion_rate_mb=64
In Vector, configure buffer to absorb bursts: buffer.type=disk, buffer.max_size=1073741824 (1GB)
In Fluent Bit: set Retry_Limit 5 and use storage.type filesystem
Monitor rate limit hits: loki_distributor_limited_samples_total metric
Check current limits via Loki API: curl http://loki:3100/loki/api/v1/status/buildinfo
Scale distributors horizontally if sustained ingestion rate is high

root_cause: Understanding recurring log patterns in Loki's own logs helps operators quickly identify operational issues. Key patterns include chunk flush failures, ring membership changes, and query timeout warnings.
fix: Chunk flush failure: 'failed to flush chunk' — check object storage connectivity and credentials
Ring member unhealthy: 'instance not found in ring' — ingester crashed without deregistering; run: kubectl exec -n loki deploy/loki-ingester -- curl -XDELETE http://localhost:3100/ring?addr=<pod-ip>
Query timeout: 'context deadline exceeded' — reduce query time range or add more specific label selectors
WAL recovery: 'replaying WAL segment' — normal after pod restart; check recovery completes
Compaction skipped: 'compaction is not needed' — normal if no new data in period
Push rejected: 'grpc: received message larger than max' — increase grpc.server.max-recv-msg-size in config
Monitor all with: kubectl logs -n loki -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=loki --since=10m | grep -E 'level=error|level=warn'

symptoms: how to parse json logs in vector
root_cause: Vector's Remap transform using VRL (Vector Remap Language) is the recommended way to parse, enrich, and normalize log records before shipping to Loki. Structuring logs consistently at the collection layer reduces the need for expensive LogQL parsing at query time.
fix: Parse JSON log body: . = parse_json!(.message) ?? { "msg": .message }
Extract level and normalize: .level = downcase(string!(.level ?? .severity ?? "info"))
Add Kubernetes metadata as top-level fields: .namespace = .kubernetes.pod_namespace; .pod = .kubernetes.pod_name
Remove noisy fields: del(.kubernetes.pod_labels, .file)
Conditionally parse: if is_string(.message) && starts_with(.message, "{") { . = merge(., parse_json!(.message)) }
Add ingestion timestamp: .ingest_time = now()
Test VRL locally: vector vrl --input sample.json --program transform.vrl

symptoms: how to deploy otel collector as daemonset in kubernetes
collect traces from all pods with otel
root_cause: Deploying the OpenTelemetry Collector as a DaemonSet ensures every node has a local collector endpoint, minimizing network hops and avoiding single-point-of-failure in trace collection. Each pod sends traces to the node-local collector via localhost or the node IP, which then forwards to Grafana Tempo.
fix: helm repo add open-telemetry https://open-telemetry.github.io/opentelemetry-helm-charts
helm install otel-collector open-telemetry/opentelemetry-collector --namespace observability --create-namespace --set mode=daemonset
Set OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://$(NODE_IP):4317 in pod env using fieldRef: fieldPath: status.hostIP
Add hostNetwork: false and use service with clusterIP: None for daemonset headless discovery
Configure resources.limits.memory: 512Mi and resources.requests.memory: 256Mi to prevent OOM on busy nodes
Verify with: kubectl get pods -n observability -l app.kubernetes.io/name=opentelemetry-collector

symptoms: how to deploy otel collector as deployment for centralized tracing
root_cause: A central OTel Collector Deployment acts as a gateway/aggregator that receives traces from DaemonSet collectors or directly from pods, applies tail-based sampling, and exports to Tempo. This pattern is recommended when you need cross-node sampling decisions or centralized filtering.
fix: helm install otel-gateway open-telemetry/opentelemetry-collector --namespace observability --set mode=deployment --set replicaCount=2
Configure pipeline in values.yaml: receivers: [otlp], processors: [batch, tail_sampling], exporters: [otlp/tempo]
Add tail_sampling processor: { decision_wait: 10s, num_traces: 100000, expected_new_traces_per_sec: 1000, policies: [{name: errors, type: status_code, status_code: {status_codes: [ERROR]}}] }
Set HPA: kubectl autoscale deployment otel-gateway --cpu-percent=70 --min=2 --max=8 -n observability
Point daemonset exporters to gateway service: endpoint: otel-gateway-collector.observability.svc.cluster.local:4317
Verify load balancing: kubectl top pods -n observability -l app=otel-gateway

symptoms: how to auto-instrument nodejs with opentelemetry
root_cause: Node.js applications require the @opentelemetry/sdk-node package initialized before any application code to automatically instrument HTTP, gRPC, Redis, and other libraries via require-in-the-middle hooks. The SDK must be imported as the first file using --require flag to intercept all module loads.
fix: npm install @opentelemetry/sdk-node @opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node @opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-grpc

Create tracing.js: const { NodeSDK } = require('@opentelemetry/sdk-node'); const { getNodeAutoInstrumentations } = require('@opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node'); const { OTLPTraceExporter } = require('@opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-grpc');
Init SDK: const sdk = new NodeSDK({ traceExporter: new OTLPTraceExporter({ url: process.env.OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT }), instrumentations: [getNodeAutoInstrumentations()] }); sdk.start();
Start with: node --require ./tracing.js server.js or set NODE_OPTIONS=--require ./tracing.js in Dockerfile/pod spec
Set env vars in pod: OTEL_SERVICE_NAME, OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES=k8s.pod.name=$(POD_NAME),k8s.namespace.name=$(NAMESPACE)
Verify spans appear in Tempo: kubectl logs -n observability <otel-collector-pod> | grep 'received spans'

root_cause: W3C Trace Context propagation requires all services to use the same propagator. If one service uses B3 headers while another uses W3C traceparent, the trace chain breaks and Tempo shows disconnected spans instead of a unified trace tree.
fix: Ensure all Node.js services configure W3C propagator: const { W3CTraceContextPropagator } = require('@opentelemetry/core'); sdk = new NodeSDK({ textMapPropagator: new W3CTraceContextPropagator(), ... })
Set env var globally: OTEL_PROPAGATORS=tracecontext,baggage in all pod specs
Verify traceparent header is forwarded through API gateways: kubectl exec <gateway-pod> -- curl -v http://backend-svc/health and check for traceparent header
For BullMQ jobs, manually propagate context: const carrier = {}; propagation.inject(context.active(), carrier); await queue.add('job', { payload, traceContext: carrier })
In worker, restore context: const parentCtx = propagation.extract(context.active(), job.data.traceContext); context.with(parentCtx, () => { /* work */ })
Validate end-to-end trace in Grafana Tempo: search by traceID and verify all service spans appear in waterfall view

symptoms: how to propagate traces through bullmq jobs
trace context across async worker queues
distributed tracing with redis bullmq workers
root_cause: BullMQ jobs are async and serialized to Redis, breaking the in-process OpenTelemetry context chain. The W3C traceparent and tracestate values must be manually serialized into the job data payload and re-extracted in the worker to maintain trace continuity across the API-to-worker boundary.
fix: In API producer: import { context, propagation } from '@opentelemetry/api'; const carrier = {}; propagation.inject(context.active(), carrier);
Add carrier to job: await queue.add('process-ai-task', { userId, prompt, _otelCtx: carrier }, { attempts: 3 });
In BullMQ worker processor: const parentCtx = propagation.extract(context.active(), job.data._otelCtx);

Wrap work in context: const tracer = trace.getTracer('worker'); return context.with(parentCtx, () => tracer.startActiveSpan('worker.process', async (span) => { try { const result = await doWork(job); span.setStatus({code: SpanStatusCode.OK}); return result; } catch(e) { span.recordException(e); span.setStatus({code: SpanStatusCode.ERROR}); throw e; } finally { span.end(); } }));
Add span attributes: span.setAttributes({ 'bullmq.queue': job.queueName, 'bullmq.job.id': job.id, 'bullmq.attempts': job.attemptsMade });
Verify parent-child relationship in Tempo: the API span traceId should match the worker span traceId

symptoms: how to trace external AI API calls openai anthropic
distributed tracing for langchain openai calls
root_cause: Calls to external AI APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini) via Cilium Egress Gateway require explicit span creation since these SDKs are not auto-instrumented by default OTel node packages. Manual instrumentation captures latency, token counts, model names, and error rates for AI cost/performance observability.
fix: const tracer = trace.getTracer('ai-service'); const span = tracer.startSpan('openai.chat.completion', { kind: SpanKind.CLIENT });

Set semantic attributes: span.setAttributes({ 'gen_ai.system': 'openai', 'gen_ai.request.model': 'gpt-4o', 'gen_ai.request.max_tokens': 1000, 'server.address': 'api.openai.com', 'server.port': 443 });
Wrap API call: const ctx = trace.setSpan(context.active(), span); const result = await context.with(ctx, () => openai.chat.completions.create({ model, messages }));

Record response metrics: span.setAttributes({ 'gen_ai.response.model': result.model, 'gen_ai.usage.input_tokens': result.usage.prompt_tokens, 'gen_ai.usage.output_tokens': result.usage.completion_tokens });
Handle errors: catch(e) { span.recordException(e); span.setStatus({code: SpanStatusCode.ERROR, message: e.message}); throw e; } finally { span.end(); }
Install @opentelemetry/instrumentation-langchain if using LangChain.js for automatic span creation per chain/tool

symptoms: otel collector receivers not receiving traces
root_cause: The OTel Collector OTLP receiver must explicitly bind to 0.0.0.0 on both gRPC (4317) and HTTP (4318) ports. Default configurations may bind only to localhost, making the receiver unreachable from other pods in the cluster.
fix: In collector config.yaml set: receivers: { otlp: { protocols: { grpc: { endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4317 }, http: { endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4318 } } } }
Verify service exposes ports: kubectl get svc otel-collector -n observability -o yaml | grep -A5 ports
Test connectivity from app pod: kubectl exec <app-pod> -- nc -zv otel-collector.observability.svc.cluster.local 4317
Check collector logs for bind errors: kubectl logs -n observability -l app=otel-collector | grep -i 'error\|bind\|listen'
Ensure NetworkPolicy allows ingress to collector on 4317/4318 from app namespace: kubectl get netpol -n observability
If using Cilium, verify policy: kubectl exec -n kube-system -l k8s-app=cilium -- cilium policy get | grep 4317

symptoms: how to configure sampling strategy opentelemetry
reduce trace volume without losing important traces
otel head vs tail sampling best practice
root_cause: Without sampling, high-throughput services generate enormous trace volumes overwhelming storage. Head-based sampling makes decisions at trace start (cheap but misses error correlation), while tail-based sampling in the OTel Collector can keep 100% of error/slow traces while dropping healthy fast ones.
fix: For head sampling in SDK: const { TraceIdRatioBased } = require('@opentelemetry/sdk-trace-base'); sdk = new NodeSDK({ sampler: new TraceIdRatioBased(0.1) }) // 10% of traces

For tail sampling in collector config: processors: { tail_sampling: { decision_wait: 10s, num_traces: 50000, policies: [ {name: errors, type: status_code, status_code: {status_codes: [ERROR]}}, {name: slow-traces, type: latency, latency: {threshold_ms: 1000}}, {name: probabilistic, type: probabilistic, probabilistic: {sampling_percentage: 5}} ] } }
Always sample 100% of error spans: set OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER=parentbased_traceidratio and OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG=0.1
Use AlwaysOn for internal health checks exclusion: configure filter processor to drop spans where http.target matches /health or /metrics
Monitor sampling impact: check tempo_distributor_spans_received_total vs tempo_ingester_spans_ingested_total metrics in Grafana
Document sampling rates in runbook: store OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG in ConfigMap for easy adjustment without redeployment

symptoms: how to correlate traces with logs grafana
link loki logs to tempo traces
trace id in log lines kubernetes
root_cause: Trace-to-log correlation requires injecting the current traceId and spanId into every log line. Grafana can then link from a Loki log entry directly to the corresponding Tempo trace when logs contain the traceId field and Grafana datasources are configured with derived fields.
fix: In Node.js, inject trace context into Winston/Pino logger: const { trace } = require('@opentelemetry/api'); logger.child({ traceId: trace.getActiveSpan()?.spanContext().traceId, spanId: trace.getActiveSpan()?.spanContext().spanId })
Use pino-opentelemetry-transport or winston-opentelemetry for automatic injection
Configure Fluent Bit or Vector to forward logs with traceId field preserved as structured JSON
In Grafana Loki datasource settings, add Derived Field: Name=TraceID, Regex=traceId":"(\w+)", URL=${__value.raw}, Internal Link=Tempo datasource
Verify correlation: run a request, find its log in Loki with LogQL: {app="api"} | json | traceId="<id>", click TraceID link to see Tempo waterfall
Add span attributes for log correlation: span.setAttributes({ 'log.file': 'api.log', 'log.iostream': 'stdout' })

symptoms: how to add exemplars to prometheus metrics for trace correlation
link metrics to traces with exemplars
root_cause: Prometheus exemplars attach a traceId to specific metric samples, enabling Grafana to show a 'jump to trace' link directly from a metric spike in a dashboard. This requires Prometheus to be configured with exemplar storage enabled and the application to emit exemplars alongside histogram metrics.
fix: Enable exemplar storage in Prometheus: add --enable-feature=exemplar-storage to prometheus args in Helm values
In Node.js with prom-client, record exemplar: histogram.observe({ traceId: trace.getActiveSpan()?.spanContext().traceId }, durationMs)
Alternatively use @opentelemetry/exporter-prometheus which auto-attaches exemplars to histograms
Verify exemplars stored: curl http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query_exemplars?query=http_request_duration_seconds
In Grafana dashboard panel using histogram_quantile, enable 'Query exemplars' toggle in the panel settings
Set Grafana Tempo as exemplar datasource: in Prometheus datasource config set exemplarTraceIdDestinations with name=traceId and datasourceUid pointing to Tempo

symptoms: too many spans per trace performance issue
otel instrumentation causing high cpu usage nodejs
trace too verbose slowing down application
root_cause: Over-instrumentation — creating spans for every function call, Redis HGET, or tight loop iteration — creates thousands of spans per request, causing CPU overhead from span creation/serialization and flooding Tempo with unusable noisy traces that obscure real latency distributions.
fix: Disable noisy auto-instrumentation modules: getNodeAutoInstrumentations({ '@opentelemetry/instrumentation-fs': { enabled: false }, '@opentelemetry/instrumentation-dns': { enabled: false } })
Set minimum duration filter in collector: processors: { filter: { spans: { exclude: { match_type: expr, expressions: ['duration < 5ms && status.code == STATUS_CODE_OK'] } } } }
Avoid creating spans in hot loops; instead aggregate: use a single span wrapping the loop with span.addEvent() for notable iterations only
For Redis, limit instrumentation to slow commands: configure @opentelemetry/instrumentation-ioredis with dbStatementSerializer to truncate large values
Profile span overhead: OTEL_LOG_LEVEL=debug node app.js and observe span creation rate in logs
Target 10-50 spans per API request; use Tempo query: count() by (root_name) in TraceQL to identify bloated trace types

root_cause: Traces can silently drop at multiple points: SDK not initialized before app code, exporter connection failures, collector queue overflow, Tempo ingestion limits, or incorrect TraceQL queries filtering out results. Systematic diagnosis must check each pipeline stage.
fix: Step 1 - Verify SDK emitting: OTEL_LOG_LEVEL=debug node --require ./tracing.js app.js and look for 'exporting spans' log lines
Step 2 - Check exporter connectivity: kubectl exec <app-pod> -- curl -v http://otel-collector.observability:4318/v1/traces -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{}'
Step 3 - Check collector receiving: kubectl logs -n observability -l app=otel-collector | grep -E 'TracesExporter|dropped|error'
Step 4 - Verify Tempo ingestion: kubectl logs -n observability -l app=tempo | grep -E 'error|warn|ingestion'
Step 5 - Check Tempo ingestion limits: kubectl exec -n observability <tempo-pod> -- wget -qO- http://localhost:3200/api/status | jq .ingestion
Step 6 - Query Tempo directly: curl 'http://tempo.observability:3200/api/search?limit=5' and verify results exist before blaming Grafana UI

symptoms: otel collector out of memory crash
root_cause: The OTel Collector's memory_limiter processor is not configured, allowing unbounded memory growth when downstream exporters (Tempo) are slow or unavailable. The batch processor's queue fills up and the collector OOMKills, losing all buffered traces.
fix: Add memory_limiter as first processor in pipeline: processors: { memory_limiter: { check_interval: 1s, limit_mib: 400, spike_limit_mib: 128 } }
Pipeline order matters: processors: [memory_limiter, batch, tail_sampling] — memory_limiter MUST be first
Configure batch processor with queue: batch: { send_batch_size: 1000, timeout: 10s, send_batch_max_size: 1500 }
Enable persistent queue for the exporter: exporters: { otlp/tempo: { sending_queue: { enabled: true, storage: file_storage, num_consumers: 4, queue_size: 1000 } } }
Set collector pod memory limits 25% above memory_limiter.limit_mib: resources: { limits: { memory: 512Mi } } with limit_mib: 400
Alert on queue drops: otelcol_exporter_queue_size and otelcol_processor_dropped_spans Prometheus metrics

symptoms: what span attributes to set for api tracing
semantic conventions for http spans opentelemetry
root_cause: Using OpenTelemetry semantic conventions for span attributes ensures consistency across services and enables Tempo's automatic service graph and Grafana's pre-built dashboards to work correctly. Non-standard attribute names create fragmented observability data.
fix: HTTP server spans must include: http.request.method, url.path, http.response.status_code, server.address, network.protocol.version
Database spans (Redis/PostgreSQL): db.system='redis'|'postgresql', db.operation, db.statement (sanitized, no PII), server.address
Message queue spans (BullMQ/NATS): messaging.system='bullmq'|'nats', messaging.operation='publish'|'receive', messaging.destination.name=<queue-name>
AI/LLM spans: gen_ai.system, gen_ai.request.model, gen_ai.usage.input_tokens, gen_ai.usage.output_tokens per GenAI semconv
K8s resource attributes: k8s.pod.name, k8s.namespace.name, k8s.node.name — inject via OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES using Downward API
Check semconv compliance: use otel-lint or review https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/ before shipping to production

symptoms: redis spans showing wrong service name
bullmq traces attributed to wrong service
root_cause: The @opentelemetry/instrumentation-ioredis package may emit Redis spans with db.system attribute missing or set to 'redis' at the wrong scope, and the peer.service attribute defaults to the Redis hostname which differs between environments, breaking service map edges in Grafana Tempo's service graph.
fix: Pin peer.service explicitly in instrumentation config: new IORedisInstrumentation({ dbStatementSerializer: (cmdName, cmdArgs) => `${cmdName} ${cmdArgs[0]}`, requireParentSpan: true })
Add spanNameHook to normalize: dbSystemHook: (host, port) => 'redis-cluster'
Set OTEL_SERVICE_NAME consistently for each service in pod spec as env var, not in code
Override peer.service in collector via attributes processor: processors: { attributes: { actions: [ {key: peer.service, value: redis-cluster, action: upsert, pattern: 'redis.*'} ] } }
Verify service graph in Tempo: Grafana > Explore > Tempo > Service Graph tab should show API→redis-cluster edge
Upgrade to latest @opentelemetry/instrumentation-ioredis: npm list @opentelemetry/instrumentation-ioredis and check against https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-js-contrib/releases

symptoms: grafana tempo slow query on large trace volumes
root_cause: Grafana Tempo's search performance degrades when querying across large time windows without using TraceQL filters. Additionally, high-cardinality span attributes like user IDs or request bodies in span names create excessive index size, slowing backend search on object storage (S3/Ceph).
fix: Always use TraceQL for search: { .service.name="api" && duration > 500ms && status = error } instead of generic search
Limit span name cardinality: never use userId or requestId in span names — use them as span attributes instead
Configure Tempo compaction in helm values: compactor: { compaction: { block_retention: 336h, compacted_block_retention: 1h } }
Enable search index: tempo.storage.trace.backend.search_encoding: snappy for faster index reads
Use time range limits: add query_timeout: 30s and max_bytes_per_tag_values_query: 5MB to Tempo config
Monitor Tempo query latency: tempodb_backend_read_latency_seconds and tempo_query_frontend_duration_seconds in Prometheus

symptoms: how to validate otel pipeline is working
root_cause: Production OTel Collector deployments require validating receivers, processors, exporters, resource limits, security, and monitoring to avoid silent data loss. Missing any component can cause trace gaps that are hard to diagnose under load.
fix: [ ] Receiver: otlp grpc/http listening on 0.0.0.0, confirmed with netstat -tlnp from within pod
[ ] Processors: memory_limiter (first), batch, optional tail_sampling — verify with /metrics endpoint: otelcol_processor_batch_batch_size_trigger_send
[ ] Exporter: tempo otlp endpoint connectivity verified with grpc_health_probe -addr=tempo:4317
[ ] Resources: memory limit set 25% above memory_limiter.limit_mib, CPU request set to avoid throttling
[ ] RBAC: collector ServiceAccount has no cluster-level write permissions unless using k8sattributes processor
[ ] Monitoring: Prometheus scraping otelcol /metrics on port 8888, alert on otelcol_exporter_send_failed_spans > 0
[ ] Security: disable unused receivers/extensions, set auth on OTLP receiver if exposed outside cluster
[ ] Persistence: file_storage extension configured for retry queue to survive pod restarts

symptoms: how to name spans in nodejs opentelemetry
root_cause: Inconsistent span naming prevents effective aggregation in Tempo's TraceQL queries and breaks service dependency graphs. OpenTelemetry semantic conventions define specific naming patterns for different span kinds to ensure uniformity across services and tools.
fix: HTTP server spans: use 'HTTP {METHOD}' format e.g. 'HTTP GET' — auto-instrumentation handles this correctly
HTTP client spans: '{METHOD} {target}' — avoid including dynamic path params: use 'GET /users/{id}' not 'GET /users/12345'
Database spans: '{db.operation} {db.name}.{collection}' e.g. 'SELECT postgres.users'
Message consumer spans: '{messaging.operation} {destination}' e.g. 'receive process-ai-task'
Internal spans for business logic: use verb-noun format: 'parseRequest', 'validateToken', 'enrichContext'
Never include variable data in span names — put in attributes: BAD: tracer.startSpan('process user 42'), GOOD: span = tracer.startSpan('processUser'); span.setAttribute('user.id', 42)

symptoms: otel collector k8s attributes missing pod name node name
root_cause: The k8sattributes processor enriches spans with Kubernetes metadata (pod name, namespace, node name, labels) by calling the Kubernetes API. Without proper RBAC permissions, the processor silently fails to enrich spans, resulting in traces missing critical infrastructure context.
fix: Create ClusterRole for collector: rules: [{apiGroups: [''], resources: [pods, namespaces, nodes], verbs: [get, list, watch]}, {apiGroups: [apps], resources: [replicasets], verbs: [get, list, watch]}]
Bind to collector ServiceAccount: kubectl create clusterrolebinding otel-collector-k8s --clusterrole=otel-k8s-reader --serviceaccount=observability:otel-collector

Configure processor in collector config: processors: { k8sattributes: { auth_type: serviceAccount, passthrough: false, extract: { metadata: [k8s.pod.name, k8s.namespace.name, k8s.node.name, k8s.deployment.name] }, pod_association: [{sources: [{from: connection}]}] } }
Verify enrichment: kubectl exec <app-pod> -- grpcurl -plaintext otel-collector:4317 opentelemetry.proto.collector.trace.v1.TraceService/Export and check returned attributes
Check processor logs: kubectl logs -n observability <collector-pod> | grep k8sattributes
For DaemonSet mode, set filter_node: ${NODE_NAME} using env var from Downward API to only watch local node's pods

symptoms: how to design tracing pipeline for api redis bullmq workers
end to end distributed tracing architecture nodejs kubernetes
trace flow from api to worker to external ai
root_cause: A complete distributed tracing architecture for a Node.js API + BullMQ + external AI call stack requires careful design of context propagation boundaries, sampling tiers, and collector topology to achieve full trace visibility without overwhelming storage or adding prohibitive latency.
fix: Tier 1 - Application: Node.js API pod emits spans via @opentelemetry/sdk-node to local DaemonSet collector (localhost:4317); W3C traceparent propagated in HTTP headers between services
Tier 2 - Queue bridge: API serializes active context into BullMQ job data (_otelCtx field); worker deserializes and creates child span linked to API parent span using context.with(parentCtx, ...)
Tier 3 - External AI calls: Worker creates CLIENT span for each OpenAI/Anthropic API call via Cilium Egress Gateway; span includes gen_ai.* attributes for token tracking
Tier 4 - Collector DaemonSet: receives spans, adds k8s metadata via k8sattributesprocessor, batches and forwards to central gateway collector
Tier 5 - Collector Gateway: applies tail-based sampling (keep all errors + slow > 1s + 5% of healthy), forwards to Grafana Tempo via OTLP gRPC
Tier 6 - Storage and Query: Tempo stores traces on Ceph/S3 object storage with 14-day retention; Grafana dashboards link traces to Loki logs (traceId derived field) and Prometheus metrics (exemplars)

symptoms: how to structure helm chart for production
root_cause: A well-structured Helm chart separates concerns into templates/, values.yaml, Chart.yaml, and optional subcharts in charts/, enabling clean overrides per environment and maintainable CI/CD pipelines.
fix: Use Chart.yaml with apiVersion: v2, name, version, appVersion, and dependencies list
Place default values in values.yaml; never hardcode environment-specific values in templates
Create templates/_helpers.tpl with named templates for labels, selectors, and fullname
Use values-<env>.yaml (e.g. values-prod.yaml, values-staging.yaml) and pass with: helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart -f values-prod.yaml
Store secrets references (not values) in values.yaml; inject via External Secrets Operator annotations
Add .helmignore to exclude README.md, tests/, .git from packaged chart

root_cause: A Helm release enters PENDING_UPGRADE state when a previous upgrade was interrupted (pod killed, timeout, SIGTERM) before Helm wrote the success secret, leaving the release lock in a broken state.
fix: Check release state: helm history myapp -n mynamespace
Identify the failed revision number from history output
Roll back to last good revision: helm rollback myapp <revision> -n mynamespace
If rollback also fails, manually delete the broken release secret: kubectl delete secret -n mynamespace -l owner=helm,name=myapp,status=pending-upgrade
Re-run the upgrade: helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart -n mynamespace
Prevent future occurrences with --atomic flag which auto-rolls back on failure

root_cause: The --atomic flag makes helm upgrade automatically roll back to the previous release if the upgrade fails, and sets --wait implicitly, ensuring zero downtime and no stuck pending states.
fix: Run: helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart -n mynamespace --atomic --timeout 5m0s
Combine with --cleanup-on-fail to remove newly created resources on rollback: helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart --atomic --cleanup-on-fail --timeout 5m0s
Set appropriate --timeout based on pod startup time; default is 5m which may be too short for large Node.js workers
Check rollback in CI logs: helm history myapp -n mynamespace --max 5
Use --wait-for-jobs to also wait for Job completion hooks before marking success

symptoms: helm chart not reusable across environments
root_cause: Hardcoding environment-specific values (image tags, replica counts, resource limits, domain names) directly in templates instead of referencing .Values keys makes the chart impossible to reuse across environments and breaks GitOps workflows.
fix: Replace hardcoded strings in templates with .Values references: {{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}
Use required function for mandatory values: {{ required "image.tag is required" .Values.image.tag }}
Set safe defaults in values.yaml and override per-environment in values-prod.yaml
Never embed base64-encoded secrets in values.yaml; reference ESO SecretStore instead
Validate chart values with helm lint ./chart before committing
Use JSON schema validation: add values.schema.json to enforce types and required fields

symptoms: helm upgrade error nil has no fields
root_cause: Accessing a nested .Values key without checking if parent exists causes 'nil pointer evaluating interface{}' errors, common when optional sections in values.yaml are omitted in environment overrides.
fix: Use the 'default' function to guard optional values: {{ default "" .Values.optional.key }}
Check parent exists before accessing child: {{ if .Values.ingress }}{{ .Values.ingress.host }}{{ end }}
Use 'dig' function for deep optional access: {{ dig "ingress" "host" "default.example.com" .Values }}
Always define all nested keys in base values.yaml even if empty: ingress: {}
Run helm template ./chart --debug to get full template render output with line numbers
Enable strict mode in CI: helm lint --strict ./chart

symptoms: how to run database migration helm hook
root_cause: Helm hooks allow running Jobs at specific lifecycle points (pre-install, pre-upgrade, post-upgrade, pre-delete) enabling database migrations, seed data, or validation before the main workload starts.
fix: Annotate a Job template with: annotations: { "helm.sh/hook": pre-upgrade, "helm.sh/hook-weight": "-5", "helm.sh/hook-delete-policy": before-hook-creation }
Set hook-weight to control execution order (lower numbers run first)
Use hook-delete-policy: before-hook-creation to clean up previous hook Job before running again
For Node.js DB migrations: set command: ["node", "dist/migrate.js"] in hook Job spec
Set backoffLimit: 0 on migration Jobs to fail fast and block helm upgrade on error
Verify hook ran: kubectl get jobs -n mynamespace -l helm.sh/chart=myapp

symptoms: how to manage helm chart dependencies
root_cause: Helm chart dependencies declared in Chart.yaml allow composing charts (e.g. Redis, PostgreSQL) without copy-pasting, but require explicit dependency update and lock file management to ensure reproducible builds.
fix: Declare dependencies in Chart.yaml: dependencies: [{name: redis, version: '>=18.0.0', repository: 'https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami', condition: redis.enabled}]
Run: helm dependency update ./chart to download charts into charts/ and generate Chart.lock
Commit Chart.lock to git for reproducible builds; optionally commit charts/ vendored tarballs
Use condition: field to toggle subcharts: helm upgrade --set redis.enabled=false ./chart
Override subchart values with parent values.yaml key matching subchart name: redis: { auth: { enabled: true } }
Pin exact versions in Chart.lock via: helm dependency build ./chart

symptoms: how to preview helm upgrade changes
root_cause: The helm-diff plugin shows a kubectl-diff-style preview of what will change in a helm upgrade before applying, critical for GitOps review workflows and preventing accidental destructive changes.
fix: Install plugin: helm plugin install https://github.com/databus23/helm-diff
Preview upgrade diff: helm diff upgrade myapp ./chart -n mynamespace -f values-prod.yaml
Add --detailed-exitcode to fail CI if any changes detected: helm diff upgrade ... --detailed-exitcode; echo $?
Use --suppress-secrets to hide Secret data in diff output: helm diff upgrade ... --suppress-secrets
Integrate in GitOps PR pipeline: run helm diff in CI and post output as PR comment
Combine with helm template: helm diff upgrade myapp <(helm template myapp ./chart) to compare rendered manifests

symptoms: how to set up argocd gitops
argocd sync helm chart from git
root_cause: ArgoCD manages Helm releases declaratively via Application CRDs pointing to a Git repo + path, enabling automated drift detection and sync without manual helm upgrade commands.
fix: Create an Application manifest: apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1, kind: Application, metadata: {name: myapp, namespace: argocd}
Set spec.source: {repoURL: 'https://github.com/org/k8s-config', targetRevision: main, path: apps/myapp, helm: {valueFiles: [values-prod.yaml]}}
Set spec.destination: {server: 'https://kubernetes.default.svc', namespace: mynamespace}
Enable auto-sync: spec.syncPolicy: {automated: {prune: true, selfHeal: true}}
Apply to cluster: kubectl apply -f application.yaml -n argocd
Monitor sync: argocd app sync myapp --watch

root_cause: ArgoCD sync failures or OutOfSync status commonly result from resource drift (manual kubectl edits), missing CRDs, RBAC issues, or Helm hook resources not tracked by ArgoCD.
fix: Check app status: argocd app get myapp --show-operation
View detailed diff: argocd app diff myapp
Check sync operation logs: argocd app logs myapp
For resource drift from manual edits: argocd app sync myapp --force to overwrite
For missing CRDs: ensure CRDs are installed before app sync; use ArgoCD app-of-apps pattern with CRD app syncing first
Annotate Helm hooks to be ignored by ArgoCD if causing sync issues: argocd.argoproj.io/hook: PreSync
Check RBAC: argocd app sync myapp --dry-run to simulate

root_cause: ArgoCD sync waves and hooks allow ordering resource creation during a sync operation, enabling CRDs to be created before CRs, namespaces before workloads, and migrations before application pods.
fix: Add wave annotation to resources: argocd.argoproj.io/sync-wave: "0" for early resources, "1" for next, etc.
Resources with lower wave numbers sync first; default wave is 0
Use PreSync hook for migration Jobs: argocd.argoproj.io/hook: PreSync
Use PostSync hook for smoke tests: argocd.argoproj.io/hook: PostSync
Set hook deletion policy: argocd.argoproj.io/hook-delete-policy: HookSucceeded
Example: annotate Namespace with wave -1, CRDs with wave 0, Deployments with wave 1, HPA with wave 2

root_cause: ArgoCD auto-sync with prune:true deletes cluster resources not present in Git, which can cause unintended deletion of manually created resources or resources managed by another tool (e.g. Operator-created objects).
fix: Add annotation to protect resources from pruning: argocd.argoproj.io/managed-by-cluster-argocd: 'false'
Use resource exclusions in argocd-cm ConfigMap: resource.exclusions to skip certain resource types
For Operator-managed resources, set argocd.argoproj.io/hook: Skip to exclude from sync
Disable prune per resource: add argocd.argoproj.io/sync-options: Prune=false annotation
Review what would be pruned before enabling: argocd app sync myapp --dry-run | grep 'pruned'
Use selective sync to sync only specific resources: argocd app sync myapp --resource apps:Deployment:myapp

symptoms: how to set up flux gitops
root_cause: Flux v2 uses GitRepository, HelmRepository, HelmRelease, and Kustomization CRDs to reconcile cluster state from Git, providing a pull-based GitOps model without an argocd server.
fix: Bootstrap Flux: flux bootstrap github --owner=org --repository=k8s-config --path=clusters/production
Create HelmRepository source: flux create source helm bitnami --url https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami --interval 1h
Create HelmRelease: flux create helmrelease myapp --chart myapp --source HelmRepository/myapp-repo --values values-prod.yaml --interval 5m
Create Kustomization for plain manifests: flux create kustomization myapp-infra --source GitRepository/k8s-config --path ./infra --prune true --interval 10m
Check reconciliation: flux get helmreleases -A and flux get kustomizations -A
Force reconcile: flux reconcile helmrelease myapp -n mynamespace

root_cause: Flux reconciliation failures surface as conditions on the HelmRelease or Kustomization CRD and in flux controller logs; root causes include chart not found, values schema validation failure, or missing RBAC.
fix: Check HelmRelease status: kubectl describe helmrelease myapp -n mynamespace | grep -A 20 'Status:'
Get Flux events: kubectl get events -n flux-system --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
Check helm-controller logs: kubectl logs -n flux-system -l app=helm-controller --tail=100
Check source-controller logs for chart fetch errors: kubectl logs -n flux-system -l app=source-controller --tail=100
Force immediate reconcile: flux reconcile helmrelease myapp -n mynamespace --with-source
Suspend and resume to reset: flux suspend helmrelease myapp -n mynamespace && flux resume helmrelease myapp -n mynamespace

symptoms: how to manage helm values overrides per environment
root_cause: Managing per-environment Helm values through a consistent override hierarchy (base → environment → cluster) in Git enables auditable, diff-reviewable configuration changes without duplicating the entire values file.
fix: Structure values files: values.yaml (base defaults), values-staging.yaml (staging overrides), values-prod.yaml (prod overrides)
In GitOps (ArgoCD): set helm.valueFiles: [values.yaml, values-prod.yaml] in Application spec; files are merged left-to-right with right taking precedence
In CI/CD Helm: helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart -f values.yaml -f values-prod.yaml --set image.tag=$CI_SHA
Never use --set for complex structures; use -f with a generated values file for multi-key overrides
For secrets, reference ESO ExternalSecret names in values.yaml: existingSecret: myapp-db-creds
Validate merged values: helm template myapp ./chart -f values.yaml -f values-prod.yaml > rendered.yaml && kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f rendered.yaml

symptoms: helm upgrade failed what to check
root_cause: Helm upgrade failures can stem from template rendering errors, resource validation failures, pod scheduling issues, hook failures, or RBAC problems; a systematic checklist reduces mean time to resolution.
fix: 1. Check release status: helm status myapp -n mynamespace
2. View history: helm history myapp -n mynamespace
3. Render templates locally: helm template myapp ./chart -f values-prod.yaml > /tmp/rendered.yaml
4. Validate manifests: kubectl apply --dry-run=server -f /tmp/rendered.yaml
5. Check pod events: kubectl describe pod -n mynamespace -l app.kubernetes.io/name=myapp
6. Check hook Job status: kubectl get jobs -n mynamespace -l helm.sh/chart=myapp
7. Review helm controller logs if using Flux: kubectl logs -n flux-system -l app=helm-controller --tail=200
8. Roll back if needed: helm rollback myapp -n mynamespace

root_cause: Named templates in _helpers.tpl centralize label generation, selector logic, and resource naming, preventing inconsistency across manifests and making chart upgrades cleaner.
fix: Define in templates/_helpers.tpl: {{- define "myapp.labels" -}} helm.sh/chart: {{ .Chart.Name }}-{{ .Chart.Version }} app.kubernetes.io/name: {{ .Chart.Name }} app.kubernetes.io/instance: {{ .Release.Name }} app.kubernetes.io/version: {{ .Chart.AppVersion }} {{- end }}
Use in Deployment template: labels: {{- include "myapp.labels" . | nindent 4 }}
Define selector labels separately: {{- define "myapp.selectorLabels" -}} app.kubernetes.io/name: {{ .Chart.Name }} app.kubernetes.io/instance: {{ .Release.Name }} {{- end }}
Selector labels must NOT change after initial install (immutable); use selectorLabels template everywhere selectors are needed
Use include not template so output can be piped: {{- include "myapp.fullname" . | nindent 2 }}
Test helpers: helm template myapp ./chart --debug | grep 'app.kubernetes.io'

symptoms: helm release timeout waiting for rollout
root_cause: Helm upgrade --wait polls all managed resources until Ready, which for large charts with many Deployments/StatefulSets can exceed the default 5-minute timeout, causing false failures even when pods are progressing.
fix: Increase timeout proportional to workload size: helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart --wait --timeout 15m0s
For StatefulSets with PVC provisioning add extra buffer: --timeout 20m0s
Use --atomic with matching --timeout to auto-rollback only after extended wait
Split large chart into smaller focused charts to reduce resources per helm upgrade
Monitor upgrade in parallel: watch -n5 kubectl get pods -n mynamespace
Disable wait for non-critical releases and handle readiness at application level: helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart (no --wait)

symptoms: how to organize gitops git repo
root_cause: A consistent GitOps repository structure separates cluster infrastructure (CRDs, operators), application Helm releases, and environment-specific overrides, enabling parallel team workflows and clear blast radius for changes.
fix: Recommended layout: /clusters/<env>/ for Flux bootstrap or ArgoCD App-of-Apps, /apps/<appname>/ for Helm chart source, /infra/<component>/ for Rook-Ceph/Cilium/MetalLB operators
Store Helm charts in the same repo under /charts/ or reference external chart repos via HelmRepository CRD
Keep environment values in /environments/<env>/values-<appname>.yaml
Use App-of-Apps pattern in ArgoCD: one root Application pointing to /clusters/prod/ that generates all other Applications
Pin all external chart versions (HelmRelease.spec.chart.spec.version) and container image tags to SHAs for reproducibility
Gate changes to /infra/ behind separate CODEOWNERS review from /apps/ changes

root_cause: Kubernetes rejects updates to immutable fields like spec.selector on StatefulSets or spec.clusterIP on Services. Helm upgrade attempts a patch which the API server denies, causing the upgrade to fail with a 'field is immutable' error.
fix: Identify the immutable resource from error: helm upgrade output will show 'invalid: spec.selector: Invalid value'
For StatefulSets: delete and recreate (causes brief downtime): kubectl delete statefulset myapp -n mynamespace --cascade=orphan && helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart
Use --cascade=orphan to keep pods running during StatefulSet deletion, minimizing downtime
For Services with clusterIP change: kubectl delete svc myapp -n mynamespace; then re-run helm upgrade
Prevent future issues: avoid changing spec.selector labels after initial deployment; use selectorLabels named template consistently
Add pre-upgrade hook that deletes the StatefulSet before upgrade if selector change is expected

symptoms: node process killed by kernel OOM
root_cause: Node.js V8 heap is not cgroup-aware by default. Without --max-old-space-size, V8 sizes the heap based on total node RAM, not the container memory limit, causing the process to allocate beyond the limit and trigger a kernel OOM kill (exit 137).
fix: Set --max-old-space-size to ~75% of the container memory limit: e.g. for a 512Mi limit use `node --max-old-space-size=384 dist/index.js`
In Dockerfile: `CMD ["node", "--max-old-space-size=384", "dist/index.js"]`
Set memory request equal to limit to guarantee QoS class Guaranteed: `resources: { requests: { memory: '512Mi' }, limits: { memory: '512Mi' } }`
Add OOMKilled detection metric: alert when `kube_pod_container_status_last_terminated_reason{reason='OOMKilled'} > 0`
Check current heap usage via `process.memoryUsage()` and expose as a Prometheus gauge on /metrics
Use `kubectl describe pod <pod> | grep -A5 'Last State'` to confirm OOMKilled exit code

symptoms: how to set Node.js heap size for kubernetes
V8 heap too large for container memory limit
node js memory limit kubernetes best practice
root_cause: Setting --max-old-space-size without coordinating with the container memory limit leads to either wasted reservation (too low) or OOMKill (too high). The V8 heap is only one part of Node.js memory; RSS also includes stack, native buffers, and shared libraries.
fix: Formula: --max-old-space-size = floor(containerMemoryLimitMi * 0.75)
For 1Gi limit: `--max-old-space-size=768`; for 2Gi: `--max-old-space-size=1536`
Set via environment variable in Deployment: `env: [{ name: NODE_OPTIONS, value: '--max-old-space-size=768' }]`
Do NOT set --max-old-space-size higher than 90% of the limit to leave headroom for native addons and buffers
Validate with `kubectl exec <pod> -- node -e "console.log(v8.getHeapStatistics().heap_size_limit)"` and compare to limit

symptoms: how to set CPU requests and limits for Node.js
best practice resource requests limits node api pod
root_cause: CPU limits in Kubernetes use CFS quota, which throttles Node.js even when the node has spare CPU cycles. Setting CPU limits too low causes severe throttling on I/O-bound Node.js APIs; omitting CPU requests leads to poor scheduling decisions.
fix: Always set CPU requests; consider omitting CPU limits for latency-sensitive Node.js APIs (let BestEffort CPU burst)
If limits are required, set limit at least 4x the request: `requests: { cpu: '250m' }, limits: { cpu: '1000m' }`
Monitor CPU throttling via `container_cpu_cfs_throttled_seconds_total` in Prometheus
Alert when `rate(container_cpu_cfs_throttled_seconds_total[5m]) / rate(container_cpu_cfs_periods_total[5m]) > 0.25`
For worker pods (BullMQ), CPU limits are safer since they are not latency-sensitive; set limit 2x request

symptoms: Node.js API high latency with CPU limits set
cpu throttled nodejs kubernetes slow response
p99 latency spikes node pod with cpu limit
root_cause: Linux CFS scheduler enforces CPU limits via 100ms quota periods. If a Node.js process burns its CPU quota early in the period, it is throttled for the remainder, causing latency spikes even when node CPU is underutilized overall.
fix: Check throttling ratio: `kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu/cpu.stat | grep throttled`
Identify which pods are throttled: `kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu` combined with Prometheus `container_cpu_cfs_throttled_periods_total`
Increase CPU limit or remove it: patch with `kubectl patch deployment <name> --type=json -p '[{"op":"remove","path":"/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources/limits/cpu"}]'`
Enable CPU Manager static policy for Guaranteed QoS pods needing low jitter: `kubelet --cpu-manager-policy=static`
Use KEDA to horizontally scale before CPU throttling occurs rather than relying on vertical CPU limits

symptoms: how to configure liveness probe for Node.js API
liveness readiness startup probe nodejs kubernetes
node api pod restart loop probe failing
root_cause: Node.js APIs need properly tuned probes: startup probes for slow initialization (loading routes, DB connections), readiness probes to gate traffic, and liveness probes to detect deadlocks. Misconfigured probes cause unnecessary restarts or traffic routing to unready pods.
fix: Add a /healthz endpoint in Express: `app.get('/healthz', (req, res) => res.sendStatus(200))`
Add /readyz endpoint that checks DB and Redis connectivity before returning 200
Startup probe config: `startupProbe: { httpGet: { path: /healthz, port: 3000 }, failureThreshold: 30, periodSeconds: 10 }` — allows up to 5 minutes for initialization
Readiness probe: `readinessProbe: { httpGet: { path: /readyz, port: 3000 }, initialDelaySeconds: 5, periodSeconds: 10, failureThreshold: 3 }`
Liveness probe: `livenessProbe: { httpGet: { path: /healthz, port: 3000 }, initialDelaySeconds: 15, periodSeconds: 20, failureThreshold: 3 }` — liveness should be simpler/faster than readiness
Never use the same deep-check endpoint for liveness and readiness; liveness failure = pod restart, readiness failure = traffic removal only

symptoms: liveness probe causes cascading restart loop
node pod restart OOMKilled then liveness fails
liveness probe too aggressive nodejs kubernetes
root_cause: Using a deep liveness probe (one that checks databases, external services, or is slow) causes pod restarts when dependencies are temporarily unavailable, creating cascading failures instead of graceful degradation.
fix: Keep liveness probe minimal: only check that the event loop is running, e.g. a lightweight in-process HTTP response
Move dependency checks to readinessProbe so that only traffic is cut, not the pod restarted
Set liveness probe with generous timeouts: `timeoutSeconds: 5, failureThreshold: 3, periodSeconds: 20`
Do NOT include Redis/Postgres/NATS pings in livenessProbe endpoints
Use a startup probe with high failureThreshold to handle slow boot instead of setting large initialDelaySeconds on liveness

symptoms: how to handle SIGTERM graceful shutdown nodejs kubernetes
node pod not draining connections before termination
root_cause: When Kubernetes terminates a pod, it sends SIGTERM and waits terminationGracePeriodSeconds (default 30s) before SIGKILL. Node.js processes must handle SIGTERM by stopping the HTTP server from accepting new connections, draining in-flight requests, and cleanly closing resources.
fix: Register SIGTERM handler: `process.on('SIGTERM', async () => { await server.close(); await closeDbPool(); process.exit(0); })`
Call `server.close()` to stop accepting new connections but finish existing ones
Set terminationGracePeriodSeconds in Deployment: `spec.terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60` for APIs with long-running requests
Use `http-graceful-shutdown` npm package or implement with a drain counter tracking active connections
Set `preStop` lifecycle hook to delay SIGTERM by a few seconds to allow kube-proxy/Endpoints to deregister: `lifecycle: { preStop: { exec: { command: ['sleep', '5'] } } }`
Verify graceful shutdown: `kubectl delete pod <pod> && kubectl logs <pod> --previous | grep 'shutdown'`

symptoms: how to drain BullMQ worker on SIGTERM kubernetes
bullmq worker graceful shutdown pod termination
bullmq jobs lost on pod restart kubernetes
root_cause: BullMQ workers that are abruptly terminated may leave jobs in an active state without completing them. A proper graceful shutdown drains the current job batch, pauses the queue, and allows in-flight jobs to finish before the process exits.
fix: Call `await worker.close()` in the SIGTERM handler — this stops the worker from picking up new jobs and waits for the current job to finish
Set a job lock duration greater than the expected job duration: `lockDuration: 30000` (30s) in worker options
Set terminationGracePeriodSeconds > longest expected job duration + 10s buffer in the Deployment spec
Use `worker.on('completed', ...)` and `worker.on('failed', ...)` to log job outcomes before shutdown
Mark long-running jobs with `removeOnComplete: false` so they remain traceable after worker restart
Pattern: `process.on('SIGTERM', async () => { await worker.close(); await queueScheduler.close(); process.exit(0); })`

symptoms: how to build multi-stage Docker image for Node.js
reduce node docker image size for kubernetes
root_cause: Single-stage Node.js Docker builds include devDependencies, build tools, and source maps in the final image, bloating image size and increasing attack surface. Multi-stage builds separate the build environment from the runtime image.
fix: Stage 1 (builder): `FROM node:20-alpine AS builder` — install all deps and compile TypeScript
Stage 2 (runtime): `FROM node:20-alpine` — copy only `dist/`, `node_modules` (production only), and `package.json`
In builder stage: `RUN npm ci && npm run build`
In runtime stage: `COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist && COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules`
Run as non-root: `RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup && USER appuser`
Set NODE_ENV=production in runtime stage to prevent dev-only behaviors

symptoms: how to use distroless image for Node.js kubernetes
distroless nodejs security hardening kubernetes pods
smallest nodejs container image for kubernetes
root_cause: Standard node:alpine images include a shell and package manager which are unnecessary for production and increase the attack surface. Google's distroless images contain only the Node.js runtime and application files with no shell, package manager, or OS utilities.
fix: Use distroless as final stage: `FROM gcr.io/distroless/nodejs20-debian12` in the runtime stage
Copy compiled artifacts from builder: `COPY --from=builder /app/dist /app/dist`
Set CMD without shell: `CMD ["/app/dist/index.js"]` (distroless uses node as entrypoint automatically)
Note: distroless has no shell so `kubectl exec` interactive debugging is not possible; use ephemeral debug containers: `kubectl debug -it <pod> --image=node:20-alpine --target=<container>`
Scan image with `trivy image gcr.io/distroless/nodejs20-debian12` to confirm minimal CVE surface
For non-root enforcement add `securityContext: { runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000 }` in pod spec

symptoms: nodejs pod OOMKilled how to diagnose
node process killed memory debugging kubernetes
root_cause: Diagnosing OOMKilled (exit 137) requires correlating pod restart events, container terminated reason, V8 heap usage metrics, and kernel OOM logs to identify whether the cause is a heap leak, native buffer growth, or an undersized memory limit.
fix: Check exit reason: `kubectl describe pod <pod> -n <ns> | grep -A10 'Last State'` — look for `Reason: OOMKilled`
Check node kernel OOM logs: `kubectl get events -n <ns> | grep OOM` and `journalctl -k | grep 'oom-kill'` on the node
Expose V8 heap metrics via `prom-client`: `const heapUsed = new Gauge({ name: 'nodejs_heap_used_bytes', help: 'V8 heap used' }); heapUsed.set(process.memoryUsage().heapUsed)`
Review Grafana dashboard for `nodejs_heap_used_bytes` trend over time before the OOM event
Enable --expose-gc and call gc() periodically in load tests to distinguish heap leaks from allocation spikes
Compare `process.memoryUsage().rss` vs container memory limit: `kubectl exec <pod> -- node -e "console.log(process.memoryUsage())"`

symptoms: what to check before deploying nodejs to kubernetes
root_cause: Node.js deployments on Kubernetes require coordinating V8 heap sizing, resource requests/limits, probe configuration, graceful shutdown, and image hardening. Missing any one element commonly causes production incidents.
fix: Set --max-old-space-size to 75% of memory limit via NODE_OPTIONS env var
Set memory requests = memory limits (Guaranteed QoS) for API pods
Configure startupProbe, readinessProbe, and livenessProbe with appropriate thresholds
Implement SIGTERM handler with server.close() and resource cleanup
Set terminationGracePeriodSeconds >= longest request timeout + 15s
Add preStop sleep hook (5-10s) to allow Endpoints deregistration before SIGTERM
Use multi-stage Dockerfile with production-only node_modules
Run container as non-root user with read-only root filesystem where possible
Set CPU requests; evaluate CPU limits based on latency requirements

symptoms: readiness probe fails intermittently nodejs kubernetes
node api marked unready under load
readinessProbe timeout causing pod removed from service
root_cause: Under high load, Node.js event loop lag increases, causing HTTP health-check endpoints to respond slowly or not at all within the probe timeoutSeconds, triggering false readiness failures and temporarily removing the pod from Service Endpoints.
fix: Increase readiness probe timeoutSeconds: `timeoutSeconds: 10` instead of default 1s
Monitor event loop lag with `prom-client` and `perf_hooks`: `const lag = new Gauge({ name: 'nodejs_eventloop_lag_seconds', help: 'Event loop lag' })`
Alert when event loop lag exceeds 100ms: `nodejs_eventloop_lag_seconds > 0.1`
Separate health-check server on a dedicated port to avoid being blocked by the main app event loop
Use `--max-semi-space-size` to tune GC frequency and reduce event loop pauses under load
Scale horizontally with KEDA before event loop lag degrades probe responses

symptoms: node_modules copied into kubernetes container image
large nodejs docker image kubernetes slow pull
devDependencies in production nodejs kubernetes image
root_cause: Copying all node_modules including devDependencies into the container image creates images that are hundreds of MB larger than necessary, increasing pull times, storage costs, and security attack surface.
fix: In multi-stage build, install production deps only in the runtime stage: `RUN npm ci --omit=dev`
Alternatively, prune in builder stage: `RUN npm prune --production` before copying node_modules to runtime stage
Use `.dockerignore` to exclude: `node_modules`, `.git`, `*.test.ts`, `coverage/`, `Dockerfile`
Verify final image size: `docker build -t myapp . && docker images myapp`
Aim for Node.js API images under 200MB using Alpine base; distroless typically 100-150MB

symptoms: startup probe not configured nodejs slow startup kubernetes
pod killed before startup complete initialDelaySeconds too short
node api takes 30 seconds to start kubernetes probe
root_cause: Node.js APIs with slow startup (loading large config files, waiting for DB migrations, warming up connection pools) fail liveness or readiness probes before they are ready if startupProbe is absent or initialDelaySeconds is set too short.
fix: Replace large initialDelaySeconds with startupProbe: allows per-attempt retries instead of a single flat wait
Example startupProbe: `httpGet: { path: /healthz, port: 3000 }, failureThreshold: 30, periodSeconds: 10` — up to 5 minutes
Once startupProbe succeeds, Kubernetes activates liveness and readiness probes
In Node.js app, only respond 200 on /healthz after all async initialization is complete: `app.listen(port, () => { appReady = true; })`
Log startup duration with `console.log('App ready in', Date.now() - startTime, 'ms')` to tune probe thresholds
