symptoms: nodejs kubernetes pod CPU throttling diagnosis
how to check if node pod is cpu throttled kubernetes
root_cause: CPU throttling in Kubernetes is silent from the application's perspective — Node.js does not receive any signal when it is throttled. Diagnosis requires Prometheus CFS metrics or node-level cgroup inspection.
fix: Query throttling percentage in Prometheus: `rate(container_cpu_cfs_throttled_periods_total{container='<name>'}[5m]) / rate(container_cpu_cfs_periods_total{container='<name>'}[5m]) * 100`
Check cgroup quota on the node: `kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu/cpu.cfs_quota_us` and `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu/cpu.cfs_period_us`
Use `kubectl top pod <pod> --containers` to see current CPU consumption vs request
Correlate throttling spikes with request latency in Grafana using Tempo traces
Set Grafana alert: throttled percentage > 25% for 5 minutes triggers PagerDuty
If throttled, either increase CPU limit, remove CPU limit, or scale out with KEDA HPA

symptoms: how to configure probes for BullMQ worker kubernetes
worker pod probe configuration no http server
root_cause: BullMQ worker pods often do not expose an HTTP server, making httpGet probes inappropriate. Worker health should be measured by queue connectivity and whether the worker is actively processing.
fix: Use exec probe with a custom health-check script: `livenessProbe: { exec: { command: ['node', 'scripts/healthcheck.js'] }, periodSeconds: 30, failureThreshold: 3 }`
healthcheck.js: connect to Redis, call `queue.isPaused()`, exit 0 if connected, exit 1 if not
Alternatively start a minimal HTTP server on a separate port within the worker: `http.createServer((req, res) => res.writeHead(worker.isRunning() ? 200 : 503)).listen(9090)`
For readiness, check if the BullMQ worker is in 'running' state: `worker.isRunning()` returns boolean
Set startupProbe with failureThreshold: 12 and periodSeconds: 5 to allow Redis connection establishment on startup

symptoms: how to structure nodejs kubernetes deployment resource config
nodejs api worker resource architecture kubernetes
resource strategy node api vs worker pods kubernetes
root_cause: Node.js API pods (latency-sensitive, bursty CPU) and worker pods (throughput-oriented, predictable CPU) have different optimal resource strategies. Applying the same resource config to both leads to either throttling or waste.
fix: API pods: set CPU request (e.g. 250m), omit CPU limit or set high limit (4x request), set memory request=limit (Guaranteed QoS), use readiness/liveness/startup probes with httpGet
Worker pods: set CPU request and limit (2x request acceptable since throughput not latency matters), set memory request=limit, use exec or TCP socket probes
Both: set --max-old-space-size via NODE_OPTIONS to 75% of memory limit
Both: implement SIGTERM graceful shutdown; API drains HTTP, workers drain BullMQ jobs
Use separate Deployments for API and worker to allow independent scaling with KEDA

symptoms: node pod restarting kubernetes how to read logs
kubectl logs previous container nodejs crash
node process exit kubernetes log investigation
root_cause: When a Node.js pod crashes and restarts, the logs of the previous container instance are critical for diagnosis. Standard `kubectl logs` shows the current container; the crash logs require the --previous flag.
fix: Get previous container logs: `kubectl logs <pod> -n <ns> --previous`
Get logs with timestamps: `kubectl logs <pod> --previous --timestamps=true`
If container restarted many times, get all events: `kubectl describe pod <pod> -n <ns>` and review 'Events' section
Stream logs in real-time during crash reproduction: `kubectl logs -f <pod> -n <ns>`
Check Node.js unhandled rejection logs: ensure `process.on('unhandledRejection', (err) => { console.error(err); process.exit(1); })` is set so crash reason appears in logs
Aggregate crash logs to Loki with Fluent Bit and query: `{namespace='<ns>', pod='<pod>'} |= 'UnhandledPromiseRejection'`

symptoms: nodejs docker image tag strategy kubernetes
how to version nodejs container images kubernetes gitops
image tag best practice kubernetes helm gitops
root_cause: Using mutable image tags like `latest` in Kubernetes Deployments causes unpredictable rollouts, makes rollbacks unreliable, and prevents GitOps tools from detecting image updates. Every production Node.js image should use an immutable content-addressed or commit-SHA tag.
fix: Tag images with git commit SHA in CI: `docker build -t myregistry/myapp:$(git rev-parse --short HEAD) .`
Never use `latest` or branch-name tags in Helm values for production; use SHA or semver
Set `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent` with immutable SHA tags to avoid unnecessary pulls
Configure Flux ImageUpdateAutomation or ArgoCD Image Updater to automatically update image SHA in GitOps repo
Add image digest pinning in Helm values: `image: { repository: myregistry/myapp, tag: 'sha-abc1234' }`
Enforce via admission policy: use Kyverno ClusterPolicy to reject Deployments with `latest` image tag

symptoms: how to prevent service disruption during node drain
root_cause: Without a PodDisruptionBudget, voluntary disruptions like node drains or cluster upgrades can simultaneously evict all replicas of a Deployment, causing full service outages. PDBs enforce a minimum availability guarantee during such operations.
fix: kubectl create pdb my-app-pdb --selector=app=my-app --min-available=2 -n production
Or declaratively: apiVersion: policy/v1, kind: PodDisruptionBudget with spec.minAvailable: '50%'
Use maxUnavailable instead of minAvailable when you want to express it as a disruption ceiling
Verify PDB status: kubectl get pdb -n production -o wide
Check disruptions allowed: kubectl describe pdb my-app-pdb -n production | grep 'Allowed disruptions'
Ensure replica count > minAvailable, otherwise drain will block indefinitely

root_cause: A PodDisruptionBudget with minAvailable equal to the current number of running pods prevents the eviction API from removing any pod. This causes kubectl drain to block waiting for the PDB constraint to be satisfiable, which never happens if replicas are not scaled up.
fix: Check which PDB is blocking: kubectl get pdb -A | grep '0 allowed'
Inspect the blocking PDB: kubectl describe pdb <name> -n <namespace>
Temporarily scale up the Deployment before draining: kubectl scale deploy <name> -n <ns> --replicas=<current+1>
If PDB is misconfigured for single-replica app, delete it: kubectl delete pdb <name> -n <ns>
Force drain only as last resort: kubectl drain <node> --disable-eviction=true --ignore-daemonsets
After drain, restore original replica count and recreate PDB correctly

symptoms: how to spread pods across availability zones
pods all scheduled on same node
root_cause: Without topologySpreadConstraints, the default scheduler may place multiple replicas on the same node or zone, creating a single point of failure. TopologySpreadConstraints enforce even distribution across failure domains.
fix: Add to pod spec: topologySpreadConstraints with topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname and maxSkew: 1
Set whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule to hard-enforce distribution
Use labelSelector matching your pod labels to count existing pods correctly
For zone spreading add a second constraint with topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
Verify node labels exist: kubectl get nodes --show-labels | grep topology.kubernetes.io/zone
Check spread: kubectl get pods -o wide | awk '{print $7}' | sort | uniq -c

symptoms: pods not scheduling on specific nodes
root_cause: requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution affinity rules prevent pod scheduling entirely if no node matches the label selector. Misconfigured label keys or missing node labels silently block scheduling, leaving pods in Pending state.
fix: Check pod events: kubectl describe pod <name> -n <ns> | grep -A5 Events
List node labels: kubectl get nodes --show-labels
Verify label exists on target nodes: kubectl get node <node-name> -o jsonpath='{.metadata.labels}'
Add missing label: kubectl label node <node-name> disktype=ssd
Switch to preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution for soft affinity if hard requirement is too strict
Use matchExpressions with operator: In and correct values array

symptoms: how to prevent multiple replicas on the same node
root_cause: Pod anti-affinity with requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution and topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname ensures the scheduler never places two pods of the same Deployment on the same node, improving fault tolerance for stateless Node.js API services.
fix: Add affinity.podAntiAffinity to pod spec with requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution
Set labelSelector.matchLabels to match the pod's own labels (e.g., app: my-api)
Set topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
Note: this limits max replicas to number of nodes; use preferred if cluster is small
Test with: kubectl get pods -o wide | grep my-api
Combine with topologySpreadConstraints for more nuanced control

symptoms: how to dedicate nodes for specific workloads
reserve nodes for GPU or critical workloads
root_cause: Taints on nodes repel all pods that do not have a matching toleration. This is the primary mechanism to dedicate nodes for specific workload classes (e.g., GPU nodes, high-memory nodes, or critical system pods) in on-premise Kubernetes clusters.
fix: Add taint to node: kubectl taint nodes <node-name> dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule
Add matching toleration to pod spec: tolerations: [{key: dedicated, operator: Equal, value: gpu, effect: NoSchedule}]
Combine with nodeAffinity to both attract and repel correctly: pods tolerate the taint AND prefer/require the node
List taints on all nodes: kubectl get nodes -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,TAINTS:.spec.taints
Remove taint: kubectl taint nodes <node-name> dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule-
Use effect: NoExecute to evict already-running pods that lack tolerations

root_cause: Kubelet eviction manager monitors node resources and evicts pods when thresholds are breached (e.g., memory.available < 100Mi or nodefs.available < 10%). Pods without requests/limits are evicted first (BestEffort QoS), then Burstable, then Guaranteed.
fix: Check node conditions: kubectl describe node <name> | grep -A10 Conditions
View eviction events: kubectl get events -A --field-selector reason=Evicted
Inspect kubelet eviction thresholds: systemctl cat kubelet | grep eviction, or check /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
Set memory requests/limits on all pods to achieve Guaranteed QoS and avoid eviction
Use PriorityClass to protect critical pods: kubectl get priorityclasses
Monitor node memory: kubectl top nodes, and set up Prometheus alert on kube_node_status_condition{condition='MemoryPressure',status='true'}

symptoms: critical pods evicted before less important pods
root_cause: Without PriorityClass assignments, all pods have equal scheduling and eviction priority. During resource contention, kubelet evicts pods based on QoS class and resource usage, which may not align with business-criticality. PriorityClass provides explicit scheduling precedence.
fix: Create a high-priority class: kubectl apply -f - with apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1, kind: PriorityClass, metadata.name: high-priority, value: 1000000
Create a low-priority class with value: 100 for batch/worker jobs
Reference in pod spec: priorityClassName: high-priority
List existing classes: kubectl get priorityclasses
System-critical classes (system-cluster-critical, system-node-critical) exist by default with values 2000000000 and 2000001000
Verify pod priority: kubectl get pod <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.priority}'

root_cause: CrashLoopBackOff occurs when a container repeatedly exits with a non-zero status code. Kubernetes applies exponential backoff (10s, 20s, 40s up to 5min) between restarts. Common causes: application crash on startup, missing env vars, failed config mount, OOM kill, or misconfigured command/args.
fix: Check exit code: kubectl describe pod <name> -n <ns> | grep 'Last State' -A5
View current logs: kubectl logs <pod> -n <ns>
View previous container logs: kubectl logs <pod> -n <ns> --previous
Check OOM kill: kubectl describe pod <name> | grep -i oom, and dmesg on the node
Exec into a debug container if pod is running briefly: kubectl debug -it <pod> --image=busybox --target=<container>
Verify all required environment variables and secrets are mounted: kubectl describe pod | grep -A20 Environment

root_cause: ImagePullBackOff means kubelet cannot pull the container image. Causes include: image tag does not exist, private registry requires authentication, registry is unreachable from the node, or imagePullPolicy Always hits a broken registry.
fix: Inspect error: kubectl describe pod <name> -n <ns> | grep -A5 'Failed to pull image'
Verify image exists: docker manifest inspect <image>:<tag> or crane digest <image>:<tag>
Check imagePullSecret exists in same namespace: kubectl get secret regcred -n <ns>
Create pull secret: kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred --docker-server=<server> --docker-username=<user> --docker-password=<pass> -n <ns>
Reference secret in pod spec: imagePullSecrets: [{name: regcred}]
Test registry reachability from node: crictl pull <image>:<tag> on the affected node

root_cause: A pod in Pending state has not been scheduled to any node. The scheduler logs a reason in pod events: insufficient resources, no nodes match affinity/taint rules, PVC unbound, or resource quota exceeded. Each cause requires a different remediation path.
fix: Read scheduler events: kubectl describe pod <name> -n <ns> | tail -20
Check resource availability: kubectl describe nodes | grep -A5 'Allocated resources'
Check ResourceQuota limits: kubectl describe resourcequota -n <ns>
Check if PVC is bound: kubectl get pvc -n <ns>
Check taints vs tolerations mismatch: kubectl get nodes -o json | jq '.items[].spec.taints'
Scale up node pool or reduce pod resource requests if nodes are fully packed

symptoms: container running as root security risk
root_cause: Containers running as root with full Linux capabilities present a significant attack surface. If a container is compromised, root access with default capabilities allows host escape or privilege escalation. Kubernetes securityContext fields enforce least-privilege at pod and container level.
fix: Set pod-level: securityContext: {runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000, fsGroup: 2000}
Set container-level: securityContext: {readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false}
Drop all capabilities and add only what is needed: capabilities: {drop: [ALL], add: [NET_BIND_SERVICE]}
Use seccompProfile: {type: RuntimeDefault} to apply default seccomp filtering
Enforce with PodSecurity admission: label namespace with pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted
Validate with kubectl-neat and kube-score: kube-score score deployment.yaml

symptoms: container needs writable filesystem but using readOnlyRootFilesystem
app crashes with read-only filesystem error
root_cause: Setting readOnlyRootFilesystem: true is a security best practice but breaks applications that write to the root filesystem (logs, temp files, pid files). The fix is to mount emptyDir volumes at the specific writable paths rather than abandoning the read-only policy.
fix: Identify writable paths needed by the app (e.g., /tmp, /var/cache, /app/logs)
Add emptyDir volume: volumes: [{name: tmp-dir, emptyDir: {}}]
Mount at the specific path: volumeMounts: [{name: tmp-dir, mountPath: /tmp}]
Keep readOnlyRootFilesystem: true on the container securityContext
For /var/run (pid files): add another emptyDir mount at /var/run
Test with: kubectl exec <pod> -- touch /tmp/test && echo 'writable'

symptoms: how to restrict network traffic between pods
root_cause: By default, Kubernetes allows all pod-to-pod communication. In a multi-tenant or security-sensitive cluster (e.g., with Cilium CNI), NetworkPolicy resources enforce ingress/egress rules at the pod level. A default-deny policy is the baseline for namespace isolation.
fix: Apply default-deny-all ingress: apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1, kind: NetworkPolicy, spec: {podSelector: {}, policyTypes: [Ingress]}
Apply default-deny-all egress similarly with policyTypes: [Egress]
Allow specific ingress: add ingress rules with from.podSelector matching allowed sources
Allow DNS egress (required): egress: [{ports: [{protocol: UDP, port: 53}]}]
With Cilium, use CiliumNetworkPolicy CRD for L7 HTTP rules and FQDN-based egress
Verify policy: kubectl get networkpolicies -n <ns>, and test with: kubectl exec <pod> -- curl -s <target-pod-ip>

symptoms: pods not creating due to quota
root_cause: ResourceQuota objects cap cumulative resource consumption in a namespace. When a Deployment tries to create a pod that would exceed the quota (CPU, memory, pod count, PVC count), the API server rejects the creation with a quota exceeded error, leaving the pod uncreated.
fix: Check quota status: kubectl describe resourcequota -n <ns>
View current usage vs limits: kubectl get resourcequota -n <ns> -o yaml | grep -A20 status
Increase quota: kubectl edit resourcequota <name> -n <ns> and adjust spec.hard values
Alternatively, reduce resource requests on pods to fit within quota
Add LimitRange to enforce default requests/limits so pods don't block quota without explicit values
Monitor with: kubectl get events -n <ns> --field-selector reason=FailedCreate | grep exceeded

symptoms: how to set default resource limits in namespace
root_cause: Without LimitRange, pods can be created without resource requests or limits, resulting in BestEffort QoS class (first to be evicted under node pressure) and potential resource starvation of other workloads. LimitRange sets namespace-wide defaults and enforces min/max bounds.
fix: Create LimitRange: apiVersion: v1, kind: LimitRange with type: Container
Set default requests: default: {cpu: 100m, memory: 128Mi}
Set default limits: defaultRequest: {cpu: 100m, memory: 128Mi}, max: {cpu: 2, memory: 2Gi}
Apply: kubectl apply -f limitrange.yaml -n <ns>
Verify: kubectl describe limitrange -n <ns>
Existing pods are not affected; new pods created without explicit requests inherit defaults

symptoms: how to set up RBAC for service account
root_cause: Misconfigured RBAC is a leading Kubernetes security issue. Granting overly broad permissions (cluster-admin) or forgetting to scope to namespace exposes sensitive resources. A systematic checklist prevents common RBAC mistakes.
fix: Create a dedicated ServiceAccount per workload: kubectl create serviceaccount my-app -n <ns>
Prefer Role (namespace-scoped) over ClusterRole unless cross-namespace access is genuinely needed
Create Role with minimal verbs: kubectl create role my-app-role --verb=get,list,watch --resource=configmaps -n <ns>
Bind Role to ServiceAccount: kubectl create rolebinding my-app-binding --role=my-app-role --serviceaccount=<ns>:my-app -n <ns>
Audit permissions: kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:my-app
Never bind cluster-admin to application service accounts; use audit logs to detect it: kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.roleRef.name=="cluster-admin")'

symptoms: kubectl get pods shows many Evicted
Evicted pod status not cleaned up
root_cause: Evicted pods remain in the API server with status Evicted and are not garbage collected automatically unless terminated pod TTL is configured. In active clusters with frequent evictions (due to node pressure), these accumulate and inflate API server storage and etcd size.
fix: Delete all evicted pods in a namespace: kubectl get pods -n <ns> --field-selector=status.phase=Failed -o name | xargs kubectl delete -n <ns>
Delete evicted pods across all namespaces: kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase=Failed | grep Evicted | awk '{print $1, $2}' | xargs -n2 kubectl delete pod -n
Enable TTL-after-finished controller for Jobs; for Pods enable: --terminated-pod-gc-threshold=100 on kube-controller-manager
Set pod TTL: add kube-controller-manager flag --terminated-pod-gc-threshold=<N> to garbage collect after N terminated pods accumulate
Monitor etcd size: etcdctl endpoint status --cluster -w table
Set up Prometheus alert: count(kube_pod_status_phase{phase='Failed'}) > 100

symptoms: pods preempted by higher priority workloads
root_cause: When a high-priority pod cannot be scheduled due to resource constraints, the Kubernetes scheduler preempts (evicts) lower-priority pods to free resources. Without careful PriorityClass design, batch or worker pods may be continuously preempted by API pods, causing job failures.
fix: Define three tiers: critical (value: 1000000), standard (value: 100000), batch (value: 1000)
Assign PriorityClass to Node.js API pods: priorityClassName: critical
Assign batch/worker pods: priorityClassName: batch
Set preemptionPolicy: Never on batch PriorityClass to prevent batch pods from preempting others while still being preemptable themselves
Monitor preemption events: kubectl get events -A --field-selector reason=Preempting
Use PodDisruptionBudgets on critical workloads to limit disruption from preemption

symptoms: how to audit RBAC permissions in cluster
root_cause: Over time, RBAC permissions accumulate and drift from the principle of least privilege. Regular auditing of RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings is essential to detect service accounts with excessive permissions, especially after Helm chart installs that default to broad RBAC.
fix: List all ClusterRoleBindings with cluster-admin: kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.roleRef.name=="cluster-admin") | .subjects[]?.name'
List all RoleBindings per namespace: kubectl get rolebindings -A -o wide
Check what a ServiceAccount can do: kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa-name> -n <ns>
Use rbac-tool: kubectl rbac-tool lookup system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa-name>
Use kube-audit or kube-bench for automated RBAC scanning: kube-bench run --targets=policies
Remove unused ServiceAccounts: if a Deployment's SA is not referenced, delete the binding
